docs.title: Crucible
docs.text:
Crucible is a CLI-first, modular EVM bridge security research testbed for historical exploit reconstruction, deterministic local reruns, baseline reset, structured research artifacts, and plug-and-play attack packs.

It ships with 11 attack packs: 6 benchmark reproductions (Wormhole, Nomad, Poly Network, Horizon, BNB Bridge, Multichain), 2 demo packs, 1 LLM agent attack framework, 1 L4 multi-chain validation pack, and 1 Nomad bridge demo — totalling $1.9B+ in historical losses. 5 at L2 reproducibility, 1 narrative (Multichain at L1), plus 4 fixture and demonstration packs.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Prerequisites: Python 3.11+, Foundry (anvil, forge, cast)
uv sync
crucible init
crucible doctor                           # validate environment
crucible status                           # show running chains
pytest tests/unit/                         # 782 unit tests, no external deps

Set archive RPC URLs in .env: 

ETH_RPC_URL=https://eth.llamarpc.com

Then run: 

crucible fork --topology single
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-baseline

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: Capability How Fork any EVM chain at any block crucible fork --topology <name> Deterministic replay Fixed gas/fee/timestamp/order — opt-out, not opt-in 3-tier reset soft (<5s) → medium (<30s) → hard (<5min) Structured artifacts 7 JSONL streams + SHA-256 integrity per run Plugin SDK AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, AgentPlugin Auto-extract attacks crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name <name> Publication gates 6 research-quality checks before every run Cache provenance Cold/warm/strict/permissive cache fingerprinting Experiment comparison crucible diff <run_a> <run_b> Export bundles Self-contained tarballs with integrity manifests Streamlit dashboard crucible dashboard or streamlit run dashboard/app.py Interactive analysis crucible notebook — Jupyter with crucible.analysis helpers Post-run analytics from crucible.analysis import load_run, load_gas, ... — pandas DataFrames

docs.title: USENIX AE Walkthrough — Crucible
docs.text: Target Time: <30 minutes for demo path Prerequisites: Docker, git, ~10GB disk, 8GB RAM, internet access

docs.title: Badge Claims
docs.text: Badge Claimed How to Verify Available [ok] Public repo with DOI, docker compose build succeeds Functional [ok] Demo experiment runs, artifacts produced, report generated Reproduced [ok] Cross-provider rerun produces matching results

docs.title: Quick Start (5 min)
docs.text:
# 0. Prerequisites
git clone <repo-url> --branch <release-tag>
cd crucible

# 1. Build
docker compose build

# 2. Verify environment
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor

docs.title: Demo Experiment (10 min)
docs.text:
# Safe preview first
docker compose run --rm orchestrator --dry-run experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml

# Run the complete demo experiment
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml

# Expected output:
#   experiment artifacts: logs/demo_experiment/DS-2026-00001/

# Check the run manifest
docker compose run --rm orchestrator logs list

# View history
docker compose run --rm orchestrator history
# Or with filters:
docker compose run --rm orchestrator history --outcome success --limit 5

docs.title: Publication Mode Experiment (5 min)
docs.text:
# Run with research-quality gates enabled
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --publication-mode

# This enforces:
# - Evidence completeness check against fidelity level
# - Baseline verification
# - Foundry version check
# - Provenance completeness

docs.title: Attack Pack Extraction (5 min — requires archive RPC)
docs.text:
# Auto-generate an attack pack from an on-chain transaction
docker compose run --rm orchestrator attack extract <tx_hash> --name my_attack --chain ethereum

# This generates:
# - attacks/my_attack/plugin.yaml, my_attack.py, evidence.yaml
# - scenarios/my_attack_pre_exploit/scenario.yaml
# - ABI cached in abis/_cache/

# Validate the generated plugin
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin validate attacks/my_attack

docs.title: Set Up A Chain Fork (5 min — requires archive RPC)
docs.text:
# Fork + bootstrap in one command
docker compose run --rm orchestrator setup --topology single

# Or fork manually
docker compose run --rm orchestrator fork --topology single

# Capture and verify baseline
docker compose run --rm orchestrator baseline capture demo_scenario --topology single
docker compose run --rm orchestrator baseline verify demo_scenario

docs.title: What to Expect
docs.text:
After running the demo experiment, you should see:

logs/demo_experiment/DS-2026-00001/
├── run_manifest.json          # Full experiment metadata + provenance
├── log_integrity.json         # SHA-256 hashes of all log streams
├── publication_report.json    # Machine-readable publication report
├── provenance.json            # Standalone provenance metadata
├── assertions.json            # Structured assertion results
├── artifact_bundle_manifest.json  # SHA-256 of all export files
├── run.jsonl                  # Step-by-step run log
├── events.jsonl               # Contract event log
├── storage_diffs.jsonl        # Storage slot modifications
├── gas_profile.jsonl          # Gas usage per step
├── cross_chain.jsonl          # Cross-chain message tracking
├── report.md                  # Human-readable report
└── REPRODUCE.md               # Reproduction instructions

docs.title: Verifying Success
docs.text:
# 1. Check experiment outcome
docker compose run --rm orchestrator history --outcome success

# 2. Verify report was generated
cat logs/*/*/report.md | head -20

# 3. Check artifact integrity
cat logs/*/*/log_integrity.json

# 4. Export publication bundle
docker compose run --rm orchestrator export <run_id>

docs.title: Minimal Hardware Requirements
docs.text: Resource Minimum Recommended CPU 2 cores 4+ cores RAM 8 GB 16 GB Disk 10 GB free 50 GB+ (for cache) Network Broadband Broadband

docs.title: Known Limitations
docs.text:
• RPC dependency: Archive nodes are required for historical fork reproduction
  • Gas non-determinism: Gas usage may vary across providers and Foundry versions
  • Cache behavior: Cold cache runs may differ from warm cache runs in timing only
  • Non-EVM boundaries: Cross-chain reproductions require explicit excluded_components declarations

docs.title: Analysis & Notebooks Guide
docs.text: Purpose: Interactive experiment analysis with Jupyter notebooks. Commands: crucible notebook | Module: crucible.analysis

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Install dependencies
pip install "crucible[jupyter]"
# or: uv sync --extra jupyter

# Launch Jupyter with the notebooks/ directory
crucible notebook

# Or with custom options
crucible notebook --port 9999 --no-browser

Jupyter opens at http://localhost:8888/tree with the notebooks/ directory as the root.

docs.title: The crucible.analysis Module
docs.text:
The analysis module provides pandas-based helpers for loading experiment run data:

from crucible.analysis import (
    load_run,          # Load run manifest as dict
    load_events,       # Load events as DataFrame
    load_gas,          # Load gas profile as DataFrame
    load_storage_diffs,# Load storage changes as DataFrame
    load_cross_chain,  # Load cross-chain messages as DataFrame
    load_balance_diffs,# Load balance changes as DataFrame
    load_agent_reasoning, # Load LLM agent reasoning traces as DataFrame
    find_run,          # Find run directory by ID
    list_runs,         # List all available runs
)

# Load run data
manifest = load_run("DS-2026-00001")
events = load_events("DS-2026-00001")
gas = load_gas("DS-2026-00001")
diffs = load_storage_diffs("DS-2026-00001")
agent_traces = load_agent_reasoning("DS-2026-00001")

# Explore
print(f"Outcome: {manifest['outcome']}")
print(f"Gas by phase: {gas.groupby('phase')['gas_used'].sum()}")

# Compare two runs
from crucible.diff import compare_runs
result = compare_runs(find_run("DS-2026-00001"), find_run("DS-2026-00002"))
print(f"Deterministic: {result['storage_equal'] and result['events_equal']}")

docs.title: Included Notebook
docs.text:
notebooks/experiment_analysis.ipynb provides 6 ready-to-run cells:

  1. Load experiment data — imports and loads a run by ID
  2. Gas analysis — bar chart, pie chart, publication-ready gas tables
  3. Event timeline — scatter plot of decoded contract events
  4. Storage diff analysis — top modified contracts and slot counts
  5. Run comparison — determinism check between two runs
  6. LaTeX export — publication-ready gas profile tables

docs.title: Discovering Run IDs
docs.text:
from crucible.analysis import list_runs

for r in list_runs():
    print(f"{r['run_id']} — {r['scenario']} ({r['outcome']})")

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Set RUN_ID at the top of the notebook to your target experiment
  • The figures/ directory is created automatically for exported charts
  • Use %matplotlib inline for inline rendering (already the default in Jupyter)

docs.title: Crucible Architecture
docs.text:
Version: 0.4.0 · Last Updated: June 2026 · Audience: Developers, contributors, reviewers

Crucible is a modular EVM bridge security research testbed. It forks historical blockchain state using Foundry's Anvil, runs exploit reproductions as plugins, and automatically captures publication-grade artifacts for academic research.

Looking for a broader overview? See the Technical Overview for system philosophy, core concepts, and design principles, or the Design Decisions document for the rationale behind every major architectural choice.

        YAML["Experiment YAML"]

    end

    subgraph Crucible
        ER["Experiment Runner"]

    ANVIL --> RPC
    CM --> IP
    IP --> LM
    LM --> LOGS
    LM --> REPORT
    ER --> MANIFEST

Core principle: The researcher writes exploit logic. Crucible handles everything else — chain forking, instrumentation, artifact capture, integrity hashing, and provenance tracking.

docs.title: Layer Rules
docs.text: Layer May Import From Module Count Key Property L4: CLI L3, L2, L1 21 Thin wrappers — zero business logic L3: Orchestration L2, L1 7 Coordinates runtime, manages state transitions L2: Runtime L1 7 Manages external processes and I/O L1: Foundation stdlib only 12 Zero upward imports, no external process deps

docs.title: 4. Data Flow: Experiment Lifecycle
docs.text:
A single crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml triggers this sequence:

sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User / CLI
    participant ER as ExperimentRunner
    participant CFG as Config Loader
    participant PG as Publication Gates
    participant SE as ScenarioEngine
    participant CM as ChainManager
    participant IP as Instrumentation Proxy
    participant PR as PluginRegistry
    participant AP as AttackPlugin
    participant LM as LogManager
    participant PROV as Provenance

    U->>CFG: load_config() + load_experiment()
    U->>ER: run(experiment_path)
    ER->>ER: _check_foundry_version()
    ER->>ER: _enforce_cleanup()
    ER->>PG: check_all() (if publication mode)
    ER->>LM: new LogManager(run_dir)

    rect rgb(230, 240, 255)
        Note over SE,CM: Phase 1 — Environment Setup
        ER->>SE: run(scenario_name, context)
        SE->>CM: start_chain("ethereum")
        CM->>CM: anvil_command() → Popen()
        CM->>CM: _await_ready() (poll eth_blockNumber)
        SE->>SE: execute YAML steps (log, assert, impersonate...)
    end

    rect rgb(230, 255, 230)
        Note over CM,IP: Phase 2 — Instrumentation
        ER->>CM: start_instrumentation("ethereum", chain_log)
        CM->>IP: attach_instrumentation(upstream_url, log)
        IP->>IP: HTTPServer.start() on random port
        Note over CM: runtime_for() now returns proxy URL
    end

    rect rgb(255, 240, 230)
        Note over PR,AP: Phase 3 — Attack Execution
        ER->>PR: load("attack", attack_name)
        PR->>AP: importlib → instantiate plugin class
        ER->>AP: plugin.run(context)
        AP->>CM: runtime_for("ethereum") → proxy URL
        AP->>IP: eth_sendTransaction (via proxy)
        IP->>IP: forward to Anvil → capture receipt → trace
        IP->>LM: emit(gas, events, storage_diffs, balances)
    end

    rect rgb(245, 230, 255)
        Note over ER,PROV: Phase 4 — Teardown & Artifacts
        ER->>CM: stop_instrumentation() (drain + stop)
        ER->>PROV: build_run_manifest()
        ER->>LM: write_report()
        ER->>LM: write_integrity()
    end

docs.title: Key Design Property
docs.text:
The attack plugin never knows it's instrumented. When start_instrumentation() is called, ChainManager stores the proxy reference and runtime_for() silently returns the proxy URL instead of the direct Anvil URL:

# chain_manager.py — transparent URL substitution
def runtime_for(self, chain_name):
    ...
    if chain_name in self._proxies:
        rpc_url = self._proxies[chain_name].proxy_url  # ← proxy, not Anvil
    return ChainRuntime(chain_name, rpc_url, ...)

This means any attack — Python, Forge script, or cast send — produces complete artifacts without per-attack instrumentation code.

docs.title: Plugin Type Hierarchy
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class CruciblePlugin {
        <<abstract>>
        +metadata() dict
    }

    class AttackPlugin {
        +run(ctx)
        +prepare(ctx)
        +execute(ctx, phase) AttackResult
        +verify(ctx) VerificationResult
        +rollback(ctx)
        +cleanup(ctx)
    }

    class BridgePlugin {
        +deploy(ctx) dict
        +configure(ctx, config)
        +connect(ctx)
        +send_message(ctx, src, dst, data) dict
        +reset(ctx)
        +destroy(ctx)
    }

    class AgentPlugin {
        +run(ctx)
        +prepare(ctx, agent_ctx)
        +observe(ctx, agent_ctx) dict
        +think(obs, ctx, agent_ctx) AgentAction
        +respond(result, ctx, agent_ctx)
        +is_done(ctx, agent_ctx) bool
        +verify(ctx, agent_ctx) VerificationResult
    }

    CruciblePlugin <|-- AttackPlugin
    CruciblePlugin <|-- BridgePlugin
    CruciblePlugin <|-- AgentPlugin

docs.title: Plugin Lifecycle Comparison
docs.text: Phase AttackPlugin BridgePlugin AgentPlugin Setup prepare() deploy() → configure() → connect() prepare() Execute execute(phase) send_message() observe() → think() → call() (loop) Verify verify() — verify() Teardown rollback() → cleanup() reset() → destroy() — Convenience run() calls prepare→execute→verify — run() calls prepare→loop→verify

docs.title: Plugin Discovery & Loading
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Filesystem
        A1["attacks/nomad_replay/plugin.yaml"]
        A2["attacks/nomad_replay/nomad_replay.py"]
        B1["bridges/demo_bridge/plugin.yaml"]
        P1["plugins/agent_llm/plugin.yaml"]
    end

    subgraph PluginRegistry
        DISC["discover()"]
        VAL["validate()"]
        LOAD["load()"]
    end

    A1 --> DISC
    B1 --> DISC
    P1 --> DISC
    DISC --> VAL
    VAL -->|"check manifest + entry + evidence"| LOAD
    LOAD -->|"importlib.util.spec_from_file_location()"| INST["Plugin Instance"]

Search directories: attacks/, bridges/, plugins/

Manifest format (plugin.yaml): 

name: nomad_replay
version: 1.0.0
type: attack
entry: nomad_replay.py
class: NomadReplayAttack     # modern format
# class_name: NomadReplayAttack  # legacy format (also supported)

Attack plugins must include evidence.yaml alongside plugin.yaml. The registry validates evidence structure during discovery.

Bridge plugins deployed: | Plugin | Contracts | Vulnerability | Lifecycle | |---|---|---|---| | nomad_bridge | NomadReplica.sol | committedRoot == 0x00 | deploy -> configure -> connect -> send_message -> reset -> destroy | | poly_network_bridge | EthCrossChainManager + EthCrossChainData | Hash collision selector dispatch | Same lifecycle | | wormhole_bridge | WormholeCore + TokenBridge | Guardian bypass mode | Same lifecycle |

All three are discovered and loaded by the PluginRegistry, validated against evidence.yaml, and wired into the experiment lifecycle via bridge_lifecycle.py.

docs.title: ExperimentContext — The Plugin's Interface to Crucible
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class ExperimentContext {
        +config: TestbedConfig
        +topology: TopologyConfig
        +chains: ChainManager
        +log: LogEmitter
        +workspace: Path
        +run_id: str
        +run_config: dict
        +bridge_plugin: BridgePlugin?
    }

Every plugin method receives an ExperimentContext. Through it, the plugin can: - Read chain state: context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum") → JsonRpcClient - Log events: context.log.step("message"), context.log.event(...) - Access config: context.config.chains["ethereum"].fork_block - Use bridges: context.bridge_plugin.send_message(...)

docs.title: Anvil Subprocess Lifecycle
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Configured: load testbed.yaml
    Configured --> Starting: start_chain()
    Starting --> WaitingReady: Popen(anvil ...)
    WaitingReady --> Running: _await_ready() (poll eth_blockNumber)
    Running --> Instrumented: start_instrumentation()
    Instrumented --> Draining: drain() (flush traces)
    Draining --> Running: stop_instrumentation()
    Running --> Stopped: stop_all()
    Stopped --> [*]: meta.json deleted

docs.title: Anvil Command Construction
docs.text:
ChainManager.anvil_command() builds a deterministic Anvil command:

anvil \
  --fork-url ${ETH_RPC_URL} \
  --fork-block-number 15259100 \
  --host 127.0.0.1 \
  --port 8545 \
  --chain-id 1 \
  --gas-price 0 \
  --block-base-fee-per-gas 1000000000 \
  --auto-impersonate \
  --order fifo \
  --cache-path .fork_cache/ethereum/15259100

docs.title: 3-Tier Reset System
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    SOFT["Soft Reset<br/>< 5 seconds"]
    MED["Medium Reset<br/>< 30 seconds"]
    HARD["Hard Reset<br/>< 5 minutes"]

    SOFT -->|"anvil_loadState"| STATE["Restore from snapshot"]
    MED -->|"kill + relaunch + restore"| RELAUNCH["Fresh Anvil + restore"]
    HARD -->|"re-fork from RPC"| REFORK["Full re-fork + bootstrap"]
Tier Method Speed Fidelity Use Case Soft anvil_loadState <5s Snapshot-perfect Matrix runs, parameter sweeps Medium Kill → restart → restore <30s Clean process After process corruption Hard Re-fork from upstream RPC <5min Full re-fetch After cache corruption, topology change

docs.title: RPC Provider Failover
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    REQ["RPC Request"]
    P1["Provider 1 (primary)"]
    P2["Provider 2 (llamarpc)"]
    P3["Provider 3 (ankr)"]
    P4["Provider 4 (publicnode)"]

    REQ --> P1
    P1 -->|"429 / timeout"| P2
    P2 -->|"429 / timeout"| P3
    P3 -->|"429 / timeout"| P4
    P4 -->|"all failed"| ERR["RpcError"]

    P1 -->|"success"| OK["JsonRpcClient"]
    P2 -->|"success"| OK
    P3 -->|"success"| OK

Providers are configured per-chain in testbed.yaml and tried in priority order. Each provider has its own TokenBucket rate limiter.

docs.title: 7. Instrumentation Proxy
docs.text: The instrumentation proxy is the architectural centerpiece — it makes artifact capture automatic and universal.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Attack Plugin"
        SEND["eth_sendTransaction"]
    end

    subgraph "Instrumentation Proxy (HTTPServer thread)"
        FWD["Forward to Anvil"]
        SCHED["Schedule trace"]
    end

    subgraph "ThreadPoolExecutor"
        RECEIPT["eth_getTransactionReceipt"]
        TRACE["debug_traceTransaction<br/>(prestateTracer diffMode)"]
        PARSE["Parse trace → artifacts"]
    end

    subgraph "Anvil Fork"
        ANVIL["Process TX"]
    end

    subgraph "LogEmitter"
        GAS["gas_profile.jsonl"]
        EVENTS["events.jsonl"]
        STORAGE["storage_diffs.jsonl"]
        BALANCE["balance_diffs.jsonl"]
    end

    SEND --> FWD
    FWD --> ANVIL
    ANVIL --> FWD
    FWD --> SCHED

    SCHED --> RECEIPT
    RECEIPT --> TRACE
    TRACE --> PARSE
    PARSE --> GAS
    PARSE --> EVENTS
    PARSE --> STORAGE
    PARSE --> BALANCE

docs.title: What Gets Captured Per Transaction
docs.text: Artifact Source Stream Gas used eth_getTransactionReceipt → gasUsed gas_profile.jsonl Contract events Receipt → logs[] → decode topic[0] events.jsonl Storage diffs debug_traceTransaction → pre/post storage storage_diffs.jsonl Balance changes debug_traceTransaction → pre/post balance balance_diffs.jsonl

docs.title: Multi-Chain Proxy Isolation
docs.text:
Each chain gets its own proxy instance with its own handler class:

Ethereum: Attack → Proxy :9001 → Anvil :8545
BSC:      Attack → Proxy :9002 → Anvil :8547
Polygon:  Attack → Proxy :9003 → Anvil :8546

The _make_handler() closure captures upstream_url, log, and chain per instance, preventing shared-state bugs across chains.

docs.title: Shutdown Sequence
docs.text:
1. proxy.drain()    → ThreadPoolExecutor.shutdown(wait=True)
                      (all pending traces complete)
2. proxy.stop()     → HTTPServer.shutdown() + server_close()
3. log.write_integrity()  → close file handles + hash files

drain() must be called before stop() and before log handles close. This ensures all async trace tasks complete before artifacts are finalized.

docs.title: Log Directory Structure
docs.text:
logs/
  nomad_experiment/
    DS-2026-00042/                    ← run directory
      run.jsonl                       ← experiment lifecycle events
      run_manifest.json               ← provenance, config, outcome
      log_integrity.json              ← SHA-256 hashes of all streams
      report.md                       ← human-readable summary
      chain_ethereum/                 ← per-chain artifacts
        run.jsonl                     ← chain-specific lifecycle
        events.jsonl                  ← contract events
        gas_profile.jsonl             ← gas usage per transaction
        storage_diffs.jsonl           ← storage slot changes
        balance_diffs.jsonl           ← ETH/token balance changes
      chain_bsc/
        ...
      bridge/                         ← cross-chain correlation
        cross_chain.jsonl             ← bridge message tracking
        events.jsonl

docs.title: 7 JSONL Artifact Streams
docs.text:
The testbed produces 6 core streams managed by LogEmitter through the standard emit API, plus 1 agent-specific stream written directly by the LLM agent plugin:

Stream Content Written By run.jsonl Experiment lifecycle (phase start/end, errors) LogManager.step() events.jsonl Contract events (Transfer, Approval, custom) LogEmitter.event() via proxy storage_diffs.jsonl Storage slot changes (contract, slot, before, after) LogEmitter.storage_diff() via proxy gas_profile.jsonl Gas used per transaction LogEmitter.gas() via proxy balance_diffs.jsonl Balance changes (ETH and token) LogEmitter.balance_diff() via proxy cross_chain.jsonl Bridge message correlation IDs LogEmitter.cross_chain() agent_reasoning.jsonl LLM agent reasoning traces (observation, action, conversation snapshot) — written outside the standard LogEmitter system, so not tracked in log_integrity.json LLMAgentPlugin._log_reasoning_step()

docs.title: LogManager vs LogEmitter
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class LogManager {
        -_root: LogEmitter
        -_chain_emitters: dict
        -_bridge_emitter: LogEmitter
        +step(message)
        +for_chain(name) LogEmitter
        +bridge() LogEmitter
        +write_report()
        +write_integrity()
    }

    class LogEmitter {
        -_handles: dict[stream, file]
        +emit(stream, fields)
        +step(message)
        +event(chain, event_name)
        +storage_diff(chain, contract, slot, before, after)
        +gas(step, gas_used)
        +balance_diff(chain, contract, account, before, after)
        +cross_chain(correlation_id, source, dest)
        +close()
        +write_integrity()
    }

    LogManager --> LogEmitter : root
    LogManager --> LogEmitter : chain_ethereum
    LogManager --> LogEmitter : chain_bsc
    LogManager --> LogEmitter : bridge

LogManager is the experiment-level orchestrator. It creates per-chain LogEmitter instances lazily via for_chain(). Attack plugins interact with context.log (which is a LogManager) — lifecycle events go to the root emitter, while chain-specific data routes to the per-chain subdirectory.

docs.title: Diff Engine — Determinism Verification
docs.text:
crucible diff <run-a> <run-b> canonicalizes and compares four metrics:

flowchart LR
    A["Run A"] --> CA["Canonical Sort"]
    B["Run B"] --> CB["Canonical Sort"]

    CA --> CMP["Compare"]
    CB --> CMP

    CMP --> S["storage_equal: true/false"]
    CMP --> E["events_equal: true/false"]
    CMP --> G["gas_equal: true/false"]
    CMP --> BL["balances_equal: true/false"]

All four must be true for deterministic reproduction.

Note: The 6 core streams appear in STREAMS in crucible/logging.py. The 7th (agent_reasoning.jsonl) is emitted by LLMAgentPlugin directly and bypasses integrity hashing — this is a known gap.

docs.title: Configuration Hierarchy
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    ENV[".env file<br/>(ETH_RPC_URL, BSC_RPC_URL...)"]
    BASE["testbed.yaml<br/>(committed to git)"]
    LOCAL["testbed.local.yaml<br/>(gitignored, per-user)"]
    TOPO["topologies/*.yaml<br/>(chain composition)"]
    EXP["experiments/*.yaml<br/>(experiment definition)"]

    ENV -->|"load_dotenv()"| MERGE
    BASE -->|"read_yaml()"| MERGE["deep_merge()"]
    LOCAL -->|"override"| MERGE
    MERGE -->|"expand_env()"| VALIDATE["Pydantic validation"]
    VALIDATE --> CFG["TestbedConfig"]

    TOPO --> TCFG["TopologyConfig"]
    EXP --> ECFG["ExperimentConfig"]

docs.title: Pydantic Config Model Hierarchy
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class TestbedConfig {
        +workspace: str
        +active_topology: str
        +foundry: FoundryConfig
        +publication: PublicationConfig
        +chains: dict[str, ChainConfig]
        +rpc_providers: dict[str, list]

        +rpc_budget: RpcBudgetConfig

    }

    class ChainConfig {
        +rpc_url: str
        +fork_block: int?
        +chain_id: int
        +port: int
        +host: str
        +determinism: DeterminismConfig
    }

    class DeterminismConfig {
        +gas_price: int = 0
        +block_base_fee: int = 1_000_000_000
        +timestamp: "fork_block"
        +auto_mine: bool = true
        +order: "fifo"
    }

    class TopologyConfig {
        +name: str
        +description: str
        +chains: list[str]
        +bridge: str?
    }

    class ExperimentConfig {
        +name: str
        +scenario: str
        +attack: str
        +topology: str = "single"
        +run: dict
        +tags: list[str]
    }

    TestbedConfig --> ChainConfig
    ChainConfig --> DeterminismConfig

docs.title: Built-In Validations
docs.text:
• Port uniqueness — no two chains can share a port (model-level validator)
  • Chain ID uniqueness — no two chains can share a chain_id
  • Port range — must be 1–65535
  • Matrix detection — load_experiment() rejects matrix files with a helpful error message

docs.title: 10. Scenario Engine
docs.text: Scenarios define the chain environment before the attack runs. They set up contract state, impersonate accounts, and configure initial conditions.

docs.title: Scenario Structure
docs.text:
# scenarios/nomad_replay/scenario.yaml
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Setting up Nomad Replica contract state"

      - type: impersonate
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0x..."

      - type: send_tx
        chain: ethereum
        to: "0xB92..."
        data: "0x..."

      - type: assert_balance
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0xB92..."
        min_value: "1000000000000000000"

  - name: attack_ready
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Environment ready for attack"

docs.title: Step Dispatch (Registry Pattern)
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    YAML["YAML Step<br/>{type: 'send_tx', ...}"] --> DISPATCH["handle_step()"]
    DISPATCH --> REG["Step Registry"]
    REG --> H1["@register_step('log')"]
    REG --> H2["@register_step('send_tx')"]
    REG --> H3["@register_step('impersonate')"]
    REG --> H4["@register_step('assert_balance')"]
    REG --> H5["@register_step('forge_script')"]
    REG --> H6["@register_step('python_script')"]
    REG --> H7["@register_step('advance_time')"]
    REG --> H8["@register_step('assert')"]
    REG --> H9["@register_step('verify_tracing')"]
    REG --> H10["@register_step('bridge_action')"]
    REG --> H11["@register_step('plugin')"]

Step handlers are registered via decorator — no if/elif chain:

@register_step("send_tx")
def _handle_send_tx(step, phase, context, workspace):
    ...

New step types can be added by registering a new handler without modifying the engine.

docs.title: Agent Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Experiment Runner"
        ER["_load_plugin()"]
    end

    subgraph "AgentPlugin (sdk.py)"
        RUN["run()"]
        LOOP["observe → think → act loop"]
        VERIFY["verify()"]
    end

    subgraph "LLMAgentPlugin (agent_loop.py)"
        PROMPT["build_system_prompt()"]
        LLM["LLM API Call"]
        PARSE["Parse tool_calls"]
    end

    subgraph "AgentContext (agent_tools.py)"
        TOOLS["9 Tools + Privilege Gate"]
        HISTORY["Tool Call History"]
    end

    subgraph "LLM Provider (llm_providers.py)"
        OAI["OpenAI"]
        ANT["Anthropic"]
    end

    ER -->|"agent_provider in config"| RUN
    RUN --> LOOP
    LOOP --> PROMPT
    PROMPT --> LLM
    LLM --> OAI
    LLM --> ANT
    LLM --> PARSE
    PARSE --> TOOLS
    TOOLS --> HISTORY
    LOOP --> VERIFY

docs.title: Agent Tool Registry
docs.text: Tool Privilege Description chain_id LOW Get chain ID of running fork get_balance LOW Get ETH balance (wei) get_code LOW Get runtime bytecode get_storage LOW Get storage at slot call_contract LOW Read-only eth_call get_logs LOW Get event logs for address impersonate MEDIUM Impersonate an account send_transaction MEDIUM Send a transaction advance_time HIGH Advance EVM timestamp

docs.title: Privilege Levels
docs.text:
LOW     → Read-only queries (5 tools)
MEDIUM  → Impersonate + transact (7 tools, default)
HIGH    → Full access including time manipulation (9 tools)

Configured per-experiment in the YAML: 

run:
  agent_provider: openai
  agent_privilege: medium
  agent_allowed_accounts: ["0x..."]

docs.title: Determinism Boundary
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Crucible Guarantees"
        DET1["Fixed gas price (0)"]
        DET2["Fixed block base fee"]
        DET3["FIFO tx ordering"]
        DET4["Pinned fork block"]
        DET5["Fork-block timestamp"]
        DET6["Auto-mine (instant)"]
    end

    subgraph "Not Guaranteed"
        ND1["Cross-RPC-provider<br/>response ordering"]
        ND2["Multi-chain relay<br/>timing"]
        ND3["Python dict ordering<br/>(mitigated by sort_keys)"]
    end

docs.title: Provenance Tracking
docs.text:
Every run produces a run_manifest.json:

{
  "run_id": "DS-2026-00042",
  "scenario": "nomad_replay",
  "attack": "nomad_replay",
  "topology": "single",
  "outcome": "success",
  "provenance": {
    "git_commit": "a1b2c3d...",
    "git_dirty": false,
    "foundry_version": "forge 0.2.0 (nightly)",
    "python_version": "3.12.4",
    "platform": "Linux-x86_64",
    "docker_mode": "container",
    "config_fingerprint": "sha256:abc123..."
  }
}

docs.title: Integrity Verification
docs.text:
log_integrity.json contains SHA-256 hashes of every artifact file:

{
  "files": {
    "run.jsonl": "sha256:...",
    "events.jsonl": "sha256:...",
    "storage_diffs.jsonl": "sha256:...",
    "gas_profile.jsonl": "sha256:...",
    "balance_diffs.jsonl": "sha256:..."
  }
}

docs.title: Fidelity Levels
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    L0["L0 — Narrative Only<br/>No code, description only"]
    L1["L1 — Conceptual<br/>Deploys vulnerable contract"]
    L2["L2 — Fork-State Behavioral<br/>Fork at historical block, check behavior"]
    L3["L3 — Exact Transaction Replay<br/>Replay real exploit tx on fork"]
    L4["L4 — Multi-Chain Full<br/>Multiple chains + bridge simulation"]

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4

    L2 -.->|"requires"| REQ2["chains + affected_contracts"]
    L3 -.->|"requires"| REQ3["exploit_transactions"]
    L4 -.->|"requires"| REQ4["excluded_components declared"]

docs.title: 7 Publication Gates
docs.text:
Gate What It Checks Evidence evidence.yaml has all required sections Fidelity Fidelity-level-specific requirements met Baseline Golden baseline exists and verifies Foundry Foundry version pinned (no nightly drift) Git Provenance Workspace is a git repo with a known commit Artifacts Complete All expected trace artifacts were captured (no auto-trace failures) Provenance Git commit clean, platform recorded

All 7 gates must pass before a run can be exported for publication.

docs.title: Export Bundle
docs.text:
datasets/DS-2026-00042/
  run_manifest.json          ← provenance + outcome
  log_integrity.json         ← artifact hashes
  report.md                  ← human-readable summary
  run.jsonl                  ← lifecycle events
  chain_ethereum/            ← per-chain artifacts
  REPRODUCE.md               ← step-by-step reproduction guide
  CITATION.cff               ← citation metadata
  artifact_bundle_manifest.json  ← SHA-256 of every file

flowchart TD

    CLI --> INIT

    INIT --> LC["lifecycle.py<br/>init, status, fork, setup, clean"]
    INIT --> DR["doctor.py<br/>doctor"]

docs.title: CLI Command Tree
docs.text:
crucible
├── init                    # Initialize workspace
├── status                  # Show running chains
├── doctor                  # Environment health check
├── fork                    # Fork chains from topology
├── setup                   # Fork + bootstrap
├── clean                   # Remove old logs/caches
├── reset [soft|medium|hard]
├── baseline
│   ├── capture             # Save golden baseline
│   └── verify              # Verify baseline integrity
├── snapshot
│   ├── create              # Manual snapshot
│   └── restore             # Restore snapshot
├── plugin
│   ├── list                # List discovered plugins
│   └── validate            # Validate a plugin
├── experiment
│   ├── run                 # Run single experiment
│   ├── matrix              # Run experiment matrix
│   ├── resume              # Resume from checkpoint
│   └── checkpoints         # List checkpoints
├── attack
│   ├── init                # Scaffold new attack plugin from template (--from-tx populates real data)
│   └── extract             # Auto-generate attack from on-chain tx (with ABI decoding)
├── cache
│   ├── inspect             # Show cache contents
│   ├── fingerprint         # Compute cache fingerprint
│   ├── verify              # Verify cache integrity
│   └── clean               # Clean cache
├── evidence
│   └── check               # Validate evidence.yaml claims against live (forked) chain state
├── bundle
│   ├── hash                # Compute bundle hash
│   └── verify              # Verify bundle integrity
├── verify                  # Verify run/bundle/archive integrity (auto-detect)
├── export                  # Export run as publication bundle
├── publish                 # Mark run as published (indefinite preservation)
├── history                 # Query experiment history
├── summary                 # One-shot workspace overview (chains, runs, disk usage)
├── diff                    # Compare two runs
├── abi
│   ├── list                # List known ABIs
│   ├── validate            # Validate ABI file
│   └── fetch               # Fetch from Etherscan/Sourcify
├── logs
│   └── list                # List run log directories
├── instrument              # Standalone instrumentation proxy for ad-hoc tx instrumentation
├── dashboard               # Launch Streamlit UI
└── notebook                # Launch Jupyter notebook

docs.title: Container Topology
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph "docker-compose.yml"
        ORCH["orchestrator<br/>Python 3.12 + Foundry + uv<br/>ENTRYPOINT: uv run crucible"]
        DASH["dashboard<br/>Streamlit UI<br/>Port 8501"]

    ORCH --> V1
    ORCH --> V2
    ORCH --> V3
    DASH --> V2

docs.title: Two Operational Modes
docs.text:
Mode Command When to Use Single-container (default) docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "fork && experiment run" Most experiments — Anvil runs inside orchestrator Daemon + orchestrator docker compose up -d anvil-ethereum && docker compose run orchestrator experiment run Multi-session workflows, streaming

Recommended: Single-container mode. Simpler, fully isolated, avoids daemon fork-block mismatch.

docs.title: 17. Key Design Decisions
docs.text: For a deeper treatment of each decision — including the tradeoffs considered and why the current path was chosen — see the Design Decisions document.

docs.title: CLI-First, UI as Wrapper
docs.text: Every action is available via crucible <command>. The Streamlit dashboard is a separate read-only viewer. This makes experiments scriptable, CI-able, and Docker-friendly.

docs.title: Topology-Driven Composition
docs.text:
Topology YAML files declare which chains to fork. The same attack can run on different topologies. Adding a new chain config doesn't affect existing experiments.

# topologies/single.yaml — Ethereum only
name: single
chains: [ethereum]
bridge: null

# topologies/bridge.yaml — Two chains + bridge (demo)
name: bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: demo_bridge

# topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml — Two chains + Nomad bridge plugin
name: nomad_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge

# topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml — Two chains + Wormhole bridge plugin
name: wormhole_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: wormhole_bridge

# topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml — Three chains + Poly Network bridge plugin
name: poly_network_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon, bsc]
bridge: poly_network_bridge

docs.title: Transparent Instrumentation
docs.text: The instrumentation proxy intercepts all RPC traffic. Researchers don't need to add logging code. Any attack method (Python, Forge, cast send) produces complete artifacts.

docs.title: Snapshot-Driven Reset
docs.text: Baselines are captured once after setup. Soft resets use anvil_loadState — no chain restart needed. This enables fast iteration during development.

docs.title: Determinism by Default
docs.text: Deterministic flags (gas_price: 0, order: fifo, timestamp: fork_block) are opt-out, not opt-in. If a researcher does nothing, their experiments are deterministic.

docs.title: Plugins for Capabilities
docs.text: New functionality arrives as plugins, not core rewrites: - attack — exploit reproductions - bridge — bridge protocol simulation - agent — LLM-driven autonomous agents

docs.title: Fidelity Levels Are Enforced
docs.text: The L0–L4 fidelity system isn't just documentation — publication gates check requirements programmatically. An L2 reproduction without affected_contracts will fail the gate.

docs.title: Separation of Scenario and Experiment
docs.text: Scenarios define environments (chain state, contract setup). Experiments pair a scenario + attack plugin for formal runs. This means the same scenario can be used with different attacks, and the same attack can run against different scenarios.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/internal/TECHNICAL_OVERVIEW.md System philosophy, core concepts, data flow, design principles (broad overview) docs/internal/DESIGN_DECISIONS.md 15 key architectural decisions with rationale, tradeoffs, and alternatives docs/internal/BRIDGE_PLUGIN_LIMITATIONS.md Bridge plugin coverage, limitations, and future improvement scope docs/REPRODUCTION_SCOPE.md Fidelity levels, in-scope vs out-of-scope attack types docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Creating attack plugins and scenarios

docs.title: Attack Pack Authoring Guide
docs.text: Purpose: A step-by-step guide to create new Crucible attack packs following the established patterns of the 6 completed benchmark reproductions. This guide explains what each component does AND why it exists.

docs.title: Quick Overview: What Is An Attack Pack?
docs.text:
An attack pack is a self-contained directory that tells Crucible everything it needs to reproduce a specific exploit. It has three parts:

attacks/<name>/                  ← The attack plugin (the "what" of the exploit)
scenarios/<name>_pre_exploit/   ← The environment setup (the "where" of the exploit)
experiments/<name>_replay.yaml  ← The wiring (ties everything together)

docs.title: Why Three Separate Parts?
docs.text:
Part Purpose Analogy Attack Plugin (attacks/) What the attack does — forge calldata, send transactions, verify success The chef's recipe Scenario (scenarios/) Where the attack happens — which chain, at what block, what preconditions The kitchen setup Experiment (experiments/) How to run it — which topology, what config, what tags The dinner menu

This separation lets you: - Reuse an attack across different environments (test on different fork blocks) - Reuse a scenario for different attack variants (test multiple exploit approaches) - Swap infrastructure (Docker vs local) without changing attack or scenario code

docs.title: plugin.yaml — The Plugin Manifest
docs.text:
Purpose: Tells Crucible how to find, validate, and load your attack code.

Why it exists: Crucible needs to know your plugin's name, type, entrypoint, chain requirements, and topology requirements before it can load and run it.

name: "your_attack_name"          # Must match the directory name
type: attack                       # Always "attack" for attack plugins
version: "1.0.0"                  # Semantic version for your attack pack
author: "your-name"               # Your name or team name
entry: "your_attack.py"           # The Python file containing your plugin class
class: "YourAttackClass"          # The class name inside the entry file
requires:
  crucible: ">=1.0.0"            # Crucible version requirement
  chains:                         # Which chains this attack needs
    - ethereum
  topology: single                 # Which topology this attack needs (single | bridge | dual | single_bsc | harmony_bridge)

Common mistakes: - Using class_name: (legacy format) instead of class: (new format) — both work but new is preferred - Forgetting to update chains: when adding multi-chain support - Setting topology: to a topology that doesn't include all required chains

docs.title: evidence.yaml — The Research Evidence
docs.text:
Purpose: Declares all the on-chain data, sources, and assertions that back up your reproduction. This is the most important research artifact — it proves you've done your homework.

Why it exists: Without evidence, a reproduction is just code. The evidence file proves that: 1. You found the real exploit data (contracts, transactions, blocks) 2. You know what the exploit did and how it worked 3. You can define what "success" looks like

incident:
  name: "Human-readable exploit name"
  date: "YYYY-MM-DD"                # Date of the actual exploit
  affected_bridge: "Bridge Name"    # Protocol name
  funds_lost_usd: 611000000         # Total funds lost across all chains
  summary: >                        # Technical summary — explain ROOT CAUSE, not impact
    One paragraph explaining the vulnerability.
    What was the bug? How was it triggered?
    What was the result?

sources:
  advisories:                        # Security advisories from the project
    - "https://..."
  postmortems:                       # Technical postmortems (Immunefi, Certik, Halborn)
    - "https://..."
  tx_explorers:                      # Links to the actual exploit transactions
    - "https://etherscan.io/tx/0x..."
  papers: []                         # Academic papers (if any)

historical_scope:
  chains:                            # Which chains were involved
    - "ethereum"
  fork_blocks:                       # Block BEFORE the first exploit tx on each chain
    # This is now the SOURCE OF TRUTH for fork blocks. Crucible auto-merges
    # these values into the chain config at runtime via `crucible experiment run`.
    # You no longer need to manually set `fork_block` in testbed.yaml or
    # testbed.local.yaml — the evidence.yaml drives the fork block automatically.
    ethereum: 15259100
  exploit_transactions:              # The exact exploit transaction hash(es)
    - "0x..."
  affected_contracts:                # Contract addresses (VERIFIED on block explorer!)
    EthCrossChainManager: "0x838bf9E9..."
    EthCrossChainData: "0xcF2afe10..."
  attacker_addresses:                # Known attacker wallets
    - "0x..."
  vulnerability_function: >          # The vulnerable function name
    verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx / putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes

reproduction_scope:
  fidelity_level: L2                 # See fidelity levels below
  exact_tx_replay: false             # True if replaying the exact historical tx
  modeled_components:                # What you CAN reproduce
    - "Ethereum-side keeper key replacement via EthCrossChainManager.call"
  excluded_components:               # What you CANNOT reproduce (off-chain, external)
    - "BSC-side asset withdrawal mechanics"
    - "Polygon-side asset withdrawal mechanics"

acceptance:
  required_assertions:               # What must be true for reproduction to be valid
    - name: vulnerability_exists
      description: "The vulnerable state exists at fork block"
      expected: true
    - name: exploit_succeeds
      description: "The exploit transaction does not revert"
      expected: true

docs.title: <name>.py — The Attack Plugin (Python)
docs.text:
Purpose: Contains the actual exploit reproduction logic — connecting to the forked chain, forging calldata, sending transactions, and verifying results.

Why it exists: YAML is great for declaring state, but exploits need code. The Python plugin gives you full access to: - Forge arbitrary calldata using ABI encoding - Impersonate accounts (run transactions as if you control them) - Read and write storage slots - Emit structured logs with metadata - Define pass/fail verification logic

from crucible.sdk import AttackPlugin, AttackResult, ExperimentContext, VerificationResult
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

class YourAttack(AttackPlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        """Describe the attack for the experiment manifest."""
        return {
            "name": "your_attack",
            "version": "1.0.0",
            "type": "attack",
            "fidelity_level": "L2",
        }

    def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase: int | None = None) -> AttackResult:
        """Execute the exploit reproduction logic."""
        # 1. Get the RPC URL for the forked chain
        rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
        client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

        # 2. Verify preconditions
        # ...

        # 3. Execute exploit logic
        # ...

        # 4. Log progress — these appear in the experiment report
        context.log.step("Exploit executed successfully",
                         phase="attack", action="exploit", status="success")

        # 5. Return result
        return AttackResult(success=True, phase_results=[...])

    def verify(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> VerificationResult:
        """Independent verification that the exploit worked."""
        # Read storage slots, check balances, verify events
        # Return pass/fail with detailed assertions
        return VerificationResult(passed=True, assertions=[...], errors=[])

docs.title: scenario.yaml — The Environment Definition
docs.text:
See docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md for a complete guide. At minimum:

name: my_attack_pre_exploit
description: >-
  Fork Ethereum at block XXXXXX for [attack name] reproduction.
chains:
  - ethereum
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Starting reproduction at fork block XXXXXX"
  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack plugin executed by experiment runner"

docs.title: experiments/<name>_replay.yaml — The Wiring
docs.text:
Purpose: Tells the experiment runner which scenario, attack plugin, and topology to use.

name: my_attack_replay              # Used for log directory naming
scenario: my_attack_pre_exploit      # Points to scenarios/<name>/
attack: my_attack                    # Points to attacks/<name>/
topology: single                     # Points to topologies/<name>.yaml
run:
  traces: true                       # Capture transaction traces
  storage_diffs: true               # Capture storage slot changes
  gas_profiling: true               # Capture gas usage
tags: [my_attack, l2]              # Tags for filtering in crucible history

docs.title: Getting Started Faster With crucible attack extract
docs.text:
💡 Scaffold from a transaction hash: crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name <name> --chain <chain> auto-generates the plugin scaffold, evidence.yaml, and scenario from an on-chain transaction. It fetches the ABI, decodes the function call, and pre-fills contract addresses, fork blocks, and the exploit date from the block timestamp.

[warn] Reliability note: This is a scaffolding tool, not a fully automated exploit generator. Expect the following limitations: - ABI decoding is best-effort (~60% success rate) — complex calldata (nested tuples, proxy patterns, fallback functions) may not decode correctly - The generated plugin.py contains TODO stubs — never working exploit logic. You must implement the actual execute() method yourself. - ABI fetch can fail for unverified contracts on Sourcify/Etherscan - Evidence metadata is incomplete — postmortem URLs, fidelity level, and affected bridge name must be filled in manually

Use attack extract to save 15–20 minutes on file scaffolding, but always review and customize every generated file. See the step-by-step guide below for how to fill in the gaps.

docs.title: Step 1: Research On-Chain Data
docs.text: Before writing any code, gather data from block explorers (Etherscan, Polygonscan, BscScan) and postmortems (rekt.news, Immunefi, Certik, Halborn).

docs.title: What To Collect
docs.text: Data Point Why You Need It Where To Find It Example Fork block Sets the chain state to moments before the exploit Block of the first exploit tx MINUS 1 15259100 (block before 15259101) Exploit tx hash(es) Used in evidence.yaml and to verify calldata Explorer tx page 0x5ea16a... Vulnerable contract(s) Target of your attack plugin Explorer address page (must show [ok] "Verified") 0xB9233675... Attacker wallet Source of the exploit transactions "From" field on the exploit tx 0xA87fB85... Postmortem links Source citation for evidence.yaml rekt.news, Immunefi, project blogs https://... Funds lost Impact metric for evidence.yaml rekt.news or on-chain analysis $611,000,000 Vulnerable function The function that was exploited Transaction input data decoded via explorer verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx

docs.title: Critical Rule: Verify Contract Addresses On Etherscan
docs.text:
This is the #1 source of bugs in attack packs. During initial validation, 3 out of 6 attack packs had wrong contract addresses — they were EOAs (regular user wallets), not contracts. The addresses looked plausible because they had similar prefixes.

How to verify: 1. Paste the address into Etherscan 2. Check the top of the page — it should say "Contract" (not "Address (EOA)") 3. Look for a blue checkmark [ok] next to the name (verified source code) 4. Check that the contract has more than 0 transactions

docs.title: How To Find The Correct Fork Block
docs.text:
1. Find the exploit transaction on Etherscan — paste the known exploit tx hash into the Etherscan search bar. If you only have the attacker's wallet address, go to their transaction history page and look for the transaction that calls the vulnerable function.
  2. Note the block number (e.g., 12996694)
  3. Subtract 1 → that's your fork block (e.g., 12996693)
  4. Double-check: at the fork block, the vulnerable contract should exist and the exploit should NOT have happened yet

docs.title: Step 2: Update evidence.yaml
docs.text:
Fill in all the data from Step 1. See the template above for the complete structure.

Key fields to get right: - fork_blocks — must be block BEFORE the exploit. This is now the source of truth for fork blocks. The auto-merge feature reads fork_blocks from evidence.yaml and applies it to the chain config at runtime. You no longer need to manually set fork_block in testbed.yaml or testbed.local.yaml. - affected_contracts — must be verified on the block explorer - exploit_transactions — must be the ACTUAL exploit tx (not an attacker's message or unrelated tx) - modeled_components — be honest about what you can and cannot reproduce - excluded_components — document everything you're NOT reproducing (off-chain, non-EVM, etc.)

docs.title: The Standard Pattern (From Working Plugins)
docs.text:
1. Get the RPC URL: context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
  2. Create a client: JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)
  3. Verify preconditions: Check that the vulnerable contract exists and has the expected state
  4. Execute the exploit: Forge calldata, impersonate accounts, send transactions
  5. Log everything: Use context.log.step() for structured logging — these appear in the experiment report
  6. Verify success: Check storage slots, balances, events
  7. Return results: AttackResult from execute(), VerificationResult from verify()

docs.title: Python (Pure)
docs.text: File What It Demonstrates attacks/demo_attack/demo_attack.py Minimal reference implementation — start here (< 15 lines) attacks/nomad_replica_fraud/nomad_replica_fraud.py Storage slot analysis, ABI encoding, multi-step exploit attacks/wormhole_guardian_bypass/wormhole_guardian_bypass.py Contract address constants, verification logic attacks/poly_network_privkey/poly_network_privkey.py Hash collision calldata construction, impersonation

docs.title: Step 4: Create The Scenario
docs.text:
See docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md for complete guide. At minimum:

name: my_attack_pre_exploit
description: >-
  Fork Ethereum at block XXXXXX for [attack name] reproduction.
chains:
  - ethereum
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Starting reproduction at fork block XXXXXX"
  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack plugin executed by experiment runner"

docs.title: Step 5: Wire It Up In An Experiment
docs.text:
# experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml
name: my_attack_replay
scenario: my_attack_pre_exploit
attack: my_attack
topology: single                    # Make sure this matches your chain requirements
run:
  traces: true
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true
tags: [my_attack, l2]

docs.title: Step 6: Validate & Test
docs.text:
# 1. Validate the plugin structure
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin validate attacks/my_attack

# 2. Confirm it's discovered
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin list --type attack

# 3. Fork and run (auto-merges fork_block from evidence.yaml)
# No testbed.local.yaml editing needed!
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml --no-baseline
"

# 4. Check the results
docker compose run --rm orchestrator logs list

# 5. Run twice for determinism proof
# (auto-merge sees fork_block matches, skips re-forking)
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml --no-baseline
"

# 6. Compare the two runs
# `diff` checks gas, storage, events, balances
docker compose run --rm orchestrator diff <run_a> <run_b>

docs.title: Common Pitfalls (And How To Avoid Them)
[fail] Wrong Contract Address (EOA instead of Contract)
docs.text:
• Error: get_code() returns "0x", eth_call returns empty
  • Why it happens: The address looks like a contract address but is actually a regular wallet
  • How to avoid: Always paste the address into Etherscan and check it says "Contract"

docs.title: [fail] Wrong Fork Block
docs.text:
• Error: Vulnerable state is not present (storage slots are wrong, contract doesn't exist yet)
  • Why it happens: Fork block is set to the exploit block (or after), or too early (before contract deployment)
  • How to avoid: Use block BEFORE the first exploit tx. Verify by checking a known storage value at the fork block.

docs.title: [fail] Wrong Exploit Transaction
docs.text:
• Error: The transaction in evidence.yaml doesn't actually exploit the vulnerability
  • Why it happens: The attacker's wallet made many transactions — you picked the wrong one
  • How to avoid: Check the transaction's function signature. The exploit tx should call the vulnerable function.

docs.title: [fail] Python sha3_256() Instead Of Ethereum keccak256
docs.text:
• Error: ABI encoding mismatches, wrong event signature hashes
  • Why it happens: Python's hashlib.sha3_256() is NIST SHA-3 (FIPS 202), NOT Ethereum's keccak256
  • How to avoid: Use Web3.keccak(bytes) or from Crypto.Hash import keccak

docs.title: [fail] LogManager.message Duplication
docs.text:
• Error: LogManager.step() got multiple values for argument 'message'
  • Why it happens: Passing message= as both positional AND keyword argument
  • How to avoid: Only pass the message as the first positional argument

docs.title: [fail] Bridge Deployment — Double 0x Prefix In Bytecode
docs.text:
• Error: RPC error for eth_sendTransaction: invalid value: string "0x0x6080..."
  • Why it happens: Foundry's compiled bytecode.object already includes the 0x hex prefix. Prepending another "0x" + bytecode produces "0x0x6080..." which Anvil rejects.
  • How to avoid: Use "data": bytecode without the "0x" + prefix. Foundry's JSON artifact always has 0x built in.
  • If it happens: Check your bridge plugin's deploy() or _deploy_contract() method. The fix is removing the extra "0x" + prefix.

docs.title: [fail] Bridge Deployment — anvil_setBalance Fails On Polygon/BSC Archive
docs.text:
• Error: anvil_setBalance failed: historical state ... is not available
  • Why it happens: Some archive nodes (especially Polygon and BSC) don't serve full historical state at older fork blocks. When anvil_setBalance is called on a new (non-existent) address, Anvil tries to look it up in the remote archive and the archive returns "state not available".
  • How to avoid: Wrap per-chain deployment in a try/except so that if one chain's archive can't serve state, it's skipped gracefully with a warning rather than crashing the whole experiment. See bridges/nomad_bridge/nomad_bridge.py for a reference implementation.
  • If it happens: The chain with the problematic archive is skipped. The attack can still run on other chains. Consider using a different RPC provider with better archive depth, or use a more recent fork block.

docs.title: [fail] Auto-Merge Kills Chain But Doesn't Restart It
docs.text:
• Error: Connection refused on the chain port after auto-merge updates the fork block
  • Why it happens: The auto-merge feature correctly reads fork_blocks from evidence.yaml and updates the chain config. It terminates the old Anvil process and deletes the stale meta file. But if the chain isn't restarted, the experiment tries to connect to a dead process.
  • How to avoid: This is a framework fix — already handled in crucible/experiment.py. The chain is now automatically re-forked at the updated block after auto-merge. If you see this error, update your Crucible version.
  • If it happens: Run --no-auto-merge to bypass auto-merge entirely, then manually manage fork blocks.

docs.title: [fail] Port TIME_WAIT After Killing Old Anvil
docs.text:
• Error: New Anvil fails to bind because the port is in TIME_WAIT state
  • Why it happens: After SIGTERM/SIGKILL, the OS keeps the port in TIME_WAIT for 30-120 seconds. If the auto-merge tries to start a new Anvil immediately on the same port, it fails.
  • How to avoid: Framework fix — Crucible now polls the port every 500ms for up to 10 seconds after killing the old process before starting the new one.
  • If it happens: Increase the polling window or wait 30 seconds before re-forking.

docs.title: [fail] Checkpoints Snapshot Dead Chains
docs.text:
• Error: RPC call failed for anvil_dumpState at http://127.0.0.1:8547: Connection refused
  • Why it happens: The checkpoint manager iterates over all .meta.json files in states/. If a stale meta file exists (from a previous topology with different chains), it tries to snapshot a chain that's no longer running.
  • How to avoid: Framework fix — checkpoint now filters to only snapshot chains in the active topology. Always clean stale meta files with rm -f states/*.meta.json before switching topologies.

docs.title: [fail] Bridge evidence.yaml Uses Placeholder Contract Address
docs.text:
• Error: Evidence validation failed: Contract "deployed by bridge plugin" has no code on any running chain
  • Why it happens: The evidence pre-flight validation checks that all affected_contracts declared in evidence.yaml have code on the running chains. If you use a placeholder string like "deployed by bridge plugin" instead of a real address, the validation code tries to call get_code() on that string, which fails.
  • How to avoid: For dynamically deployed contracts (bridge plugins), set affected_contracts: [] (empty list) since there are no static contract addresses to verify.

docs.title: [fail] L4 Fidelity Without Transaction Traces
docs.text:
• Error: Fidelity violation: No transaction actions were found in run.jsonl
  • Why it happens: L4 fidelity requires transaction traces. If the bridge deployment failed on one chain, there may not be enough trace data. Or the experiment is a demo/simulation, not a real exploit reproduction.
  • How to avoid: Run demo experiments with --no-fidelity to skip fidelity checks. For production experiments, ensure all chains deploy correctly and transactions are captured.

docs.title: [fail] set_balance On Precompile Address
docs.text:
• Error: anvil_setBalance: failed to get account for 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000001
  • Why it happens: Address 0x...0001 is the ECDSA-recover precompile. Anvil has special handling for precompile addresses and may reject set_balance on them, especially on forked chains.
  • How to avoid: Use a random EOA address (e.g., 0xDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaD) instead of a precompile address for the deployer. Or use an address that exists in the forked state (like a real contract address).

docs.title: From Research To Code: A Practical Reference
docs.text: This section is for a researcher who has on-chain data (fork block, contract addresses, tx hashes) and needs to write the Python attack plugin. It explains the mindset shift from Solidity-based exploit replication, maps Crucible APIs to exploit operations, and gives a clear learning path.

docs.title: Crucible API Map
docs.text: This table maps what you want to do → the Crucible API to use.

docs.title: Getting Connected
docs.text: You Want To... Use This Example Get the RPC URL for a chain context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url Create a JSON-RPC client JsonRpcClient(rpc_url) client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

docs.title: Reading Chain State
docs.text: You Want To... Use This Example Check if a contract exists client.get_code(address) code = client.get_code("0x...") → returns "0x" if no code Read a storage slot client.get_storage_at(address, slot) val = client.get_storage_at("0x...", "0x1") Check an account's balance client.get_balance(address) bal = client.get_balance("0x...") Call a contract function (read-only) client.call(method, params) client.call("eth_call", [{"to": addr, "data": "0x..."}, "latest"]) Get the current block number client.call("eth_blockNumber", []) block = int(client.call("eth_blockNumber", []), 16)

docs.title: Executing Transactions
docs.text: You Want To... Use This Example Impersonate an account client.impersonate_account(address) client.impersonate_account("0x...") Set an account's balance client.set_balance(address, wei_hex) client.set_balance("0x...", "0xDE0B6B3A7640000") Send a transaction client.send_transaction(tx_dict) client.send_transaction({"from": addr, "to": addr, "data": "0x...", "gas": hex(300_000)})

docs.title: Logging & Results
docs.text: You Want To... Use This Example Log a structured step context.log.step(message, phase, action, status) context.log.step("Exploit sent", phase="attack", action="send_tx", status="success") Return success AttackResult(success=True, phase_results=[...]) return AttackResult(success=True, phase_results=...]) Return failure AttackResult(success=False, phase_results=[...], error=msg) return AttackResult(success=False, ...) Pass verification VerificationResult(passed=True, assertions=[...]) return VerificationResult(passed=True, assertions=[{"name": "...", "passed": True, ...}])

docs.title: The Learning Path (What To Read In Order)
Pure Python Route
docs.text: Step What To Read What You'll Learn 1 attacks/demo_attack/demo_attack.py Minimal plugin — just a run() method that logs. Under 15 lines. 2 crucible/sdk.py (the AttackPlugin class) The base class — all methods you can override (execute, verify, prepare, cleanup) 3 The Crucible API Map table in this guide (above) All available RPC methods — get_code, get_storage_at, send_transaction, impersonate_account, etc. 4 attacks/wormhole_guardian_bypass/wormhole_guardian_bypass.py Simple 2-contract verification pattern. Good next step after demo. 5 attacks/nomad_replica_fraud/nomad_replica_fraud.py Storage slot analysis + ABI-encoded calldata + actual state-changing tx. The most instructive single file. 6 attacks/poly_network_privkey/poly_network_privkey.py Complex ABI encoding, keccak256 hash collision, impersonating a contract (not an EOA).

docs.title: From Exploit Tx To Python Code: A Worked Example
docs.text:
Here's how to translate an actual exploit transaction from Etherscan into Crucible Python code.

Step 1: Find the exploit tx on Etherscan. Copy these details:

Tx: 0x5ea16a...
From: 0xa87fB85... (attacker)
To: 0x838bf9E... (EthCrossChainManager)  ← target contract
Function: verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx       ← the vulnerable function
Input Data: 0x41973cd9...                 ← 4-byte selector + ABI-encoded args
Block: 12996694

Step 2: Decompose what the tx does:

The exploit calls verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx() on EthCrossChainManager.
That function internally calls putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes() on EthCrossChainData
via a hash collision (selector 0x41973cd9 matches both functions).
This replaces the bridge keeper key.

Step 3: Decompose what the transaction does — use Etherscan's "Click to see More" on Input Data to see the decoded function arguments. This tells you the parameter structure.

Step 4: Translate each action to Crucible code:

# 1. Connect to the forked chain
client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

# 2. Verify pre-condition: target contract exists
code = client.get_code("0x838bf9E...")  # EthCrossChainManager
if code == "0x":
    return AttackResult(success=False, ...)

# 3. Impersonate the privileged account
client.impersonate_account("0x838bf9E...")  # EthCrossChainManager has onlyManager role

# 4. Build the calldata — DON'T copy the raw hex from Etherscan!
#    The hex embeds specific addresses and values. You need to
#    RECONSTRUCT it programmatically so it works on YOUR fork.
#    Use Etherscan's decoded view to understand the parameter structure,
#    then build it with proper ABI encoding (see poly_network_privkey.py
#    for a complete example of dynamic calldata construction).
calldata = build_put_keeper_calldata(ATTACKER_ADDRESS)

# 5. Send the transaction (same function, on the forked chain)
tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
    "from": "0x838bf9E...",
    "to": "0xcF2afe10...",  # EthCrossChainData
    "data": calldata,
    "gas": hex(300_000),
})

# 6. Log success
context.log.step("keeper key replaced", phase="attack", action="exploit", status="success")

# 7. Return
return AttackResult(success=True, ...)

Key rule: Never copy-paste calldata hex from Etherscan. The calldata contains addresses, amounts, and values specific to the real exploit's chain state. Your fork has a different chain state, so you must reconstruct the calldata with the correct parameters for your forked environment. The Etherscan decoded view tells you the structure — you implement the structure.

The pattern is always the same: connect → verify preconditions → impersonate → build calldata → send tx → log → return.

docs.title: External Resources Every Researcher Needs
docs.text: Topic Why You Need It Resource Ethereum ABI encoding To forge calldata matching the vulnerable function signature Solidity ABI Spec — especially "Function Selector" and "Encoding" sections keccak256 vs SHA-3 Python's hashlib.sha3_256() is WRONG — it's NIST SHA-3, not Ethereum's keccak256 eth-hash library docs — use from eth_hash.auto import keccak EVM Storage Layout To find which storage slot contains the state you need to read/verify Solidity Storage Layout Docs — slot = keccak256(key . slot) for mappings Etherscan Tx Decoder To see the decoded function call and arguments of an exploit tx Just paste a tx hash into Etherscan — the "Input Data" tab decodes it 4-byte Selector Database To look up unknown function selectors 4byte.directory — search by selector like 0x41973cd9 eth_abi For reliable ABI encoding/decoding of complex function arguments (nested tuples, dynamic arrays) instead of manual bytes construction eth-abi on PyPI — from eth_abi import encode; encode(["address", "uint256"], [addr, value]) OpenChain ABI To download verified contract ABIs programmatically Sourcify API — Crucible's ABI registry uses this under the hood

docs.title: Pure Python Checklist
docs.text:
from crucible.sdk import AttackPlugin, AttackResult, ExperimentContext, VerificationResult
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

class MyAttack(AttackPlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        return {"name": "my_attack", "version": "1.0.0", "type": "attack", "fidelity_level": "L2"}

    def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase: int | None = None) -> AttackResult:
        # ── 1. Get RPC ──
        rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
        client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

        # ── 2. Verify preconditions ──
        code = client.get_code("0xContract...")
        if code == "0x":
            return AttackResult(success=False, phase_results=[{"step": "check_code", "passed": False}])

        # ── 3. ABI-encode calldata and send tx ──
        client.impersonate_account("0xAttacker...")
        tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
            "from": "0xAttacker...",
            "to": "0xTarget...",
            "data": "0x...",  # ABI-encoded calldata
        })

        # ── 4. Log ──
        context.log.step("Exploit sent", phase="attack", action="send_tx", status="success")

        # ── 5. Return ──
        return AttackResult(success=True, phase_results=[{"step": "exploit", "passed": True}])

    def verify(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> VerificationResult:
        # ── 6. Verify ──
        client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url)
        storage = client.get_storage_at("0xTarget...", "0x1")
        passed = storage != "0x0"
        return VerificationResult(
            passed=passed,
            assertions=[{"name": "state_changed", "passed": passed, "actual": storage}],
            errors=[] if passed else ["Storage slot did not change"]
        )

docs.title: Fidelity Enforcement
docs.text:
Fidelity enforcement is active by default — it validates runtime behavior against the fidelity level declared in evidence.yaml. Use --no-fidelity to skip:

crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml
Level What's Enforced Required In Evidence L3 All L2 + exact_tx_replay=true, exploit_transactions non-empty, tx actions found in artifacts exploit_transactions: ["0x..."], exact_tx_replay: true L4 All L3 + multi-chain topology + bridge plugin + excluded_components declared Same as L3 + L4 topology with bridge plugin

docs.title: L3: Exact Transaction Replay
docs.text:
L3 replays the exact historical exploit transaction on a forked chain. This requires: 1. Setting exact_tx_replay: true in evidence.yaml 2. Reconstructing the exact calldata from the original transaction (use crucible attack extract) 3. Verifying gas, events, and storage match the original

# evidence.yaml
reproduction_scope:
  fidelity_level: L3
  exact_tx_replay: true
historical_scope:
  exploit_transactions:
    - "0xebf283515720d2d3a33f431f9d659ccf919163273e5f299166f2878d655f4625"

# Run with fidelity enforcement (enabled by default)
crucible experiment run experiments/my_l3_replay.yaml

docs.title: L4: Multi-Chain With Bridge Plugin
docs.text:
L4 requires multiple chains + a bridge plugin in the topology. Use one of the 3 bridge topology files:

Topology Plugin Chains topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml nomad_bridge ethereum + polygon topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml poly_network_bridge ethereum + polygon + bsc topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml wormhole_bridge ethereum + polygon 
# experiments/my_l4_experiment.yaml
name: my_l4_experiment
scenario: my_l4_scenario
attack: my_l4_attack
topology: nomad_bridge               # Bridge topology
run:
  traces: true
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true

# evidence.yaml
reproduction_scope:
  fidelity_level: L4
  exact_tx_replay: true
  excluded_components:
    - "Off-chain relayer timing"
    - "Non-EVM chain components"

# Fork + run with bridge topology + fidelity enforcement
crucible fork --topology nomad_bridge
crucible experiment run experiments/my_l4_experiment.yaml

docs.title: L4: Accessing The Bridge Plugin From Code
docs.text:
The bridge plugin is available via the experiment context:

def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase=None) -> AttackResult:
    # The bridge is already deployed and configured by the lifecycle
    bridge = context.bridge_plugin

    # Send a forged cross-chain message
    result = bridge.send_message(
        context=context,               # Required first argument
        source_chain="ethereum",
        dest_chain="polygon",
        data="0xdeadbeef..."  # forged calldata
    )

    # Use client for chain-specific operations
    eth_client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url)
    poly_client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains.runtime_for("polygon").rpc_url)

docs.title: Validate The Bridge Plugin
docs.text:
crucible plugin list --type bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/nomad_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/poly_network_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/wormhole_bridge

docs.title: Upgrade Existing L2 Packs To L3
docs.text:
# Reconstruct calldata from original transaction
crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name my_attack --chain ethereum --rpc-url <archive_rpc>

# Update evidence.yaml fidelity_level to L3 and exact_tx_replay to true
# Re-run with enforcement
crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml

docs.title: Benchmark Attack Packs
docs.text:
Crucible includes 6 benchmark attack packs reproducing $1.9B+ in historical bridge exploit losses. Each pack includes a concrete exploit plugin, evidence manifest, scenario definitions, and experiment YAML ready for deterministic reproduction.

New to Crucible? The Technical Overview explains the system architecture, core concepts (Experiment, Topology, Fidelity Levels), and how benchmarks fit into the research workflow. For the rationale behind every major design choice — including why fidelity levels exist and how they're enforced — see the Design Decisions document.

docs.title: Current Status
docs.text: Attack Pack Experiment Fidelity Status wormhole_guardian_bypass experiments/wormhole_replay.yaml L2 [ok] Complete — fork block 14125553, verified contract addresses, determinism proven nomad_replica_fraud experiments/nomad_replay.yaml L2 [ok] Complete — fork block 15259100, determinism proven (storage/events/gas ≡ equal), gold-standard exemplar poly_network_privkey experiments/poly_network_replay.yaml L2 [ok] Complete — fork block 12996693, verified addresses, hash collision proven; 3-chain topology available horizon_multisig_drain experiments/horizon_replay.yaml L2 [ok] Complete — fork block 15005526, 3 bridge contracts verified, determinism proven bnb_token_hub_proof experiments/bnb_token_hub_replay.yaml L2 [ok] Complete — fork block 22166659, CrossChain contract (0x...2000) identified, selector 0x84013b6a verified multichain_router_permit experiments/multichain_replay.yaml L1 [ok] Complete — MPC infrastructure compromise, correctly classified as L1 narrative

docs.title: Bridge Plugins (Infrastructure)
docs.text: Bridge Plugin Protocol Chains Contracts Deployed nomad_bridge Nomad (optimistic bridge) ethereum + polygon NomadReplica — committedRoot == 0x00 vulnerability poly_network_bridge Poly Network (cross-chain manager) ethereum + polygon + bsc EthCrossChainManager + EthCrossChainData — hash collision wormhole_bridge Wormhole (guardian bridge) ethereum + polygon WormholeCore + TokenBridge — guardian bypass demo_bridge Reference stubs ethereum + polygon None (no-op)

docs.title: Validate All Packs
docs.text:
crucible plugin list --type attack
crucible plugin validate attacks/wormhole_guardian_bypass
crucible plugin validate attacks/nomad_replica_fraud
crucible plugin validate attacks/poly_network_privkey
crucible plugin validate attacks/horizon_multisig_drain
crucible plugin validate attacks/multichain_router_permit
crucible plugin validate attacks/bnb_token_hub_proof

# Bridge plugins
crucible plugin list --type bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/nomad_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/poly_network_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/wormhole_bridge

docs.title: Run All Attacks (Matrix Mode)
docs.text:
For control-plane validation without baseline verification:

crucible experiment matrix experiments/benchmark_matrix.yaml --no-baseline --continue-on-error

Note on error handling: The multichain_router_permit experiment is classified as an L1 narrative (MPC infrastructure compromise), precluding on-chain reproduction. Consequently, its run() method returns None instead of an AttackResult, causing the matrix runner to flag a failure. Utilizing --continue-on-error ensures the remaining 5 experiments execute to completion despite this expected outcome.

For formal research, do not use --no-baseline.

docs.title: Authoring New Attacks
docs.text:
New attacks can be authored by following the patterns established in existing packs:

  1. Choose a fork block — the block number before the first exploit transaction
  2. Verify contract addresses — use Etherscan or cast code to confirm they are contracts, not EOAs
  3. Write the exploit plugin — subclass AttackPlugin in attacks/<name>/<name>.py (see the Plugin Development Guide for a complete walkthrough)
  4. Declare evidence — attacks/<name>/evidence.yaml with fork block, contracts, fidelity level
  5. Create scenario YAML — scenarios/<name>/scenario.yaml with pre-exploit setup steps
  6. Create experiment YAML — experiments/<name>.yaml wiring topology, scenario, and attack
  7. Optionally use crucible attack extract <tx_hash> to auto-generate the scaffold from a real exploit transaction

docs.title: Per-Attack Fidelity Assessment
docs.text:
The table below delineates each attack pack's declared fidelity level and outlines the specific constraints preventing it from achieving higher reproduction fidelity. This transparency is an architectural requirement—it explicitly documents the boundaries of the reproduction to facilitate rigorous peer review.
