  "balances_equal": true
}

To opt out (if you need non-deterministic behavior for your research):

# testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    determinism:
      gas_price: null        # Use real gas pricing
      order: ""              # Default Anvil ordering
      auto_mine: false       # Wait for block production

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
A runtime verification system that checks whether your experiment's behavior matches its declared reproducibility level. Each level has specific requirements:

Level Name Requirements L0 Narrative Description only ΓÇö no on-chain reproduction L1 Conceptual Plugin returns a result L2 Fork-State Chains + contracts verified, trace artifacts exist L3 Exact Replay Exploit transactions declared and replayed L4 Multi-Chain 2+ chains, bridge plugin, excluded_components declared

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Prevents overclaiming ΓÇö you cannot claim L3 without actually replaying an exploit transaction
  • Builds trust ΓÇö reviewers can verify the declared level programmatically
  • Self-documents ΓÇö every experiment manifest contains the fidelity check result

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
Declare your fidelity level in evidence.yaml:

reproduction_scope:
  fidelity_level: L2

Fidelity runs automatically after every experiment. Check the result:

# After running an experiment
cat logs/my_experiment/DS-2026-00001/run_manifest.json | python3 -m json.tool

# Look for the fidelity_check field:
# {
#   "fidelity_check": {
#     "passed": true,
#     "declared_level": "L2",
#     "failures": [],
#     "warnings": []
#   }
# }

To disable fidelity (e.g., for L4 bridge demos that don't meet formal requirements):

crucible experiment run experiments/my_experiment.yaml --no-fidelity

docs.title: Blockers
docs.text: Fidelity Failure Likely Cause Fix no artifact streams found Attack didn't interact with chain Check your plugin sends transactions chains declared not in topology evidence.yaml lists a chain not in your topology Update evidence.yaml or topology no exploit transactions declared L3 requires exploit_transactions in evidence.yaml Add transaction hashes no bridge plugin L4 requires a bridge in the topology Use a bridge topology

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
Starting Anvil without --fork-url ΓÇö as a standalone local EVM with zero external dependencies. No archive RPC needed, no historical state, instant startup.

# testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    fork_block: null   # ΓåÉ Triggers blank fork mode
    rpc_url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"  # Still required by schema (unused)

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Works offline ΓÇö no internet, no RPC provider needed
  • Instant startup ΓÇö <1 second vs 30sΓÇô5 minutes for archive forks
  • Fully deterministic ΓÇö no archive state variability
  • Perfect for development ΓÇö iterate on attack packs without waiting for forks
  • Great for L4 bridge demos ΓÇö deploy contracts on blank chains, test cross-chain messaging

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
# config/testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    rpc_url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"
    fork_block: null
    chain_id: 1
    port: 8545

Then fork and run normally:

crucible clean --all
crucible fork --topology single
crucible experiment run experiments/my_experiment.yaml --no-baseline

Note: The instrumentation proxy works identically in blank fork mode. Same artifacts, same format, same integrity hashing.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text: What You Get What You Don't Get Instant startup, offline capable, deterministic Historical chain state (no pre-deployed contracts) 10 pre-funded Anvil accounts Real token balances or DeFi protocols Full instrumentation + artifact capture eth_getCode for real contracts (they don't exist) Deterministic diff verification L2 fidelity verification (requires archive fork)

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
A set of 7 research-quality checks that must pass before an experiment can be exported for publication. They enforce:

Gate What It Checks Evidence evidence.yaml has all required sections Fidelity Fidelity-level-specific requirements met Baseline Golden baseline exists and verifies Foundry Foundry version pinned Git Provenance Workspace has a known git commit Artifacts Complete All expected trace artifacts captured Provenance All provenance fields present

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Quality assurance ΓÇö catches incomplete or non-reproducible experiments before publication
  • Reviewer trust ΓÇö gates create an auditable chain of checks
  • Self-documenting ΓÇö failures tell you exactly what's missing

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
# Run with publication mode gates
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --publication-mode

If publication mode is enabled in config:

# testbed.yaml
publication:
  enabled: true
  require_foundry_pinned: true
  require_baseline_verified: true
  require_provenance_complete: true
  fidelity_enforcement: true

Check gate results in the experiment output. Each gate passes or fails with a clear message.

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
The fork cache stores Anvil's downloaded state so subsequent forks at the same block are instant. The CacheManager fingerprints each cache entry with:

Fingerprint Field What It Tracks Provider identity Which RPC provider was used (ankr, llamarpc, etc.) Fork block Which block was forked Chain ID Which chain Foundry version Which Foundry version created the cache Config hash Which testbed config was active

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Cold cache ΓÇö no data cached ΓåÆ slow fork (30sΓÇô5min)
  • Warm cache ΓÇö data cached from a previous run ΓåÆ instant fork (<5s)
  • Stale cache ΓÇö cache exists but was created by a different provider/version ΓåÆ invalidated in strict mode

Cache poisoning (using wrong data from a different provider) was responsible for countless irreproducible results in other testbeds. Cache fingerprinting eliminates this.

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
# Inspect cache
crucible cache inspect

# Result shows every cached chain/block with provider, version, and size:
# ΓöîΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö¼ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö¼ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö¼ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö¼ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÉ
# Γöé Chain    Γöé Fork Block Γöé Provider Γöé Size   Γöé Mode     Γöé
# Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö╝ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö╝ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö╝ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö╝ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöñ
# Γöé ethereum Γöé 15259100   Γöé llamarpc Γöé 2.1 GB Γöé warm     Γöé
# Γöé bsc      Γöé 22166659   Γöé ankr     Γöé 1.8 GB Γöé warm     Γöé
# ΓööΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö┤ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö┤ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö┤ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓö┤ΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÇΓöÿ

# Clear cache
crucible cache clean

# Compute cache fingerprint (for inclusion in run manifests)
crucible cache fingerprint

docs.title: Cache Policies
docs.text:
Policy Behavior permissive (default) Warns on mismatch but does not invalidate ΓÇö best for development strict Invalidates cache on any mismatch ΓÇö best for publication runs

Set in testbed.yaml:

fork_cache:
  cache_policy: strict

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
Three ways to reset chain state, each with a different speed/fidelity tradeoff:

Tier Method Speed When to Use Soft anvil_loadState snapshot restore <5s Same-chain, same-block iteration Medium Kill + relaunch + restore <30s After Anvil process corruption Hard Re-fork from archive RPC <5min After topology change, cache corruption

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Soft ΓÇö fastest, perfect for iterating on attack parameters
  • Medium ΓÇö recovers from process issues without re-fetching state
  • Hard ΓÇö nuclear option that guarantees clean state

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
crucible reset soft
crucible reset medium
crucible reset hard

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
LLM-driven agents have configurable privilege levels that gate which tools they can access:

Level Access Tools Available LOW Read-only queries chain_id, get_balance, get_code, get_storage, call_contract, get_logs MEDIUM Impersonate + transact (default) Above + impersonate, send_transaction HIGH Full access Above + advance_time

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Safety ΓÇö prevents autonomous agents from accidentally causing damage
  • Progressive disclosure ΓÇö start at LOW, increase as you gain confidence
  • Auditability ΓÇö every agent action is logged with its privilege level

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
# experiments/my_agent.yaml
run:
  agent_provider: openai
  agent_privilege: medium
  agent_allowed_accounts:
    - "0xattacker..."

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text: The bridge plugin system simulates cross-chain message passing. When a bridge plugin is deployed, messages sent on one chain can be relayed to another.

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• L4 reproductions ΓÇö reproduce multi-chain exploits that span Ethereum, BSC, and Polygon
  • Bridge protocol validation ΓÇö test how bridge contracts handle forged messages
  • Cross-chain artifact tracking ΓÇö cross_chain.jsonl correlates messages across chains

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
Use a bridge topology:

# experiments/my_experiment.yaml
name: my_experiment
scenario: my_scenario
attack: my_attack
topology: nomad_bridge    # Uses nomad_bridge topology

Configure the topology:

# topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml
name: nomad_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge

From an attack plugin:

bridge = context.bridge_plugin
result = bridge.send_message(
    source_chain="ethereum",
    dest_chain="polygon",
    data="0xforged_calldata",
    correlation_id="msg-001",
)

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text: A self-contained directory with all experiment artifacts, integrity manifests, and reproduction instructions. Suitable for publication, archiving, or sharing with reviewers.

docs.title: Bundle Structure
docs.text:
datasets/DS-2026-00042/
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ run_manifest.json           # Provenance + outcome
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ log_integrity.json          # SHA-256 hashes of all streams
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ artifact_bundle_manifest.json  # SHA-256 of every file
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ report.md                   # Human-readable summary
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ REPRODUCE.md                # Step-by-step reproduction guide
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ run.jsonl                   # Lifecycle events
Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ chain_ethereum/             # Per-chain artifacts
Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ events.jsonl
Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ gas_profile.jsonl
Γöé   Γö£ΓöÇΓöÇ storage_diffs.jsonl
Γöé   ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ balance_diffs.jsonl
ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ chain_bsc/
    ΓööΓöÇΓöÇ ...

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Self-verifying ΓÇö SHA-256 hashes detect tampering
  • Self-contained ΓÇö everything needed to verify the reproduction
  • Portable ΓÇö can be uploaded to Zenodo, sent to reviewers, or archived

docs.title: How to Use It
docs.text:
# Export a run
crucible export DS-2026-00042

# Verify the bundle
crucible verify datasets/DS-2026-00042

# Publish (mark as permanent)
crucible publish DS-2026-00042

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Technical Overview System architecture and core concepts Design Decisions Why each feature was built this way Plugin Development Guide Using features from plugin code User Guide Getting started and daily usage Troubleshooting Common blockers and fixes

docs.title: Fidelity Guide
docs.text: Purpose: Understand Crucible's 5-level fidelity system — what each level guarantees, when to enforce it, and what to do when enforcement fails. This guide helps researchers decide the right fidelity level for their attack pack and debug fidelity violations.

docs.title: Quick Summary
docs.text:
Level What It Means When To Use When To Skip (--no-fidelity) L0 Narrative only — no reproduction code Research notes, incident descriptions N/A (always passes) L1 Conceptual — attack plugin returns a result Initial attack development, prototype First few iterations of a new attack L2 Fork-state behavioral — executes on a forked chain Most reproductions — standard benchmark target During early plugin development L3 Exact transaction replay — replays the exploit tx Formal reproductions, publication Demo/simulation experiments L4 Multi-chain full — cross-chain with bridge plugin Full bridge exploit reproductions Bridge demo experiments (e.g., nomad_bridge_l4)

Golden rule: Use the LOWEST fidelity level that truthfully describes your experiment. L2 is sufficient for most reproductions. Only use L3/L4 if you actually replay exact transactions or use a multi-chain bridge topology.

docs.title: L0 — Narrative (always passes)
docs.text:
No runtime checks. The experiment is a description or research note, not a reproduction.

What's enforced: Nothing.

Typical use case: Writing an incident report, documenting research findings, or planning an attack pack.

docs.title: L1 — Conceptual (attack plugin ran)
docs.text:
What's enforced: - The attack plugin's execute() returned a non-None result

What passes without this level: A plugin that crashes before returning a result.

What's NOT checked: Whether the result was success=True or success=False. L1 only checks that the plugin ran.

AgentPlugin note: LLM-driven agent experiments don't produce AttackResult — the fidelity check will fail for them. Always use --no-fidelity for agent experiments.

docs.title: L2 — Fork-State Behavioral (standard)
docs.text:
What's enforced: - All L1 checks - historical_scope.chains in evidence.yaml is non-empty - Each declared chain exists in the active topology - Trace artifacts exist (events.jsonl, gas_profile.jsonl, or storage_diffs.jsonl) - No transactions failed auto-instrumentation

Common failure reasons and fixes:

Error Message Cause Fix Fidelity level L2 requires historical_scope.chains to be non-empty in evidence.yaml Evidence missing chain list Add chains: under historical_scope: in evidence.yaml Chain 'X' declared in evidence.yaml is not in the active topology Evidence says chain X but topology doesn't have it Either add chain X to topology, or remove from evidence Fidelity level L2 requires fork-state execution evidence... No artifact streams found Attack didn't send any transactions Ensure the plugin calls send_transaction() or eth_call() Fidelity level L2 requires complete trace artifacts, but N transaction(s) failed auto-instrumentation debug_traceTransaction failed for some txs Check Anvil version; older nightlies may not support the tracer combo

docs.title: L3 — Exact Transaction Replay
docs.text:
What's enforced: - All L2 checks - historical_scope.exploit_transactions in evidence.yaml is non-empty - reproduction_scope.exact_tx_replay is true - At least one transaction action found in run.jsonl

Common failure reasons and fixes:

Error Message Cause Fix Fidelity level L3 requires historical_scope.exploit_transactions to be non-empty No exploit tx hashes in evidence Add the real exploit tx hash(es) from Etherscan Fidelity level L3 requires reproduction_scope.exact_tx_replay to be true Flag not set Set exact_tx_replay: true in evidence.yaml Fidelity level L3 requires transaction execution evidence. No transaction actions found in run.jsonl. Attack didn't send any txs, or ran in a different run dir Check that the plugin actually calls send_transaction()

docs.title: L4 — Multi-Chain Full
docs.text:
What's enforced: - All L3 checks - historical_scope.chains has at least 2 chains - reproduction_scope.excluded_components is declared - Topology has a bridge plugin configured

Common failure reasons and fixes:

Error Message Cause Fix Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires at least 2 chains in historical_scope.chains Only 1 chain declared Add the second chain to chains: Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires reproduction_scope.excluded_components to be declared No excluded_components field Add realistic exclusions (e.g., off-chain relayer, non-EVM state) Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires a bridge plugin in the topology configuration Topology has no bridge Use a bridge topology: nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, or wormhole_bridge

docs.title: When To Use --no-fidelity
docs.text: The --no-fidelity flag skips all fidelity enforcement — the experiment runs to completion regardless of whether the runtime behavior matches the declared fidelity level.

docs.title: Use --no-fidelity when:
docs.text: Situation Why Demo experiments (nomad_bridge_l4, demo_agent) Demo experiments are simulations — they don't need to meet formal fidelity requirements During attack development You're writing a new plugin and iterating quickly — fidelity checks would slow you down The declared fidelity level is aspirational Your evidence.yaml says L4 but you haven't implemented full multi-chain yet Archive limitations prevent L2+ Polygon/BSC archives can't serve state for all chains — you get partial results LLM agent experiments Agent behavior is non-deterministic — fidelity checks on agent runs are meaningless Skipping baseline on first run First run has no baseline yet — but fidelity is unrelated to baselines

docs.title: Do NOT use --no-fidelity when:
docs.text: Situation Why You're publishing a formal reproduction Reviewers expect the declared fidelity to be verified Running benchmark comparisons All benchmarks should meet their declared level for fair comparison Debugging a failing experiment Fidelity failures tell you WHAT went wrong — skipping them hides the root cause

docs.title: How to specify:
docs.text:
# Skip fidelity enforcement
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_bridge_l4.yaml --no-fidelity

# Keep fidelity enforcement (default)
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

# Keep fidelity enforcement explicitly (enabled by default)
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

docs.title: When fidelity PASSES
docs.text:
[ok] runtime fidelity verified

This means the experiment's runtime behavior matches the declared L2/L3/L4 level. The manifest will contain:

"fidelity_check": {
  "passed": true,
  "declared_level": "L2",
  "failures": [],
  "warnings": []
}

docs.title: When fidelity FAILS
docs.text:
[fail] runtime fidelity FAILED
         • Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires at least 2 chains in historical_scope.chains
         • Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires a bridge plugin in the topology configuration

The reasons are printed directly below the FAILED line — no need to dig through logs. The manifest also stores the full details:

"fidelity_check": {
  "passed": false,
  "declared_level": "L4",
  "failures": [
    "Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires at least 2 chains in historical_scope.chains",
    "Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires a bridge plugin in the topology configuration"
  ],
  "warnings": []
}

The experiment still writes the manifest — it doesn't crash at fidelity failure. The outcome is set to "failure" with exit code 7 (assertion failed).

docs.title: When you should read the run.jsonl
docs.text:
If you see [fail] runtime fidelity FAILED, your next action depends on what the error says:

  1. Evidence.yaml is missing fields → Open attacks/<name>/evidence.yaml and add them
  2. No trace artifacts found → Your attack didn't send transactions — is that expected?
  3. Missing chain/topology → Your topology doesn't match what evidence.yaml declares
  4. No transaction actions → The attack ran but didn't interact with the chain

If fidelity fails but you're okay with that (e.g., you're developing the attack), rerun with --no-fidelity.

docs.title: How Fidelity Differs From Other Checks
docs.text:
Check What It Does When It Runs Can You Skip It? Fidelity enforcement Validates runtime vs declared level After attack plugin runs --no-fidelity Baseline verification Validates fork state matches golden fingerprint Before experiment starts --no-baseline Evidence pre-flight Validates contracts have code on running chains Before attack plugin runs Cannot skip Publication gates Validates evidence, baseline, and provenance completeness Before experiment (if publication mode) Only without --publication-mode

Key insight: Fidelity is the LAST check before the manifest is written. If everything else passes but fidelity fails, the experiment still completes — it just records a failure.

docs.title: Designing An Attack Pack's Fidelity Level
When choosing a level, ask:
docs.text:
1. Is this a full reproduction? → L2+ (need fork state)
  2. Are you replaying the exact exploit transaction? → L3+ (need calldata reconstruction)
  3. Does the exploit span multiple chains? → L4 (need bridge topology)
  4. Is this a simulation or demonstration? → L2 without --no-fidelity, or L0-L1

docs.title: Retrospective upgrades:
docs.text:
# Convert L2 → L3: Reconstruct exact calldata
crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name my_attack --chain ethereum --rpc-url <archive_rpc>

# Convert L3 → L4: Add bridge topology
# Use nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, or wormhole_bridge topology
# Update evidence.yaml: add bridge chain, excluded_components

Important: Don't claim a higher fidelity than you've actually implemented. Reviewers verify fidelity claims against your code and artifacts.

docs.title: Reference: All Fidelity Error Messages
docs.text: Every possible fidelity violation, what it means, and what to do:

docs.title: L1 Errors (1 possible)
docs.text:
Attack plugin returned no result — cannot verify fidelity
Meaning: The plugin's execute() returned None. Either it crashed, or it's an AgentPlugin. Fix: Ensure execute() returns an AttackResult. For agent experiments, the fidelity check is implicitly skipped.

docs.title: L2 Errors (4 possible)
docs.text:
"Fidelity level L2 requires historical_scope.chains to be non-empty in evidence.yaml"
Fix: Add chains: [ethereum] (or whatever chain you're using) under historical_scope in evidence.yaml.

"Chain 'X' declared in evidence.yaml is not in the active topology"
Fix: Either add chain X to the topology, or remove it from evidence.yaml's chains list.

"Fidelity level L2 requires fork-state execution evidence (events.jsonl, gas_profile.jsonl, or storage_diffs.jsonl). No artifact streams found"
Fix: Ensure your attack plugin actually sends transactions. The instrumentation proxy creates artifact files when it intercepts RPC calls.

"Fidelity level L2 requires complete trace artifacts, but N transaction(s) failed auto-instrumentation"
Fix: Check run.jsonl for failed auto_trace entries. This is often an Anvil version issue — some nightly builds fail with prestateTracer + diffMode.

docs.title: L3 Errors (3 possible)
docs.text:
"Fidelity level L3 requires historical_scope.exploit_transactions to be non-empty in evidence.yaml"
Fix: Add the real exploit transaction hash(es) from Etherscan.

"Fidelity level L3 requires reproduction_scope.exact_tx_replay to be true in evidence.yaml"
Fix: Set exact_tx_replay: true under reproduction_scope.

"Fidelity level L3 requires transaction execution evidence. No transaction actions found in run.jsonl."
Fix: Ensure the attack sends at least one transaction that gets recorded in run.jsonl.

docs.title: L4 Errors (3 possible)
docs.text:
"Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires at least 2 chains in historical_scope.chains"
Fix: Add the second chain (e.g., polygon) to evidence.yaml's chains list.

"Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires reproduction_scope.excluded_components to be declared in evidence.yaml"
Fix: Add excluded_components: with realistic off-chain limitations.

"Fidelity level L4 (multi-chain) requires a bridge plugin in the topology configuration"
Fix: Use a bridge topology (nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, wormhole_bridge). Plain single/dual topologies don't have bridge plugins.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Creating attack plugins — including fidelity level setup docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md Fidelity enforcement and --no-fidelity flag docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Provenance and determinism docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md Common errors and fixes

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The instrumentation layer is an RPC proxy that sits transparently between any code that talks to the local Anvil fork (attack packs, Foundry scripts, LLM agents, manual cast send calls) and the Anvil process itself.

Every transaction is automatically traced — the attack pack does zero artifact collection work.

docs.title: Data Flow Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    ARCHIVE["Archive RPC Provider<br/>eth.llamarpc.com / ankr / publicnode"]

    subgraph Researcher["Researcher"]
        AP["Attack Pack<br/>(Python Plugin)"]
        FS["Foundry Script<br/>(forge script)"]
        LLM["LLM Agent<br/>(future — dashed outline)"]
        CS["cast send / curl"]
    end

    subgraph Crucible["Crucible Testbed"]
        ER["ExperimentRunner"]
        CM["ChainManager"]
        subgraph Proxy["RpcProxy (per chain)"]
            HS["HTTP Server<br/>127.0.0.1:random"]
            EX["ThreadPoolExecutor<br/>max 10 workers"]
        end
        LE["LogEmitter"]
        DIFF["diff.py compare_runs"]
    end

    subgraph Anvil["Anvil Fork (local)"]
        ANV["anvil --fork-url <ARCHIVE_RPC>"]
    end

    subgraph Artifacts["Artifact Streams"]
        GAS["gas_profile.jsonl"]
        EVT["events.jsonl"]
        STO["storage_diffs.jsonl"]
        BAL["balance_diffs.jsonl"]
    end

    subgraph Downstream["Downstream"]
        REPORT["report.md"]
        INTEGRITY["log_integrity.json"]
        DIFF_OUT["storage_equal<br/>events_equal<br/>gas_equal<br/>balances_equal"]
    end

    ARCHIVE -->|eth_getBlockByNumber, eth_getStorageAt| ANV

    ER --> CM
    CM -->|start_instrumentation| Proxy
    ER -->|attack.run| AP

    AP -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    FS -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    LLM -.->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    CS -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS

    HS -->|forward all requests| ANV
    ANV -->|response| HS
    HS -->|response| Researcher

    HS -->|eth_sendTransaction detected| EX
    EX -->|trace_async| ANV
    ANV -->|receipt + debug_traceTransaction| EX
    EX -->|gas_used| LE
    EX -->|events| LE
    EX -->|storage_diffs| LE
    EX -->|balance_diffs| LE

    LE --> GAS
    LE --> EVT
    LE --> STO
    LE --> BAL

    GAS --> DIFF
    EVT --> DIFF
    STO --> DIFF
    BAL --> DIFF

    LE --> REPORT
    LE --> INTEGRITY
    DIFF --> DIFF_OUT

Legend: Solid arrow = synchronous call · Dashed arrow = planned/future · Thick arrow = data artifact

docs.title: Blank Fork Mode — Zero Remote RPC Dependency
docs.text: The diagrams above show the archive fork path: Anvil fetches historical state from a remote archive RPC provider. However, the instrumentation proxy is completely agnostic to how Anvil was started — it only cares that Anvil is listening on an HTTP port.

docs.title: How Blank Forks Work
docs.text:
In blank fork mode (fork_block: null), Anvil starts without --fork-url:

# Archive fork: Anvil fetches remote state
anvil --fork-url https://eth.llamarpc.com --fork-block-number 15259100 --accounts 0 --balance 0

# Blank fork: No remote dependency — Anvil is a standalone local EVM
anvil --accounts 0 --balance 0

The proxy forwards to whatever URL it's given — it does not inspect or care about the upstream chain state. The same RpcProxy class, the same attach_instrumentation() call, the same ThreadPoolExecutor background tracing. No code changes, no config changes.

docs.title: Data Flow (Blank Fork)
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Researcher["Researcher"]
        AP["Attack Pack<br/>(Python Plugin)"]
        FS["Foundry Script<br/>(forge script)"]
        CS["cast send / curl"]
    end

    subgraph Crucible["Crucible Testbed"]
        ER["ExperimentRunner"]
        CM["ChainManager"]
        subgraph Proxy["RpcProxy (per chain)"]
            HS["HTTP Server<br/>127.0.0.1:random"]
            EX["ThreadPoolExecutor<br/>max 10 workers"]
        end
        LE["LogEmitter"]
    end

    subgraph Anvil["Anvil (local, no fork)"]
        ANV["anvil --accounts 0 --balance 0<br/>(standalone EVM — no --fork-url)"]
    end

    subgraph Artifacts["Artifact Streams"]
        GAS["gas_profile.jsonl"]
        EVT["events.jsonl"]
        STO["storage_diffs.jsonl"]
        BAL["balance_diffs.jsonl"]
    end

    ER --> CM
    CM -->|start_instrumentation| Proxy
    ER -->|attack.run| AP

    AP -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    FS -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS
    CS -->|JSON-RPC via proxy_url| HS

    HS -->|forward all requests| ANV
    ANV -->|response| HS
    HS -->|response| Researcher

    HS -->|eth_sendTransaction detected| EX
    EX -->|trace_async| ANV
    ANV -->|receipt + debug_traceTransaction| EX
    EX -->|gas_used| LE
    EX -->|events| LE
    EX -->|storage_diffs| LE
    EX -->|balance_diffs| LE

    LE --> GAS
    LE --> EVT
    LE --> STO
    LE --> BAL

Notice what's missing: The "Archive RPC Provider" box and the eth_getBlockByNumber/ eth_getStorageAt arrows. They simply don't exist in blank fork mode. Everything else — the proxy, the artifact streams, the trace pipeline — is identical to archive fork mode.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Property Archive Fork Blank Fork Remote RPC needed? Yes — archive provider No — fully offline Startup time 30s–5 min (cold cache) <1s — instant Determinism Depends on archive state Fully deterministic CI/hermetic testable Needs fixture replay Yes — directly Artifact collection Same proxy + streams Same proxy + streams Fidelity levels L2 (verified fork state) L3/L4 (validation, no history)

The artifact collection pipeline is identical in both modes. The instrumented data — gas, events, storage diffs, balance diffs — has the same format, the same integrity hashing, the same diffability. The only difference is whether Anvil fetches historical state at startup.

docs.title: Practical Use Cases
docs.text:
• L4 validation experiments (l4_full_validation, nomad_bridge_l4): Two blank forks (ethereum + polygon), bridge plugin deployed on both, bidirectional cross-chain messaging. Zero RPC calls during the entire run — the proxy only talks to local Anvil instances.
  • Offline demo/testing: Run crucible experiment run with blank forks on a plane, in CI, or on an air-gapped machine. Artifact collection works identically.
  • Development workflow: Iterate on attack packs without waiting for archive forks. Switch to archive forks only when ready for L2 fidelity verification.

Important caveat: Blank forks can fail on first startup due to an Anvil nightly-build quirk with --accounts 0 --balance 0. See Troubleshooting §7C and Testbed Scope Guide — Blank Fork Caveats for recovery steps.

docs.title: Sequence: Experiment Run with Instrumentation
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant ER as ExperimentRunner
    participant CM as ChainManager
    participant SC as ScenarioEngine
    participant PX as RpcProxy
    participant AN as Anvil Fork
    participant ARC as Archive RPC
    participant AK as Attack Pack
    participant LE as LogEmitter

    ER->>SC: run(scenario)
    SC->>CM: start_topology()
    CM->>AN: spawn anvil --fork-block 15259100
    AN->>ARC: fetch historical state (lazy, on-demand)
    ARC-->>AN: accounts, storage, code
    AN-->>CM: ready at http://127.0.0.1:8545
    CM-->>SC: runtime
    SC-->>ER: done

    ER->>CM: start_instrumentation("ethereum", log)
    CM->>CM: read meta file for upstream URL
    CM->>PX: attach_instrumentation(upstream, log)
    PX->>PX: start HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:random
    PX-->>CM: proxy_url = http://127.0.0.1:port
    Note over AK,AN: Now runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url == proxy_url

    ER->>AK: run(context)

    AK->>PX: eth_sendTransaction({...})
    PX->>AN: forward eth_sendTransaction
    AN-->>PX: tx_hash
    PX-->>AK: tx_hash

    par Background Trace (via ThreadPoolExecutor)
        PX->>AN: poll eth_getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
        AN-->>PX: receipt {gasUsed, logs, status}
        PX->>AN: debug_traceTransaction(tx_hash, prestateTracer + diffMode)
        AN-->>PX: {pre: {accounts}, post: {accounts}}
        PX->>LE: gas(step="auto_tx", gas_used=21000)
        PX->>LE: event(chain, event_name="Transfer", ...)
        PX->>LE: storage_diff(chain, contract, slot, before, after)
        PX->>LE: balance_diff(chain, contract, account, before, after)
    end

    AK-->>ER: AttackResult(success=true)

    ER->>CM: stop_instrumentation()
    CM->>PX: drain() — wait for pending traces
    PX->>PX: executor.shutdown(wait=True, timeout=10s)
    CM->>PX: stop() — shutdown HTTP server

    ER->>LE: write_report(manifest_path)
    ER->>LE: write_integrity() — hashes + closes handles

docs.title: Integration Points
docs.text: Component Role File RpcProxy HTTP proxy + background tracer (gas, events, storage diffs, balance) crucible/instrumentation.py ChainManager Starts/stops proxy per chain; runtime_for() returns proxy URL transparently crucible/chain_manager.py ExperimentRunner Starts proxy before attack, drains/stops in finally block crucible/experiment.py LogEmitter Receives traced artifacts into typed JSONL streams crucible/logging.py diff.py Compares artifact streams across runs for determinism check crucible/diff.py

docs.title: Proxy URL Transparency
docs.text:
# Before instrumentation:
runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url  # → "http://127.0.0.1:8545"

# After start_instrumentation("ethereum", log):
runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url  # → "http://127.0.0.1:54321" (proxy)

All existing code continues to work unchanged — the proxy is invisible to attack packs, scenario steps, and bridge plugins.

docs.title: Standalone Mode (crucible instrument)
docs.text:
Beyond experiment-mode auto-instrumentation, crucible instrument starts the proxy standalone — no fork, no experiment runner, no scenario. This is useful for ad-hoc research where you:

  1. Start an Anvil fork manually (or use an existing one)
  2. Point the proxy at it with --upstream
  3. Send transactions through the proxy URL (via cast send, Foundry scripts, Python scripts)
  4. Get complete artifact streams without writing any instrumentation code
# Terminal 1: Start Anvil manually
anvil --fork-url https://eth.llamarpc.com --fork-block-number 15259100

# Terminal 2: Start the instrumentation proxy
crucible instrument --upstream http://127.0.0.1:8545 --chain ethereum --output-dir ./logs/my_run

# Terminal 3: Send transactions through the proxy
cast send --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:<proxy-port> 0xTarget --value 1ether

docs.title: Standalone Options
docs.text:
Flag Default Description --upstream, -u http://127.0.0.1:8545 Upstream RPC URL to proxy (typically the Anvil fork) --chain, -c ethereum Chain name for artifact tagging and subdirectory naming --output-dir, -o ./instrument_output Directory to write artifact streams into --port, -p 0 (random) Proxy listen port. Use a specific port for scripting/chaining --timeout, -t 0 (run until Ctrl+C) Auto-exit after N seconds. Useful for scripted runs

Artifacts are written to --output-dir with the same JSONL stream structure as experiment-mode captures.

docs.title: Thread Safety
docs.text: Concern Solution Multi-chain topologies _make_handler() factory creates a fresh handler class per proxy instance — each chain's proxy forwards to its own Anvil independently Rapid-fire tx loops ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) bounds concurrent traces; excess txs queue Race with LogEmitter.close() drain() → stop() ordering: executor shutdown waits for pending traces, then HTTP server stops, then write_integrity() closes handles

docs.title: Fallback Behavior
docs.text:
When debug_traceTransaction is unavailable or returns an error:

Artifact Captured? Source Gas used [ok] Yes eth_getTransactionReceipt Events / logs [ok] Yes eth_getTransactionReceipt Storage diffs [fail] Warning logged N/A — no trace available Balance changes [fail] Warning logged N/A — no trace available

A prominent failure step is logged showing the tx hash, what was lost (storage diffs + balance changes), what was saved (gas + events), and a reference to TROUBLESHOOTING.md for Anvil version advice.

docs.title: Lab Deployment Guide
docs.text: How to host Crucible on a shared lab Linux workstation and make it accessible to all researchers. Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Important Notes Before Starting
  3. Push to Remote Repository
  4. Lab Workstation Setup
  5. SSH Clone from Lab Workstation
  6. Docker Build & Configuration
  7. Multi-User Access
  8. Shared Data & Storage
  9. Streamlit Dashboard on the Network
  10. Pulling Updates
  11. Daily Operations
  12. Backup Strategy
  13. Security Best Practices
  14. Quick-Start Reference
  15. Troubleshooting

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph DevMachine["Your Dev Machine (Windows)"]
        A["git push origin v0.4.0"]
    end
    subgraph GitRepo["GitHub/GitLab (Remote Origin)"]
        B["Repository<br>with SSH deploy<br>key"]
    end
    subgraph LabWorkstation["Lab Linux Workstation (Ubuntu 24.04 LTS)"]
        C["git clone --branch v0.4.0<br>to ~/crucible_testbed"]
        D["docker compose build<br>docker compose up -d"]
        E["Streamlit Dashboard<br>http://lab-ip:8501"]
        C --> D
        D --> E
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C

Your role: You develop on your Windows machine, push releases. The lab workstation runs the testbed 24/7 for all researchers to use.

Deployment path: This guide uses ~/crucible_testbed as the deploy directory. Replace this with your actual clone path throughout.

docs.title: The Entrypoint Adds crucible Automatically
docs.text:
The Docker container's entrypoint already runs uv run crucible "$@". So commands are:

# [ok] CORRECT — entrypoint adds crucible
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run ...

# [fail] WRONG — this becomes "crucible crucible experiment run"
docker compose run --rm orchestrator crucible experiment run ...

docs.title: experiment run Requires a Fork First
docs.text:
experiment run connects to a running Anvil chain. You must fork before running. The standard pattern chains both in one container session:

# [ok] CORRECT — always fork first, then run
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline
"

# [fail] WRONG — this fails because no chain is running
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

Why --entrypoint bash? The container's entrypoint is crucible. To run multiple commands (fork + experiment), you need a shell. --entrypoint bash gives you bash, then uv run crucible <cmd> calls the tool.

docs.title: Fresh Deployments Need --no-baseline
docs.text: On a fresh lab machine, no baselines exist yet. Add --no-baseline to skip the baseline comparison. Once a baseline is captured (see Operations Runbook), you can omit it.

docs.title: ANKR API Key Required
docs.text:
Free RPC providers like LlamaRPC rate-limit Anvil's archive requests. You need an ANKR API key:

  1. Sign up at app.ankr.com
  2. Go to RPC → Ethereum → Active Endpoint
  3. Get a URL like https://rpc.ankr.com/eth/YOUR_KEY
  4. Set it as ETH_RPC_URL in .env

docs.title: 2. Push to Remote Repository
docs.text: Your local repo currently has no remote configured. You need to create a remote repository and push the v0.4.0 tag.

docs.title: Step 1: Create a Remote Repository
docs.text:
Create a repository on your Git provider of choice (GitHub, GitLab, self-hosted):

Provider URL GitHub (recommended) github.com/new GitLab gitlab.com/projects/new Self-hosted GitLab/Gitea Your lab's internal git server

Settings: - Repository name: crucible (or crucible-testbed) - Visibility: Private (lab research — you control access) - Initialize: Do NOT add README, .gitignore, or license (your repo already has these)

docs.title: Step 2: Link and Push
docs.text:
# From your Windows dev machine (in the crucible directory)

# Add the remote
git remote add origin git@github.com:<your-org>/crucible.git
# or: git remote add origin git@gitlab.com:<your-org>/crucible.git

# Push the v0.4.0 tag
git push origin v0.4.0

# Push the main branch
git push origin main

# Verify
git remote -v
git log --oneline -3

Note: Use SSH (git@github.com:...) not HTTPS. SSH key authentication is more secure for automated operations. If you need to set up an SSH key for your Windows machine first: 

# Windows PowerShell
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-lab-email"
# Add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub to GitHub/GitLab → Settings → SSH Keys

docs.title: Step 3: Grant Lab Access
docs.text:
Add the lab's Linux workstation SSH key as a Deploy Key with read-only access: - GitHub: Repository → Settings → Deploy Keys → Add deploy key - GitLab: Repository → Settings → Repository → Deploy Keys

This lets the lab workstation clone without a user account.

docs.title: 3. Lab Workstation Setup
docs.text: On the Linux workstation that will host Crucible:

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: Requirement Minimum Recommended OS Ubuntu 22.04+ Ubuntu 24.04 LTS CPU 4 cores 8+ cores RAM 8 GB 16+ GB (Anvil forks consume 2-4 GB per chain) Disk 50 GB free 200 GB+ SSD (fork caches grow large) Docker 24.0+ 27.0+ Python 3.11 (host, for scripts) 3.12

docs.title: Install Docker
docs.text:
# One-time: install Docker
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com -o get-docker.sh
sudo sh get-docker.sh

# Add the lab user(s) to the docker group
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER

# Log out and back in (or: newgrp docker)
# Verify:
docker --version
docker compose version

docs.title: Create the Deployment Directory
docs.text:
# Clone to ~/crucible_testbed (or any path you prefer)
mkdir -p ~/crucible_testbed

Note: The directory is created automatically by git clone in the next step. This step is just informational.

docs.title: Step 1: Generate an SSH Deploy Key
docs.text:
# On the lab workstation
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "crucible-lab-deploy" -f ~/.ssh/crucible_deploy_key
cat ~/.ssh/crucible_deploy_key.pub

docs.title: Step 2: Add as Deploy Key
docs.text: Copy the output and add it to your Git provider: - GitHub: Repository → Settings → Deploy Keys → Add deploy key - Check "Allow write access" (so the lab can also push experiment artifacts later if needed) - GitLab: Repository → Settings → Repository → Deploy Keys → Add key

docs.title: Step 3: Configure SSH
docs.text:
# On the lab workstation — create SSH config
cat >> ~/.ssh/config << 'EOF'
Host github.com gitlab.com
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/crucible_deploy_key
    IdentitiesOnly yes
EOF

chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config

Note: If using a self-hosted Git server, replace github.com with your server's hostname.

docs.title: Step 4: Clone
docs.text:
# Clone into your home directory
cd ~
git clone --branch v0.4.0 --single-branch \
  git@github.com:<your-org>/crucible.git crucible_testbed

cd ~/crucible_testbed

# Verify
git log --oneline -3
git status

Why --single-branch: Only pulls the v0.4.0 tag's branch — smaller clone, cleaner deployment. Path note: This guide assumes ~/crucible_testbed. If you cloned elsewhere, adjust paths accordingly.

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Environment Variables
docs.text:
cd ~/crucible_testbed

# Copy the template
cp .env.example .env

# Edit .env with your lab's archive RPC URLs
nano .env

Recommended lab configuration:

# ── Ethereum Archive (ANKR — sign up for a free API key) ────
ETH_RPC_URL=https://rpc.ankr.com/eth/YOUR_ANKR_API_KEY

# ── Multi-Chains (GetBlock paid plan recommended for BSC/Polygon) ─
# POLYGON_RPC_URL=https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_GETBLOCK_TOKEN
# BSC_RPC_URL=https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_GETBLOCK_TOKEN
# HARMONY_RPC_URL=https://rpc.ankr.com/harmony

# ── Suppress nightly build warning from Foundry ────────────
FOUNDRY_DISABLE_NIGHTLY_WARNING=true

# ── Dashboard ────────────────────────────────────────────────
STREAMLIT_SERVER_RUNONSAVE=false

Why ANKR and not LlamaRPC? Free providers like eth.llamarpc.com rate-limit Anvil's heavy archive requests during fork initialization. ANKR's free tier (sign up at app.ankr.com) provides a dedicated API key with better throughput.

docs.title: Step 2: Build the Docker Images
docs.text:
cd ~/crucible_testbed

# Build both images (first build: ~5-10 minutes)
docker compose build orchestrator
docker compose build dashboard

# Verify
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor

docs.title: Step 3: Run Initial Validation
docs.text:
Note on startup delay: Every docker compose run --rm orchestrator <command> first runs uv sync (~3-5 seconds) to ensure all dependencies are installed. This is expected behavior, not a performance issue.

# Full validation — no RPC needed for these:
docker compose run --rm orchestrator init
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin list
docker compose run --rm orchestrator abi validate

# Demo experiment — no RPC needed:
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-baseline
"

# Check results
docker compose run --rm orchestrator logs list
docker compose run --rm orchestrator history

docs.title: Step 4: Verify Anvil Can Fork
docs.text:
Test that your ANKR API key works with Anvil:

docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  timeout 20 anvil \
    --fork-url \$ETH_RPC_URL \
    --fork-block-number 12996693 \
    --host 127.0.0.1 \
    --port 8545 \
    --chain-id 1 \
    --gas-price 0 \
    --block-base-fee-per-gas 1000000000 \
    --auto-impersonate \
    --order fifo 2>&1 || echo 'EXIT:' \$?
"

Expected output: Anvil starts, shows "Listening on 127.0.0.1:8545" and a block number of 12996693. The EXIT: 124 at the end is just timeout killing the process — Anvil runs forever as a server.

If you see "rate limited" or "failed to fetch network chain ID", your RPC provider is blocking archive requests. See the troubleshooting section below.

docs.title: Step 5: Run a Real Experiment
docs.text:
# Fork the chain and run the Nomad replay in one go:
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline \
    --name 'my-first-run'
"

Why --no-baseline? On a fresh deployment, no baselines exist yet. The --no-baseline flag skips the baseline comparison step. Once a baseline is captured (see docs/OPERATIONS_RUNBOOK.md), you can omit it.

docs.title: 6. Multi-User Access
docs.text: Multiple researchers will use the same workstation. Here's how to set it up properly.

docs.title: User Group Setup
docs.text:
# Create a 'crucible' group for all researchers
sudo groupadd crucible

# Add each researcher to the group
sudo usermod -aG crucible alice
sudo usermod -aG crucible bob
sudo usermod -aG crucible carol

# Make the deploy directory group-owned
sudo chgrp -R crucible ~/crucible_testbed
sudo chmod -R g+rw ~/crucible_testbed
sudo find ~/crucible_testbed -type d -exec chmod g+s {} \;  # new files inherit group

docs.title: Recommended Workflow per Researcher
docs.text:
Each researcher should run experiments via Docker. The entrypoint already adds crucible, so just use the subcommand:

# Each researcher runs (note: no 'crucible' before 'experiment'):
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline \
    --name 'alice-nomad-run1'
"

# Their artifacts go to: ~/crucible_testbed/logs/nomad_replay/DS-2026-XXXXX/
# All researchers in the 'crucible' group can read each other's logs

Researchers can also name runs with --name to distinguish their work:

docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml \
    --name 'alice-nomad-v2' --no-baseline
"

docs.title: Isolating Work with testbed.local.yaml
docs.text:
Each researcher can create and modify testbed.local.yaml for their experiment-specific fork blocks — it's gitignored so it won't conflict:

# testbed.local.yaml — per-user override, gitignored
chains:
  ethereum:
    fork_block: 15259100  # Alice is working on Nomad

docs.title: Coordinating Anvil Ports (Optional)
docs.text:
If multiple researchers run crucible fork simultaneously, both will try port 8545 and get a conflict. For concurrent use, researchers can override ports in their testbed.local.yaml:

# testbed.local.yaml — Alice uses port 9545
chains:
  ethereum:
    fork_block: 15259100
    port: 9545  # Different port to avoid conflict

In practice, lab researchers typically run experiments one at a time — the workstation is shared, not used simultaneously. If concurrency becomes an issue, Docker Compose profiles or separate compose files can isolate environments.

docs.title: Directory Layout
docs.text:
~/crucible_testbed/       # Deploy root (git clone)
├── crucible/              # Code (git repo)
├── docs/                  # Documentation (git repo)
├── experiments/           # Experiment YAMLs (git repo)
├── .env                   # Shared RPC credentials (gitignored)
├── testbed.local.yaml     # Per-user overrides (gitignored)
├── docker-compose.yml     # Service definitions
│
├── logs/                  # ← SHARED: All experiment artifacts
│   └── <experiment>/      #   One dir per experiment
│       └── DS-2026-xxxxx/ #   One dir per run
│
├── states/                # ← SHARED: Anvil state files
├── datasets/              # ← SHARED: Exported publication bundles
├── .fork_cache/           # ← SHARED: Anvil RPC cache (can be large!)
│
└── docker-compose.override.yml  # Lab-specific overrides (gitignored)

docs.title: Docker Volumes
docs.text:
The docker-compose.yml uses a combination of bind mounts and named volumes:

volumes:
  - .:/workspace              # Bind-mount the host repo (all code, cache, config)
  - states_data:/workspace/states  # Named volume for chain state
  - logs_data:/workspace/logs      # Named volume for experiment artifacts

The named volumes (states_data, logs_data) persist across container restarts. On the host, they live in:

# Docker named volume locations:
/var/lib/docker/volumes/crucible-testbed_states_data/
/var/lib/docker/volumes/crucible-testbed_logs_data/

Note: The fork_cache named volume was removed because .fork_cache/ is now managed on the bind-mounted working directory (.:/workspace) directly. This avoids "Device/resource busy" conflicts between the Docker volume and the host filesystem. The .fork_cache/ directory is gitignored and lives alongside the code.

Since the working directory is bind-mounted (.:/workspace), the logs/, states/, datasets/, and .fork_cache/ directories on the host filesystem are all accessible directly by all researchers in the crucible group.

docs.title: Disk Usage Monitoring
docs.text:
# Quick disk check
docker compose run --rm orchestrator status --disk

# Full breakdown per directory
du -sh logs/ states/ datasets/ .fork_cache/

# Clean old scratch runs (auto-cleanable)
docker compose run --rm orchestrator clean --logs-older-than 30

# Remove all fork cache (re-downloaded on next fork)
docker compose run --rm orchestrator clean --cache

docs.title: 8. Streamlit Dashboard on the Network
docs.text: The dashboard gives all researchers a browser UI to view experiment results.

docs.title: Step 1: Create docker-compose.override.yml
docs.text:
# ~/crucible_testbed/docker-compose.override.yml (gitignored)
cat > docker-compose.override.yml << 'EOF'
services:
  dashboard:
    ports:
      - "0.0.0.0:8501:8501"   # Listen on all network interfaces
    restart: always
    environment:
      - STREAMLIT_SERVER_RUNONSAVE=false
EOF

docs.title: Step 2: Start the Dashboard as a Daemon
docs.text:
# Start dashboard in background (survives terminal close)
docker compose up -d dashboard

# Verify it's running
docker compose ps
# Should show: dashboard   Up  0.0.0.0:8501->8501/tcp

docs.title: Step 3: Access from Lab Network
docs.text:
Researchers access the dashboard at:

http://<LAB_WORKSTATION_IP>:8501

To find the IP: 

# On the lab workstation
hostname -I | awk '{print $1}'
# Example output: 192.168.1.100
# → Dashboard at: http://192.168.1.100:8501

docs.title: Step 4: Security (Lab Network Only)
docs.text:
For a lab-internal deployment (no external exposure), the dashboard is safe as-is. If the workstation has a public IP, add a firewall rule:

# Allow only lab subnet
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.0.0/16 to any port 8501
sudo ufw deny 8501

docs.title: Starting Everything for Full Service
docs.text:
# Start dashboard + optional daemon Anvil chains
docker compose up -d dashboard

# Optionally pre-start Anvil chains (if researchers frequently fork)
docker compose up -d anvil-ethereum

docs.title: 9. Pulling Updates
docs.text:
When you tag a new release on your dev machine, update the lab workstation:

# On the lab workstation
cd ~/crucible_testbed

# 1. Pull the new tag
git fetch origin --tags
git checkout v0.X.Y

# 2. Rebuild Docker images
docker compose build orchestrator

# 3. Run validation
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin list

# 4. Check nothing is broken
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --no-baseline
"

# 5. Restart dashboard if it was running
docker compose up -d dashboard

docs.title: Morning Health Check
docs.text:
# Quick check that everything is alive
docker compose ps
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor
docker compose run --rm orchestrator status --disk

docs.title: Running an Experiment
docs.text:
# Standard: fork + run in one container (--no-baseline for fresh runs)
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline \
    --name 'alice-nomad-2026-06-08'
"

docs.title: Browsing Results
docs.text:
# List completed runs
docker compose run --rm orchestrator logs list
docker compose run --rm orchestrator history

# Or use the browser dashboard
# http://<LAB_WORKSTATION_IP>:8501

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
# Weekly: clean old scratch runs
docker compose run --rm orchestrator clean --logs-older-than 7

# Monthly: rebuild history index
docker compose run --rm orchestrator history --rebuild

# As needed: remove all states
docker compose run --rm orchestrator clean --states

docs.title: Full Shutdown
docs.text:
# Stop everything (containers + remove volumes)
docker compose down

# To also delete all data:
docker compose down -v

docs.title: What to Back Up
docs.text: Data Location Frequency Size Critical? Experiment logs logs/ Weekly Variable [ok] Yes — primary research output Exported bundles datasets/ Per-publish Small [ok] Yes — publication artifacts Fork cache .fork_cache/ Never 10-50 GB [fail] Can re-download Baselines/snapshots states/ Event-based Small [warn] Nice to have Config .env, testbed.local.yaml Events Tiny [ok] Yes — backup manually

docs.title: Backup Commands
docs.text:
# Backup experiment logs (compress with date)
tar czf "backup-logs-$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz" logs/

# Backup specific run
docker compose run --rm orchestrator export DS-2026-00001
tar czf "backup-export-DS-2026-00001.tar.gz" datasets/DS-2026-00001/

# Automated: add to crontab
# crontab -e
# 0 2 * * 0 cd ~/crucible_testbed && tar czf /backup/logs-$(date +\%Y\%m\%d).tar.gz logs/

docs.title: Restore
docs.text:
# Extract backed-up logs
tar xzf backup-logs-20260608.tar.gz

# Rebuild history index after restore
docker compose run --rm orchestrator history --rebuild

docs.title: Critical: .env is GitIgnored
docs.text:
# Verify .env is NOT tracked
git check-ignore .env
# Should output: .env  (if it's properly gitignored)

# NEVER commit .env — it contains real API keys

docs.title: File Permissions
docs.text:
# Set correct permissions after any change
sudo chgrp -R crucible ~/crucible_testbed
sudo chmod -R g+rw ~/crucible_testbed
sudo find ~/crucible_testbed -type d -exec chmod g+s {} \;

docs.title: Firewall (If Workstation Has External IP)
docs.text:
# Allow SSH
sudo ufw allow ssh

# Allow dashboard — lab network only
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.0.0/16 to any port 8501

# Allow Anvil RPC — localhost only (these are NOT exposed externally by default)
sudo ufw deny 8545
sudo ufw deny 8546
sudo ufw deny 8547

# Deny everything else
sudo ufw default deny incoming
sudo ufw default allow outgoing
sudo ufw enable

docs.title: Regular Maintenance
docs.text:
# Keep Docker updated
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli

# Clean unused Docker resources (disk space)
docker system prune -f --volumes

# Rotate logs
sudo journalctl --vacuum-time=30d

docs.title: First-Time Setup (One Command)
docs.text:
# On the lab workstation, after SSH key is configured:
git clone --branch v0.4.0 --single-branch \
  git@github.com:<your-org>/crucible.git ~/crucible_testbed && \
cd ~/crucible_testbed && \
cp .env.example .env && \
nano .env && \
docker compose build orchestrator && \
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor && \
echo "Setup complete!"

docs.title: Daily Commands Cheatsheet
docs.text:
cd ~/crucible_testbed

# Health check
docker compose ps
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor
docker compose run --rm orchestrator status --disk

# Run experiment (--no-baseline until a baseline is captured)
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline --name 'my-run'
"

# Browse results
docker compose run --rm orchestrator logs list
docker compose run --rm orchestrator history
# Or: open http://<lab-ip>:8501

# Export & verify
docker compose run --rm orchestrator export DS-2026-XXXXX
docker compose run --rm orchestrator verify datasets/DS-2026-XXXXX

# Cleanup
docker compose run --rm orchestrator clean --logs-older-than 30
docker compose run --rm orchestrator clean --states

# Update to new release
git fetch origin --tags && git checkout v0.X.Y && docker compose build orchestrator

# Full shutdown
docker compose down

docs.title: Anvil Fails: "rate limited" / "failed to fetch network chain ID"
docs.text:
Error: failed to fetch network chain ID
Context:
- Max retries exceeded HTTP error 503 ... "Rate Limited"

Cause: The RPC provider (e.g., LlamaRPC) is rate-limiting Anvil's archive requests.

Fix: Use ANKR with a free API key: 1. Sign up at app.ankr.com 2. Get your Ethereum endpoint: https://rpc.ankr.com/eth/YOUR_KEY 3. Set ETH_RPC_URL in .env

docs.title: Anvil Fails: "Unauthorized: You must authenticate"
docs.text:
Error: failed to fetch network chain ID
Context:
- server returned an error response: error code -32000: Unauthorized: You must authenticate

Cause: ANKR requires an API key in the URL.

Fix: Append your API key: https://rpc.ankr.com/eth/PASTE_YOUR_KEY_HERE

docs.title: "No such command 'crucible'"
docs.text:
Cause: The Docker entrypoint already runs uv run crucible "$@". Adding crucible yourself becomes crucible crucible experiment run.

Fix: Run the subcommand directly: 

# [ok] Correct
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run ...

# [fail] Wrong
docker compose run --rm orchestrator crucible experiment run ...

docs.title: "baseline manifest not found"
docs.text:
ERROR: baseline manifest not found for scenario nomad_pre_exploit

Cause: No baseline captured yet on this machine.

Fix: Add --no-baseline flag and fork first: 

docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline
"

docs.title: "Connection refused" on experiment run
docs.text:
ERROR: Experiment failed: RPC call failed for anvil_dumpState at http://127.0.0.1:8545: Connection refused

Cause: You ran experiment run without forking first. Anvil isn't running on port 8545.

Fix: Always fork before running — use the chained pattern: 

docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline
"

docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/USER_GUIDE.md Quick-start and daily Crucible usage docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md All CLI commands and options docs/OPERATIONS_RUNBOOK.md Daily operations and checklists docs/MAINTENANCE.md Cleanup and lifecycle management docs/TROUBLESHOOTING.md Common issues and solutions docs/RPC_PROVIDER_GUIDE.md Archive RPC provider comparison docs/RELEASE_CHECKLIST.md How to create future releases

docs.title: Crucible Project Guide
docs.text: How to understand, manage, maintain, and extend the Crucible codebase. Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Project Overview
  2. Directory Structure
  3. Running Tests
  4. Adding New Content
  5. CLI Reference (Quick)
  6. Dashboard Overview
  7. Publication Workflow
  8. Stray Directories Explained
  9. Known Limitations
  10. Future Work Guide

docs.title: 1. Project Overview
docs.text:
Crucible is a CLI-first, modular EVM bridge security research testbed. It allows researchers to:

  • Fork any EVM chain at any block using Foundry/Anvil
  • Replay historical bridge exploits deterministically
  • Collect structured research artifacts (JSONL logs, state diffs, gas profiles)
  • Reset environments in seconds (3-tier reset: soft <5s → medium <30s → hard <5min)
  • Validate patches against known attack vectors
  • Publish exportable artifact bundles with integrity guarantees

docs.title: Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
CLI (crucible/cli.py → crucible/commands/*.py)
  │
  ├── Chain Manager — starts/stops Anvil subprocesses, manages lifecycle
  ├── Scenario Engine — loads and executes attack scenarios
  ├── Experiment Runner — orchestrates formal research runs
  ├── Plugin Registry — discovers and validates plugins
  ├── Baseline System — golden baseline capture and fingerprint verification
  ├── Structured Logger — writes 5 JSONL streams per run
  └── Dashboard (Streamlit) — browser UI for all of the above

docs.title: Key Files
docs.text: File Purpose crucible/cli.py Thin entry point, delegates to commands/ crucible/config.py YAML config parsing with Pydantic models crucible/chain_manager.py Anvil process lifecycle crucible/rpc.py JSON-RPC client + provider fallback + rate limiting crucible/baseline.py Baseline capture, fingerprint, restore crucible/scenario.py Scenario loading and YAML-step execution crucible/experiment.py Formal experiment runner, matrix, resume crucible/logging.py JSONL emitter for 5 log streams crucible/plugin.py Plugin discovery and validation crucible/sdk.py Plugin interfaces (AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin) crucible/rpc.py JSON-RPC client with provider failover + rate limiting crucible/interfaces/__init__.py Public API re-exports (AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, etc.) crucible/checkpoint.py Phase-level checkpoint/resume crucible/diff.py Canonicalized run comparison crucible/history.py SQLite experiment index

docs.title: Root Directory
docs.text: File/Dir Keep? Purpose crucible/ [ok] Core Python package — all CLI and orchestration logic dashboard/ [ok] Dashboard Streamlit app (app.py + panels/) docs/ [ok] Docs All documentation (see below for structure) attacks/ [ok] Attacks 6 benchmark attack packs + demo scenarios/ [ok] Scenarios 7 scenario definitions (1 per attack + demo) experiments/ [ok] Experiments Experiment YAML files for each attack topologies/ [ok] Topologies 5 topology profiles (single, dual, bridge, etc.) abis/ [ok] ABIs Contract ABIs organized by protocol bridges/ [ok] Bridges Bridge plugin implementations (demo_bridge, nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, wormhole_bridge) tests/ [ok] Tests Unit tests notebooks/ [ok] Keep Jupyter analysis notebook scripts/ [ok] Keep AE reproducibility shell scripts patches/ [ok] Keep Patch validation directory (future use) plugins/ [ok] Keep Plugin directory (future use) bootstrap.py [ok] Keep Optional state preparation script .env.example [ok] Keep Environment variable template pyproject.toml [ok] Keep Python package config + dependencies uv.lock [ok] Keep Lockfile — committed for reproducibility foundry.toml [ok] Keep Foundry configuration testbed.yaml [ok] Keep Main workspace configuration docker-compose.yml [ok] Keep Docker Compose setup Dockerfile [ok] Keep Docker image definition README.md [ok] Keep Project README LICENSE [ok] Keep MIT License CITATION.cff [ok] Keep Citation metadata streamlit_app.py [fail] Removed Deprecated — redirects to dashboard/app.py crucible_*.md (root) [fail] Moved Moved to docs/design/ *_review.md (root) [fail] Removed Internal review docs — not for publication
