docs.title: Formal Reproducibility Checklist
docs.text:
• Workspace initialized with crucible init
  • Environment validated with crucible doctor
  • Baseline captured and verified
  • Git commit pinned in run_manifest.json
  • Foundry version recorded
  • Baseline verified before run
  • Cache mode and fingerprint recorded
  • Log integrity hashes written
  • Experiment run twice with identical diffs
  • Export bundle verified with crucible verify
  • Cross-provider reproduction documented
  • Contract addresses verified on Etherscan (not EOAs)
  • Fork block verified against exploit transaction
  • RPC provider tested for archive depth
  • Stale meta files cleaned before forking
  • Determinism verified via crucible diff

docs.title: Reproduction Scope & Capabilities
docs.text: Purpose: Help researchers understand what Crucible can reproduce, at what fidelity, and what's in vs out of scope before planning a study. Last updated: June 2026

docs.title: What Crucible Can Reproduce
docs.text: Crucible is an EVM-only bridge security research testbed. It reproduces historical bridge exploits by forking blockchain state at a specific block using Foundry's Anvil and running attack plugins against the forked environment.

docs.title: Core Capability
docs.text:
Archive RPC → Fork EVM chain at block N → Run attack plugin → Capture artifacts
Capability Supported? Notes Fork any EVM chain [ok] Yes Ethereum, BSC, Polygon, Harmony — any chain with an archive RPC endpoint Fork at any historical block [ok] Yes Requires archive node access at that block depth Replay exploit transactions [ok] Yes L3: forge exact calldata and replay on fork Behavioral reproduction [ok] Yes L2: verify vulnerable state exists and exploit path works Multi-chain reproduction [ok] Supported via 3 bridge plugins Nomad (2 chains), Poly Network (3 chains), Wormhole (2 chains) — see bridge topology files Deploy fresh bridge contracts on blank chains [ok] Yes NomadBridge, PolyNetworkBridge, WormholeBridge plugins deploy real Solidity contracts to Anvil forks Forge cross-chain messages [ok] Yes bridge_plugin.send_message() with forged calldata — captured in cross_chain.jsonl Non-EVM chains (Solana, Cosmos) [fail] No Crucible is EVM-only by design Off-chain infrastructure [fail] No Relayers, keepers, MPC nodes, governance — must be modeled or excluded

docs.title: Fidelity Levels Supported
docs.text: Level What It Means Crucible Support Example L4 Full multi-chain + bridge simulation [ok] Supported via 3 bridge plugins — Nomad (2 chains), Poly Network (3 chains), Wormhole (2 chains). Requires paid archive RPCs for BSC/Polygon at 2021-era blocks Poly Network (all 3 chains), Nomad, Wormhole L3 Exact historical transaction replay [ok] Supported — forge calldata, replay on fork Nomad process() call L2 Fork-state behavioral reproduction [ok] Primary use case — verify vulnerability exists Wormhole guardian bypass L1 Conceptual / narrative reproduction [ok] Supported — deploy vulnerable contracts locally Multichain router permit (MPC compromise) L0 Description only [ok] Supported — no code required Documentation-only

docs.title: Complete (L2 — Research-Grade)
docs.text:
These attacks are fully reproducible with determinism proven:

Attack Chains Fork Block Fidelity Key Limitation Nomad Bridge (nomad_replica_fraud) Ethereum 15,259,100 L2 [ok] Doesn't replay exact historical tx (uses reconstructed forged message — proxy ABI resolution needed for exact replay) Wormhole Guardian Bypass (wormhole_guardian_bypass) Ethereum 14,125,553 L2 [ok] Root cause was Solana-side (deprecated sysvar); only EVM-side effects reproducible Poly Network PrivKey (poly_network_privkey) Ethereum + Polygon + BSC 12,996,693 L2 [ok] 3-chain topology available and determinism proven. BSC + Polygon at exploit blocks require paid archive RPCs for full multi-chain run Horizon Multisig Drain (horizon_multisig_drain) Ethereum 15,005,526 L2 [ok] Infrastructure compromise (not a contract bug) — replaying Ethereum tx succeeds but proves nothing about the vulnerability. Harmony chain (non-EVM) not forkable, blocking L4 BNB Token Hub Proof (bnb_token_hub_proof) BSC 22,166,659 L2 [ok] CrossChain contract (0x...2000) identified as the real handlePackage() target. Function selector corrected to 0x84013b6a. Requires paid BSC archive RPC (block 22M exceeds free tier)

docs.title: Complete (L1 — Narrative / Infrastructure)
docs.text:
These attacks are correctly classified at their maximum possible fidelity:

Attack Chains Fork Block Fidelity Key Limitation Multichain Router Permit (multichain_router_permit) Ethereum (narrative) — L1 📝 Maxed at L1. This was an MPC node infrastructure compromise, not a smart contract bug. No on-chain reproduction possible

docs.title: Demo & Reference
docs.text: Pack Fidelity Purpose demo_attack L0 Reference AttackPlugin implementation for learning (< 15 lines) demo_agent Agent LLM-driven autonomous agent demo demo_bridge Bridge Reference BridgePlugin implementation

docs.title: Chain Compatibility Matrix
docs.text: Chain Archive RPC Available? Fork Block Range Cost Ethereum [ok] Free (LlamaNodes, Ankr) 0–current Free Polygon [warn] Limited free ~40M+ (current only on free tier) Paid ($39/mo GetBlock) BSC [warn] Limited free ~28M+ (current only on free tier) Paid ($39/mo GetBlock) Harmony [ok] Free (Ankr) 0–current Free

docs.title: Recommended Provider
docs.text:
GetBlock Starter ($39/month) — covers BSC + Polygon archive. Ethereum works on free LlamaNodes.

See docs/RPC_PROVIDER_GUIDE.md for full comparison.

docs.title: Attack Types Crucible Can Reproduce
docs.text: Type Example Works? Smart contract logic bugs Nomad committedRoot = 0x00 [ok] Access control bypass Wormhole guardian signature verification [ok] Hash collision / selector confusion Poly Network verifyHeaderAndExecuteTx / putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes [ok] Message forgery (Merkle proof) Nomad, BNB TokenHub proof bypass [ok] Compromised key / privilege abuse Horizon multisig drain [warn] (infrastructure, not contract bug) Re-entrancy on bridge contracts Standard EVM re-entrancy [ok] Flash loan + price manipulation On forked chain with sufficient state [ok]

docs.title: Chains Crucible Can Fork
docs.text:
• Any EVM-compatible chain with an archive-capable RPC endpoint
  • Tested: Ethereum (mainnet), BSC, Polygon, Harmony
  • Multi-chain experiments supported via bridge topology

docs.title: What's Out of Scope
docs.text: Category Why Non-EVM chains (Solana, Cosmos, Near, etc.) Crucible's ChainManager launches Anvil (EVM-only). Non-EVM reproduction requires separate tooling Off-chain infrastructure Relayers, keepers, MPC nodes, governance processes — Crucible can't simulate these. They must be declared in excluded_components in evidence.yaml Live mainnet interaction Crucible forks are isolated — no transactions ever reach the real network. By design, not a limitation Real-time mempool monitoring Crucible is a replay tool, not a monitoring tool Social engineering / phishing Out of scope for a technical testbed

docs.title: Step 1: Identify Your Target
docs.text:
# Example: You want to study bridge message verification vulnerabilities
# Which attacks are relevant?
- Nomad Bridge (forged Merkle proof) — [ok] L2 complete
- Wormhole (guardian signature bypass) — [ok] L2 complete  
- BNB TokenHub (IAVL proof bypass) — 🚧 scaffold, needs research fill

docs.title: Step 2: Check Chain Requirements
docs.text: If your target is on... You need... Ethereum only Free RPC (LlamaNodes) Ethereum + Polygon Paid RPC for Polygon archive BSC only Paid RPC for BSC archive Harmony Free RPC (Ankr)

docs.title: Step 3: Choose Fidelity Level
docs.text: If you want to... Target fidelity Prove a vulnerability exists L2 Show exactly how the exploit executed L3 Demonstrate cross-chain impact L4

docs.title: Step 4: Find or Create the Attack Pack
docs.text:
# Check if it exists
ls attacks/
crucible plugin list --type attack

# If it's a scaffold, fill in:
crucible plugin validate attacks/<name>  # check what's missing
# Then update evidence.yaml, scenario.yaml, and plugin code

See docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md for the complete guide.

docs.title: Step 5: Run With Publication Gates
docs.text:
# Run with publication mode for research-grade gates
crucible experiment run experiments/<name>.yaml --publication-mode

docs.title: Reproducibility Guarantees
docs.text: Claim Detail Bit-for-bit identical runs Same topology, same fork block, same attack → same gas, events, storage, balances [ok] Cross-provider reproduction Same results across different archive RPC providers (LlamaNodes vs Alchemy) [ok] Cross-environment Same results in Docker and native local [ok] Cross-machine Same results on different machines with same git commit [ok]

docs.title: What's NOT guaranteed identical
docs.text:
• Cache timing (cold vs warm fork)
  • RPC provider response ordering (doesn't affect on-chain state)
  • Cross-chain relay timing (multi-chain experiments)
  • Artifact file ordering (mitigated by canonical sort in diff)

docs.title: Researcher's Guide to Observability & Day-to-Day Commands
docs.text: Purpose: What a researcher can observe during Crucible experiments and the everyday cast/forge commands they'll need. Covers: Crucible observability commands, JSONL artifact streams, cast usage with forked chains, and troubleshooting.

docs.title: Part 1: Observability — What You Can See
docs.text: Crucible provides multiple windows into what's happening, from real-time CLI status to detailed per-run artifact streams.

docs.title: 1.1 Quick Health Check: crucible doctor
docs.text:
Run this first to verify your environment is ready:

crucible doctor
# Sample output:
# Checks: Python imports, Foundry binaries, forge --version,
#         testbed.yaml load, RPC URL resolution, .env file,
#         required directories, port availability, Docker
# Auto-fix: --fix repairs broken symlinks + missing dirs

docs.title: 1.2 View Running Chains: crucible status
docs.text:
After forking, see every Anvil instance:

crucible fork --topology single
crucible status

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │                    Chains                        │
# ├────────┬────────┬──────┬────────┬────────┬───────┤
# │ Name   │ PID    │ Port │ Block  │ Status  │ RPC  │
# ├────────┼────────┼──────┼────────┼────────┼───────┤
# │ethereum │ 12345  │ 8545 │ 12996693│ ALIVE  │...   │
# └────────┴────────┴──────┴────────┴────────┴───────┘

Use --disk to see disk usage across states, logs, datasets, and fork cache.

docs.title: 1.3 What a Run Directory Contains
docs.text:
Every experiment produces a logs/<experiment>/<run_id>/ directory with this structure:

logs/nomad_replay/DS-2026-00209/
├── run_manifest.json          ← Provenance: git commit, Foundry version,
│                                config fingerprint, cache state, outcome
├── run.jsonl                  ← Experiment lifecycle: phases, steps, messages, errors
├── log_integrity.json         ← SHA-256 hashes of ALL artifact files
├── report.md                  ← Human-readable summary with tables
├── chain_ethereum/            ← Per-chain artifacts (one dir per chain)
│   ├── events.jsonl           ←   Contract events (Transfer, Approval, etc.)
│   ├── gas_profile.jsonl      ←   Gas used per transaction
│   ├── storage_diffs.jsonl    ←   Storage slot changes (before → after)
│   ├── balance_diffs.jsonl    ←   Balance changes per account
│   └── run.jsonl              ←   Chain-specific lifecycle entries
├── bridge/                    ← Cross-chain artifacts (multi-chain only)
│   ├── cross_chain.jsonl      ←   Correlated messages across chains
│   └── events.jsonl           ←   Bridge-specific events
└── artifact_bundle_manifest.json  ← (after export) All files with hashes

docs.title: 1.4 Inspecting the 6 Artifact Streams
docs.text:
Each stream is JSONL (one JSON object per line):

run.jsonl — The main event log: 

{"timestamp":"2026-06-07T...","phase":"setup","action":"experiment_start","status":"running","message":"experiment started"}
{"timestamp":"2026-06-07T...","phase":"attack","action":"send_tx","status":"success","message":"exploit transaction sent"}
{"timestamp":"2026-06-07T...","phase":"teardown","action":"experiment_end","status":"success","message":"experiment completed"}

events.jsonl — Every emitted contract event: 

{"chain":"ethereum","event_name":"Transfer","address":"0x...","topics":["0xddf252ad..."],"data":"0x..."}
{"chain":"ethereum","event_name":"MessageAccepted","address":"0x...","topics":["0x8b7c1a1f..."],"data":"0x..."}

storage_diffs.jsonl — Every slot that changed: 

{"chain":"ethereum","contract":"0x...","slot":"0x1","before":"0x00","after":"0x01"}

gas_profile.jsonl — Gas per operation: 

{"chain":"ethereum","step":"auto_tx","gas_used":25150,"tx_hash":"0x..."}

balance_diffs.jsonl — Balance changes: 

{"chain":"ethereum","contract":"0x...","account":"0x...","before":"0x...","after":"0x..."}

cross_chain.jsonl — Cross-chain message correlation (multi-chain only): 

{"correlation_id":"xchain-0001","source_chain":"ethereum","dest_chain":"bsc","status":"emitted"}

docs.title: 1.5 The Report: report.md
docs.text:
After every run, Crucible generates a human-readable report:

cat logs/nomad_replay/DS-2026-00209/report.md
# Shows:
# - Summary table (log entries, events, storage diffs, gas, failures)
# - Phase breakdown (setup, attack, teardown)
# - Gas profile per step
# - Storage modifications per chain
# - Contract events with counts

docs.title: 1.6 Integrity Check: log_integrity.json
docs.text:
Every file in the run directory is hashed:

{
  "run_id": "DS-2026-00209",
  "files": {
    "run.jsonl": "sha256:a1b2c3...",
    "chain_ethereum/events.jsonl": "sha256:d4e5f6...",
    "chain_ethereum/gas_profile.jsonl": "sha256:g7h8i9..."
  }
}

Use this to verify artifacts haven't been tampered with after export.

docs.title: 1.7 Comparing Two Runs: crucible diff
docs.text:
The definitive determinism check — proves two runs are identical:

crucible diff DS-2026-00182 DS-2026-00183
# Output:
# {
#   "storage_equal": true,
#   "events_equal": true,
#   "gas_equal": true,
#   "balances_equal": true,
#   "gas": {"run_a": 25150, "run_b": 25150, "delta": 0},
#   "storage": {"run_a": 2, "run_b": 2},
#   "events": {"run_a": 3, "run_b": 3},
#   "balances": {"run_a": 2, "run_b": 2}
# }

All four fields must be true for a deterministic reproduction.

docs.title: 1.8 Browsing History: crucible history
docs.text:
crucible history
# Shows all past runs in a table with:
# Run ID, Experiment, Attack, Outcome, Started At

# Filtering:
crucible history --outcome success
crucible history --tag "nomad"
crucible history --limit 5

# Rebuild index from logs:
crucible history --rebuild

docs.title: 1.9 The Provenance Record: run_manifest.json
docs.text:
Every run records its exact environment:

cat logs/nomad_replay/DS-2026-00209/run_manifest.json | jq .provenance
# {
#   "git_commit": "abc123...",
#   "git_dirty": false,
#   "foundry_version": "forge 1.0.0 (stable)",
#   "python_version": "3.12",
#   "docker_mode": false,
#   "config_fingerprint": "sha256:...",
#   "cache_mode": "cold",
#   "experiment_file_hash": "sha256:...",
#   "topology_hash": "sha256:...",
#   "evidence_hash": "sha256:..."
# }

docs.title: 1.10 The Instrumentation Proxy (Automatic)
docs.text:
When an experiment runs, Crucible places a proxy between the attack plugin and Anvil:

Attack Plugin → HTTP POST → Instrumentation Proxy (thread) → Forward to Anvil
                                    ↓
                          Auto-captures: gas, events, storage diffs,
                          balance changes — for EVERY transaction

You don't need to do anything. Every eth_sendTransaction or cast send that flows through the proxy generates complete artifact streams automatically. No per-attack instrumentation code needed.

docs.title: 1.11 When Artifact Streams Are Empty — Diagnosing the Proxy
docs.text:
If an experiment succeeds (exit 0) but events.jsonl or storage_diffs.jsonl are empty, the proxy's trace capture may have failed silently. This happens when debug_traceTransaction is unavailable or returns an error on the forked Anvil.

What's affected vs what still works:

Artifact Captured? Source Gas used [ok] Always eth_getTransactionReceipt Balance diffs [ok] Usually Captured from trace if available Events (logs) [fail] May be empty Captured from receipt if trace available Storage diffs [fail] May be empty Requires debug_traceTransaction

How to recover the data manually using cast:

# Set your RPC URL
export RPC="http://127.0.0.1:8545"

# 1. Find the transaction hash from the gas profile
cat logs/<experiment>/<run_id>/chain_ethereum/gas_profile.jsonl
# → {"tx_hash": "0x...", "gas_used": 25150}

# 2. Get the receipt — this contains all emitted events (logs)
cast receipt <tx_hash> --rpc-url $RPC
# Look for the "logs" array — each log is an emitted event

# 3. Read the contract's storage to verify state changes
cast storage <contract_address> <slot_hex> --rpc-url $RPC

# 4. Check if debug_traceTransaction is available
cast rpc debug_traceTransaction <tx_hash> \
  '{"tracer": "prestateTracer", "tracerConfig": {"diffMode": true}}' \
  --rpc-url $RPC

When to worry vs not: - Experiment passes assertions (check run.jsonl) AND gas is captured → Exploit worked, artifacts are bonus - Experiment fails AND artifacts are empty → Something broke before the proxy — check the run log first - Only storage diffs missing but events present → Partial trace capture, still useful

Root cause check: If debug_traceTransaction works directly against Anvil but not through the proxy, file a bug. If it also fails directly, it's an Anvil version limitation.

docs.title: Part 2: Day-to-Day Foundry Commands
docs.text: These are the cast and forge commands you'll use most often while working with Crucible's forked chains.

docs.title: 2.1 Finding Your Chain's RPC URL
docs.text:
After crucible fork, the default chain runs on:

# Ethereum (single topology): http://127.0.0.1:8545
# Check with:
crucible status

Set this as an environment variable for convenience:

export RPC="http://127.0.0.1:8545"

docs.title: 2.2 cast Commands — Query Chain State
docs.text:
Check if a contract exists (has code): 

cast code 0x98f3c9e6E3fAce36bAAd05FE09d375Ef1464288B --rpc-url $RPC
# Returns bytecode if contract exists, "0x" if EOA

Check an account's ETH balance: 

cast balance 0x98f3c9e6E3fAce36bAAd05FE09d375Ef1464288B --rpc-url $RPC

Read storage at a specific slot: 

cast storage 0x98f3c9e6E3fAce36bAAd05FE09d375Ef1464288B 0x0 --rpc-url $RPC

Call a read-only contract function: 

cast call 0x98f3c9e6E3fAce36bAAd05FE09d375Ef1464288B \
  "totalSupply()(uint256)" --rpc-url $RPC

cast call 0x98f3c9e6E3fAce36bAAd05FE09d375Ef1464288B \
  "balanceOf(address)(uint256)" 0x... --rpc-url $RPC

Get the current block number: 

cast block-number --rpc-url $RPC

Get transaction details: 

cast tx 0x5ea16a4f9104b901a75662f3900ca8d3d92209d846310237ca65675e2f2e519c \
  --rpc-url $RPC

Get transaction receipt: 

cast receipt 0x5ea16a4f9104b901a75662f3900ca8d3d92209d846310237ca65675e2f2e519c \
  --rpc-url $RPC

Decode calldata (with ABI): 

cast calldata-decode \
  "process(bytes,bytes)" 0x61bd0e46... --rpc-url $RPC

Get event logs: 

cast logs --rpc-url $RPC \
  --address 0x98f3c9e6E3fAce36bAAd05FE09d375Ef1464288B \
  --from-block 1 --to-block latest

Get the 4-byte selector for a function signature: 

cast sig "transfer(address,uint256)"
# 0xa9059cbb

Compute keccak256 of arbitrary data: 

cast keccak "process(bytes,bytes)"
# 0x61bd0e46...

docs.title: 2.3 cast Commands — Send Transactions
docs.text:
Send ETH: 

cast send --from 0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  --to 0x... --value 1ether --rpc-url $RPC

Call a contract function: 

cast send --from 0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  --to 0x... "transfer(address,uint256)" 0x... 1000 --rpc-url $RPC

Send raw calldata: 

cast send --from 0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  --to 0x... --data 0x61bd0e46... --rpc-url $RPC

docs.title: 2.4 cast Commands — Impersonation
docs.text:
Anvil's --auto-impersonate is enabled by default, so you can impersonate any account:

# Check cast supports impersonate:
cast rpc anvil_impersonateAccount 0x... --rpc-url $RPC

# Send as the impersonated account:
cast send --from 0x... --unlocked \
  --to 0x... "transfer(address,uint256)" 0x... 1000 --rpc-url $RPC

# Stop impersonating:
cast rpc anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount 0x... --rpc-url $RPC

docs.title: 2.5 cast Commands — EVM Manipulation
docs.text:
Advance time (for time-dependent exploits): 

cast rpc evm_increaseTime 86400 --rpc-url $RPC  # +1 day
cast rpc evm_mine --rpc-url $RPC                 # mine a block

Set storage directly: 

cast rpc anvil_setStorageAt 0x... 0x0 0x... --rpc-url $RPC

Set account balance: 

cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0x... 0x... --rpc-url $RPC

Dump/load full state: 

cast rpc anvil_dumpState --rpc-url $RPC > state.json
cast rpc anvil_loadState "$(cat state.json)" --rpc-url $RPC

docs.title: 2.6 forge Commands — Running Scripts
docs.text:
Run a Solidity script (simulation mode, no broadcast): 

forge script Exploit.s.sol:Exploit --rpc-url $RPC

Run and broadcast transactions: 

forge script Exploit.s.sol:Exploit --rpc-url $RPC --broadcast

Compile without running: 

forge build

docs.title: 2.7 Using the Hybrid Pattern (Solidity + Python)
docs.text:
When forge script --broadcast doesn't work (Foundry simulation differs from Anvil), use this pattern:

// Exploit.s.sol — Pure calldata computation, no broadcast
contract MyScript is Script {
    function run() external {
        bytes memory data = abi.encodeWithSelector(
            bytes4(0x61bd0e46), arg1, arg2
        );
        console.log("DATA:%s", vm.toString(data));
    }
}

# Run forge script, capture calldata from console.log output:
forge script Exploit.s.sol:MyScript --rpc-url $RPC 2>&1 | grep "DATA:"

# Then send the calldata via cast:
cast send --from 0x... --to 0x... --data <calldata> --rpc-url $RPC

docs.title: 2.8 Useful Anvil Flags (for manual runs)
docs.text:
When running Anvil directly outside Crucible:

anvil \
  --fork-url $ETH_RPC_URL \
  --fork-block-number 12996693 \
  --host 127.0.0.1 \
  --port 8545 \
  --chain-id 1 \
  --gas-price 0 \
  --block-base-fee-per-gas 1000000000 \
  --auto-impersonate \
  --order fifo \
  --timestamp "fork_block"    # "fork_block" for deterministic; $(date +%s) for live

docs.title: Morning: Set Up
docs.text:
# 1. Check environment
crucible doctor

# 2. Clean previous state
crucible clean --states

# 3. Fork the chain
crucible fork --topology bridge
crucible status  # verify chains are alive

# 4. Run your experiment
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline

docs.title: During: Observe
docs.text:
# Watch the run manifest for real-time provenance
tail -f logs/nomad_replay/DS-2026-00209/run.jsonl

# Or inspect artifacts after
cat logs/nomad_replay/DS-2026-00209/report.md

# Query chain state directly
cast call 0x... "committedRoot()(bytes32)" --rpc-url $RPC
cast balance 0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 --rpc-url $RPC

docs.title: Afternoon: Verify + Diff
docs.text:
# Run a second time
crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline

# Verify determinism
crucible diff DS-2026-00182 DS-2026-00183
# Expected: all 4 fields equal = true

# Browse history
crucible history

# Export for publication
crucible export DS-2026-00182
crucible verify datasets/DS-2026-00182/

docs.title: Crucible Commands
docs.text: Command What It Does When to Use crucible doctor Validate environment First thing daily crucible status Show running Anvil processes After fork crucible history Browse past runs Track progress crucible diff <a> <b> Compare two runs Verify determinism crucible clean Remove artifacts Before fresh start crucible export <id> Create publication bundle When publishing

docs.title: Cast Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose cast balance <addr> Check ETH balance cast code <addr> Check if contract exists cast storage <addr> <slot> Read storage at slot cast call <addr> "sig" <args> Call read-only function cast send <addr> "sig" <args> Send transaction cast tx <hash> Get transaction details cast receipt <hash> Get transaction receipt cast logs --address <addr> Filter event logs cast block-number Current block cast sig "func(args)" Get 4-byte selector cast keccak "data" Compute keccak256 cast rpc <method> <params> Call any RPC method

docs.title: Anvil RPC Methods (via cast rpc)
docs.text: Method Purpose anvil_impersonateAccount Impersonate any address anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount Stop impersonation anvil_setBalance Change account balance anvil_setStorageAt Write storage directly anvil_dumpState Snapshot full chain state anvil_loadState Restore from snapshot evm_increaseTime Advance timestamp evm_mine Mine a new block

docs.title: Research Workflow
docs.text: This document describes how Crucible should be used for academic bridge exploit reproduction.

docs.title: Reproduction Standard
docs.text:
Do not describe a benchmark as "historically reproduced" until the attack pack has:

  1. A declared fidelity level.
  2. A complete evidence.yaml.
  3. Explicit fork blocks.
  4. Affected contract addresses.
  5. Historical exploit transactions, if the target fidelity is L3 or L4.
  6. Expected state, balance, and event assertions.
  7. A deterministic rerun comparison.
  8. An exported artifact bundle.

Crucible can run scaffold packs before those fields are complete, but scaffold runs are engineering validation, not research evidence.

docs.title: Fidelity Levels
docs.text:
Level Meaning Requirements L0 Narrative simulation only None (description only) L1 Local contract-level reproduction Attack plugin returns a result L2 Historical fork-state reproduction Chains exist in topology, trace artifacts present L3 Historical transaction-path reconstruction All L2 + exact_tx_replay=true, exploit transactions non-empty L4 Cross-chain causal reconstruction All L3 + multi-chain topology + bridge plugin + excluded components declared

L4 bridge plugins available: | Plugin | Topology | Chains | Vulnerability | |---|---|---|---| | nomad_bridge | topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml | ethereum + polygon | committedRoot == 0x00 | | poly_network_bridge | topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml | ethereum + polygon + bsc | Hash collision selector | | wormhole_bridge | topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml | ethereum + polygon | Guardian bypass |

Enforcing fidelity levels at runtime: 

crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml

Fidelity enforcement is enabled by default. Use --no-fidelity to skip.

docs.title: 1. Scaffold
docs.text:
For a complete step-by-step guide covering research, evidence filling, plugin writing, and validation, see docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md.

Quick path (from tx hash — 10 seconds):

crucible attack extract <tx_hash> --name my_attack --chain ethereum

This generates the complete scaffold (plugin.yaml, evidence.yaml, attack.py, scenario.yaml, ABI mapping).

Manual path:

attacks/<attack>/
  plugin.yaml
  evidence.yaml
  <attack>.py

Validation:

crucible plugin validate attacks/<attack>

docs.title: 2. Evidence Fill
docs.text:
Add verified:

  • postmortems
  • explorer links
  • contract addresses
  • vulnerable function names
  • fork block numbers
  • exploit transaction hashes
  • state/balance expectations

docs.title: 3. Scenario Fill
docs.text:
Update scenarios/<scenario>/scenario.yaml with concrete assertions.

At minimum, every real attack should prove:

  • the vulnerable path succeeds
  • the attacker position improves
  • protected bridge state changes
  • the same action should fail after a patch, if patch validation is in scope

docs.title: 4. Baseline Capture
docs.text:
Start the topology (or use setup for fork + bootstrap in one step):

crucible setup --topology bridge              # fork + bootstrap + prepare
# or manually:
crucible fork --topology bridge

Capture:

crucible baseline capture <scenario> --topology bridge
crucible baseline verify <scenario>

docs.title: 5. Formal Run
docs.text:
crucible experiment run experiments/<attack>_replay.yaml
crucible history --rebuild
crucible logs list

docs.title: 6. Export & Verify
docs.text:
crucible export <run_id>                          # export as directory
crucible export <run_id> --format tar-gz          # export as compressed archive
crucible verify datasets/<run_id>                  # verify bundle integrity (auto-detects bundle vs run)

Archive datasets/<run_id>/ or the .tar.gz/.zip file.

docs.title: Determinism Check
docs.text:
Run the same experiment twice after restoring the same baseline:

crucible reset --scenario <scenario>
crucible experiment run experiments/<attack>_replay.yaml
crucible reset --scenario <scenario>
crucible experiment run experiments/<attack>_replay.yaml
crucible diff <run_a> <run_b>

The diff result should show:

  • storage_equal: true
  • events_equal: true
  • gas_equal: true, unless the experiment declares a gas tolerance

docs.title: Patch Validation Workflow
docs.text:
Patch validation should use two experiments:

  1. Vulnerable replay: exploit succeeds.
  2. Patched replay: exploit fails and normal bridge behavior still passes.

The patch artifact should include:

patches/<name>/
  patch.yaml
  validation.yaml
  contracts-or-scripts/

Formal patch acceptance requires both positive and negative assertions.

docs.title: Why Archive Nodes Are Required
docs.text: Crucible reproduces historical blockchain exploits by forking the chain at a specific block using Foundry's anvil. To do this, anvil needs an archive node (also called a "full archive node") that can serve historical state data.

docs.title: Archive vs Full Node
docs.text: Feature Full Node Archive Node Latest block state [ok] [ok] Historical state (eth_getBalance at old block) [fail] (pruned after ~128 blocks) [ok] (stores ALL state since genesis) eth_getBlockByNumber (old blocks) [ok] (block headers kept) [ok] eth_getCode / eth_call (old blocks) [fail] [ok] Disk space (Ethereum) ~1 TB ~12+ TB Disk space (BSC) ~1 TB ~10+ TB

docs.title: Which Chains Need Archive Access
docs.text:
Attack Chains Forked Fork Block Archive Required? Nomad Bridge Ethereum + Polygon 15,259,100 / — [ok] Ethereum [ok] Polygon Wormhole Guardian Ethereum 14,125,553 [ok] Ethereum Poly Network Ethereum (only) 12,996,693 [ok] Ethereum Poly Network (full multi-chain) Ethereum + BSC + Polygon 12,996,693 / 9,755,172 / 16,332,982 [ok] All 3 chains

Key insight: All three attacks need Ethereum archive. Ethereum has the best free archive support. For multi-chain (BSC + Polygon at 2021-era blocks), paid archive providers are required.

docs.title: Ethereum (Free Archive Available [ok])
docs.text: Provider Archive Depth Rate Limit Cost Works with Crucible? Ankr (rpc.ankr.com/eth) Full archive (with API key) 300M CU/month Free tier [ok] Best free option — sign up at app.ankr.com LlamaNodes (eth.llamarpc.com) Full archive 25 req/s Free [warn] Rate-limits Anvil's heavy requests PublicNode (ethereum.publicnode.com) Limited (~128 blocks) 10 req/s Free [fail] Fails for old blocks Alchemy Full archive Varies Free tier + paid [ok] (set ETH_RPC_URL in .env) QuickNode Full archive Varies Paid [ok] (set ETH_RPC_URL in .env)

docs.title: BSC (No Free Archive [fail])
docs.text: Provider Archive Depth Rate Limit Cost Works with Crucible? GetBlock (go.getblock.io) Full archive 20 RPS $39/mo (Starter) [ok] Tested via curl PublicNode (bsc.publicnode.com) Limited (~128 blocks) 10 req/s Free [fail] Fails at block 9,755,172 meowrpc (bsc.meowrpc.com) Limited 15 req/s Free [fail] eth_getBlockByNumber fails QuickNode Full archive Varies Paid [ok] Chainstack Full archive Varies Paid [ok] Ankr (rpc.ankr.com/bsc) Full archive (with key) Varies Free tier + paid [warn] Requires Ankr API key

docs.title: Polygon (No Free Archive [fail])
docs.text: Provider Archive Depth Rate Limit Cost Works with Crucible? GetBlock (go.getblock.io) Full archive 20 RPS $39/mo (Starter) [ok] Included in same plan LlamaNodes (polygon.llamarpc.com) Limited 25 req/s Free [fail] Fails at block 16,332,982 PublicNode (polygon-bor.publicnode.com) Limited (~128 blocks) 10 req/s Free [fail] Fails at block 16,332,982 QuickNode Full archive Varies Paid [ok] Chainstack Full archive Varies Paid [ok] Ankr (rpc.ankr.com/polygon) Full archive (with key) Varies Free tier + paid [warn] Requires Ankr API key

docs.title: Recommended Provider: GetBlock
docs.text:
Based on testing, GetBlock is the most practical option for our needs:

Feature GetBlock Starter ($39/mo) BSC Archive [ok] Full archive Polygon Archive [ok] Full archive Ethereum Archive [ok] (but we already have free) Requests 50,000 CU/day Rate Limit 20 RPS Endpoints 2 (use for BSC + Polygon) Setup Time Minutes — just generate API key Endpoint Format https://go.getblock.io/<ACCESS-TOKEN>

docs.title: Alternatives (if GetBlock doesn't fit)
docs.text: Provider BSC Archive Polygon Archive Min Cost Notes QuickNode [ok] [ok] ~$29/mo Per-chain pricing Chainstack [ok] [ok] ~$39/mo Good archive support Alchemy [warn] Growth tier [warn] Growth tier ~$49/mo Ethereum-focused Ankr [ok] (with key) [ok] (with key) Free tier Premium tier may be needed dRPC [warn] Free tier [warn] Free tier Free Didn't work at our blocks

docs.title: Step 1: Get an API Key
docs.text:
1. Sign up at GetBlock
  2. Go to Dashboard → Create Access Token
  3. Select BSC Mainnet and Polygon Mainnet
  4. Copy the provided endpoint URLs

docs.title: Step 2: Set Environment Variables
docs.text:
Add to your .env file:

# Ethereum archive — ANKR free tier (sign up at https://app.ankr.com)
# LlamaNodes (eth.llamarpc.com) also works but may rate-limit Anvil
ETH_RPC_URL=https://rpc.ankr.com/eth/YOUR_ANKR_API_KEY

# BSC Archive (GetBlock or your provider)
BSC_RPC_URL=https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN

# Polygon Archive (GetBlock or your provider)
POLYGON_RPC_URL=https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Rate Limiting
docs.text:
Crucible uses an internal TokenBucket algorithm to ensure Anvil does not exceed your provider's rate limits (which would cause 429 Too Many Requests errors and experiment failures).

You can configure rate limits per-provider in testbed.yaml:

rpc_providers:
  ethereum:
    - name: ankr_free
      url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"
      rate_limit: 30       # 30 requests per second
      burst_limit: 50      # Allow temporary bursts up to 50

If Anvil requests data faster than the rate_limit, the proxy will pause the request until tokens replenish.

docs.title: Step 4: Verify Connectivity
docs.text:
# Quick test from orchestrator container
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c '
curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"eth_blockNumber\",\"params\":[]}" \
  https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN
'

docs.title: Step 4: Run Multi-Chain Experiment
docs.text:
# Update fork blocks in testbed.yaml first (or use testbed.local.yaml)
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json
  uv run crucible fork --topology poly_network
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/poly_network_replay.yaml --no-baseline
"

docs.title: "Historical state not available" Error
docs.text:
Error: Failed to get block for block number: 9755172

Cause: The RPC endpoint is a full node, not an archive node. It doesn't store historical state.

Fix: Switch to an archive-capable provider (GetBlock, QuickNode, etc.).

docs.title: "anvil_setBalance" Fails with "Historical State Not Available"
docs.text:
RPC error for anvil_setBalance: failed to get account for 0xDeaDDeaD...:
server returned an error response: error code -32000:
state 0x34f7657d... is not available

Cause: When anvil_setBalance is called on an address that doesn't exist in the forked state, Anvil tries to look up the address in the remote archive. If the archive node doesn't have full historical state at the fork block, it returns "state not available". This is most common on Polygon and BSC archives at older blocks (2021-2022).

Why it happens: 1. Crucible starts Anvil with --accounts 0 --balance 0 (no prefunded test accounts) 2. The bridge plugin calls anvil_setBalance(deployer, amount) to fund a deployer address 3. Since the deployer address doesn't exist in the forked state, Anvil queries the remote archive 4. If the archive has pruned historical state (common with free Polygon/BSC providers), the query fails

Note: This also affects eth_sendTransaction — even with gasPrice=0, Anvil checks if the sender account exists in the forked state, which triggers the same remote lookup.

Fix (already applied to all 3 bridge plugins): Per-chain deployment is wrapped in try/except in bridges/nomad_bridge/nomad_bridge.py, bridges/poly_network_bridge/poly_network_bridge.py, and bridges/wormhole_bridge/wormhole_bridge.py. If a chain's archive can't serve state, it's skipped gracefully with a warning.

Workaround: - Use a different RPC provider with better archive depth (GetBlock typically works for all 3 chains) - Test archive depth with: 

# This queries an address that definitely exists at the fork block
curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_getBalance","params":["0x838bf9E95CB12Dd76a54C9f9D2E3082EAF928270", "0xE7E30C"]}' \
  https://your-rpc-provider
# 0xE7E30C = 15197964 (Poly Network fork block on Ethereum)
# Returns "0x..." if archive works, error if not
- If a chain's archive is unreliable, run experiments with only the chains that work (use a topology without the problematic chain)

docs.title: "eth_sendTransaction" From New Address Fails
docs.text:
RPC error for eth_sendTransaction: failed to get account for 0xDeaDDeaD...:
historical state ... is not available

Cause: Same root cause as the anvil_setBalance error above. Even sending a transaction with gasPrice=0 from a new (non-existent) address triggers a remote archive lookup.

Fix: Same as above — the bridge plugins handle this gracefully now. For custom code, use an address that already exists in the forked state (like a real contract address) instead of a new random EOA.

docs.title: How To Test Archive Depth
docs.text:
Run this from the orchestrator container to verify a provider has the required historical state:

docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c '
  # Use the hex block number for the chain you are testing:
  #   Ethereum (Nomad):   0xE8DB9C  (15,259,100)
  #   Ethereum (PolyNet): 0xC654B5  (12,996,693)
  #   Polygon:            0xF91D96  (16,332,982)
  #   BSC:                0x94E334  (9,755,172)
  HEX_BLOCK=0xE8DB9C
  RPC_URL="$ETH_RPC_URL"

  # Test 1: Can the provider serve a block by number?
  echo "=== Test 1: Block exists ==="
  curl -s --max-time 10 -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d "{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"eth_getBlockByNumber\",\"params\":[\"$HEX_BLOCK\",false]}" \
    "$RPC_URL" | head -c 200
  echo

  # Test 2: Can the provider serve a balance at that block?
  echo "=== Test 2: Balance at block ==="
  curl -s --max-time 10 -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d "{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"eth_getBalance\",\"params\":[\"0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000\",\"$HEX_BLOCK\"]}" \
    "$RPC_URL" | head -c 200
  echo

  # Test 3: Can eth_getCode at the fork block?
  echo "=== Test 3: Code at block ==="
  curl -s --max-time 10 -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d "{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"eth_getCode\",\"params\":[\"0x838bf9E95CB12Dd76a54C9f9D2E3082EAF928270\",\"$HEX_BLOCK\"]}" \
    "$RPC_URL" | head -c 200
  echo
'

If Test 1 passes but Test 2 or 3 fail, the provider doesn't have true archive state — it can serve block headers but not historical state queries. Switch to a paid archive provider.

docs.title: Fork Block Depth Reference
docs.text:
Attack Ethereum Block Polygon Block BSC Block Nomad Bridge 15,259,100 — — Wormhole Guardian 14,125,553 — — Poly Network 12,996,693 16,332,982 9,755,172 Horizon Multisig 14,399,491 — — BNB Token Hub — — 22,166,659 Multichain Router 16,329,241 — —

Archive availability at these blocks:

Provider Ethereum (12-16M) Polygon (16M) BSC (9-22M) Ankr (free) ✅ Full archive ⚠️ May fail on non-existent addresses ⚠️ May fail on non-existent addresses GetBlock (paid $39/mo) ✅ Full archive ✅ Full archive ✅ Full archive LlamaNodes (free) ✅ Full archive ❌ No archive at 16M ❌ No archive PublicNode (free) ❌ No archive ❌ No archive at 16M ❌ No archive at 9M QuickNode (paid) ✅ Full archive ✅ Full archive ✅ Full archive

Key finding: Even "archive" providers like Ankr may have incomplete historical state for Polygon/BSC when querying non-existent addresses at old blocks. The state root for the fork block is stored, but the full state trie for addresses that didn't exist at that block is not available. This causes anvil_setBalance to fail when creating new addresses.

Recommended provider setup for multi-chain:

# testbed.yaml
rpc_providers:
  ethereum:
    - name: ankr_free
      url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"  # Ankr free tier — reliable for Ethereum
  polygon:
    - name: getblock
      url: "${POLYGON_RPC_URL}"  # GetBlock paid — reliable archive
    - name: ankr
      url: "https://rpc.ankr.com/polygon"
      priority: 2  # fallback
  bsc:
    - name: getblock
      url: "${BSC_RPC_URL}"  # GetBlock paid — reliable archive
    - name: ankr
      url: "https://rpc.ankr.com/bsc"
      priority: 2  # fallback

docs.title: "Connection refused" on Fork
docs.text:
ERROR: chain bsc did not become ready within 30s on http://127.0.0.1:8547

Cause 1: The ${BSC_RPC_URL} env var is empty or points to an unreachable endpoint.

Fix: Verify the URL in .env and test with curl.

Cause 2: The fork block is too old for the configured provider.

Fix: Check the provider's archive depth. For BSC block 9,755,172 (2021), you need a paid archive provider.

docs.title: Rate Limited
docs.text:
error code: -32000: rate limit exceeded

Cause: Free tier rate limits (typically 10-25 req/s).

Fix: Add more providers as fallbacks in testbed.yaml under rpc_providers.bsc. Crucible will automatically retry with the next provider.

docs.title: Summary: What We Need vs What's Free
docs.text:
Chain Block Needed Free Available? Paid Option Ethereum 12,996,693 - 15,259,100 [ok] ANKR free tier (sign up) GetBlock/QuickNode BSC 9,755,172 [fail] No free provider GetBlock $39/mo Polygon 16,332,982 [fail] No free provider GetBlock $39/mo

Minimum viable paid plan: GetBlock Starter ($39/month) gives access to all 3 chains with archive depth.

docs.title: Running LLM Agents in Crucible
docs.text: This guide explains how to configure and run LLM-driven autonomous agents in the Crucible testbed. Agents replace traditional hand-coded attack plugins with an AI loop: the agent observes the forked chain environment, decides which tools to call, executes them, and repeats until done.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# 1. Ensure your .env has the API key for your chosen provider
#    (Ollama users: no key needed — just have Ollama running locally)

# 2. Run the demo agent experiment (simple chain exploration)
crucible experiment run experiments/demo_agent.yaml

# 3. Or run the Nomad exploit agent (targets a real bridge vulnerability)
crucible experiment run experiments/llm_exploit_agent.yaml

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The agent lifecycle has five stages:

  1. Prepare — The agent loads its configuration from the experiment YAML's run: block, including the LLM provider, model, privilege level, task, and objective. Provider is auto-configured via the from_run_config() factory (see below).
  2. Observe — The agent gathers the current state: tool history, available tools, and recent results.
  3. Think — The agent sends the current state + conversation history to the LLM provider. The LLM decides which tool to call next (or signals completion).
  4. Act — The agent executes the chosen tool via the Crucible RPC layer (impersonation, balance checks, storage reads, etc.).
  5. Repeat — Steps 2–4 loop until is_done() returns True or the agent signals completion.
  6. Verify — The agent verifies the outcome (by default, always passes).

docs.title: Experiment YAML Configuration
docs.text:
Create an experiment YAML file with the following structure:

name: my_agent_experiment
scenario: demo_scenario          # any scenario with a single-chain topology
attack: demo_agent               # plugin name from attacks/<name>/plugin.yaml
topology: single

run:
  # ── Provider Configuration ────────────────────────────────────
  agent_provider: openai         # openai | anthropic | ollama | local | custom
  agent_model: gpt-4o            # model name for the chosen provider
  # agent_base_url: ...          # optional: override the provider's default base URL

  # ── Agent Runtime Configuration ───────────────────────────────
  agent_privilege: medium        # low | medium | high
  agent_temperature: 0.3         # Lower = more deterministic (default: 0.7)
  agent_max_iterations: 30       # Max tool calls before timeout (default: 50)
  agent_llm_budget: 10000        # Max total tokens (stops agent if exceeded)
  agent_task: "Explore the forked chain and report findings"
  agent_objective: "Use available tools to check chain ID, balances, and contract code"
  agent_allowed_accounts: []     # empty = no address restriction

  # ── Recording for Deterministic Replay ──────────────────────
  # record_path: tests/fixtures/my_agent.jsonl  # uncomment to capture RPC fixture

  # ── Standard Experiment Config ────────────────────────────────
  traces: false
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true

tags:
  - agent
  - demo

docs.title: Provider Types
docs.text: Type Default Model API Key Required Notes openai gpt-4o OPENAI_API_KEY in .env Also works with Azure, vLLM, etc. via agent_base_url anthropic claude-sonnet-4-20250514 ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in .env Uses Anthropic Messages API ollama llama3.1 No Auto-connects to http://localhost:11434/v1 local local-model Optional Requires explicit agent_base_url custom local-model Optional Requires explicit agent_base_url

docs.title: Privilege Levels
docs.text: Level Tools Available Description low 6 tools (read-only) chain_id, get_balance, get_code, get_storage, call_contract, get_logs medium 8 tools Low tools + impersonate, send_transaction high 9 tools Medium tools + advance_time

docs.title: Advanced Configuration
docs.text: Key Default Description agent_temperature 0.7 LLM sampling temperature. Lower values (0.1–0.3) produce more deterministic, focused behaviour. Higher values encourage creativity. agent_max_iterations 50 Maximum number of tool calls before the agent loop stops. Prevents runaway agents. agent_llm_budget None (unlimited) Maximum total tokens the LLM provider can consume. When the budget is exceeded, the agent stops and logs a budget_exceeded warning. A 90% threshold warning is also logged. Set to a reasonable value like 10000 to cap costs. agent_allowed_accounts [] (all) List of allowed Ethereum addresses the agent can interact with. Empty = no restriction. record_path None Path to a JSONL file where all RPC calls are recorded for later replay. See RPC Recording.

docs.title: Provider Auto-Configuration (from_run_config)
docs.text:
When you run crucible experiment run, the experiment runner automatically detects agent_provider in the experiment YAML's run: block and creates the correct LLM provider via the from_run_config() factory on LLMAgentPlugin.

This means you don't need to modify Python code to change providers — just edit the YAML:

# Switch from Ollama to OpenAI:
agent_provider: openai
agent_model: gpt-4o

# Or use Anthropic:
agent_provider: anthropic
agent_model: claude-sonnet-4-20250514

# Or a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint:
agent_provider: custom
agent_model: my-model
agent_base_url: http://my-proxy:8080/v1

The prepare() method in LLMAgentPlugin checks the _configured_from_run_config flag and skips redundant provider re-creation when the factory was used.

docs.title: Included Agent Packs
docs.text: Crucible ships with two agent packs:

docs.title: attacks/demo_agent/ — Simple Chain Explorer
docs.text:
A minimal example that explores a forked chain, checks chain ID, balances, and contract code. Best starting point for understanding the agent system.

crucible experiment run experiments/demo_agent.yaml

docs.title: attacks/llm_exploit_agent/ — Nomad Bridge Exploit Agent
docs.text:
A more sophisticated agent targeting the Nomad bridge Replica contract (0xB923...). The agent:

  1. Verifies the Replica contract exists and has code
  2. Reads the current committedRoot (storage slot 0x1)
  3. Constructs a forged process(bytes,bytes) call using the function selector 0x61bd0e46
  4. Sends the transaction to exploit the authentication bypass
  5. Verifies the committedRoot changed
crucible experiment run experiments/llm_exploit_agent.yaml

Requires: A running LLM provider (Ollama by default) and an archive RPC URL for Ethereum at fork block 15,259,100 (the Nomad pre-exploit block).

docs.title: Creating a Custom Agent Plugin
docs.text:
To create a custom agent, subclass LLMAgentPlugin and override the methods you need:

from crucible.agent_loop import LLMAgentPlugin
from crucible.agent_tools import AgentContext

class MyAgent(LLMAgentPlugin):
    def metadata(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "name": "my_agent",
            "version": "1.0.0",
            "type": "agent",
        }

    def build_objective(self, agent_ctx: AgentContext) -> str:
        """Custom objective for your agent."""
        return "Your custom objective here."

Then register it in attacks/<name>/plugin.yaml:

name: my_agent
type: agent
version: "1.0.0"
entry: my_agent.py
class: MyAgent
requires:
  crucible: ">=1.0.0"
  chains:
    - ethereum
  topology: single

docs.title: Available Override Points
docs.text: Method Purpose Called When metadata() Return plugin metadata (name, version, type) Plugin loading build_objective(ctx) Return the objective string for the LLM Each think() cycle build_system_prompt(ctx) Return the full system prompt (override for full control) First think() cycle prepare(context, agent_ctx) Set up agent state before the main loop Before the agent loop starts observe(context, agent_ctx) Gather observations from the environment Each loop iteration think(observation, context, agent_ctx) Decide the next action given the observation Each loop iteration respond(result, context, agent_ctx) Process the result of a tool call After each tool execution is_done(context, agent_ctx) Check if the agent should stop Each loop iteration verify(context, agent_ctx) Verify the agent's outcome After the loop ends

docs.title: Reasoning Chain Logging
docs.text:
Every agent run produces an agent_reasoning.jsonl file in the run's log directory. Each line is a JSON record containing:

{
  "step": 5,
  "observation": "## Current State\n  Privilege: medium\n  Tools available: 8\n  Total tool calls so far: 4\n\n## Most Recent Tool Results\n\n  \u2713 chain_id:\n    {\"chain_id\": 1337}\n\n  \u2713 get_balance:\n    {\"balance\": \"1000000000000000000\"}\n",
  "action": {
    "tool": "get_code",
    "params": {"address": "0xB92336759618F55bd0F8313bd843604592E27bd8"}
  },
  "conversation_snapshot": [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are an autonomous security research agent..."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "## Current State..."},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "I will check the contract code at..."}
  ]
}

This provides a full reasoning trace for reproducibility and review.

docs.title: RPC Recording for Deterministic Replay
docs.text:
Agent runs can be recorded to a fixture file for deterministic replay:

# Run the agent experiment with recording enabled
crucible experiment run experiments/llm_exploit_agent.yaml \
  --record tests/fixtures/llm_exploit_agent.jsonl

Or set record_path in the experiment YAML's run: block:

run:
  record_path: tests/fixtures/llm_exploit_agent.jsonl

The recorded fixture can then be served by the ReplayServer (from crucible.recording) for hermetic tests that don't need network access. See the existing tests/e2e/test_hermetic_nomad.py for a complete example.

docs.title: Budget and Token Management
docs.text:
The agent_llm_budget config caps total token consumption:

run:
  agent_llm_budget: 5000        # Hard stop at 5000 total tokens

When the budget is configured:

  • The agent checks the budget before each LLM call and stops if exceeded
  • A warning is logged at 90% of the budget
  • The budget status is reported in verify() output
  • The agent loop's is_done() also checks the budget every iteration

Budget takes precedence from the constructor if both constructor and run_config set it.

docs.title: Offline / Testing Without an LLM
docs.text:
To test the agent lifecycle without an actual LLM call, create a subclass that overrides think():

class TestAgent(LLMAgentPlugin):
    def think(self, observation, context, agent_ctx):
        """Return a fixed action for testing."""
        from crucible.agent_tools import AgentAction
        return AgentAction(tool="chain_id", params={})

You can also use a mock provider (see tests/unit/test_llm_providers.py for examples):

from unittest.mock import MagicMock
from crucible.llm_providers import LLMProvider, LLMResponse

mock = MagicMock(spec=LLMProvider)
mock.model_name = "mock-model"
mock.chat.return_value = LLMResponse(
    content='{"status": "done", "summary": "test"}'
)

agent = LLMAgentPlugin(provider=mock)

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Fix OpenAI API key not set Missing OPENAI_API_KEY in .env Add the key or switch provider to ollama Anthropic API key not set Missing ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in .env Add the key Agent plugin 'xyz' not found Plugin YAML has wrong type: or path Ensure type: agent in plugin.yaml Agent loops without making progress Model too small or objective too vague Use a capable model (gpt-4o, claude-sonnet-4) and a specific objective. Lower agent_temperature to 0.1–0.3 for more focused behaviour. Agent stops immediately with budget_exceeded Budget is too low for the objective Increase agent_llm_budget or disable it (remove the key) can't connect to localhost:11434 Ollama not running or reachable Start Ollama (ollama serve) and pull the model (ollama pull llama3.1). For a remote Ollama instance, set agent_base_url: http://<remote-ip>:11434/v1 in the experiment YAML. Ollama returns empty tool calls Model doesn't support function calling Use a model with tool-calling support (llama3.1:70b, mistral:7b, qwen2.5:7b) llama-server process has terminated: signal: segmentation fault CPU-only compatibility issue with some Intel Xeon/older processors Update Ollama to the latest version (curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh), or try qwen2:0.5b (352MB, lower CPU requirements). If it still segfaults, use a remote Ollama instance or switch to an OpenAI/Anthropic provider with an API key. Ollama segfaults on model load (CPU-only) Specific CPU architectures may have llama.cpp compatibility issues Workarounds: (1) Run Ollama on a different machine and set agent_base_url to its URL. (2) Add num_threads: 2 to the generate options. (3) Switch to a cloud API provider (OpenAI, Anthropic — add API key to .env). experiment run doesn't load agent config agent_provider not set in YAML Add agent_provider: ollama to the run: block Agent ignores the objective Prompt engineering issue Make the objective concrete and step-by-step. See attacks/llm_exploit_agent/llm_exploit_agent.py for a detailed example.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/USER_GUIDE.md General Crucible usage and setup docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Creating attack plugins (the attacks/<name>/ directory) docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md Writing scenario YAML files docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md All CLI commands and options docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Provenance and determinism tests/unit/test_llm_providers.py 75+ unit tests for LLM providers, agent loop, and the from_run_config factory tests/unit/test_agent.py 52 unit tests for AgentContext, privilege enforcement, and AgentPlugin lifecycle

docs.title: What Is A Scenario?
docs.text:
A scenario defines the blockchain environment at a specific point in time — usually moments before a known exploit. It tells Crucible:

  1. Which chain(s) to fork (e.g., Ethereum at block 15,259,100)
  2. What state to verify (e.g., check that a storage slot is at a specific value)
  3. What setup steps to run (e.g., deploy a helper contract, fund an account)
  4. What assertions to make (e.g., the exploit succeeds, state changes as expected)

Think of a scenario as the "before" snapshot — it sets up the chessboard so the attack plugin can make its move.

docs.title: Why Are Scenarios Separate From Attack Plugins?
docs.text:
Component Purpose Responsible For Scenario (scenarios/<name>/scenario.yaml) Environment setup — fork the chain, prepare state, run preconditions Declarative YAML Attack Plugin (attacks/<name>/<name>.py) Exploit execution — forge calldata, send transactions, verify success Python code Experiment (experiments/<name>_replay.yaml) Orchestration — wires scenario + attack plugin + topology together Declarative YAML

This separation means you can: - Reuse the same scenario with different attack plugins (test variations) - Reuse the same attack plugin with different scenarios (test on different fork blocks) - Swap topologies without changing the attack code

docs.title: Anatomy Of A Scenario
docs.text:
name: my_attack_pre_exploit                        # Must match directory name
description: >-
  One or two paragraphs explaining what this scenario does.
  What chain is being forked? At what block? What state
  is being verified? What contract is the target?
version: "1.0.0"
chains:                                              # Which chains to fork
  - ethereum

phases:                                              # Ordered list of phases
  - name: setup                                      # Phase 1: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Forking Ethereum at block 15259100 for Nomad reproduction"
  - name: attack                                     # Phase 2: attack (plugin runs here)
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack plugin will be executed by the experiment runner"
  - name: verify                                     # Phase 3: verification
    steps:
      - type: assert
        name: exploit_succeeds
        expected: true
        actual: true

docs.title: Alternative: Top-Level Steps (Simple Format)
docs.text:
For simpler scenarios that don't need distinct setup, attack, and verify phases, Crucible supports a flat list of steps at the root level using the steps: key:

name: simple_scenario
chains: [ethereum]
steps:
  - type: log
    message: "This scenario runs as a single block of steps"
  - type: impersonate
    chain: ethereum
    account: "0xAttacker"
When using this format, the scenario runs entirely before the attack plugin executes.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
scenarios/
  my_attack_pre_exploit/
    scenario.yaml          # The scenario definition (required)
    steps.py               # Optional Python steps (only if using python_script step type)

docs.title: Phases And Execution Order
docs.text:
Scenarios run in ordered phases. Each phase contains one or more steps that execute sequentially.

flowchart LR
    P1["Phase 1: setup<br>Run setup steps<br>(log, impersonate, send_tx, etc.)"]
    P2["Phase 2: attack<br>Experiment runner loads and executes<br>the attack plugin between phases"]
    P3["Phase 3: verify<br>Run verify steps<br>(assert, log)"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3

Important: The attack plugin runs between the scenario phases, not inside them. The experiment runner: 1. Executes setup phase steps 2. Loads and runs the attack plugin (execute() method) 3. Runs the attack plugin's verify() method 4. Executes the remaining phase steps

docs.title: Checkpoint System: Per-Phase Snapshots
docs.text:
As each phase completes, Crucible's CheckpointManager saves a phase checkpoint — a full EVM state snapshot for each running chain, hashed for integrity verification.

What gets saved: 

states/checkpoints/
  <run_id>/
    checkpoint.json             # Manifest: run_id, experiment, attack, completed_phases, timestamps
    integrity.json              # SHA-256 hashes of all snapshot files
    setup.ethereum.json         # EVM state snapshot after 'setup' phase
    attack.ethereum.json        # EVM state snapshot after 'attack' phase
    verify.ethereum.json        # EVM state snapshot after 'verify' phase

How it works: 1. After each phase completes, CheckpointManager.save_phase_checkpoint() dumps anvil_dumpState for each chain in the active topology 2. Each snapshot is validated (JSON parse + re-serialize) before being hashed 3. All SHA-256 hashes are written to integrity.json — the authoritative record of snapshot integrity 4. The checkpoint.json manifest tracks which phases are complete

On resume: 1. CheckpointManager.verify_checkpoint() loads the manifest and verifies ALL snapshot file hashes against integrity.json 2. If any file is missing or corrupt, CheckpointIntegrityError is raised — preventing silent data corruption 3. The experiment runner restores each chain's state from the most recent phase snapshot via anvil_loadState — this is done by ExperimentRunner.resume(), not by the CheckpointManager itself 4. Execution continues from the next uncompleted phase

# Resume an interrupted experiment
crucible experiment resume DS-2026-00155

# List all available checkpoints
crucible experiment checkpoints

Note: Checkpoint snapshots only capture chains in the active topology. Stale .meta.json files from previous topologies are skipped automatically — see Troubleshooting — Stale Meta Files.

When to use checkpoints: - Long-running experiments — if a multi-hour experiment crashes at phase 3 of 5, you don't need to re-run phases 1-2 - L4 cross-chain experiments — bridge deployment + configuration can take several minutes; checkpointing saves that work - Multiple attack attempts — run attack variant A, reset --mode soft to checkpoint state, run attack variant B

Checkpoint storage limits: 

# testbed.yaml
cleanup:
  max_checkpoints: 10            # Max checkpoint runs to keep (oldest removed)

Checkpoints are cleaned by crucible clean --checkpoints or crucible clean --all. See CLI Reference — Clean for details.

docs.title: 1. log — Record A Message
docs.text:
Logs a message to the experiment run log. Useful for documenting what's happening.

- type: log
  message: "Fork block verified — contract exists at expected address"

Purpose: Provides human-readable documentation in the experiment report. Every important step should be logged.

docs.title: 2. assert — Verify A Condition
docs.text:
Asserts that a condition is true. Fails the experiment if the condition is false.

- type: assert
  name: vulnerability_exists          # Unique name for this assertion
  expected: true                      # What we expect
  actual: true                        # What we observe (can reference state)

Purpose: Formal verification that the exploit state or outcome meets expectations. These appear in the experiment report as pass/fail.

docs.title: 3. impersonate — Impersonate An Account
docs.text:
Instructs Anvil to impersonate an account (bypass private key checks). Useful for sending transactions from accounts you don't control.

- type: impersonate
  chain: ethereum                     # Which chain
  account: "0xRelayerAddress..."      # Address to impersonate

Purpose: Allows the scenario to send transactions from privileged addresses (relayers, governors, keepers) that are controlled by the protocol, not by you.

docs.title: 4. send_tx — Send A Transaction
docs.text:
Sends a transaction to a contract. Supports symbolic address resolution for bridge contracts.

- type: send_tx
  chain: ethereum                     # Which chain
  from: "0xAttackerAddress..."        # Sender (must be impersonated first if not yours)
  to: "bridge.ethereum"               # Recipient (can use symbolic name like "bridge.ethereum")
  value: 1000000000000000000          # Value in wei (1 ETH = 10^18 wei)
  data: "0x123456..."                 # Calldata (hex-encoded)

Purpose: Execute a transaction on the forked chain. Used to trigger contract functions, send funds, etc.

docs.title: 5. advance_time — Skip Time Forward
docs.text:
Increases the EVM timestamp and mines a new block. Used to bypass time-based restrictions (challenge windows, vesting periods, etc.).

- type: advance_time
  chain: polygon                      # Which chain
  seconds: 3600                       # Number of seconds to advance (3600 = 1 hour)

Purpose: Simulate the passage of time in the EVM. Useful for exploits that rely on time-based state changes.

docs.title: 6. assert_balance — Check Account Balance
docs.text:
Asserts that an account's balance meets a minimum threshold. Fails if the balance is below the expected value.

- type: assert_balance
  chain: ethereum                     # Which chain
  account: "0xVaultAddress..."        # Account to check
  min_value: 0                        # Minimum balance in wei

Purpose: Verify that funds moved as expected after an exploit. For example, assert that the attacker's balance increased.

docs.title: 7. forge_script — Run A Foundry Script
docs.text:
Executes a Foundry (Solidity) script via subprocess. The RPC URL is automatically injected.

- type: forge_script
  chain: ethereum                     # Which chain to target
  script: "Exploit.s.sol:ExploitScript"  # Foundry script path
  extra_args:                         # Optional arguments
    - "--sender"
    - "0xAttackerAddress..."

Purpose: Run sophisticated exploit logic written in Solidity. Use this when the exploit logic is too complex for simple YAML steps.

Requirements: The Foundry script must be in the scenarios/<name>/ directory (or a scripts/ directory in the project root).

docs.title: 8. python_script — Run A Python Script
docs.text:
Dynamically imports and executes a steps.py file located in the scenario directory.

- type: python_script

Your steps.py must define a run(context: ExperimentContext) function:

from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient
from crucible.sdk import ExperimentContext

def run(context: ExperimentContext):
    # Get the RPC URL for the forked chain
    rpc_url = context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url
    client = JsonRpcClient(rpc_url)

    # Custom logic goes here
    # ...

    context.log.step("Custom Python step executed",
                     phase="attack", action="custom", status="success")

Purpose: Execute arbitrary Python logic that can't be expressed in YAML steps alone.

docs.title: 9. verify_tracing — Verify Transaction Trace Capture
docs.text:
Triggers a verification that the instrumentation proxy correctly captured transaction traces for a given chain. Raises an error if trace artifacts are missing or incomplete.

- type: verify_tracing
  chains:                             # Which chain(s) to verify traces for (list; defaults to all chains if omitted)
    - ethereum
    - polygon

Purpose: Ensures that all transactions sent during a phase were captured by the instrumentation proxy. Useful in multi-phase scenarios where each phase must produce complete artifacts.

docs.title: 10. bridge_action — Execute A Bridge Protocol Action
docs.text:
Performs a bridge-specific action through the active bridge plugin. The available actions depend on the bridge plugin loaded for the topology.

- type: bridge_action
  action: send_message                # Bridge action name (e.g. send_message, relay_messages, check_delivery, verify_state)
  source_chain: ethereum              # Source chain
  dest_chain: polygon                 # Destination chain
  data: "0x..."                       # Action-specific data

Purpose: Interact with bridge plugins from within a scenario. Used for cross-chain workflows where bridge state needs to be set up before or verified after an attack.

L4 bridge workflow example:

# scenarios/my_bridge_scenario/scenario.yaml
name: my_bridge_scenario
chains:
  - ethereum
  - polygon

phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Bridge deployed automatically via lifecycle — ready for attack"

  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack plugin runs here — both chains and bridge are ready"

  - name: verify
    steps:
      - type: bridge_action
        action: verify_state
        chain: ethereum
      - type: log
        message: "Cross-chain state verified"

Topology for L4:

# topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml
name: nomad_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge    # Points to bridges/nomad_bridge/

# experiments/my_l4_experiment.yaml
name: my_l4_experiment
scenario: my_bridge_scenario
attack: my_l4_attack
topology: nomad_bridge
run:
  traces: true
  storage_diffs: true
tags: [l4, bridge]

Available bridge plugins: | Plugin | Chains | Vulnerability Model | |---|---|---| | nomad_bridge | ethereum + polygon | committedRoot == 0x00 — forged message accepted | | poly_network_bridge | ethereum + polygon + bsc | Hash collision in _executeCrossChainTx | | wormhole_bridge | ethereum + polygon | Guardian bypass — forged VAA accepted |

docs.title: 11. plugin — Run A Plugin Step (Deprecated)
docs.text:
Note: This step type is currently a no-op — it only logs a "skipped" status. The documented dispatch behavior (method:/phase:) is not yet implemented. See [#issue] for tracking.

- type: plugin
  method: "prepare"                   # Not yet dispatched
  phase: 0                            # Not yet handled

docs.title: How Scenarios Relate To Experiments
docs.text:
The experiment file wires everything together:

# experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml
name: my_attack_replay              # Experiment name (used in log directory path)
scenario: my_attack_pre_exploit      # Which scenario to use (from scenarios/<name>/)
attack: my_attack                    # Which attack plugin to load (from attacks/<name>/)
topology: single                     # Which topology to use (from topologies/<name>.yaml)
run:
  traces: true                       # Enable transaction tracing
  storage_diffs: true               # Enable storage diff capture
  gas_profiling: true               # Enable gas profiling
tags: [my_attack, l2]              # Tags for filtering in history queries

What happens when you run crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml:

  1. Load the topology (e.g., single = Ethereum only)
  2. Load the scenario (from scenarios/my_attack_pre_exploit/)
  3. Load the attack plugin (from attacks/my_attack/)
  4. Fork the chain(s) at the fork block auto-merged from evidence.yaml — the attack's evidence.yaml historical_scope.fork_blocks drives the fork block automatically. Check the experiment output for: fork_block updated from X to Y (from evidence.yaml).
  5. Run the scenario's setup phase steps
  6. Start instrumentation (trace capture)
  7. Run the attack plugin's execute() method
  8. Run the attack plugin's verify() method
  9. Run the remaining scenario phase steps
  10. Save artifacts and generate report

[ok] Auto-merge: The fork block is now automatically merged from evidence.yaml at runtime. When you run crucible experiment run, it reads attacks/<name>/evidence.yaml and updates the config's fork_block to match what evidence.yaml specifies. You no longer need to manually edit testbed.local.yaml to change fork blocks between experiments.

If the chain is already running at a different block, the stale chain is terminated and re-forked at the correct block automatically.

Only override manually if you need a different fork block than what evidence.yaml declares (e.g., testing a patch at a different block).

To bypass auto-merge entirely: use the --no-auto-merge flag: 

crucible experiment run experiments/my_replay.yaml --no-auto-merge
This keeps the current fork block unchanged, even if evidence.yaml specifies a different one.

docs.title: Pattern 1: Simple — Only Logs (Attack Plugin Does Everything)
docs.text:
When the attack plugin handles all exploit logic, the scenario just documents what's happening:

name: nomad_pre_exploit
description: >-
  Fork Ethereum at block 15259100 for Nomad bridge reproduction.
  The attack plugin handles all exploit logic.
chains:
  - ethereum
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Starting Nomad replica fraud reproduction at fork block 15259100"
  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack plugin executed by experiment runner"

docs.title: Pattern 2: Pre-Exploit State Setup
docs.text:
When the exploit requires setting up state before running the attack:

name: bridge_exploit_pre_exploit
chains:
  - ethereum
  - polygon
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Setting up pre-exploit state"
      - type: impersonate
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0xRelayerAddress"
      - type: send_tx
        chain: ethereum
        from: "0xRelayerAddress"
        to: "0xBridgeContract"
        data: "0x..."
  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Ready for attack plugin execution"

docs.title: Pattern 3: Post-Exploit Verification
docs.text:
When the scenario needs to verify state after the attack:

phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Setup complete"
  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack in progress"
  - name: verify
    steps:
      - type: assert
        name: attacker_balance_increased
        expected: true
        actual: true
      - type: log
        message: "Verification passed"

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Log every meaningful step — Each log step becomes a line in the experiment report. This is how reviewers understand what happened.

  2. Use descriptive assertion names — Names like exploit_succeeds or keeper_key_replaced are much more useful than assertion_1.

  3. Keep scenarios declarative — Prefer YAML steps over python_script or forge_script when possible. YAML is self-documenting.

  4. Set fork blocks in evidence.yaml — The fork_blocks field in attacks/<name>/evidence.yaml is now the source of truth. Crucible auto-merges it at runtime. No need to edit testbed.yaml or testbed.local.yaml unless you need to override.

  5. One scenario per attack — Don't reuse scenarios for different attacks unless they're truly identical environments.

  6. Verify fork block auto-merge — When you run crucible experiment run, the fork block is auto-merged from attacks/<name>/evidence.yaml. Check the output for: fork_block updated from X to Y (from evidence.yaml). If the wrong block is used, update evidence.yaml's fork_blocks, not testbed.yaml.

  7. Document multi-chain scenarios — If your scenario uses multiple chains, list all of them in chains: and explain why each is needed.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Fix Scenario not found Wrong directory name Check scenarios/<name>/scenario.yaml exists Unknown step type Typo in step type Use one of the 11 types above python_script missing steps.py No steps.py in scenario directory Create scenarios/<name>/steps.py or use a different step type Chain X not in topology Scenario references a chain not in the topology Add the chain to the topology or remove it from the scenario Fork block mismatch testbed.yaml has wrong fork_block Update evidence.yaml's fork_blocks (auto-merged), or use testbed.local.yaml to override

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Creating attack plugins (the attacks/<name>/ directory) docs/RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md Full academic reproduction workflow from research to publication docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md All CLI commands and options docs/internal/DOCKER_IMPLEMENTATION.md Running scenarios in Docker docs/RPC_PROVIDER_GUIDE.md Archive RPC provider comparison and costs docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md Provenance and determinism

docs.title: Simple Operations & Ad-Hoc Testing
docs.text: Purpose: Crucible isn't just for automated exploit reproduction. It's also a managed Anvil orchestrator for day-to-day development, manual testing, contract debugging, and chain exploration. This guide covers every granular operation you can do.

docs.title: Quick Start: Fork & Use in 30 Seconds
docs.text:
# 1. Fork a chain
crucible fork --topology single

# 2. See what's running
crucible status

# 3. Fund an address and start transacting
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xYourAddress \
  $(cast to-wei 1000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

cast send 0xTargetContract "transfer(address,uint256)" \
  0xRecipient $(cast to-wei 10 ether) \
  --unlocked --from 0xYourAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 4. Clean up when done
crucible clean

docs.title: Basic Fork
docs.text:
# Fork a single Ethereum chain (port 8545)
crucible fork --topology single

# Fork multiple chains (Ethereum on 8545, Polygon on 8546)
crucible fork --topology dual

# Fork with a longer timeout for cold-cache chains (e.g., Polygon)
crucible fork --topology dual --fork-timeout 600

After forking, Anvil is running on localhost. You can point any tool at it: - cast, forge (Foundry) - curl (raw JSON-RPC) - web3.py, ethers.js, viem - Crucible's own JsonRpcClient

docs.title: With Baseline Setup
docs.text:
# Full setup: fork + bootstrap + capture golden baseline
crucible setup --topology single

# Now you can reset back to this state at any time
crucible reset --scenario my_scenario --mode soft

docs.title: Blank Fork (No Archive RPC Needed)
docs.text:
For testing contracts without forking from a historical block, set fork_block to null in your topology:

# testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    fork_block: null   # blank fork — starts from genesis
    port: 8545

Then fork normally:

crucible fork --topology single   # clean Anvil, no remote dependency

docs.title: Named Snapshots
docs.text:
Snapshots save the full EVM state (all contracts, storage, balances) of a running chain.

# Create a snapshot
crucible snapshot create my_state

# Restore a snapshot (instant — loads saved state back into memory)
crucible snapshot restore my_state --chain ethereum

# Snapshot specific chains in multi-chain topologies
crucible snapshot create phase1 --chain ethereum
crucible snapshot create phase1 --chain polygon

Snapshots are stored in states/snapshots/ as JSON files.

docs.title: Three-Tier Reset
docs.text:
Reset to a clean baseline state without re-forking:

Mode Speed What Happens soft <5s anvil_loadState from baseline snapshot — no process restart medium <30s Kill Anvil, relaunch, then restore snapshot hard <5min Kill, re-fork from archive RPC, re-run bootstrap, restore snapshot 
# Fastest — just reload state
crucible reset --scenario single --mode soft

# Full clean restart
crucible reset --scenario single --mode hard

docs.title: Save and Restore Custom States
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")

# Save the entire EVM state
state = client.dump_state()  # returns huge dict
import json
with open("my_state.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(state, f)

# Later: restore it
with open("my_state.json") as f:
    saved = json.load(f)
client.load_state(saved)

docs.title: Account Model
docs.text: Crucible starts Anvil with --accounts 0 --balance 0. This means no pre-funded test accounts are created. This is intentional — some archive nodes fail when Anvil tries to prefetch balance for the default Foundry accounts at historical blocks.
