docs.title: Fund Any Address
docs.text:
Since --auto-impersonate is always enabled, you can use any address — you just need to give it balance first.

With cast: 

# Fund with 1000 ETH
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xYourWalletAddress \
  $(cast to-wei 1000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Fund with 10000 ETH (for contract deployment)
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xDeployerAddress \
  $(cast to-wei 10000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

With Python: 

from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")
client.set_balance("0xYourWalletAddress", hex(1000 * 10**18))

With curl: 

curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8545 -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,
    "method":"anvil_setBalance",
    "params":["0xYourWalletAddress", "0x3635C9ADC5DEA00000"]
  }'

💡 Tip: Create a shell script fund.sh that funds your test accounts: 

#!/bin/bash
RPC="http://127.0.0.1:8545"
ETH1000=$(cast to-wei 1000 ether)
for addr in "$@"; do
  cast rpc anvil_setBalance "$addr" "$ETH1000" --rpc-url "$RPC"
done
Usage: ./fund.sh 0xAlice 0xBob 0xCharlie

docs.title: Check Balances
docs.text:
cast balance 0xYourWalletAddress --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast balance 0xTargetContract --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Use Auto-Impersonation (No Private Keys Needed)
docs.text:
With --auto-impersonate, you can send transactions from any address without knowing its private key:

# Send from a contract owner address (no private key needed!)
cast send 0xVulnerableContract "approve(address,uint256)" \
  0xSpender 1000000 \
  --unlocked --from 0xOwnerAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

The --unlocked flag tells Anvil to skip signature verification because the sender is impersonated.

docs.title: Stop Impersonating
docs.text:
cast rpc anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount 0xOwnerAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: With cast send
docs.text:
# Simple ETH transfer
cast send 0xRecipient \
  --value $(cast to-wei 1 ether) \
  --unlocked --from 0xYourAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Contract call
cast send 0xTokenContract \
  "transfer(address,uint256)" \
  0xRecipient $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --unlocked --from 0xTokenOwner \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# With custom gas
cast send 0xContract "exploit()" \
  --gas-limit 500000 \
  --unlocked --from 0xAttacker \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: With Python (Crucible's built-in client)
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient
from web3 import Web3

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")

# Fund the sender first
client.set_balance("0xSender", hex(10 * 10**18))

# Impersonate and send
client.impersonate_account("0xSender")
tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
    "from": "0xSender",
    "to": "0xRecipient",
    "value": hex(10**18),  # 1 ETH
    "gas": hex(21000),
    "gasPrice": hex(0),
})
print(f"Tx hash: {tx_hash}")

docs.title: With forge script
docs.text:
forge script DeployMyContract \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545 \
  --unlocked --broadcast

⚠️ Note: When using forge script against a Crucible fork, the deployer address (default: 0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266) needs to be funded first with anvil_setBalance since Crucible uses --accounts 0.

docs.title: With cast call
docs.text:
# Query any contract state
cast call 0xTokenContract "totalSupply()" \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

cast call 0xTokenContract "balanceOf(address)" \
  0xSomeAddress --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# With block specification
cast call 0xContract "owner()" \
  --block 12996693 --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: With Python
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")

# Raw eth_call
result = client.call("eth_call", [{
    "to": "0xTokenContract",
    "data": "0x18160ddd",  # totalSupply() selector
}, "latest"])

# Higher-level helpers
balance = client.get_balance("0xSomeAddress")  # returns int (wei)
code = client.get_code("0xContract")           # returns hex bytecode
storage = client.get_storage_at("0xContract", "0x0")  # slot 0

docs.title: Set Storage Slots
docs.text:
# Directly write to a contract's storage
cast rpc anvil_setStorageAt \
  0xContractAddress \
  0xStorageSlotHex \
  0xNewValueHex \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

Python: 

client.set_storage_at("0xContract", "0x0", "0xNewValue")

docs.title: Set Code
docs.text:
Replace a contract's bytecode at runtime (useful for testing upgrade patterns):

cast rpc anvil_setCode \
  0xContractAddress \
  0xNewBytecode \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

client.call("anvil_setCode", ["0xContractAddress", "0xNewBytecode"])

docs.title: Set Nonce
docs.text:
cast rpc anvil_setNonce \
  0xSomeAddress \
  0x10 \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Advance Time
docs.text:
# Jump forward 1 day
cast rpc evm_increaseTime 86400 --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast rpc evm_mine --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Or use Crucible's helper
python -c "
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient
c = JsonRpcClient('http://127.0.0.1:8545')
c.advance_time(86400)  # advances time + mines a block
"

docs.title: Mine Blocks
docs.text:
# Mine a single block
cast rpc evm_mine --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Mine with specific timestamp
cast rpc evm_mine $(date +%s) --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Mine N blocks
for i in {1..10}; do cast rpc evm_mine --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545; done

docs.title: Using forge create
docs.text:
# Fund the deployer first
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Deploy
forge create src/MyContract.sol:MyContract \
  --constructor-args arg1 arg2 \
  --unlocked --from 0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Using forge script
docs.text:
# 1. Fund the default deployer
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 2. Deploy with forge script
forge script script/Deploy.s.sol:DeployScript \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545 \
  --unlocked --broadcast --slow

docs.title: Using Python (Crucible SDK)
docs.text:
from crucible.rpc import JsonRpcClient

client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")
client.set_balance("0xDeployer", hex(100 * 10**18))
client.impersonate_account("0xDeployer")

# Deploy by sending to null address with init code
tx_hash = client.send_transaction({
    "from": "0xDeployer",
    "data": "0x" + bytecode_hex,  # contract init code
    "gas": hex(2000000),
    "gasPrice": hex(0),
})

docs.title: 9. Working With Artifacts (Free Instrumentation)
docs.text:
Even during ad-hoc manual testing, Crucible's instrumentation proxy captures everything automatically if you use crucible setup instead of crucible fork:

crucible setup --topology single   # fork + bootstrap + instrumentation

Now every transaction — whether sent via cast, curl, Python, or forge script — produces artifact streams:

# See captured events
cat logs/setup/bootstrap/events.jsonl | head -5

# See gas used per transaction
cat logs/setup/bootstrap/gas_profile.jsonl

# See storage changes
cat logs/setup/bootstrap/storage_diffs.jsonl

# See balance changes
cat logs/setup/bootstrap/balance_diffs.jsonl

Each JSONL line is structured and queryable:

# Find all Transfer events
grep '"event_name":"Transfer"' logs/setup/bootstrap/events.jsonl

# Find storage changes for a specific contract
grep "0xTargetContract" logs/setup/bootstrap/storage_diffs.jsonl

To use the proxy URL instead of the raw Anvil URL — the proxy port is dynamically assigned and printed in the setup output:

# After crucible setup, the proxy URL is shown in the CLI output.
# Example output: "started instrumented proxy for ethereum on http://127.0.0.1:34567"
# Use that URL for all your cast calls to get automatic artifact capture:
cast call 0xContract "owner()" --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:34567

Note: The proxy URL changes on every crucible setup since the OS assigns an available port. Always get the current URL from the setup output or crucible status. To find the proxy URL programmatically, check the chain's meta file in states/*.meta.json for the proxied rpc_url field.

docs.title: Cross-Chain Testing (Dual Fork)
docs.text:
# Fork Ethereum + Polygon simultaneously
crucible fork --topology dual

# Ethereum on :8545, Polygon on :8546
cast block-number --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast block-number --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8546

# Fund addresses on both chains
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xAddr $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xAddr $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8546

docs.title: Interactive Jupyter Analysis
docs.text:
# Launch Jupyter with Crucible analysis helpers
crucible notebook

# In a notebook cell:
from crucible.analysis import load_run, list_runs
runs = list_runs()                     # all experiment runs
events = load_run(runs[0], "events")   # load events as DataFrame

docs.title: Record and Replay RPC Fixtures
docs.text:
Record all RPC traffic during a session for later replay:

# Run with record mode
crucible experiment run experiments/attack.yaml \
  --record tests/fixtures/my_session.jsonl

Then replay in CI without needing a live archive node:

from crucible.recording import ReplayServer
server = ReplayServer("tests/fixtures/my_session.jsonl")
server.start()
# Now ethereum RPC is served from the fixture, not a real node

docs.title: 11. Cleaning Up
docs.text:
# Stop all chains
crucible clean         # stops Anvil processes, removes meta files

# Full cleanup — also remove fork cache and snapshots
crucible clean --all

# Selective cleanup
crucible clean --cache         # remove .fork_cache only
crucible clean --checkpoints   # remove checkpoints only
crucible clean --states        # remove non-baseline snapshots

# Remove old logs (respects saved/published tiers)
crucible clean --logs
crucible clean --logs-older-than 7

# Remove exported datasets (requires --confirm)
crucible clean --datasets --confirm

docs.title: Fresh Start
docs.text:
If you want to completely reset the workspace:

crucible clean --all --logs --datasets --confirm
crucible init  # recreate runtime directories

docs.title: Workflow: Contract Development & Debugging
docs.text:
# 1. Fork a clean chain
crucible fork --topology single

# 2. Fund the default Forge deployer
cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  $(cast to-wei 10000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 3. Deploy and test iteratively
forge script Deploy --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545 --unlocked --broadcast
forge test --fork-url http://127.0.0.1:8545 --match-test testFeature

# 4. Quick state check
cast call 0xDeployed "balanceOf(address)" 0xAddr --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast rpc anvil_setStorageAt 0xDeployed 0xSlot 0xValue --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 5. Snapshot before risky experiments
crucible snapshot create before_hack

# 6. If something breaks — restore instantly
crucible snapshot restore before_hack --chain ethereum

docs.title: Workflow: Historical State Exploration
docs.text:
# 1. Fork at a specific block
crucible fork --topology single

# 2. Check what state looked like at that block
cast balance 0xOldContract --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545
cast call 0xOldContract "owner()" --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 3. Try to exploit a known vulnerability
cast send 0xOldContract "vulnerableFunction(bytes)" \
  $(cast calldata "exploit()") \
  --unlocked --from 0xAttacker \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 4. Did it work? Check storage
cast storage 0xOldContract 0xKeySlot --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 5. Reset and try a different approach
crucible reset --scenario single --mode soft

docs.title: Workflow: Gas Optimization Testing
docs.text:
# 1. Fork and setup instrumentation
crucible setup --topology single

# 2. Fund deployer
cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xDeployer $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 3. Deploy optimized contract
forge create --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:<proxy-port> \  # use proxy URL from crucible setup output
  --unlocked --from 0xDeployer \
  src/Optimized.sol:Optimized

# 4. Check gas profile
grep "Deploy" logs/setup/bootstrap/gas_profile.jsonl

# 5. Snapshot, deploy old version, compare
crucible snapshot create optimized
# ... deploy old version ...
grep "Deploy" logs/setup/bootstrap/gas_profile.jsonl
crucible snapshot restore optimized --chain ethereum

docs.title: Address Has No Balance
docs.text:
Problem: cast send fails with "insufficient funds" or "sender doesn't have enough funds".

Fix: Fund the address before using it: 

cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xYourAddress $(cast to-wei 100 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: "No matching key" When Using --unlocked
docs.text:
Problem: cast send --unlocked fails because the account isn't impersonated.

Fix: --auto-impersonate is enabled by default in Crucible, but cast may need the --from flag explicitly: 

cast send 0xContract "method()" \
  --unlocked --from 0xYourAddress \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Forge Script Default Deployer Not Working
docs.text:
Problem: forge script fails with "insufficient funds" for the default deployer.

Fix: The default Foundry deployer (0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266) is not pre-funded in Crucible (because of --accounts 0). Fund it: 

cast rpc anvil_setBalance \
  0xf39Fd6e51aad88F6F4ce6aB8827279cffFb92266 \
  $(cast to-wei 10000 ether) \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
Problem: Can't fork because port 8545 is taken.

Fix: Check what's running and clean up: 

crucible clean   # stop all Crucible-managed chains
# Or manually: kill the old Anvil process
lsof -i :8545
kill <pid>

docs.title: Cold-Cache Fork Too Slow
docs.text:
Problem: Forking Polygon takes 3-5 minutes.

Fix: Increase timeout or use a faster archive RPC: 

crucible fork --topology dual --fork-timeout 600

The second fork will use the cache and be much faster (<30s).

docs.title: 14. All Available Anvil Cheatcodes
docs.text:
These are all forwarded through Crucible's instrumentation proxy:

Method Purpose Example anvil_setBalance Set ETH balance cast rpc anvil_setBalance 0xAddr 0xAmount anvil_impersonateAccount Impersonate any address anvil_impersonateAccount(addr) (auto-enabled) anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount Stop impersonating anvil_stopImpersonatingAccount(addr) anvil_setCode Replace contract bytecode anvil_setCode(addr, bytecode) anvil_setStorageAt Write storage slot anvil_setStorageAt(addr, slot, value) anvil_setNonce Set account nonce anvil_setNonce(addr, nonce) anvil_mine Mine blocks anvil_mine(blocks) anvil_setCoinbase Set coinbase address anvil_setCoinbase(addr) anvil_setChainId Set chain ID anvil_setChainId(id) anvil_dumpState Export full EVM state dump_state() → JSON anvil_loadState Import full EVM state load_state(json) evm_increaseTime Jump forward increaseTime(seconds) evm_setNextBlockTimestamp Set next block time setNextBlockTimestamp(timestamp) evm_snapshot Create snapshot ID snapshot() → id evm_revert Revert to snapshot revert(id)

All of these work through:

# cast (direct to Anvil port — all methods work)
cast rpc <method> <params> --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Python (direct to Anvil port — all methods work)
client.call("<method>", [params])

# curl (direct to Anvil port — all methods work)
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8545 -d '{"method":"<method>","params":[...]}'

Proxy vs Direct: When using the instrumentation proxy URL, only a subset of Anvil methods are forwarded (see the proxy allowlist in Instrumentation Architecture). For unrestricted access to all cheatcodes, connect to the raw Anvil port directly (http://127.0.0.1:8545).

docs.title: 15. RPC Endpoints Reference
docs.text: Chain Default Port RPC URL Notes Ethereum 8545 http://127.0.0.1:8545 Default for --topology single Polygon 8546 http://127.0.0.1:8546 For --topology dual BSC 8547 http://127.0.0.1:8547 For custom topologies Harmony 8548 http://127.0.0.1:8548 For custom topologies Proxy (Ethereum) Dynamic http://127.0.0.1:<dynamic> OS-assigned, printed by crucible setup

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Technical Overview System architecture, core concepts, design principles User Guide Full setup, Docker vs local, environment config CLI Reference All CLI commands and flags Plugin Development Guide Writing attack, bridge, and agent plugins Troubleshooting Guide Common errors and fixes Fidelity Guide Fidelity levels and enforcement

docs.title: Testbed Scope Guide — What Can Be Tested
docs.text: Determining whether an attack is reproducible on Crucible. Covers EVM forkability, archive RPC requirements, fidelity level gating, and practical edge cases learned from real validation sessions.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Quick Decision Tree
  2. EVM Forkability — The Primary Gate
  3. Fidelity Level Requirements
  4. Chain-Specific Availability
  5. Multi-Chain & Bridge Requirements
  6. In-Scope vs Out-of-Scope
  7. Common Failure Modes
  8. Edge Cases From Validation Sessions

docs.title: 1. Quick Decision Tree
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the attack on an<br>EVM-compatible chain?"}

    No1["❌ Cannot be reproduced.<br>Classify as L1 (narrative only).<br><i>Examples: Solana, Harmony, Cosmos, MPC</i>"]

    Q2{"Can the chain be forked<br>at the exploit block?"}

    No2["No (archive doesn't serve state)<br>Try a different RPC provider (paid).<br>Result: ❌ Limited to L1/L2 on available chains."]

    Q3{"What fidelity level is achievable?"}

    L1["L1 (narrative)<br>No runtime checks needed"]
    L2["L2 (fork-state)<br>Chains + contracts verified"]
    L3["L3 (exact replay)<br>Need exploit tx hash + calldata"]
    L4["L4 (multi-chain)<br>Need bridge topology + all chains forkable"]

    Q1 -- No --> No1
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2

    Q2 -- No --> No2
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3

    Q3 --> L1
    Q3 --> L2
    Q3 --> L3
    Q3 --> L4

docs.title: Quick Sizing Guide
docs.text: Scenario Estimated Time Complexity Archive RPC Needed? L1 narrative only 5-10 min Low No L2 single-chain replay 15-30 min Medium Yes (Ethereum only) L2 multi-chain (2 chains) 30-60 min Medium-High Yes (2 chains) L3 exact tx replay 20-40 min High Yes L4 bridge demo (blank forks) 10-20 min Medium No (blank forks) L4 production (real history) 45-120 min Very High Yes (all chains)

docs.title: What Makes A Chain Forkable
docs.text:
Crucible uses Foundry's Anvil to fork EVM chains. A chain is forkable if:

  1. It's an EVM chain — runs the Ethereum Virtual Machine
  2. An archive RPC is available at the target block
  3. The RPC provider serves historical state (not just recent blocks)
  4. The fork block is within the provider's archive depth

docs.title: Check Forkability
docs.text:
# 1. Test RPC connectivity
curl -s -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_blockNumber","params":[]}' \
  $YOUR_RPC_URL

# 2. Test archive depth (request a specific historical block)
curl -s -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_getBlockByNumber","params":["0xE4E334",false]}' \
  $YOUR_RPC_URL
# Returns block data if archive is available, error if not

# 3. Test contract state at fork block
curl -s -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_getCode","params":["0xContractAddress","0xE4E334"]}' \
  $YOUR_RPC_URL
# Returns bytecode if archive serves state, "0x" if no code at that block

docs.title: What Makes A Chain NOT Forkable
docs.text: Issue Example Workaround Non-EVM chain Solana, Cosmos, Harmony (non-EVM side) Cannot fork at all — classify as L1 No archive RPC Some L2s (ZK-rollups) without archive nodes Use a paid provider or skip the chain Archive too shallow Free RPCs only keep 128 recent blocks Paid archive provider (GetBlock, QuickNode) Rate limited Free tier limits (10-25 req/s) Add fallback providers, use fork cache Block too old BSC block 22M+ exceeds free tier depth Paid provider; or use a more recent fork block if applicable

docs.title: Blank Forks (No RPC Needed)
docs.text:
For validation testing (not historical reproduction), use blank forks:

# testbed.local.yaml — blank forks (no RPC, no archive)
chains:
  ethereum:
    fork_block: null
  polygon:
    fork_block: null

Blank forks start at block 0 with no state. They're useful for: - Testing bridge plugin lifecycle - Validating cross-chain messaging - Exercising the instrumentation pipeline - Determinism checks

Advantage: Zero RPC dependency, runs entirely offline, fully deterministic.

docs.title: Blank Fork Caveats (Known Issues)
docs.text:
Despite being simpler than archive forks, blank forks can fail in non-obvious ways:

  1. --accounts 0 --balance 0 anvil crash — Anvil nightly builds may reject this combination on blank forks, exiting silently with no error message. The fork command waits 300s before timing out with Connection refused. Fix: Clean stale caches and retry, or remove --accounts 0 --balance 0 and start anvil directly.

  2. Stale fork cache interference — If .fork_cache/ethereum/latest/ has data from a previous archive fork (different chain ID or block), anvil may fail when initializing the cache for a blank fork. Fix: rm -rf .fork_cache/* before switching between archive and blank forks.

  3. Orphan processes from previous forks — A stale uv run crucible fork process from a prior attempt may still be running, holding the port. Fix: pkill -9 -f anvil; pkill -9 -f 'crucible fork'; sleep 2; rm -f states/*.meta.json.

  4. First attempt often fails — The very first blank fork may fail due to cache population. A second attempt (after cleanup) typically succeeds. This is a known anvil behavior.

In short: Blank forks are the most reliable mode once running, but the FIRST fork attempt may need cleanup. See Troubleshooting — Blank Fork Never Ready for detailed recovery.

docs.title: 3. Fidelity Level Requirements
docs.text: Each fidelity level has specific requirements that must be met for the experiment to pass enforcement.

docs.title: L4 — Multi-Chain Full Reproduction
docs.text:
Requirement Check How To Meet ≥2 chains in topology historical_scope.chains Configure topology with 2+ chains Bridge plugin topology.bridge Use nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, or wormhole_bridge excluded_components declared reproduction_scope.excluded_components List what's not reproduced Fork-state execution evidence Artifact streams (events, gas, storage) Attack must interact with forked chain(s) Attack result available Attack plugin returns AttackResult Implement execute() method Trace artifacts complete All transactions traced No auto_trace failures

L4 production experiments require archive RPC on all chains. L4 validation experiments (like l4_full_validation) use blank forks and skip archive RPC entirely.

docs.title: L3 — Exact Transaction Replay
docs.text:
Requirement Check How To Meet All L2 requirements — — exact_tx_replay: true reproduction_scope.exact_tx_replay Set in evidence.yaml exploit_transactions not empty historical_scope.exploit_transactions List the real exploit tx hashes Transaction execution evidence tx actions in run.jsonl Attack must submit the real calldata

L3 is the most demanding level because it requires: - Reconstructing the exact calldata of the historical exploit transaction - Matching the same gas usage, event emissions, and storage changes - Dealing with proxy contracts, delegate calls, and complex ABI resolution

docs.title: L2 — Fork-State Behavioral Reproduction
docs.text:
Requirement Check How To Meet Attack result available _check_result_available() Plugin returns AttackResult Chains in topology _check_chain_state() Evidence chains exist in active topology Trace artifacts complete artifacts_complete No auto_trace failures Contract existence Artifact streams found events.jsonl, gas_profile.jsonl, etc. exist

L2 is the standard benchmark level — all 6 attack packs in the benchmark matrix target L2.

docs.title: L1 — Conceptual / Narrative Only
docs.text:
Requirement Check How To Meet Attack result available _check_result_available() Plugin returns something (even None is OK)

L1 is for attacks that cannot be reproduced on-chain — MPC compromises, non-EVM infrastructure hacks, social engineering attacks.

docs.title: L0 — No Reproduction Attempted
docs.text: Requirement Check How To Meet None — Description only

docs.title: Ethereum ❖ Best Coverage
docs.text: Aspect Status Archive RPC (free) ✅ LlamaNodes, Ankr, PublicNode work for all attack fork blocks (12M-16M) Forkable from Block 0 (genesis) Free provider limit ~25 req/s (enough for single-chain forks) Paid alternatives Alchemy, Infura, GetBlock, QuickNode Verdict ✅ Fully supported. All 6 benchmark packs run on Ethereum.

docs.title: Polygon ❖ Partial Coverage
docs.text: Aspect Status Archive RPC (free) ⚠️ Ankr works up to ~13M blocks; block 16,332,982 (Poly Network) may fail Forkable from Depends on provider archive depth anvil_setBalance at old blocks ⚠️ Some archives can't serve state for new accounts at old blocks Verdict ⚠️ Works with paid archive. Free tier may fail at exploit blocks. Bridge plugins skip gracefully on failure.

docs.title: BSC ❖ Limited Coverage
docs.text: Aspect Status Archive RPC (free) ❌ Free tier doesn't cover block 22M+ (BNB Token Hub) Forkable from Depends on provider Verdict ⚠️ Paid archive required for exploit blocks. See RPC Provider Guide.

docs.title: Harmony ❖ Not Forkable (EVM Side Only)
docs.text: Aspect Status Harmony chain (non-EVM) ❌ Not forkable — uses different consensus Ethereum-side bridge contracts ✅ Forkable on Ethereum Verdict ⚠️ Ethereum only. The Harmony chain itself cannot be forked. The Horizon multisig drain is reproduced at L2 on Ethereum.

docs.title: Non-EVM Chains (Not Forkable)
docs.text: Chain Reason Classification Solana Not EVM L1 narrative (wormhole_guardian_bypass) Cosmos / Beacon Chain Not EVM L1 narrative (bnb_token_hub_proof) Fantom (Multichain) MPC compromise, not EVM bug L1 narrative (multichain_router_permit) Moonriver (Multichain) MPC compromise L1 narrative

docs.title: When Multi-Chain Works
docs.text:
A multi-chain experiment succeeds when all chains in the topology are forkable at their respective blocks with the configured RPC providers.

Configuration Works? Notes 2 chains, both blank forks (no RPC) ✅ Always L4 validation tests, deterministic Ethereum + Polygon (archive RPC) ✅ Works Ethereum fine; Polygon may need paid archive at old blocks Ethereum + Polygon + BSC (archive RPC) ⚠️ Partial All 3 need working archives; BSC typically needs paid provider Bridge topology without bridge plugin ❌ Fails L4 enforcer requires topology.bridge to be set

docs.title: When Multi-Chain Fails
docs.text:
1. Chain not forkable — Non-EVM chain in topology
  2. Archive can't serve state — Polygon/BSC at old blocks with free RPC
  3. Bridge plugin not loaded — Topology doesn't specify a bridge
  4. Port conflict — Previous fork processes still running (see Troubleshooting)

docs.title: What L4 Bridge Validation Tests
docs.text:
The l4_full_validation attack plugin exercises the full L4 pipeline:

Phase What It Validates 1. Multi-chain topology Both chains configured and reachable 2. Bridge plugin Bridge loaded and operational 3. Bridge deployment Contracts deployed on both chains 4. Chain reachability Both chains respond to RPC 5. Message A→B Cross-chain message sent on source chain 6. Message B→A Bidirectional messaging confirmed 7. Acceptance verification Destination chain accepts the forged message 8. Artifact capture Instrumentation streams exist 9. Correlation tracking Cross-chain.jsonl entries recorded

To run: 

# Validates without fidelity enforcement (validation mode)
uv run crucible experiment run experiments/l4_full_validation.yaml --no-baseline --no-fidelity

docs.title: ✅ In-Scope (Reproducible)
docs.text: Category Examples Fidelity EVM smart contract bugs Reentrancy, access control, integer overflow L2-L4 Bridge protocol vulnerabilities Nomad committedRoot == 0x00, Wormhole guardian bypass L2-L4 Cross-chain message forgery Forged IAVL Merkle proofs, fake VAAs L2-L4 Compromised key on EVM Using stolen private key to call contract functions L2 Malformed calldata Calling functions with crafted arguments L2-L3

docs.title: ❌ Out-of-Scope (Not Reproducible)
docs.text: Category Examples Classify As Non-EVM chain state Solana, Cosmos, Beacon Chain L1 narrative Off-chain infrastructure MPC node compromise, social engineering L1 narrative Private key compromise Attacker stole keys outside EVM L1 narrative (effects may be L2) Centralized exchange hacks Funds stolen from CEX hot wallet L1 narrative DNS / BGP attacks Infrastructure-level attacks on RPC endpoints L1 narrative

docs.title: ⚠️ Partial Scope
docs.text: Scenario What's Reproducible What's Not Wormhole guardian bypass (Feb 2022) EVM-side VAA processing via completeTransfer() Solana-side sysvar validation (root cause) BNB Token Hub (Oct 2022) EVM-side handlePackage() call on CrossChain contract Beacon Chain IAVL proof verification (root cause) Horizon multisig (Jun 2022) Ethereum-side bridge contracts, multisig authorization Harmony chain state, off-chain key compromise Multichain router (Jul 2023) Nothing — MPC infrastructure compromise Entire attack happened outside EVM

docs.title: "Port 8545 is already in use"
docs.text:
Cause: A previous crucible fork or manual anvil process is still running.

Diagnose: 

# Check what's on the port
fuser 8545/tcp        # Linux
lsof -i :8545         # macOS/Linux
netstat -ano | findstr :8545   # Windows

# Check if crucible still tracks it
crucible status

Fix: 

# Proper cleanup
crucible clean --all

# Force kill orphaned processes
pkill -9 -f anvil
rm -f states/*.meta.json

# If the port stays in TIME_WAIT, wait 30-60s or use a different port

Prevention: Always run crucible clean between experiments. If running in CI/automation, clean stale meta files before forking.

docs.title: "Chain did not become ready within 300s"
docs.text:
Cause: Anvil was spawned but never started listening. Usually because: 1. The RPC URL is unreachable or empty 2. The fork block exceeds the provider's archive depth 3. Anvil crashed on startup (check logs/anvil/<chain>.log)

Fix: 

# Check the anvil log for crash details
cat logs/anvil/ethereum.log

# Test RPC connectivity
curl -s --max-time 10 -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_blockNumber","params":[]}' \
  $YOUR_RPC_URL

docs.title: "Fidelity violation"
docs.text:
Cause: The experiment's runtime behavior doesn't match the declared fidelity level in evidence.yaml.

Fix: Either: - Update the attack to meet the requirement (e.g., add trace executions) - Lower the fidelity level in evidence.yaml - Use --no-fidelity to skip enforcement (validation mode)

docs.title: "Evidence validation against live chain failed"
docs.text:
Cause: A contract address in evidence.yaml has no code at the fork block — probably a wrong address or EOA.

Fix: Verify the address on Etherscan — confirm it's a Contract (not an EOA).

docs.title: Orphaned Anvil Processes
docs.text:
Each crucible fork spawns anvil as a child process. If the parent crucible fork process is killed (timeout, Ctrl+C, crash), the child anvil may survive as an orphan, still holding the port.

How to detect: 

# Find orphaned anvil processes
ps aux | grep anvil | grep -v grep

# Check for zombie (defunct) processes
ps aux | grep 'defunct' | grep anvil || echo 'No zombie anvils'

How to clean: 

# Kill all anvil processes
pkill -9 -f anvil 2>/dev/null

# Remove stale meta files
rm -f states/*.meta.json

# Verify ports are free
ss -tlnp | grep -E '854[5-8]' || echo 'All free'

docs.title: Zombie Processes
docs.text:
Zombie (defunct) processes have exited but haven't been reaped by their parent. They: - Still appear in the process table - Cannot receive signals (SIGKILL has no effect) - Don't hold ports or consume resources (except a PID table entry) - Are harmless but clutter ps output

Detection: Crucible's ChainManager.process_is_zombie() reads /proc/<pid>/status for State: Z on Linux.

Cleanup: Zombies can only be reaped when their parent calls wait(). If the parent is dead, init (PID 1) will eventually reap them. They will not block new forks.

docs.title: Persistent uv run crucible fork Processes
docs.text:
Each uv run crucible fork spawns a process tree: 

uv run crucible fork --topology <name>
  └── crucible fork --topology <name>
       └── anvil --port 8545 ...

If multiple fork commands were run (e.g., through subagent spawns), multiple process trees accumulate. Each holds a different port.

Cleanup strategy: 

# Kill ALL crucible fork + anvil processes at once
pkill -9 -f 'anvil'
pkill -9 -f 'crucible fork'
sleep 2
rm -f states/*.meta.json

Prevention: Run crucible clean before forking, and use the same port configuration consistently.

docs.title: Ports Being Reoccupied After Kill
docs.text:
If a port keeps getting reoccupied after you kill the process: 1. Check for process trees — killing the child anvil doesn't kill the parent crucible fork, which may restart it 2. Check for tmux sessions or systemd services managing the process 3. Check for Docker containers that expose the port 4. Use fuser to find exactly what's on the port, then kill the entire process tree

# Find the exact PID on the port
fuser -v 8545/tcp

# Kill the PID and its parent
kill -9 <PID>
# Or kill the parent PID to prevent respawning
kill -9 <PPID>

# As a last resort, kill all anvil instances
pkill -9 -f anvil

docs.title: Fork Succeeds But Polygon/BSC Archive Doesn't Serve State
docs.text:
Free RPC providers may not serve full historical state for Polygon or BSC at older fork blocks. The bridge plugins handle this gracefully — the chain is skipped with a warning, and the experiment continues on other chains.

If you need the bridge deployed on all chains: 1. Use a paid archive provider (GetBlock, QuickNode) 2. Try a more recent fork block if applicable 3. Run with a topology that excludes the problematic chain

See also: - Troubleshooting Guide — Symptom → cause → fix - Operations Runbook — Daily ops - RPC Provider Guide — Provider comparison - Fidelity Enforcement in CLI Reference - Benchmarks — Per-attack fidelity assessment

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: Quick-reference for common Crucible issues. Symptom → Cause → Fix. See also: CLI Reference, User Guide, Operations Runbook

docs.title: Quick Reference — At a Glance
docs.text: Symptom Most Likely Cause First Action anvil not found on PATH Foundry not installed in Docker/local Rebuild Docker or run foundryup Connection refused on 127.0.0.1:8547 Stale meta file in states/ volume rm -f states/*.meta.json chain bsc did not become ready within 30s RPC env var empty or unreachable Check echo $BSC_RPC_URL experiment failed then unhelpful traceback Multiple possible causes Check run.jsonl first sh -c fails with invalid choice: 'sh' Entrypoint is crucible, not a shell Use --entrypoint bash evidence.yaml missing sections Attack pack scaffold incomplete Run crucible plugin validate reproduction fidelity invalid L0-L4 level mis-spelled in evidence.yaml Check acceptance.fidelity field port 8545 already in use Previous Anvil not cleaned up crucible clean or docker compose down ConfigError: YAML file not found Missing testbed.yaml crucible init or create from template Fidelity violation Runtime behavior doesn't match declared level Check enforcer output for specifics Transaction reverted without error Wrong calldata, address, or preconditions cast call with --trace to see revert reason crucible: command not found Environment not activated or uv sync not run uv sync && source .venv/bin/activate (Linux) or .venv\Scripts\activate (Windows)

docs.title: Entrypoint Is Crucible, Not a Shell
docs.text:
$ docker compose run --rm orchestrator sh -c "uv run crucible doctor"
Fails with: crucible: error: argument command: invalid choice: 'sh'

Cause: The orchestrator's entrypoint is [\"uv\", \"run\", \"crucible\"], not a shell. sh -c passes sh as a subcommand to crucible.

Fix: Override the entrypoint with --entrypoint bash:

docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  uv run crucible doctor && \
  uv run crucible plugin list
"

Tip: For single commands, you don't need --entrypoint bash:

# [ok] This works fine:
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor
docker compose run --rm orchestrator plugin validate attacks/demo_attack

# [fail] Only needed when chaining:
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "cmd1 && cmd2"

docs.title: Stale Meta Files
docs.text:
ERROR: RPC call failed for anvil_dumpState at http://127.0.0.1:8547: Connection refused

Cause: The states/ Docker volume persists .meta.json files from previous forks. When you switch topologies, stale meta files reference chains and ports that aren't running.

Fix: Always clean stale meta files before forking:

rm -f states/*.meta.json
# Or inside Docker:
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single
"

Prevention: Add the cleanup to your run script or use crucible clean --states which also handles this.

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
Error: error: EOF: Anvil failed to start
# or
Error: error binding to 127.0.0.1:8545: address already in use

Cause: - A previous Anvil instance is still running - Another process (e.g., another Crucible, a manual anvil) occupies the port

Fix: 

# Stop all Crucible chains
crucible clean

# In Docker:
docker compose down

# Check what's on the port (Windows):
netstat -ano | findstr :8545
# Then taskkill /PID <pid> /F

docs.title: Fork Cache Bind-Mount Interference
docs.text:
# Blank fork inside Docker fails with Connection refused
# BUT same config works on the host machine

Cause: The orchestrator container bind-mounts the host's .fork_cache/ directory (.:/workspace). If the host has stale cache data from a previous archive fork (e.g., .fork_cache/ethereum/latest/ with different chain ID or block), Anvil running inside the container reads that stale cache and crashes on init.

This is a common failure mode when switching between archive forks and blank forks inside Docker — the cache directory persists on the host but the container's Anvil doesn't expect it.

Diagnose: 

# Inside the container, check if stale cache exists
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  ls -la .fork_cache/ethereum/latest/ 2>/dev/null || echo 'No stale cache'
"

Fix: 

# Clean cache from the host (affects the container via bind-mount)
rm -rf .fork_cache/*

# Or clean inside the container:
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "rm -rf .fork_cache/*"

Prevention: - Always clean .fork_cache/ before switching between archive and blank forks - Add the cache cleanup to your smoke test scripts (see RELEASE_CHECKLIST.md §4)

docs.title: Container Build Fails
docs.text:
$ docker compose build orchestrator
...
Error: error loading environment variables from .env: file not found

Cause: Missing .env file. The Dockerfile references env vars from .env.

Fix: 

cp .env.example .env
# Edit with your RPC URLs

docs.title: Docker Daemon Not Running (Windows)
docs.text:
error during connect: ... The docker daemon is not running

Fix: 

# Start Docker Desktop
Start-Process "C:\Program Files\Docker\Docker\Docker Desktop.exe"

# Wait for it to be ready (10-30s)
docker ps

# If it doesn't start, check:
# - Windows WSL2 is installed
# - Virtualization is enabled in BIOS
# - Docker Desktop has permission in Windows Security

docs.title: Foundry Not on PATH
docs.text:
crucible: error: anvil not found on PATH — required to fork chain 'ethereum'

Inside Docker: Foundry should be pre-installed. If missing, rebuild:

docker compose build --no-cache orchestrator

If the Dockerfile's foundryup step failed, check build logs:

docker compose build --no-cache orchestrator 2>&1 | grep -i foundry

Locally (Windows):

# Install Foundry (one-time)
cd ~
curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz -o foundryup.bat
.\foundryup.bat
foundryup

# If foundryup fails, install via uv or pip:
uv tool install foundry-devops
# Or download binaries from https://github.com/foundry-rs/foundry/releases

Verify installation:

forge --version
anvil --version
cast --version

docs.title: Foundry Version Mismatch
docs.text:
Publication gate: Foundry version mismatch. Expected: forge X.Y.Z, Got: forge A.B.C

Cause: The testbed.yaml foundry.required_version doesn't match the installed version.

Fix: 

# Option A: Update the config to match your version
foundry:
  required_version: ""                    # skip version check
  skip_version_check: true

# Option B: Pin to a specific version
foundry:
  required_version: "2025-03-01"          # nightly date format

docs.title: Anvil Fails to Fork
docs.text:
Error: Failed to get block for block number: 15259100

Cause 1 — Not an archive node: The RPC provider doesn't serve historical state at the requested block.

Cause 2 — Fork block too old: The provider only keeps recent history (~128 blocks for public nodes).

Fix: - Check the provider's archive depth (see RPC Provider Guide) - Switch to a paid archive provider (GetBlock, QuickNode) for BSC/Polygon - For Ethereum, LlamaNodes works for all attack fork blocks

Test archive depth: 

curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_getBlockByNumber","params":["0x94E334",false]}' \
  https://your-rpc-provider
# Returns block data if archive, error if not

docs.title: Environment Variable Not Set
docs.text:
Chain 'bsc' has no RPC URL configured. Check your .env file.

Cause: ${BSC_RPC_URL} is referenced in testbed.yaml but not defined in .env.

Fix: 

# Check what's set:
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c 'echo "BSC=$BSC_RPC_URL"'

# Add to .env if missing:
echo 'BSC_RPC_URL=https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_TOKEN' >> .env

docs.title: Chain Not Ready (Timeout)
docs.text:
ERROR: chain bsc did not become ready within 30s on http://127.0.0.1:8547: Connection refused

Cause 1 — RPC URL unreachable: The upstream RPC endpoint is down, slow, or the env var is empty.

Fix: Test connectivity:

docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c '
  curl -s --max-time 10 -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d "{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"eth_blockNumber\",\"params\":[]}" \
    https://go.getblock.io/YOUR_TOKEN
'

Cause 2 — Provider rate limiting: The free tier is rate-limited, causing timeouts during history fetching.

Fix: Add fallback providers in testbed.yaml:

rpc_providers:
  bsc:
    - name: primary
      url: "${BSC_RPC_URL}"
    - name: publicnode
      url: "https://bsc.publicnode.com"
      priority: 2

Crucible automatically retries with the next provider on failure.

Cause 3 — Fork block too deep: Provider doesn't archive that far back.

Fix: Use a paid archive provider (GetBlock, QuickNode). See RPC Provider Guide.

Cause 4 — Stale fork cache (blank forks only): Even on blank forks (fork_block: null), Anvil uses --cache-path .fork_cache/<chain>/latest to cache genesis state. If that cache directory has stale data from a previous archive fork (different chain ID, block, or RPC provider), Anvil may fail to initialize and crash silently — producing the same Connection refused timeout.

This can happen when switching from archive forks to blank forks or between different archive forks on the same chain.

Diagnose: 

# Check if cache directory exists from a previous run
ls -la .fork_cache/ethereum/latest/ 2>/dev/null && echo "Stale cache detected"
# If it contains data from a different block/provider, that's the issue

Fix: 

rm -rf .fork_cache/ethereum/latest
# Or nuke all cached fork data:
rm -rf .fork_cache/*

Prevention: - Always clean .fork_cache/ before switching between archive forks and blank forks - Add rm -rf .fork_cache/* to your CI/automation scripts before forking - See Blank Fork Failure Recovery for detailed recovery

docs.title: Rate Limited
docs.text:
error code: -32000: rate limit exceeded

Free tier limits vary by provider:

Provider Limit Action LlamaNodes 25 req/s Usually sufficient; add fallback PublicNode 10 req/s Single-chain only, add fallback Ankr (no key) ~15 req/s Free tier may hit limits

Fix: Add multiple providers as fallbacks. Crucible rotates through them:

rpc_providers:
  ethereum:
    - name: primary
      url: "${ETH_RPC_URL}"
    - name: llamarpc
      url: "https://eth.llamarpc.com"
      priority: 2
    - name: publicnode
      url: "https://ethereum.publicnode.com"
      priority: 3

docs.title: Empty Artifact Streams (No Events or Storage Diffs Captured)
docs.text:
Experiment succeeds (exit 0) but events.jsonl and/or storage_diffs.jsonl are empty.

Cause: The instrumentation proxy captures events and storage diffs via debug_traceTransaction on the forked Anvil. Certain Anvil nightly builds may fail silently on the prestateTracer + diffMode combination, producing 0 events and 0 storage diffs even though the exploit transaction succeeded.

How to confirm: Gas is still captured (from eth_getTransactionReceipt), and balance diffs may also be captured. Check:

cat logs/<experiment>/<run_id>/chain_ethereum/gas_profile.jsonl
cat logs/<experiment>/<run_id>/chain_ethereum/balance_diffs.jsonl

If gas is present but events/storage are empty, the proxy hit the trace fallback.

New behavior: Crucible now logs a prominent failure step when debug_traceTransaction fails, showing: 

[FAIL] trace failed for tx 0xabc... — storage diffs + balance changes lost. Gas + events captured from receipt.
See TROUBLESHOOTING.md for Anvil version advice.

Fix (manual recovery):

# 1. Get the tx hash from gas_profile.jsonl
# 2. Use cast to fetch the receipt manually (events live here)
cast receipt <tx_hash> --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 3. Check if debug_traceTransaction works directly
cast rpc debug_traceTransaction <tx_hash> \
  '{"tracer": "prestateTracer", "tracerConfig": {"diffMode": true}}' \
  --rpc-url http://127.0.0.1:8545

# 4. If the direct call works but the proxy didn't capture it,
#    this is a proxy bug worth reporting. If it also fails,
#    it's an Anvil version limitation.

Workaround: The attack plugin's verify() method reads storage independently via RPC — check run.jsonl for pass/fail assertions. A passed verification means the exploit worked, even if the artifact streams are incomplete.

Prevention: Pin your Foundry version to a known-good stable release (not nightly) if complete artifact capture is required for publication:

# testbed.yaml
foundry:
  skip_version_check: true  # see TROUBLESHOOTING.md for version pinning

docs.title: Experiment Fails With Unhelpful Traceback
docs.text:
Error: Experiment failed: ...

First: Check the run log, not the traceback. The run.jsonl file contains all lifecycle events:

# If you have a run directory:
cat logs/demo_experiment/*/run.jsonl | tail -20

# Inside Docker:
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c '
  cat logs/demo_experiment/*/run.jsonl | tail -20
'

Each line shows phase, action, status, and message — look for "status":"failure".

docs.title: Baseline Mismatch
docs.text:
BaselineMismatch: Baseline verification failed

Cause: The chain state at fork block doesn't match the captured baseline (e.g., different block, different provider).

Fix: 1. Don't overwrite the baseline immediately. 2. Understand WHY the state changed — different RPC provider? Different fork block? Different topology? 3. If the change is intentional (e.g., new experiment), delete the old baseline and capture a new one:

crucible clean --states
crucible baseline capture <scenario> --topology <topology>

docs.title: Fork Block Wrong for Experiment
docs.text:
Attack verification failed: EthCrossChainManager has no code at fork block 12996062

Cause: The testbed.yaml fork_block is set for a different experiment, OR the evidence.yaml doesn't specify the correct fork block.

Auto-fix: Since Crucible now auto-merges fork_block from evidence.yaml at runtime, most cases are handled automatically. Check the experiment output for: 

ethereum: fork_block updated from 12996693 to 15259100 (from evidence.yaml)

If auto-merge doesn't fix it: - Verify evidence.yaml has historical_scope.fork_blocks.ethereum set to the correct block - Or manually override with testbed.local.yaml:

# testbed.local.yaml
chains:
  ethereum:
    fork_block: 15259100  # Nomad Bridge fork block

To bypass auto-merge entirely: use --no-auto-merge: 

crucible experiment run experiments/my_replay.yaml --no-auto-merge
This keeps the current fork block unchanged, even if evidence.yaml specifies a different one. Useful when testing against a specific block without auto-override.

Fork block reference:

Attack Ethereum Polygon BSC Nomad Bridge 15,259,100 — — Wormhole Guardian 14,125,553 — — Poly Network 12,996,693 16,332,982 9,755,172 Horizon Multisig 14,399,491 — — BNB Token Hub — — 22,166,659 Multichain Router 16,329,241 — —

docs.title: Contract Address Wrong (EOA Instead of Contract)
docs.text:
Attack verification failed: ContractName has no code at fork block

Cause: The contract address in the attack pack is actually an EOA (user wallet), not a contract. This was the most common bug found during validation (3 out of 13 bugs).

Fix: 1. Paste the address into Etherscan 2. Verify the page shows "Contract" (not "Address (EOA)") 3. Update the address in the attack pack's plugin.py and evidence.yaml

docs.title: Fidelity Violation
docs.text:
ERROR:crucible.experiment:Fidelity violation: Fidelity level L2 requires fork-state execution evidence

Cause: The experiment's runtime behavior doesn't match the fidelity level declared in the attack plugin's evidence.yaml. For example, an attack claiming L3 (exact transaction replay) but not producing transaction artifacts.

Fix: - Check the enforcer error message for the specific unmet requirement - Either adjust the attack to meet the requirement (e.g., add exact_tx_replay) or - Lower the declared fidelity level in evidence.yaml - Use --no-fidelity to skip fidelity checks entirely (useful for demo/simulation experiments)

See the Fidelity Enforcement section in CLI Reference for per-level requirements.

docs.title: Bridge Deployment — Double 0x Prefix
docs.text:
ERROR: Experiment failed: RPC error for eth_sendTransaction: invalid value: string "0x0x6080..."

Cause: Foundry's compiled bytecode.object already includes the 0x hex prefix. The bridge plugin's deploy method prepends another "0x" + bytecode, producing "0x0x6080..." which Anvil rejects.

Fix: 

# [fail] Double prefix:
"data": "0x" + bytecode,  # "0x0x6080..."

# [ok] Correct:
"data": bytecode,  # bytecode from Foundry already has "0x"

Prevention: All 3 bridge plugins (nomad_bridge, poly_network_bridge, wormhole_bridge) have been fixed. If creating a new bridge plugin, use bytecode directly without prepending "0x".

docs.title: Bridge Deployment — Archive Can't Serve State (Polygon/BSC)
docs.text:
RPC error for anvil_setBalance: failed to get account for 0xDeaDDeaD...: historical state ... is not available

Cause: Some archive nodes (especially Polygon and BSC) don't serve full historical state at older fork blocks. When anvil_setBalance is called on a new (non-existent) address, Anvil tries to look it up in the remote archive and the archive returns "state not available".

Fix: This is handled gracefully in all 3 bridge plugins — the offending chain is skipped with a warning and the experiment continues on other chains. If you need the bridge deployed on all chains: - Use a different RPC provider with better archive depth (see RPC Provider Guide) - Use a more recent fork block - Run with a topology that doesn't include the problematic chain

Prevention: All bridge plugins now wrap per-chain deployment in try/except, so one chain's failure doesn't crash the whole experiment.

docs.title: Auto-Merge Kills Chain But Never Restarts It
docs.text:
ERROR: Experiment failed: Connection refused on http://127.0.0.1:8545
# After a message: fork_block updated from X to Y (from evidence.yaml)

Cause: The auto-merge feature correctly reads fork_blocks from evidence.yaml and terminates the old Anvil. But if the chain isn't restarted at the new block, any subsequent RPC calls fail with "Connection refused".

Fix (already applied): This was a framework bug in crucible/experiment.py — the chain is now automatically re-forked after auto-merge terminates the old process.

Workaround (if still happening): Use --no-auto-merge to bypass auto-merge entirely: 

crucible experiment run experiments/my_replay.yaml --no-auto-merge

docs.title: Bridge evidence.yaml Uses Placeholder String
docs.text:
ERROR: Evidence validation against live chain failed:
  - Contract "deployed by bridge plugin" has no code on any running chain

Cause: The evidence.yaml affected_contracts section has a placeholder string instead of a real contract address (e.g., NomadReplica: "deployed by bridge plugin"). The evidence pre-flight validation tries to call get_code() on that string, which fails.

Fix: 

# [fail] Placeholder string:
affected_contracts:
  NomadReplica: "deployed by bridge plugin"

# [ok] Empty list for dynamically deployed contracts:
affected_contracts: []  # contracts deployed at runtime by bridge plugin

docs.title: Checkpoint Tries To Snapshot Dead Chains
docs.text:
ERROR: RPC call failed for anvil_dumpState at http://127.0.0.1:8547: Connection refused

Cause: The checkpoint manager iterates over all .meta.json files in states/. If a stale meta file exists (from a previous topology with different chains), it tries to snapshot a chain that's no longer running.

Fix: Framework fix — checkpoint now filters to only snapshot chains in the active topology. Use rm -f states/*.meta.json before switching topologies to clean up stale files.

docs.title: set_balance Fails On Precompile Address
docs.text:
RPC error for anvil_setBalance: failed to get account for 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000001

Cause: Address 0x...0001 is the ECDSA-recover precompile. Anvil has special handling for precompile addresses and anvil_setBalance may fail on them, especially on forked chains.

Fix: Use a random EOA address (e.g., 0xDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaD) that has no special meaning on any chain: 

# [fail] Precompile address:
self._deployer = "0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000001"

# [ok] Random EOA:
self._deployer = "0xDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaDDeaD"

docs.title: Port TIME_WAIT After Killing Anvil
docs.text:
Error: error binding to 127.0.0.1:8545: address already in use
# After auto-merge message about updating fork_block

Cause: After SIGTERM/SIGKILL, the OS keeps the port in TIME_WAIT state (30-120 seconds). When auto-merge tries to start a new Anvil immediately on the same port, it fails.

Fix: Framework fix — Crucible now polls the port every 500ms for up to 10 seconds before starting the new Anvil. If the port is still busy, a warning is printed but the experiment continues.

docs.title: Demo/Agent Experiments Require LLM API Key
docs.text:
ERROR: OpenAI API key not set. Set the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable

Cause: The experiment uses an AgentPlugin (LLM-driven attack) configured with agent_provider: openai but no API key is set.

Fix: Use the mock provider to run agent experiments without any LLM: 

# experiments/demo_agent.yaml
run:
  agent_provider: mock    # instead of "openai" or "ollama"
  agent_model: mock
The mock provider returns a hardcoded "done" response immediately, allowing the agent workflow to complete without any API call.

docs.title: Experiment Hangs / Never Completes
docs.text:
# Command just sits there, no output for minutes

Possible causes: - RPC provider is slow to respond (check rate limits) - Anvil is still syncing historical state (check docker compose logs anvil-ethereum) - The attack plugin has an infinite loop

Diagnose: 

# Check if Anvil is alive:
curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eth_blockNumber","params":[]}' \
  http://127.0.0.1:8545

# Check Docker logs:
docker compose logs anvil-ethereum --tail=20

# Check if the attack is making progress:
docker compose logs orchestrator --tail=20

docs.title: Plugin Not Found
docs.text:
PluginInvalid: plugin not found: attack/my_cool_attack

Common causes: - Plugin directory doesn't exist: attacks/my_cool_attack/ - Wrong plugin type: --type bridge when the plugin is an attack - Missing plugin.yaml in the plugin directory

Fix: 

# List all discovered plugins:
crucible plugin list

# Validate a specific plugin for detailed errors:
crucible plugin validate attacks/my_cool_attack

docs.title: Missing plugin.yaml
docs.text:
PluginInvalid: missing plugin.yaml: attacks/my_cool_attack

Fix: The plugin directory must contain plugin.yaml. Generate a scaffold:

crucible attack init my_cool_attack
# Creates attacks/my_cool_attack/ with plugin.yaml, evidence.yaml, and plugin.py

docs.title: Missing Evidence Sections
docs.text:
PluginInvalid: evidence.yaml missing sections: incident, sources, historical_scope, reproduction_scope, acceptance

Cause: The evidence.yaml file is incomplete. Required sections for attack plugins: - incident (name, date, description) - sources (at least one source URL) - historical_scope (how many transactions/blocks) - reproduction_scope (chains, fidelity level) - acceptance (required_assertions)

Fix: Look at a complete example:

cat attacks/demo_attack/evidence.yaml

docs.title: Bridge Evidence Missing
docs.text:
PluginInvalid: bridge plugin missing evidence.yaml: bridges/demo_bridge

Cause: Bridge plugins also require evidence.yaml, but with different sections: - protocol (name, version) - features (list of modeled features) - limitations (list of known limitations) - chains (optional — list of supported chains)

Fix: See the demo bridge for a complete example:

cat bridges/demo_bridge/evidence.yaml

docs.title: Entry File Not Found
docs.text:
PluginInvalid: entry file not found: attacks/my_cool_attack/plugin.py

Cause: The plugin.yaml references an entry file that doesn't exist.

Fix: 

# plugin.yaml — make sure the entry field matches the actual filename:
name: my_cool_attack
type: attack
version: "1.0.0"
entry: plugin.py          # ← must match the .py file on disk
class: MyCoolAttackPlugin

docs.title: Class Doesn't Implement Expected Interface
docs.text:
PluginInvalid: MyCoolAttackPlugin must implement AttackPlugin

Cause: The plugin class doesn't inherit from the correct base class.

Fix: 

# Attack plugins must inherit from AttackPlugin:
from crucible.sdk import AttackPlugin

class MyCoolAttackPlugin(AttackPlugin):   # ← correct
    ...

# Bridge plugins must inherit from BridgePlugin:
from crucible.sdk import BridgePlugin

class MyCoolBridgePlugin(BridgePlugin):   # ← correct
    ...

docs.title: Missing testbed.yaml
docs.text:
ConfigError: YAML file not found: C:\path\to\testbed.yaml

Fix: 

# The file should be in the project root. If missing:
copy testbed.yaml.example testbed.yaml
# or on Linux/macOS:
cp testbed.yaml.example testbed.yaml

docs.title: Duplicate Port or Chain ID
docs.text:
ValueError: chains ethereum and polygon share port 8545
# or
ValueError: chains bsc and polygon share chain_id 100

Cause: Two chains in testbed.yaml have the same port or chain_id.

Fix: Assign unique ports and chain IDs:

chains:
  ethereum:
    port: 8545
    chain_id: 1
  polygon:
    port: 8546    # was 8545 — conflict resolved
    chain_id: 137  # was 100 — use real chain ID

docs.title: Config Parse Errors
docs.text:
ConfigError: 1 validation error for TestbedConfig

Cause: YAML structure doesn't match the expected schema (e.g., wrong field name, wrong type).

Fix: Check the error message for the specific field, then validate:

python -c "
from crucible.config import load_config
cfg = load_config()
print('Config OK')
"

docs.title: uv sync Fails
docs.text:
$ uv sync
  × No solution found when resolving dependencies

Cause: Python version mismatch — Crucible requires Python 3.11+.

Fix: 

# Check your Python version
python3 --version

# Install Python 3.12 on Ubuntu:
sudo apt-get install -y python3.12 python3.12-venv

# Use the correct Python:
uv python pin 3.12
uv sync

docs.title: uv Command Not Found
docs.text:
$ uv sync
bash: uv: command not found

Fix: 

# Install uv via pip (recommended):
pip install uv

# Or via the standalone installer:
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Or install Crucible without uv (system-wide):
pip3 install --break-system-packages -e ".[dev,analysis,ui,jupyter]"

docs.title: crucible: command not found After Activating Venv
docs.text:
# First check if venv is active:
which python  # should point to .venv/bin/python

# If not, activate it:
source .venv/bin/activate  # Linux/macOS
.venv\Scripts\activate     # Windows

# If still not found, reinstall:
uv sync

docs.title: .venv Permission Denied / Can't Remove
docs.text:
error: failed to remove directory `.venv/lib': Permission denied (os error 13)
# When running: uv run pytest or uv sync

Cause: The .venv directory was created by a different user (e.g., root during Docker setup or an earlier build). The current user can read and execute files inside but cannot modify or delete them. uv tries to recreate the venv when packages change and fails because it can't remove the root-owned directory.

Check ownership: 

ls -la .venv/ | head -3
# If owner is 'root' and you're not root, this is the issue

Fix (without sudo):

# Use Docker to remove root-owned .venv (runs as root inside container):
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "rm -rf /workspace/.venv"

# Then recreate with current user's permissions:
uv sync

The Docker container runs as root and can remove files owned by any user when they're bind-mounted into the container.

Alternative fix (with sudo):

sudo rm -rf .venv
uv sync

Prevention: Always create the .venv with your own user (uv sync without sudo). If the venv was created inside a Docker container that ran as root, it will be root-owned on the host — recreate it with uv sync after removal.

docs.title: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'crucible'
docs.text:
Cause: The package hasn't been installed in editable mode, or the venv was created but uv sync hasn't run.

Fix: 

# Make sure you're in the project root
cd /path/to/crucible_testbed

# Install the package
uv sync

# Or if not using uv:
pip install -e .

docs.title: Foundry Not Installed Locally
docs.text:
crucible: error: anvil not found on PATH — required to fork chain 'ethereum'

Fix: 

# One-time install
curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash
source ~/.bashrc
foundryup

# Verify:
anvil --version
forge --version
cast --version

docs.title: Port Conflict When Running Locally
docs.text:
Error: error binding to 127.0.0.1:8545: address already in use

Cause: A previous Crucible run or manual anvil instance is still running.

Fix: 

# Stop all Crucible chains
crucible clean

# Force-kill any orphaned anvil processes
pkill -f anvil 2>/dev/null || true

# Check what's using the port
lsof -i :8545
# Kill: kill -9 <PID>

docs.title: --break-system-packages Warning
docs.text:
error: externally-managed-environment
× This environment is externally managed

Cause: PEP 668 prevents installing Python packages outside a venv.

Fix: Either use a venv (uv sync && source .venv/bin/activate) or bypass on dedicated machines: 

pip3 install --break-system-packages -e ".[dev,analysis,ui,jupyter]"
The --break-system-packages flag is safe on machines dedicated to the testbed where you don't need environment isolation.

docs.title: crucible doctor Shows Failing Checks
docs.text:
Common failing checks and their solutions:

Check Fails Fix foundry binary: anvil not found on PATH Install Foundry or use Docker config: testbed.yaml file not found Create from template rpc: ethereum not resolved Set ETH_RPC_URL in .env .env file not found cp .env.example .env required dirs not found crucible init port 8545 already in use Kill process or change port Docker not available Only matters for Docker mode streamlit not available Only matters for dashboard; install via uv sync --extra ui 
# Targeted checks:
crucible doctor --python --foundry   # Python + Foundry only
crucible doctor --ports              # Port availability
crucible doctor --fix                # Auto-fix broken symlinks + missing dirs

docs.title: Ports Already In Use (Windows)
docs.text:
# Find what's using port 8545:
netstat -ano | findstr :8545
# Output: TCP 127.0.0.1:8545 0.0.0.0:0 LISTENING 12345

# Kill the process:
taskkill /PID 12345 /F

# If it's WSL-bound:
wsl --shutdown

docs.title: Permission Denied (Windows Docker)
docs.text:
permission denied while trying to connect to the Docker daemon socket

Fix: 1. Ensure Docker Desktop is running (check system tray) 2. Run PowerShell as Administrator 3. If running in WSL2, ensure your user is in the docker group:

sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
newgrp docker

docs.title: Zombie (Defunct) Anvil Processes
docs.text:
# ps aux shows: [anvil] <defunct>

Cause: A zombie (defunct) process has exited but hasn't been reaped by its parent. Zombies can't receive signals (SIGKILL has no effect) and don't hold ports, but they clutter the process table.

How to detect:

ps aux | grep defunct | grep anvil
# Example output:
# testbed  2006869  0.0  0.0      0     0 pts/3    Z+   14:20   0:00 [anvil] <defunct>
# The "Z" state flag means zombie

How Crucible handles it: Crucible's ChainManager.process_is_zombie() reads /proc/<pid>/status for State: Z on Linux. When detected, the process is treated as already terminated — no signals are sent.

Manual cleanup: Zombies are reaped by their parent when it calls wait(). If the parent is already dead, init (PID 1) will eventually reap them. They don't block new forks.

docs.title: Orphaned Anvil & Crucible Fork Process Trees
docs.text:
# Multiple anvil instances keep appearing on different ports

Cause: Each uv run crucible fork --topology <name> command spawns a process tree: 

uv run crucible fork --topology <name>
  └── crucible fork --topology <name>
       └── anvil --port 8545 ...
When the parent uv run is killed (timeout, crash, Ctrl+C), the child crucible fork and its anvil children may survive as orphans, still holding their ports.

How to detect: 

# Find all crucible fork + anvil processes
ps aux | grep -E '(anvil|crucible fork)' | grep -v grep

# Check which ports are occupied
ss -tlnp | grep -E '854[5-8]'

Complete cleanup: 

# Kill ALL at once
pkill -9 -f 'anvil'
pkill -9 -f 'crucible fork'
sleep 2

# Remove stale meta files
rm -f states/*.meta.json

# Verify all ports are free
ss -tlnp | grep -E '854[5-8]' || echo 'All free'

docs.title: Port Keeps Getting Reoccupied After Kill
docs.text:
Symptom: You kill the anvil process on port 8545, verify it's free, but when you run crucible fork again, the port is already occupied.

Root cause: One of: 1. Process tree — Killing the child anvil doesn't kill the parent crucible fork, which may be restarting it 2. TIME_WAIT — The port is in TIME_WAIT state (30-120s) after the process exits 3. Another crucible fork command — A previous uv run crucible fork from a subagent call is still running

Step-by-step recovery:

# Step 1: Find exactly what's on the port
fuser -v 8545/tcp
# Shows PID, user, and command

# Step 2: Trace the parent chain
ps -o pid,ppid,args -p <PID>
# If the parent is `crucible fork`, kill the parent too

# Step 3: Kill the entire process tree
# Kill the leaf process first, then the parent
kill -9 <anvil_PID>
kill -9 <crucible_fork_PID>

# Step 4: Or nuke everything at once (faster)
pkill -9 -f 'anvil'
pkill -9 -f 'crucible fork'
sleep 3
rm -f states/*.meta.json

# Step 5: Verify truly free
ss -tlnp | grep 8545 || echo 'Free'

Prevention: - Use a consistent port configuration and don't switch between runs - Run crucible clean --all between experiments - In CI/automation, clean stale meta files before forking - If using subagents to run fork commands, add a cleanup step after

docs.title: Tmux Sessions Managing Anvil
docs.text:
# Anvil keeps running even after crucible clean

Cause: An anvil process may be running inside a tmux session. Killing it from outside the session won't stop the tmux-managed process.

Check: 

tmux list-sessions
tmux capture-pane -t <session> -p | head -20

Fix: Kill the tmux session or switch to its pane and stop the process.

docs.title: Chain Never Becomes Ready on Blank Fork (No Archive)
docs.text:
ERROR: chain ethereum did not become ready within 300s on http://127.0.0.1:8545:
       Connection refused
# BUT you set fork_block: null — no remote RPC should be needed

Symptom: The fork command hangs for 300s and eventually times out with Connection refused, even though you configured a blank fork (fork_block: null) with no RPC dependency.

Root cause: Anvil crashed silently on startup. Despite being a blank fork (no --fork-url flag, no archive fetch needed), anvil can fail to start for these reasons:

  1. --accounts 0 --balance 0 conflict — Anvil nightly builds may reject --accounts 0 combined with --balance 0 on blank forks. The process exits immediately with exit code 0 and no stderr output. Crucible uses these flags to avoid prefetching default accounts from remote archives; on blank forks this is unnecessary but usually harmless.

  2. Stale fork cache — If .fork_cache/ethereum/latest/ has stale data from a previous archive fork (different chain ID or block), anvil may fail when initializing the cache for the blank fork. The cache path --cache-path .fork_cache/ethereum/latest is used even for blank forks.

  3. Port occupied by orphan — A previous orphan anvil process still holds the port (see Orphaned Processes & Port Recovery).

  4. Stale fork process — A previous uv run crucible fork command may still be alive. Check with ps aux | grep 'crucible fork'.

How to distinguish from archive fork failure: - You configured fork_block: null — no remote RPC should be needed - The error tends to appear quickly (not a slow 300s timeout waiting for RPC) - There's no --fork-url in the anvil command line (check logs/anvil/<chain>.log header) - crucible status shows no running chain even though you just forked

Diagnose: 

# 1. First thing: check the anvil log for crash details
cat logs/anvil/ethereum.log
# Empty log → anvil never started (crashed on init)
# Has eth_blockNumber requests → anvil IS running, check port/process

# 2. Check if anvil is actually in the process table
ps aux | grep anvil | grep -v grep
# [anvil] <defunct> → zombie (dead, harmless)
# Live anvil but not responding → check log for errors

# 3. Check if the port is actually free
ss -tlnp | grep 8545

# 4. Try starting anvil manually to see the error
test_port=8550  # Different port to avoid conflict
anvil --host 127.0.0.1 --port $test_port --chain-id 1 --gas-price 0 \
  --block-base-fee-per-gas 1000000000 --auto-impersonate --order fifo \
  --accounts 0 --balance 0 2>&1 | head -20
# If anvil exits silently → --accounts 0 bug
# Try without --accounts 0 --balance 0:
anvil --host 127.0.0.1 --port $test_port --chain-id 1 --auto-impersonate 2>&1 | head -10

Fix: 

# 1. Clean up stale caches
rm -rf .fork_cache/ethereum/latest .fork_cache/polygon/latest

# 2. Kill ALL orphaned processes
pkill -9 -f 'anvil' 2>/dev/null
pkill -9 -f 'crucible fork' 2>/dev/null
sleep 2

# 3. Remove stale meta files
rm -f states/*.meta.json

# 4. Verify ports are truly free
ss -tlnp | grep -E '854[5-8]' || echo 'All free'

# 5. Retry the blank fork
crucible fork --topology nomad_bridge

# If STILL failing, start anvil directly:
anvil --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8545 --chain-id 1 --auto-impersonate &
sleep 2
curl -s -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  --data '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"eth_blockNumber","params":[],"id":1}' \
  http://127.0.0.1:8545

Prevention: - Always clean cache before switching from archive forks to blank forks - Always kill orphaned processes before starting a new fork - The --accounts 0 --balance 0 issue is an Anvil nightly quirk — update Foundry or use a stable release

docs.title: First: Run These
docs.text:
# 1. Full environment check:
crucible doctor

# 2. List running chains:
crucible status

# 3. List plugins:
crucible plugin list

# 4. Validate config:
python -c "from crucible.config import load_config; load_config(); print('OK')"

docs.title: When an Experiment Fails
docs.text:
# 1. Check the run log:
cat logs/<experiment>/*/run.jsonl | grep failure

# 2. Validate evidence:
crucible plugin validate attacks/<attack_name>

# 3. Check integrity:
crucible verify logs/<experiment>/<run_id>

# 4. Compare with a known-good run:
crucible diff <failed_run> <known_good_run>

docs.title: When Docker Doesn't Work
docs.text:
# Check container status:
docker compose ps

# View orchestrator logs:
docker compose logs orchestrator --tail=30

# View Anvil logs:
docker compose logs anvil-ethereum --tail=30

# Test RPC connectivity:
docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c '
  curl -s --max-time 10 -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d "{\"jsonrpc\":\"2.0\",\"id\":1,\"method\":\"eth_blockNumber\",\"params\":[]}" \
    http://anvil-ethereum:8545
'

# Full reset:
docker compose down -v
docker compose build --no-cache orchestrator

docs.title: Check Disk Usage
docs.text:
crucible status --disk
# Or inside Docker:
docker compose run --rm orchestrator status --disk

docs.title: 9. Error Codes & Exception Hierarchy
docs.text: Crucible defines a typed exception hierarchy for programmatic error handling. Each exception carries both a message and an exit code.

docs.title: Exit Code Reference
docs.text: Exit Code Python Exception Meaning Common Cause 0 — Success — 1 CrucibleError General failure Experiment crashed, unknown error 2 ConfigError Config invalid Bad YAML, wrong field types 3 BaselineMismatch Baseline mismatch State changed, different provider/block 4 — Command failed Subprocess returned non-zero 5 EnvironmentError Environment invalid Missing Foundry, Python version 6 RpcError RPC unavailable Empty env var, provider down 7 AssertionFailed Assertion failed Attack plugin's verify() returned False 8 PluginInvalid Plugin invalid Bad manifest, missing class 9 PublicationGateFailed Publication gate failed Evidence/baseline/provenance incomplete 10 EvidenceIncomplete Evidence incomplete Missing required evidence sections 11 ProvenanceIncomplete Provenance incomplete Git commit, config fingerprint missing 130 — Interrupted Ctrl+C or SIGINT

docs.title: Exception Hierarchy
docs.text:
CrucibleError (base, exit_code=1)
├── ConfigError (exit_code=2)
│   └── Raised when: YAML parse error, validation error, missing file
├── BaselineMismatch (exit_code=3)
│   └── Raised when: Baseline file hash doesn't match, live state differs from baseline
├── EnvironmentError (exit_code=5)
│   └── Raised when: anvil not on PATH, Python too old, port in use
├── RpcError (exit_code=6)
│   └── Raised when: RPC provider unreachable, rate limited, connection timeout
├── AssertionFailed (exit_code=7)
│   └── Raised when: Attack plugin's verify() returns False, scenario assert fails
├── PluginInvalid (exit_code=8)
│   └── Raised when: Missing plugin.yaml, wrong class, bad manifest
├── PublicationGateFailed (exit_code=9)
│   └── Raised when: --publication-mode gate checks fail (fidelity, baseline, provenance)
├── EvidenceIncomplete (exit_code=10)
│   └── Raised when: evidence.yaml missing required sections
└── ProvenanceIncomplete (exit_code=11)
    └── Raised when: Git commit not available, config fingerprint incomplete

docs.title: When to Catch Specific Exceptions
docs.text:
from crucible.errors import (
    CrucibleError, ConfigError, BaselineMismatch, RpcError,
    AssertionFailed, PluginInvalid, EvidenceIncomplete,
)

# Example: Gracefully handle a missing RPC provider
try:
    chain_manager.start_topology(topo)
except RpcError as exc:
    print(f"RPC provider unavailable: {exc}")
    # Fall back to blank fork or use a different provider

# Example: Catch and log baseline mismatches
try:
    baselines.verify("my_scenario")
except BaselineMismatch as exc:
    print(f"Baseline changed — recapture: {exc}")
    baselines.capture("my_scenario", topo)

Still stuck? Check the detailed guide for your scenario: - User Guide — Getting started - Operations Runbook — Daily ops - CLI Reference — All flags - RPC Provider Guide — Provider setup - Reproducibility Guide — Determinism checks - Deployment Guide — SSH deployment

docs.title: Tutorial: Reproduce the Nomad Bridge Hack in 10 Minutes
docs.text:
What you'll accomplish: Fork Ethereum at block 15,259,100, run the Nomad Bridge Replica exploit, verify the forged transaction was accepted, confirm the reproduction is deterministic across two runs, and export a verifiable publication bundle.

Total time: ~10 minutes (2 min setup + 5 min first run + 3 min second run) Dependencies: Docker (or Python 3.11+ + Foundry)

docs.title: Before You Start
docs.text:
Make sure you have an Ethereum archive RPC endpoint. If you don't have one, create a free account at LlamaNodes — their free tier works for all Crucible experiments.

  1. Clone the repo and configure RPC
git clone <repo-url> && cd crucible
cp .env.example .env
  1. Edit .env and set your archive RPC URL:
ETH_RPC_URL=https://eth.llamarpc.com   # or your provider
  1. Build the Docker image (one-time, ~3 min):

bash docker compose build> Local alternative: If you have Python 3.11+ and Foundry installed,

run uv sync && source .venv/bin/activate && crucible init instead.

docs.title: Docker Caveats
docs.text:
Fork block management: Different experiments need different fork blocks. The shared testbed.yaml has one block per chain. Override per experiment using testbed.local.yaml (gitignored, deep-merged):

# testbed.local.yaml — overrides testbed.yaml chains section
chains:
  ethereum:
    fork_block: 15259100  # Nomad Bridge fork block

Stale Anvil meta files: When switching topologies or re-forking, old .meta.json files in the states/ Docker volume cause connection errors. Always clean before forking:

rm -f states/*.meta.json

Chaining commands requires --entrypoint bash: The orchestrator's entrypoint is uv run crucible, not a shell. To run multiple commands (fork + experiment), use:

docker compose run --rm --entrypoint bash orchestrator -c "
  rm -f states/*.meta.json && \
  uv run crucible fork --topology single && \
  uv run crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml --no-baseline
"

Streamlit not available in orchestrator: The crucible doctor check for streamlit will fail — this is expected. Streamlit runs in a separate dashboard service (docker compose up -d dashboard), not in the orchestrator.

.venv/CACHEDIR.TAG warning: A benign error about removing /workspace/.venv/CACHEDIR.TAG may appear on startup. It occurs because the host's .venv is mounted into the container. Safe to ignore.

docs.title: Step 1: Validate your environment
docs.text:
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor

Expected output (all checks pass):

Checks:
  [ok] Python imports — all present
  [ok] Foundry — forge 1.0.0 (stable)
  [ok] Config — testbed.yaml loaded
  [ok] RPC — ETH_RPC_URL resolves
  [ok] Directories — workspace, logs, datasets, states
  [ok] Docker — running

If any check fails, fix the issue before continuing. The most common failure is a missing or incorrect ETH_RPC_URL in your .env file.

docs.title: Step 2: Fork Ethereum and run the exploit
docs.text:
