docs.title: 19. Container Strategy
docs.text:
Docker Compose for local development, CI, and shared-machine deployment. Kubernetes deferred to V2.

Primary docker-compose.yml: 

# docker-compose.yml
services:
  orchestrator:
    build: .
    volumes:
      - .:/workspace
      - ./states:/workspace/states
      - ./logs:/workspace/logs
      - ./.fork_cache:/workspace/.fork_cache    # shared fork cache
    depends_on:
      anvil-ethereum:
        condition: service_healthy
      anvil-polygon:
        condition: service_healthy
    env_file: .env

  anvil-ethereum:
    image: ghcr.io/foundry-rs/foundry:nightly-cafc2606a2187a42b236df4aa65f4e8cdfcea970
    command: >
      anvil
      --fork-url ${ETH_RPC_URL}
      --fork-block-number ${ETH_FORK_BLOCK}
      --host 0.0.0.0
      --port 8545
      --chain-id 1
      --gas-price 0
      --block-base-fee-per-gas 1000000000
      --fork-cache-path /cache/ethereum
    ports: ["8545:8545"]
    volumes:
      - fork_cache:/cache
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "cast", "bn", "--rpc-url", "http://localhost:8545"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10
      start_period: 30s
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 8G
        reservations:
          memory: 2G

  anvil-polygon:
    image: ghcr.io/foundry-rs/foundry:nightly-cafc2606a2187a42b236df4aa65f4e8cdfcea970
    command: >
      anvil
      --fork-url ${POLYGON_RPC_URL}
      --fork-block-number ${POLYGON_FORK_BLOCK}
      --host 0.0.0.0
      --port 8546
      --chain-id 137
      --gas-price 0
      --block-base-fee-per-gas 1000000000
      --fork-cache-path /cache/polygon
    ports: ["8546:8546"]
    volumes:
      - fork_cache:/cache
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "cast", "bn", "--rpc-url", "http://localhost:8546"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10
      start_period: 30s
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 8G
        reservations:
          memory: 2G

volumes:
  fork_cache:
    driver: local

Per-researcher port override (docker-compose.override.yml — gitignored): 

# docker-compose.override.yml — each researcher creates their own
# Researcher Bob uses port range 9545-9546 to avoid colliding with Alice's 8545-8546
services:
  anvil-ethereum:
    ports: ["9545:8545"]
  anvil-polygon:
    ports: ["9546:8546"]

Key design decisions: - Health checks use cast bn (block number) — a lightweight RPC call that confirms Anvil is fully forked and responsive. The orchestrator container waits for service_healthy before starting. - Memory limits (8 GB per Anvil) prevent a single forked chain from consuming all host RAM. Adjust based on available memory. - Shared fork cache volume (fork_cache) is shared between Anvil services and persists across container restarts. This eliminates redundant RPC calls on re-fork. - Override files are .gitignored — each researcher creates their own docker-compose.override.yml for port and resource customisation. - Foundry image pinning — the compose file uses the exact same Foundry commit hash as foundry.toml. Never use :latest.

Note: Docker Compose deploy.resources requires docker compose v2. If using the legacy docker-compose v1, move resource limits into mem_limit syntax.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Core Infrastructure (Start here)
docs.text:
• chain_manager.py — start, stop, snapshot, restore, all cheatcodes
  • testbed.yaml parsing with pydantic validation
  • Basic CLI: init, fork, status, clean, status --disk
  • Three-tier reset model
  • Golden baseline fingerprinting and verification
  • LogEmitter with all five JSONL streams
  • log_integrity.json generation
  • Determinism guarantees — gas price pinning, timestamp control, automine enforcement (§2.1)
  • RPC provider registry with fallback chain and rate limiting (§5.1)
  • Fork cache layer using Anvil's --fork-cache-path (§5.2)
  • Graceful shutdown — signal handling, PID file hygiene, orphan detection (§22.1)
  • uv for dependency management — pyproject.toml + uv.lock (§1.1)
  • Unit test suite baseline
  • Exit code contract implemented

docs.title: Phase 2 — Scenario Execution & Modularity
docs.text:
• crucible/interfaces/ — all four abstract bases
  • PluginRegistry — discovery, validation, loading
  • scenario_engine.py — all three execution modes
  • Bridge registry (bridges.yaml)
  • Topology profiles (single, dual, bridge)
  • Baseline verification gating before every run
  • Run artifact bundle generation
  • ABI registry — bundled ABIs for all 6 benchmark bridges, auto-decode in events.jsonl (§10.7)
  • Experiment checkpoint & resume — phase-level snapshots, crucible experiment resume (§4.8)
  • Storage cleanup policies — auto-cleanup in testbed.yaml, crucible clean --logs-older-than (§7.1)
  • Integration test suite

docs.title: Phase 3 — Research Tooling
docs.text:
• experiment_runner.py — scenario/attack separation
  • Dataset versioning (DS-YYYY-NNNNN)
  • Run provenance manifest with exit_code
  • crucible diff command
  • crucible export <run_id> with REPRODUCE.md
  • All 6 benchmark attacks implemented, validated, and passing scenario tests
  • Slot maps for all benchmark contracts
  • Patch validation workflow
  • Experiment tagging & index — SQLite index, crucible history command (§12.1)
  • Research analysis module — crucible.analysis pandas helpers, starter Jupyter notebook (§11.2)
  • report.md auto-generation — publication-quality experiment summaries (§11)
  • crucible estimate — dry-run with RPC call cost estimation

docs.title: Phase 4 — Automation & Polish
docs.text:
• Matrix runner with parallel execution
  • Streamlit UI (dashboard, live logs, diff viewer)
  • CI integration templates (GitHub Actions)
  • crucible plugin validate command
  • Notification hooks — webhook/command notifiers on run completion/failure (§16.1)
  • Multi-researcher workspace isolation — testbed.local.yaml, port allocation, lock files (§17.5)
  • crucible abi fetch — pull verified ABIs from Etherscan/Sourcify
  • Docker Compose with health checks, resource limits, per-researcher overrides (§19)
  • Full developer documentation (docs/)

docs.title: 21. Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
The testbed is complete when all of the following are true:

  1. All 6 benchmark attacks satisfy their declared fidelity targets and evidence assertions.
  2. Soft reset completes in under 5 seconds
  3. Hard reset completes in under 5 minutes
  4. Baseline verification catches any state drift and exits with code 3
  5. Adding a new attack requires zero changes to core code — drop a folder, run crucible plugin validate
  6. A researcher can switch from single-chain to full-bridge topology with one flag change
  7. Every formal run produces a complete artifact bundle including log_integrity.json
  8. All experiments are reproducible across machines given the same foundry.toml, uv.lock, and RPC
  9. The matrix runner executes parameter sweeps fully unattended
  10. crucible export <run_id> produces a bundle sufficient for independent reproduction
  11. pytest tests/unit/ tests/integration/ passes with zero external dependencies
  12. All JSONL output passes schema compliance validation on every run
  13. Determinism is enforced — gas price, timestamps, and block base fees are pinned; two runs of the same experiment produce identical canonicalized state diffs.
  14. Crash recovery works — interrupting a run with Ctrl+C and resuming with crucible experiment resume completes the experiment with no data loss
  15. Multi-researcher isolation — two researchers can run experiments simultaneously on the same machine without port conflicts or state corruption
  16. Notifications fire — webhook/command notifiers trigger on run success, failure, and crash
  17. Disk cleanup is automated — crucible clean --logs-older-than 30d correctly removes old data; crucible status --disk reports accurate usage
  18. Events are decoded — events.jsonl contains human-readable event names and argument values for all 6 benchmark bridge contracts

docs.title: 22. Technical Gotchas
docs.text:
Archive node required. RPC endpoints must support eth_getBlockByNumber with full historical state. Crucible validates this on startup and exits with code 5 if not satisfied.

Port conflicts. Chain Manager reads ports from testbed.yaml. If a port is occupied, it exits with code 5 and prints the PID of the conflicting process.

Chain ID uniqueness. Every Anvil instance must have a unique --chain-id. Without this, cast and web3.py will route transactions to the wrong chain silently. Always set explicitly in testbed.yaml.

State file size. anvil_dumpState outputs 100–500 MB per chain. The states/ directory is .gitignored. Only baseline YAML fingerprints (a few KB) are committed.

Time manipulation. evm_increaseTime behaviour varies across Foundry versions. Pin the version in foundry.toml and use the same pin in Docker Compose. Never run foundryup without a version hash.

Bootstrap must use cheatcodes. bootstrap.py must not rely on DEX liquidity, price feeds, or any external state that may not exist at the fork block. Use anvil_setBalance, anvil_impersonateAccount, anvil_setCode, and anvil_setStorageAt exclusively for setup operations.

Fork cache invalidation. Changing fork_block in testbed.yaml does not automatically invalidate .fork_cache/. Delete the relevant cache entries manually or run crucible clean --cache.

Foundry --broadcast against local Anvil. The scenario engine sets ETH_RPC_URL to the Anvil URL before any forge script invocation. Never hardcode RPC URLs in Forge scripts — always read from the environment.

anvil_dumpState vs evm_snapshot. These are different mechanisms. evm_snapshot returns an integer ID and lives only in memory — it is lost if Anvil restarts. anvil_dumpState writes to disk and survives restarts. Crucible uses anvil_dumpState for all persistent baseline and named snapshots.

Log schema compliance. If a new log field is added, update crucible/logging/schema.py before opening a PR. The schema test runs on every CI build — a missing field definition will break the build.

RPC rate limiting. Free-tier RPC providers (LlamaRPC, Ankr, PublicNode) enforce 10–25 requests/second. During initial fork, Anvil issues hundreds of eth_getStorageAt calls. If the provider returns 429 (Too Many Requests), Anvil silently retries but slows to a crawl. Use --fork-retry-backoff and configure the provider rate limit in testbed.yaml (see §5.1).

Orphaned Anvil processes. If the orchestrator crashes (segfault, OOM, Ctrl+C during startup), Anvil subprocesses may survive as orphans. They hold ports and consume RAM indefinitely. Always run crucible status before starting a new session — it detects orphans via PID files. See §22.1 for the cleanup protocol.

ABI discovery for event decoding. The logger decodes contract events using ABIs from abis/<protocol>/. If no ABI is found for a contract address, events are logged as raw hex with decoded: false. This is not an error — but decoded events are vastly more useful for research. Always add ABIs for new bridge contracts before running experiments. See §10.7.

Disk space exhaustion. A single anvil_dumpState produces 100–500 MB. With 6 benchmark attacks × multiple snapshots × 4–10 researchers, the states/ directory can exceed 50 GB. Monitor with crucible status --disk and configure auto-cleanup policies in testbed.yaml. See §7.1.

Port conflicts between researchers. On a shared machine, two researchers running crucible setup with default ports (8545, 8546) will collide. Each researcher must configure unique ports via testbed.local.yaml (see §17.5) or use the Docker-based isolation strategy.

docs.title: 22.1 Graceful Shutdown & Orphan Cleanup
docs.text: Purpose: Ensure that Crucible always cleans up after itself — whether a run succeeds, fails, or crashes mid-experiment.

docs.title: Signal Handling
docs.text:
The CLI orchestrator must trap the following signals:

Signal Trigger Crucible Behavior SIGINT Ctrl+C Stop current step, flush logs, save checkpoint, kill Anvil processes, exit 130 SIGTERM kill <pid>, Docker stop Same as SIGINT — graceful shutdown SIGKILL kill -9, OOM killer Cannot trap — orphan cleanup handles this case on next startup

Shutdown sequence (on SIGINT/SIGTERM): 

1. Set global shutdown flag — no new steps will start
2. Wait for current step to complete (max 10 seconds, then force-kill)
3. Flush all LogEmitter writers
4. Save checkpoint snapshot if experiment was in progress
   → states/checkpoints/<run_id>/interrupted_phase_<N>.json
5. Log a "run_interrupted" entry to run.jsonl with exit reason
6. Kill all managed Anvil subprocesses (SIGTERM, then SIGKILL after 5s)
7. Remove PID files
8. Write partial run_manifest.json with outcome: "interrupted"
9. Exit with code 130

Implementation: 

# crucible/shutdown.py
import signal
import sys

class GracefulShutdown:
    def __init__(self):
        self._shutdown_requested = False
        signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, self._handler)
        signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, self._handler)

    def _handler(self, signum, frame):
        if self._shutdown_requested:
            # Second signal — force exit
            sys.exit(1)
        self._shutdown_requested = True

    @property
    def requested(self) -> bool:
        return self._shutdown_requested

docs.title: Orphan Process Detection & Cleanup
docs.text:
On every crucible status and crucible setup, the Chain Manager checks for orphaned Anvil processes:

1. Scan states/*.meta.json for PID files
2. For each PID file:
   a. Check if the process is alive (os.kill(pid, 0))
   b. If alive but no active Crucible session owns it → orphan
   c. If dead but PID file exists → stale PID file
3. Report findings:
   [warn] Orphaned Anvil process: PID 12345 (ethereum, port 8545) — started 3 hours ago
   [warn] Stale PID file: states/polygon.meta.json — process no longer running
4. Offer cleanup:
   crucible clean --orphans    # kill orphaned processes + remove stale PID files

Orphan cleanup is also run automatically at the start of crucible setup. If orphans are found: - In interactive mode: prompt the researcher to confirm cleanup - In non-interactive mode (--yes flag or CI): clean automatically and log a warning

docs.title: Lock File for Concurrent Access
docs.text:
To prevent two researchers from accidentally running experiments against the same Anvil instances:

.crucible.lock — created by crucible setup, removed by crucible clean
Contents: { "researcher": "alice", "pid": 54321, "started": "2026-06-05T10:00:00Z" }

If crucible setup finds an existing lock file: - Check if the owning process is alive - If alive: abort with message "Testbed is locked by alice (PID 54321, started 2 hours ago). Run crucible clean first or use a separate workspace." - If dead: warn and auto-clean the stale lock

docs.title: 23. Benchmark Target Suite
docs.text:
These six attacks are the acceptance test for the platform. All must replay deterministically according to their declared fidelity levels before V1 is declared complete.

Attack Bridge Target Fidelity Modeled Components Known Limitations for V1 Guardian signature bypass Wormhole L2 / L4 EVM-side logic, forged VAAs Solana/Core bridge logic simulated Replica fraud message Nomad L2 Flawed validation, replica processing Relayer latency excluded Private key compromise Poly Network L2 On-chain execution via compromised key Key extraction process excluded Multi-sig validator drain Horizon (Harmony) L2 Multisig threshold execution Off-chain multisig signing excluded Router contract exploit Multichain L2 Router contract permit exploit - Token hub vulnerability BNB Bridge L2 BSC token hub execution Non-EVM proof generation simulated

Each attack has a corresponding directory in attacks/ following the pack structure in Section 8. All six are implemented and validated in Phase 3.

Crucible — forge conditions, find the breaking point.

docs.title: A Modular Blockchain Bridge Security Research Testbed
docs.text: Research Group Project | Status: In Development | Version: 1.0

docs.title: What We Are Building
docs.text:
Crucible is a reproducible, CLI-first research testbed for systematic security analysis of blockchain bridges. It enables controlled replay of historical bridge exploits, structured telemetry collection, and patch validation — all from a single, version-controlled configuration.

The primary research objective is to create a rigorous, repeatable experimental platform for studying the attack surface of cross-chain bridge protocols, with a target benchmark suite of six major historical exploits totalling over $1.9 billion in real-world losses.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text:
Cross-chain bridge protocols represent the most financially damaging category of smart contract exploits to date. Despite this, reproducible research infrastructure for studying these attacks is essentially non-existent. Researchers typically reconstruct attacks manually against live testnets — a process that is slow, non-deterministic, and impossible to compare across experiments.

Crucible addresses this gap by providing: - Exact historical state reconstruction via block-level EVM forking (Foundry/Anvil) - A verified baseline system that guarantees experiment starting conditions are identical across runs and machines - Structured telemetry that produces publication-quality datasets from every run

docs.title: Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    UI["CLI / Streamlit UI"]
    Core["Orchestration Core<br>Scenario Engine • Experiment Runner"]

    subgraph Execution[" "]
        direction LR
        C1["Chain A<br>(Anvil)"]
        C2["Chain B<br>(Anvil)"]
        B1["Bridge<br>(optional)"]
    end

    BL["Golden Baseline System<br>Fingerprinted, verified per run"]
    SL["Structured Logger<br>JSONL • Events • Diffs • Manifest"]

    UI --> Core
    Core --> C1
    Core --> C2
    Core --> B1
    C1 --> BL
    C2 --> BL
    B1 --> BL
    BL --> SL

    style Execution fill:transparent,stroke:transparent

The architecture is modular and composable. Researchers select a topology profile — single chain, dual chain, or full bridge — and the system assembles only the necessary components. Adding a new attack requires dropping a folder into the attacks/ directory; no core code changes.

docs.title: Key Design Properties
docs.text:
Reproducibility. Every experiment is defined entirely in YAML and anchored to a specific git commit, Foundry version, fork block, and baseline fingerprint. Given these four things, any run can be independently reproduced on any machine.

Immutable Baselines. Before every experiment, Crucible computes a cryptographic fingerprint of the chain state (block number, state hash, balance hash, storage hash) and compares it against a stored golden baseline. If they do not match, the experiment aborts. This guarantees that every experiment starts from an identical, verified state.

One-click Reset. Resetting to the clean baseline takes under 5 seconds (Anvil state restore) without restarting any process. This is essential for rapid iteration across experiment phases.

Scenario-as-Code. Attacks, environments, and experiments are version-controlled artifacts — not shell scripts. Every run produces a run_manifest.json embedding git commit, scenario version, baseline fingerprint, and researcher identity. This is the foundation for publication-quality provenance.

docs.title: Research Outputs Per Experiment Run
docs.text:
Every run automatically produces:

Artifact Contents run.jsonl Timestamped, structured log of every action events.jsonl Decoded contract events (transfers, bridge messages) storage_diffs.jsonl Per-transaction storage slot changes run_manifest.json Full provenance record — git, versions, fingerprint, outcome report.md Auto-generated human-readable summary

These outputs are designed for direct ingestion into pandas, jq, or any analytics pipeline. The dataset versioning system assigns each run a monotonic ID (DS-2026-NNNNN) for unambiguous citation in research notes and publications.

docs.title: Benchmark Target Suite
docs.text:
The platform is considered complete when all six attacks replay deterministically:

Exploit Bridge Year Reported Loss Guardian signature bypass Wormhole 2022 $326M Replica fraud message Nomad 2022 $190M Private key compromise Poly Network 2021 $611M Multi-sig validator drain Horizon (Harmony) 2022 $100M Router contract exploit Multichain 2023 $125M Token hub vulnerability BNB Bridge 2022 $570M

These cases collectively cover the main bridge attack categories: signature/validation bypass, message fraud, key management failure, and smart contract logic errors.

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text: Layer Technology EVM forking & simulation Foundry / Anvil Smart contract scripting Forge scripts (Solidity) Orchestration & CLI Python (Typer) Structured logging structlog → JSONL Container environment Docker Compose UI wrapper Streamlit (Phase 4) Version control Git — all scenarios, experiments, and baselines

docs.title: Development Phases
docs.text: Phase Scope Outcome 1 — Core Chain Manager, CLI, baseline system, 3-tier reset, structured logging Running forks with verified baselines 2 — Scenarios Scenario Engine, topology profiles, bridge engine, experiment runner Executable attack scenarios 3 — Research Dataset versioning, plugin SDK, all 6 benchmark attacks Publication-ready experiment outputs 4 — Polish Matrix runner, Streamlit UI, CI integration, export command Fully automated research pipeline

docs.title: Scope Boundaries
docs.text:
In scope for V1: Foundry/Anvil integration, historical EVM chain forks, multi-chain environments, bridge simulation, attack replay, snapshot management, structured telemetry, dataset generation, patch validation.

Deferred to V2: Kubernetes deployment, real-time production monitoring, AI-assisted analysis, non-EVM chains (Solana, Cosmos, Aptos).

Crucible — controlled conditions for finding where things break.

docs.title: Crucible Agent System — Phases 4–6 Review
docs.text: Date: June 2026 Status: Ready for peer review

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary
docs.text:
Over Phases 4–6 we built an autonomous AI agent system for the Crucible blockchain security research testbed. The system lets LLM-driven agents:

  1. Explore forked blockchain environments through a privileged tool API
  2. Formulate and execute exploit strategies autonomously
  3. Verify outcomes against configurable acceptance criteria

The system supports OpenAI-compatible APIs (including local Ollama instances), Anthropic Claude, and any custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint. All LLM communication uses raw urllib to avoid heavy SDK dependencies.

Codebase impact: ~1,600 new lines of Python across 6 new files, 75 new unit tests, 449 total tests passing.

docs.title: 2.1 Agent Core (Phase 4 — crucible/agent_tools.py, crucible/sdk.py)
docs.text: Component Purpose Status AgentPlugin ABC Abstract base class with observe → think → act loop, verify, prepare, respond lifecycle hooks [ok] Done AgentContext Wraps ExperimentContext with privilege enforcement, tool history/audit, parameter filtering [ok] Done 9 agent tools chain_id, get_balance, get_code, get_storage, call_contract, get_logs, impersonate, send_transaction, advance_time [ok] Done 3 privilege levels low (read-only), medium (impersonate + send), high (full access incl. time manipulation) [ok] Done Tool security Parameter filtering against JSON Schema; privilege gating on every tool call [ok] Done run_config wiring ExperimentContext.run_config carries experiment YAML's run: block; agent privilege, allowed accounts, objective, task all configurable per experiment [ok] Done

docs.title: 2.2 Scenario Steps (Phase 5 — crucible/scenario_steps.py)
docs.text: Step Type Purpose Status verify_tracing Reads per-chain artifact JSONL streams and validates event/gas/storage/balance data was captured correctly; supports per-chain minimum thresholds and optional cross-stream consistency checks [ok] Done bridge_action Four sub-actions: send_message, relay_messages (via bridge_plugin.connect()), check_delivery (log-only), verify_state (log-only) [ok] Done BridgePlugin.send_message() Default method added to BridgePlugin ABC; subclasses override for real bridge-specific message sending [ok] Done

docs.title: 2.3 LLM Provider Integrations (Phase 6 — crucible/llm_providers.py)
docs.text: Provider Backend Auth Default Model Status OpenAI urllib → POST /v1/chat/completions OPENAI_API_KEY env var gpt-4o [ok] Done Anthropic urllib → POST /v1/messages ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var claude-sonnet-4-20250514 [ok] Done Ollama OpenAI-compatible → http://localhost:11434/v1 None (auto-detected localhost) llama3.1 [ok] Done Local/Custom Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint Optional local-model [ok] Done

docs.title: 2.4 LLM Agent Loop (Phase 6 — crucible/agent_loop.py)
docs.text: Component Purpose Status LLMAgentPlugin Concrete AgentPlugin that uses an LLM provider for tool-calling decisions [ok] Done System prompt builder Constructs prompts with tool descriptions (name, params, defaults), task, objective, constraints, output format [ok] Done Observation prompt builder Builds user messages from agent state (privilege level, tool history, results) [ok] Done Response parser Handles OpenAI function_calls, Anthropic tool_use, and plain-text "done" JSON signal [ok] Done Conversation management Truncates at 50 messages (keeps system prompt + last 49), retries once on transient LLM failures [ok] Done tool_def_to_openai() Converts AgentContext tool definitions to OpenAI-compatible tool format with correct required/optional parameter handling [ok] Done

docs.title: 2.5 Configuration Model
docs.text:
Agent behaviour is configured via the experiment YAML's run: block:

# experiment.yaml
name: agent_exploit
scenario: wormhole_pre_exploit
attack: my_agent_plugin         # or "LLMAgentPlugin" for default
topology: single
run:
  agent_privilege: medium       # low | medium | high
  agent_provider: openai        # openai | anthropic | ollama | local | custom
  agent_model: gpt-4o           # model name override
  agent_base_url: ""            # optional, for custom endpoints
  agent_objective: "Drain the bridge vault..."
  agent_task: "The Wormhole bridge contract is deployed at 0x..."
  agent_allowed_accounts:
    - 0xattacker1
    - 0xattacker2

docs.title: 3. Review Feedback from SOM's Team — Our Responses & Rationale
docs.text:
Two comprehensive reviews were provided by SOM and his team before Phases 4–6 began:

  1. crucible_assessment_and_critique.md — System-level assessment, gap analysis, critique of the plan, architectural suggestions
  2. crucible_code_quality_review.md — Code-level quality review with scored dimensions and prioritized recommendations

Below we document every substantial piece of feedback, whether we accepted it, what we changed, and why when we chose a different path.

docs.title: 3.1 Feedback We Accepted and Implemented
docs.text: Feedback Source What We Changed "AgentPlugin system at 0% — entirely planned, nothing implemented" Assessment §2 (Req 3) Built the full agent system across Phases 4–6: AgentPlugin ABC, AgentContext, 9 tools, 3 privilege levels, LLMAgentPlugin, 154 new tests. "Agent tool layer / toolbox doesn't exist" Assessment §2 (Req 3) Built crucible/agent_tools.py with ToolDef, AgentAction, ToolResult, AgentContext, and 9 tool registrations. "Semantic auto-logging / ActionRecorder doesn't exist" Assessment §2 (Req 4) Each agent tool call is automatically logged via AgentContext.do() which emits structured audit records. Tool history is preserved across the agent loop. "Attack scaffolding CLI not started" Assessment §1 (Scorecard) Note: This was partially addressed (Phase 3 added crucible attack init before our Phases 4–6). The command exists but templates are basic. "No logging module usage — can't set CRUCIBLE_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG" Code Quality §11 (Smell 5) We added proper logging.getLogger(__name__) to all new files (llm_providers.py, agent_loop.py) and use logger.error() / logger.warning() instead of bare prints in agent loop code. The remaining codebase is not yet addressed. "Missing cost/budget awareness — no llm_budget config" Assessment §3 (Issue 6) Documented as a known gap in §4.6. The existing max_iterations cap (default 50) provides basic protection. A dedicated llm_budget field is deferred to future work.

docs.title: 3.2 Feedback We Partially Accepted
docs.text:
Feedback Source Our Response "Design the agent as a wrapper that drives AttackPlugin, not a separate plugin type" Assessment §4.3 Partially accepted. We kept LLMAgentPlugin as an AgentPlugin subclass (consistent with the existing AttackPlugin/BridgePlugin pattern), but the agent lifecycle IS designed as a wrapper: it receives tools from AgentContext, calls LLM, parses responses, executes tools, and feeds results back — conceptually equivalent to SOM's AgentDriver proposal. The key difference is we didn't make it drive an AttackPlugin instance because that would require the attack plugin to expose a tool-compatible interface, which AttackPlugin currently doesn't. This is a valid future integration path.

| "The agent needs DeFi-aware tools (deploy_contract, decode_events, get_token_balance, read_contract, flashloan)" | Assessment §3 (Issue 3) | Partially accepted. We built 9 raw EVM-level tools (get_balance, get_storage, call_contract, send_transaction, etc.) which cover the primitive operations. The tool system is designed to be extensible — new ToolDef entries can be added to AgentContext without changing the agent loop. DeFi-aware tools (token balance by symbol, ABI-driven contract reads, flashloan primitives) are best implemented as plugin-defined tools, not built into the core, because they require domain-specific ABI knowledge. We documented the tool extension pattern in the tool definitions.

| "Agent run reproducibility — reasoning chain, tool calls, decisions need to be logged as part of the artifact bundle" | Assessment §3 (Issue 4) | Accepted and implemented. The agent loop now writes a full reasoning chain to logs/<run>/agent_reasoning.jsonl after each think() step. Each record includes the observation prompt sent to the LLM, the action chosen (tool + parameters), a conversation snapshot (last 3 exchanges, truncated), and the step counter. This provides a replayable trace for reproducibility. Logged in LLMAgentPlugin._log_reasoning_step(). |

| "Ease of use should be the top priority — simplify the researcher workflow" | Assessment §4.1 | Partially accepted. We agree on the importance but prioritised building the agent engine first (it was the explicit requirement). The crucible attack init scaffolding (Phase 3) addressed some of this. A DeFiHackLabs-level experience (1 file → run) remains a downstream goal. |

docs.title: 3.3 Feedback We Did NOT Accept (with Rationale)
docs.text: Feedback Source Why We Went a Different Way "Put attack scaffolding (Phase 4) before the agent system (Phase 1)" — SOM said scaffolding should be Phase 1, agent Phase 4 Assessment §3 (Issue 1) We put the agent system first because it was the stated requirement (Req 3: "Agent plugin to plug AI agent into the testbed"). The scaffolding UX (crucible attack init) is important for adoption but was not the mandated requirement. We also believe the agent system is the higher-leverage capability — an agent can generate attack packs itself, making scaffolding less critical. We scheduled scaffolding improvements for the next phase. "Use Foundry (Forge) as the primary execution engine, not Python" — SOM proposed making the Python layer an orchestrator over Solidity execution Assessment §4.2 We intentionally chose Python-first. The reasons: (1) Python is the lingua franca of security research tooling — the existing web3.py ecosystem, Jupyter notebooks, and ML tooling all integrate with Python. (2) Our RPC proxy instrumentation is Python-native and captures artifacts from any execution method (Python, Solidity, cast, external tools) — switching to post-capture of Forge trace output would lose real-time feedback. (3) A hybrid approach (Python orchestration + optional Forge script execution) is possible but was outside scope. We agree that a Solidity-native path would unlock DeFiHackLabs users, and have documented it as a future consideration. "Treat bridges as topology-level configuration, not a plugin type" — SOM proposed moving bridge setup to declarative YAML Assessment §4.5 We kept bridges as plugins. The BridgePlugin lifecycle (deploy→configure→connect→destroy) is already integrated into the experiment runner and works. Moving bridge setup to YAML would require restructuring the entire lifecycle — it's a valid architectural opinion but a major refactor with no functional gain over the current approach. The YAML-as-topology pattern is complementary and could be added without removing the plugin system. "Make the RPC proxy optional in favour of post-capture trace processing" — SOM proposed tracing blocks after execution instead of real-time proxying Assessment §4.4 We kept the real-time proxy. The proxy provides immediate feedback for the agent loop (the LLM needs to see results before deciding the next action). Post-capture processing is a complementary approach for verification but cannot replace real-time feedback. The ability to support both is preserved in the architecture. "Live-state forking UX needs addressing" — SOM noted there's no fork_block: "latest" mode Assessment §2 (Req 1) Out of scope for Phases 4–6. This is a configuration-level change (5 lines per SOM's estimate) that affects the chain manager, not the agent system. It remains a valid improvement but was not part of the agent system requirements.

docs.title: 3.4 Code Quality Feedback — Addressed vs Deferred
docs.text:
The crucible_code_quality_review.md contained several P0–P1 code hygiene recommendations. Our response to each:

P0 Item Verdict Notes Replace all except Exception: pass with logger.debug(..., exc_info=True) 🟢 Done Fixed 6 modules with bare excepts: instrumentation.py (4 instances — the critical threaded proxy path), extract.py (simplified redundant double-catch), bridge_lifecycle.py (added missing logger, now logs properly). The conftest.py helper has a single remaining bare except in a test helper — acceptable low risk. Add import logging; logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) to all modules 🟢 Done comprehensively Verified across 24/24 core Python modules. bridge_lifecycle.py was missing logging entirely — fixed during this pass. Fix TokenBucket class-level state 🟢 Done set_budget() is now a no-op; budget tracking moved to RpcProviderManager instance level. Race condition eliminated. Fix _correlation_counter global 🟢 Done Both run() and resume() reset _correlation_counter = 0 at the start. SOM's review missed this — it was already fixed before the second review. P1 Item Verdict Notes Split utils.py 🟢 Done Split into 6 focused modules: hashing.py, provenance.py, filesystem.py, env.py, ids.py, and a slimmed utils.py (70 lines). This was the #1 code quality issue from the original review. Replace f-string code generation with Jinja2 templates 🔴 Deferred Affects extract.py only. Not related to the agent system. Narrow ExperimentContext — create AttackContext 🟡 Partially addressed AgentContext wraps ExperimentContext and provides a filtered, privilege-aware interface. This is conceptually similar to SOM's proposed AttackContext but scoped to the agent system. Add file lock to next_dataset_id 🔴 Deferred Affects ids.py. TOCTOU race is documented with a comment; fix deferred until parallel execution is needed.

docs.title: 3.5 Summary of What SOM Was Right About
docs.text:
Several of SOM's predictions proved accurate during implementation:

  1. Tool design matters. SOM warned that raw EVM tools are insufficient for complex exploits. Our testing confirmed that the agent struggles with multi-step exploits requiring ABI decoding or token balance reasoning — higher-level tools are needed.

  2. Agent reproducibility is hard. SOM predicted the reasoning chain would be ephemeral. We addressed this in the fix pass — now captured to agent_reasoning.jsonl per run. This was the right call.

  3. Researcher UX is the bottleneck. Even with the agent system wired into the runner, the workflow requires creating a plugin.yaml, experiment YAML, and agent subclass. SOM's emphasis on scaffolding and ease-of-use was correct.

docs.title: 3.6 Where We Believe SOM Was Wrong (or Our Context Differs)
docs.text:
1. The Foundry-first approach underestimates the instrumentation challenge. SOM's proposal of running forge test and post-processing traces would lose real-time feedback — critical for an agent loop that needs to observe transaction results before deciding the next action. The RPC proxy approach is not just "clever infrastructure" but a fundamental requirement for interactive agent-driven experimentation.

  2. Moving bridges to YAML doesn't solve the content problem. The bottleneck for bridge attacks isn't the lifecycle management (which works) — it's the lack of actual bridge contract implementations and relay simulations. Restructuring the lifecycle wouldn't produce working bridge attacks faster; building the contracts would.

  3. The agent-as-wrapper (driving AttackPlugin) overcomplicates the architecture. Creating yet another abstraction layer between the LLM and the testbed would make debugging harder (three layers of indirection: agent loop → wrapper → attack plugin → testbed). The direct plugin approach (LLMAgentPlugin is an AgentPlugin) is simpler to trace and debug.

docs.title: 4.1 Agent → Experiment Runner Integration 🟡 WIRED (Partial)
docs.text:
Item Status ExperimentRunner.run() now loads agent-type plugins [ok] Done — Added _load_plugin() method that auto-detects plugin type. If experiment.run.agent_provider is set, tries agent type first; otherwise falls back to attack type for backward compatibility. Both run() and resume() paths use this method. _load_plugin() logic [ok] If the preferred type fails, falls back to the other type. Works with any plugin in attacks/ or plugins/ directories — just set type: agent in plugin.yaml and agent_provider: openai in the experiment YAML. Auto-wire provider config from YAML 🔴 Not yet — remaining gap. LLMAgentPlugin.__init__() accepts provider_type, model, api_key as constructor args, but the experiment runner doesn't auto-read these from experiment.run_config at instantiation time. A subclass must currently hardcode its provider config or the user must pass it programmatically. A factory or wiring layer in _load_plugin() is needed to bridge this gap. Plugin discovery 🟡 Existing — PluginRegistry.search_dirs includes ["plugins", "attacks", "bridges"]. No dedicated "agents" directory needed; agent plugins live in attacks/ and are distinguished by their type: agent manifest field.

Impact: LLMAgentPlugin subclasses that hardcode their provider config (e.g., defaulting to Ollama) are triggerable through crucible experiment run <file.yaml>. Full YAML-driven provider configuration is a remaining gap.

docs.title: 4.2 Example Agent Attack Pack (NOT YET CREATED)
docs.text: Missing Item Why No example agent plugin in attacks/ No plugin.yaml + Python entry file demonstrating a concrete agent attack exists. This blocks users from seeing the pattern end-to-end. No scenario YAML with agent config No experiment YAML shows the agent run: config block in context.

docs.title: 4.3 Ollama Tool Calling Caveat (KNOWN LIMITATION)
docs.text:
Ollama's /v1/chat/completions endpoint supports tool/function calling on some models (llama3.1, mistral, codellama) but not all models. Testing with a model that doesn't support tool calling will produce empty or malformed tool call responses. The system will log an error and fall back to calling chain_id.

Workaround: Use a model known to support tool calling (e.g., llama3.1:70b, mistral:7b, qwen2.5:7b).

docs.title: 4.4 Anthropic Tool Result Format (KNOWN LIMITATION)
docs.text:
The respond() method appends tool results in OpenAI format (role: "tool" with tool_call_id). When using AnthropicProvider, this format does not match Anthropic's expected role: "user" with content: [{"type": "tool_result", ...}]. For pure-Anthropic usage, LLMAgentPlugin.respond() would need to be overridden.

Impact: The conversation history may not be perfectly readable by Claude, but tool calls and responses still function because the Anthropic API processes the user/assistant message structure.

docs.title: 4.5 No Streaming Support (BY DESIGN)
docs.text:
LLM responses are fetched synchronously. Streaming would improve perceived latency but adds complexity (SSE parsing, per-chunk aggregation). We use raw urllib to avoid SDK dependencies — streaming would require http.client or a third-party library.

Verdict: Acceptable for batch-style experiments where a single turn takes 1–5 seconds. Streaming is a future optimisation.

docs.title: 4.6 No Retry Budget / Cost Tracking (NOT YET IMPLEMENTED)
docs.text:
The system tracks total tokens per provider (_total_tokens) but does not enforce a budget. An agent could theoretically run 50 iterations of expensive model calls with no cost cap. The experiment YAML already has rpc_budget.max_usd_estimate — a similar llm_budget field would be needed.

Verdict: Low priority for V1; the max_iterations (default 50) cap limits worst-case cost.

docs.title: 4.7 Code Quality Items from SOM's Review [ok] MOSTLY DONE
docs.text: Nearly all P0/P1 code hygiene items from the code quality review have been addressed. See §3.4 above for the full updated table. The only remaining deferred items are: - Jinja2 template refactor (extract.py) — f-string code generation, not agent-system related - File lock on next_dataset_id — documented limitation, only matters under parallel execution

docs.title: 5.1 Raw urllib Instead of SDK Packages
docs.text:
Decision: Use urllib.request.urlopen instead of the openai or anthropic PyPI packages.

Rationale: - Avoids adding 2+ hard dependencies to pyproject.toml - Keeps the install footprint small (~50KB vs 5MB+) - Works offline / in air-gapped environments - Easy to audit (no black-box SDK internals)

Trade-off: No automatic retry, no connection pooling, no streaming. Acceptable for our use case.

docs.title: 5.2 Agent Plugin as a Plugin Type (Not a Wrapper Around AttackPlugin)
docs.text:
Decision: LLMAgentPlugin extends AgentPlugin as a first-class plugin type alongside AttackPlugin and BridgePlugin, rather than being a wrapper that drives an AttackPlugin instance.

Rationale: See §3.3 for the full discussion. In short: simpler architecture, consistent with existing patterns, easier to debug. SOM's recommended approach (agent-as-wrapper) was considered but rejected because it would require AttackPlugin to expose a tool-compatible interface that doesn't currently exist.

docs.title: 5.3 Python-First Execution (Not Foundry-First)
docs.text:
Decision: The agent system executes tools through Python (web3 RPC calls), not by generating and running Forge scripts.

Rationale: See §3.3 (decision) and §3.6 (detailed reasoning) for the full discussion. Real-time feedback is critical for the agent loop; Foundry's batch execution model doesn't support interactive decision-making. A hybrid approach is possible but was outside scope.

docs.title: 5.4 verify_state is Log-Only
docs.text:
Decision: bridge_action verify_state does not call bridge_plugin.reset() (which is documented as destructive — "Reset bridge state to post-deploy baseline").

Rationale: Using a destructive method for verification is semantically wrong. A future BridgePlugin.verify() method would provide real state checks.

docs.title: 5.5 LLMAgentPlugin is Instantiable Without Subclassing
docs.text:
Decision: LLMAgentPlugin has a default metadata() that uses the class name. This means LLMAgentPlugin() can be used directly, not just as a base class.

Rationale: Faster iteration during research — no need to define a subclass just to test provider integration.

docs.title: 6. Test Summary
docs.text: Test File Tests What's Covered tests/unit/test_agent.py 52 AgentContext construction, privilege, tool call, history, AgentPlugin lifecycle, loop tests/unit/test_scenario_steps.py 27 verify_tracing thresholds, consistency checks; bridge_action all 4 actions, error handling tests/unit/test_llm_providers.py 75 LLMResponse, OpenAIProvider (11 tests), AnthropicProvider (6), factory (9), tool conversion (4), truncation (4), prompt builders (10), response parsing (9), LLMAgentPlugin (16) Total (Phases 4–6) 154 Grand total 449 All tests pass

docs.title: 7. Next Steps / Recommended Priorities
docs.text:
1. 🔥 High — Create example agent plugin — Build attacks/llm_exploit_agent/ with plugin.yaml and a concrete LLMAgentPlugin subclass targeting Ollama by default. Include an experiment YAML with the run: config block.
  2. 🟡 Medium — User documentation — Add a "Running LLM Agents" page to the docs covering experiment YAML config, provider setup, and troubleshooting.
  3. 🟡 Medium — Add higher-level DeFi tools — Following SOM's suggestion and our own findings: get_token_balance(token, account), decode_events(tx_hash), deploy_contract(bytecode, abi). These significantly improve agent effectiveness on real exploit scenarios.
  4. 🟢 Low — Anthropic tool result format — Make respond() format tool results based on provider type for correct Anthropic conversation history.
  5. 🟢 Low — LLM budget enforcement — Add llm_budget.max_tokens config analogous to rpc_budget.max_requests_per_run.

docs.title: 8. Post-Review Fix Pass (June 2026)
docs.text:
Following SOM's second review (somandteam_review2.md), the following items were addressed in a single fix pass:

Item Files Changed get_docker_mode() duplication — removed from provenance.py, imports from env.py provenance.py DRY JsonRpcClient imports — extracted _get_client() helper, eliminated 9 inline deferred imports agent_tools.py Bare except fixes — 6 modules: instrumentation.py (4), extract.py (double catch), bridge_lifecycle.py (missing logger) instrumentation.py, extract.py, bridge_lifecycle.py conftest PID tracking — anvil cleanup now tracks PIDs from meta files, only kills processes started by the test tests/conftest.py Agent wired into runner — _load_plugin() auto-detects agent vs attack type, both run() and resume() paths updated experiment.py Reasoning chain capture — _log_reasoning_step() writes JSONL to logs/<run>/agent_reasoning.jsonl agent_loop.py Lazy imports — heavy modules use __getattr__ lazy loading instead of eager import __init__.py bridge_lifecycle.py missing import — added import logging bridge_lifecycle.py agent_loop.py None guard — handles context.workspace being None in unit tests agent_loop.py init.py dead code — removed unused _ExperimentContext, simplified lazy tuple format __init__.py Docs link test — added AGENT_SYSTEM_REVIEW.md reference to README README.md

Result: 449 tests passing (was 448).

docs.title: 9. Reference: File Map
docs.text:
crucible/
├── agent_tools.py      # ToolDef, AgentAction, ToolResult, AgentContext, 9 tools, _get_client() helper
├── agent_loop.py       # LLMAgentPlugin, prompt builders, response parser, reasoning chain logger

├── sdk.py              # AgentPlugin ABC (+ AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, ExperimentContext)
├── experiment.py       # ExperimentRunner.run() & resume(), _load_plugin() agent wiring
├── __init__.py         # Lazy imports via __getattr__ for heavy modules

tests/unit/
├── test_agent.py           # Phase 4 tests (52)
├── test_scenario_steps.py  # Phase 5 tests (27)
