tests/
└── conftest.py             # PID-tracking anvil cleanup fixture

This document is intended for engineering and research leadership review. For the detailed implementation specification, see crucible_implementation_spec.md and docs/ARCHITECTURE.md.

docs.title: Architectural Review of Crucible Strategic Plan
docs.text: Reviewer perspective: Systems architect + USENIX Security program committee Date: June 8, 2026

docs.title: 1. The Threat Model Is Implicit — It Needs to Be Explicit
docs.text:
The strategic plan says Crucible is "infrastructure, not a framework" and that the testbed "proves" fork state matches claims. But the document never states what Crucible actually guarantees and what it doesn't.

Every research instrument needs a clear threat model. A USENIX reviewer will ask: "What can go wrong between your fork and the real chain, and how do you know your reproduction is faithful?"

docs.title: What Crucible Actually Guarantees (EVM-level fidelity)
docs.text:
• [ok] State fidelity: The fork starts with identical storage, balance, and code as the real chain at block N (because Anvil fetches it from the archive node)
  • [ok] Execution fidelity: The EVM opcodes execute identically (Anvil uses the same EVM as Ethereum clients)
  • [ok] Gas accounting: Gas is metered identically (same opcode costs)
  • [ok] Event emission: Events are emitted from the same code paths

docs.title: What Crucible Does NOT Guarantee (protocol-level fidelity)
docs.text:
• [fail] Mempool dynamics: Anvil auto-mines with FIFO ordering. Real attacks may depend on MEV, front-running, or specific block inclusion order
  • [fail] Block timing: timestamp: fork_block freezes time. Real attacks may depend on block.timestamp changes across blocks
  • [fail] Cross-chain relay timing: The bridge plugin is a synchronous mock. Real bridge exploits depend on relay latency, guardian quorum timing, and message ordering
  • [fail] Consensus behavior: Anvil is a single-node devnet. Attacks that depend on reorgs, uncle blocks, or finality delays are not reproducible
  • [fail] External oracle state: Chainlink/TWAP oracles at the fork block reflect historical prices, not the prices that existed during the actual exploit window

This matters. The Wormhole exploit depended on guardian set validation. The Nomad exploit depended on a specific state of the Replica contract. The Poly Network exploit depended on cross-chain relay behavior. Crucible can reproduce the EVM-side effects of these attacks, but it cannot prove the full attack path was as described.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
Add a section to the strategic plan (and eventually to publication bundles) called "Fidelity Boundary" that explicitly states:

"Crucible reproduces the EVM execution of bridge exploits. It does not reproduce mempool ordering, cross-chain relay timing, consensus behavior, or external oracle dynamics. The fidelity level (L0–L4) describes how much of the original attack's EVM behavior is replayed, not the completeness of the end-to-end attack path."

This isn't a weakness — it's scientific honesty. A USENIX reviewer will respect this. They will NOT respect a tool that claims "faithful reproduction" without qualifying what "faithful" means.

docs.title: 2. The Instrumentation Proxy Has an Observability Boundary the Plan Doesn't Acknowledge
docs.text:
The plan's core claim is: "the researcher writes zero logging code — every transaction is auto-traced." This is true if and only if all transactions flow through the proxy.

Looking at the code (instrumentation.py:160-174), the proxy intercepts eth_sendTransaction and eth_sendRawTransaction. But:

docs.title: What the Proxy Captures
docs.text: Method Captured How eth_sendTransaction via proxy [ok] do_POST → _schedule_trace eth_sendRawTransaction via proxy [ok] Same path Read calls (eth_call, eth_getBalance) [fail] Forwarded, not traced Transactions sent by forge script --broadcast via proxy [ok] Forge uses the proxy as its RPC Transactions sent by cast send via proxy [ok] Same

docs.title: What the Proxy CANNOT Capture
docs.text:
Case Why Plugin opens a direct connection to Anvil (bypassing proxy) Plugin has the chain config, could connect directly Forge script uses vm.startBroadcast() with a hardcoded RPC URL Forge talks directly to Anvil Plugin uses a different web3 library with its own connection pool Connection won't go through the HTTPServer proxy Internal Anvil state changes (auto-impersonate, cheatcodes) These happen inside Anvil, not via RPC

The proxy's transparent URL substitution (runtime_for() returns proxy URL) makes the common case work. But there's no enforcement mechanism — a plugin CAN bypass it. The plan should acknowledge this and the artifacts_complete flag (item 1) should also check whether the transaction count in gas_profile.jsonl matches the transaction count in Anvil's block range.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
Add a post-run consistency check:

expected_tx_count = eth_getBlockByNumber(latest).transactions.length
                  - eth_getBlockByNumber(fork_block).transactions.length
actual_traced     = count(gas_profile.jsonl entries)
if expected_tx_count != actual_traced:
    artifacts_complete = false
    warning: "N transactions were not captured by instrumentation"

This closes the observability gap without requiring proxy enforcement.

docs.title: 3. The Bridge Simulation Model Is Architecturally Weak — and the Plan Doesn't Address It
docs.text:
This is the most significant architectural concern for a USENIX paper on bridge security.

Looking at the BridgePlugin interface (sdk.py:106-167):

class BridgePlugin(CruciblePlugin):
    def deploy(self, context) -> dict[str, str]: ...
    def send_message(self, context, source_chain, dest_chain, data, correlation_id) -> dict: ...

And the default send_message() implementation:

def send_message(self, context, source_chain, dest_chain, data, correlation_id=None):
    context.log.step(f"bridge send_message {source_chain} -> {dest_chain}", ...)
    return {"source_tx_hash": "0x", "correlation_id": correlation_id or ""}

The default bridge is a no-op stub that logs a message and returns. A real bridge plugin must be written by the researcher to simulate the actual bridge protocol. This means:

  1. The bridge behavior is whatever the researcher codes, not a replay of the real bridge
  2. There is no validation that the bridge plugin's behavior matches the real protocol
  3. The send_message() → correlation_id tracking is just bookkeeping, not protocol simulation

For the validated attacks (Nomad, Wormhole, Poly Network), this doesn't matter because the exploits happen on a single chain — the bridge is attacked from the Ethereum side only, and the cross-chain relay is irrelevant to the EVM execution. But the strategic plan says Crucible is for "bridge security research" — so the bridge model matters.

docs.title: The Problem for a Reviewer
docs.text:
A USENIX reviewer will ask: "You say you reproduce bridge exploits. But your bridge is a mock. How do you know the mock is faithful to the real protocol?"

For L2/L3 fidelity (single-chain fork), this question doesn't apply — the reviewer will accept it. For L4 fidelity (multi-chain + bridge), the question is fatal unless you can show the bridge plugin is validated against the real protocol.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
The plan should explicitly state that bridge plugins at L4 fidelity require: 1. Protocol specification reference — which bridge protocol is being simulated (Wormhole VAA format, Nomad Merkle proof format, etc.) 2. Behavioral assertions — the evidence.yaml should specify expected bridge behavior (e.g., "message is relayed within 1 block," "guardian quorum is 13/19") 3. Excluded components declaration — this already exists in L4 but should be expanded to include WHY each component is excluded and what impact the exclusion has on fidelity

This doesn't require code changes — it requires the evidence model to be more rigorous about what the bridge mock does and doesn't simulate.

docs.title: 4. The Diff Engine Proves Structural Equality, Not Semantic Equivalence
docs.text:
diff.py's compare_runs() (diff.py:86-104) compares: - Storage diffs (sorted by chain, contract, slot) - Events (sorted by chain, address, event_name, args) - Gas totals - Balance diffs

Two runs are "deterministic" if all four match. But this is structural equality, not semantic equivalence.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
Consider two runs of the same Nomad exploit: - Run A: attacker sends process(fraudulent_message) at fork_block + 1 - Run B: attacker sends process(fraudulent_message) at fork_block + 1

Both produce identical storage diffs, events, gas, and balances. The diff engine says "deterministic." [ok]

Now consider: - Run A: attacker sends process(message_A) — drains 100 ETH - Run B: attacker sends process(message_B) — drains 100 ETH, different message but same effect

Both produce the same balance diffs (100 ETH drained) but different storage diffs (different message hash in Replica's messages mapping). The diff engine catches this. [ok]

But: - Run A: attacker drains 100 ETH via one tx - Run B: attacker drains 50 + 50 ETH via two txs

Different gas totals, different event counts, different storage diffs — but semantically the same exploit. The diff engine says "not deterministic." [fail]

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text: The diff engine is fine for its intended purpose (proving determinism of identical runs). But the plan should not overload it for crucible verify (item 6). Verification should compare artifact hashes (from log_integrity.json), not semantic equivalence. The plan already implies this, but it should be explicit: verify = hash-compare, not diff-compare.

docs.title: 5. The Fidelity Level System Needs Formal Definitions
docs.text:
The current fidelity levels (evidence.py) are:

Level Name Enforcement L0 Narrative only No code required L1 Conceptual No additional checks L2 Fork-state behavioral Must have chains + affected_contracts L3 Exact transaction replay Must have exploit_transactions L4 Multi-chain full Must have excluded_components

The problem: These levels describe what metadata is present, not what behavior is demonstrated. An L3 attack that lists exploit_transactions in evidence.yaml but doesn't actually replay them is still classified as L3. There's no runtime check that the declared fidelity matches the actual execution.

docs.title: What a USENIX Reviewer Expects
docs.text:
A fidelity taxonomy should define: 1. What was replayed — the exact transactions, the contract deployment, or just the vulnerability concept 2. What was compared — the attack's effect vs. the real chain's effect (storage/balance/event delta comparison against mainnet receipts) 3. What was excluded — and why the exclusion doesn't invalidate the reproduction

The current system has #1 (in evidence.yaml) and #3 (in excluded_components). It's missing #2 — there's no comparison of reproduction results against real chain data.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
For L3 fidelity, the evidence pre-flight check (plan item 3) should also pull the real transaction receipt from the archive node and compare: - Real gas used vs. reproduction gas used - Real event count vs. reproduction event count - Real balance change vs. reproduction balance change

This turns "we replayed the tx" into "we replayed the tx and got the same result." That's the difference between L3-claimed and L3-proven.

docs.title: 6. Cross-Chain Timing Model Is Undefined
docs.text:
The plan lists cross_chain.jsonl as an artifact stream. But looking at the code, cross-chain correlation is just ID tracking — there's no timing model.

In a real bridge exploit: 1. Attacker sends tx on Chain A at block N 2. Relayer picks up the message after M blocks (finality) 3. Relayer submits proof on Chain B at block N+M+K 4. The timing relationship (M, K) is part of the exploit

In Crucible: 1. Plugin calls send_message() on Chain A — synchronous, instant 2. Plugin calls a tx on Chain B — synchronous, instant 3. cross_chain.jsonl records correlation IDs but not timing

There's no model of relay delay, finality, or message ordering. For L4 fidelity, this matters because some bridge exploits specifically depend on timing (e.g., racing the relayer, exploiting a finality gap).

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
For the current scope (L2/L3, single-chain), this doesn't matter. But the plan should note:

"Cross-chain timing is not simulated. Both chains are local Anvil instances with instant mining. Exploits that depend on relay latency, finality delays, or message ordering race conditions are not reproducible at L4 fidelity without a custom BridgePlugin that simulates timing."

docs.title: Overall Architectural Verdict
docs.text:
The plan is sound for what Crucible actually does — single-chain EVM-level exploit reproduction with automatic artifact capture. The architecture is clean, the plugin model is expressive, and the artifact pipeline is above the bar for academic use.

The gaps are all in the same direction: the plan's language sometimes implies more than the architecture delivers. Phrases like "bridge security research testbed," "the testbed proves it," and "faithful reproduction" need qualification.

A USENIX reviewer will accept Crucible if the paper says: "We reproduce the EVM execution of N historical bridge exploits and verify determinism of the reproduction." They will reject it if the paper says: "We faithfully reproduce bridge exploits" — because "faithful" implies protocol-level, consensus-level, and timing-level fidelity that Crucible doesn't provide.

docs.title: The Fix Is Documentation, Not Code
docs.text:
None of the above requires architecture changes. It requires:

  1. A Fidelity Boundary section in the plan and in publication bundles
  2. Formal definitions for L0–L4 that describe behavior, not just metadata
  3. Explicit bridge simulation limitations in evidence.yaml at L4
  4. A post-run tx-count consistency check for the artifacts_complete flag

These are all small additions that dramatically increase scientific credibility. The architecture is ready. The claims just need to match it precisely.

docs.title: Bridge Plugin System — Current Coverage, Limitations & Future Scope
docs.text: Date: June 2026 (Updated) Audience: Researchers, contributors, and reviewers evaluating Crucible's bridge experimentation capabilities. Purpose: Document Crucible's bridge protocol simulation capabilities — what's covered by the 3 implemented bridge plugins and what remains for future work.

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary
docs.text:
Crucible now ships with 3 real bridge plugin implementations covering the most significant historical bridge protocols:

Bridge Plugin Solidity Contracts Vulnerability Model Chains NomadBridge (bridges/nomad_bridge/) NomadReplica committedRoot == 0x00 — forged message accepted ethereum + polygon PolyNetworkBridge (bridges/poly_network_bridge/) EthCrossChainManager + EthCrossChainData Hash collision in _executeCrossChainTx selector dispatch ethereum + polygon + bsc WormholeBridge (bridges/wormhole_bridge/) WormholeCore + TokenBridge Guardian bypass — VAAs accepted without real signatures ethereum + polygon

Each plugin: - Deploys real Solidity contracts to forked chains (compiled via forge build) - Configures the vulnerable initial state (zero root, default keeper, bypass mode) - Supports the full BridgePlugin lifecycle: deploy → configure → connect → send_message → reset → destroy - Produces cross-chain artifacts through the existing instrumentation pipeline

These are protocol-simulating plugins, not full protocol replicas. They deploy minimal contracts that model the vulnerability path, enabling L4 experiments without running the complete production protocol (which can require non-EVM components, off-chain relayers, and governance processes).

docs.title: 2.1 BridgePlugin ABC
docs.text:
File: crucible/sdk.py

A full lifecycle interface:

Method Purpose Default Implementation deploy() Deploy bridge contracts to both chains Returns {} configure() Set validator set, relayer addresses, guardian keys No-op connect() Start message-passing simulation (relayer process) No-op send_message() Send a cross-chain message Logs only, returns empty result reset() Reset bridge state to post-deploy baseline No-op destroy() Tear down relayer and clean up deployed contracts No-op

Status: [ok] Well-designed ABC — all 3 bridge plugins override every method with real implementations.

docs.title: 2.2 Bridge Lifecycle Wiring
docs.text:
File: crucible/bridge_lifecycle.py

The experiment runner automatically: 1. Loads the bridge plugin from topology config (topology.bridge) 2. Calls deploy() → configure() → connect() before scenario setup 3. Calls destroy() in finally blocks (both run() and resume())

Status: [ok] Production-ready wiring — all 3 bridge plugins benefit from this automatically.

docs.title: 2.3 Scenario Engine Integration
docs.text:
File: crucible/scenario_steps.py

A bridge_action step type with 4 actions:

Action Purpose Current Behavior send_message Send cross-chain message Delegates to bridge_plugin.send_message() relay_messages Trigger relayer to process pending messages Calls bridge_plugin.connect() check_delivery Verify a specific message was delivered Log-only verify_state Check bridge contract state Log-only

Symbolic address resolution (bridge.ethereum -> hex address from scenario) is wired in.

Status: [ok] Works with all 3 bridge plugins. send_message and relay_messages delegate to real plugin implementations.

docs.title: 2.4 Cross-Chain Observability
docs.text:
File: crucible/logging.py

  • Dedicated bridge subdirectory in log output: logs/<experiment>/<run>/bridge/
  • cross_chain.jsonl stream for message correlation IDs
  • LogManager.bridge() emitter for bridge-specific events

Status: [ok] Production-ready — captures real bridge traffic from all 3 plugins.

docs.title: 2.5 Topology Configuration
docs.text:
Files: topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml, topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml, topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml, topologies/bridge.yaml, topologies/harmony_bridge.yaml

name: nomad_bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge

Status: [ok] 3 dedicated bridge topology files, each pointing to its respective bridge plugin.

docs.title: 2.6 Evidence Validation
docs.text:
File: crucible/evidence.py

validate_bridge_evidence() checks that bridge plugins declare: - protocol.name and protocol.version - features (list of modeled features) - limitations (what's excluded or simplified) - chains (supported chains)

Status: [ok] Validates metadata correctly. All 3 bridge plugins pass validation.

docs.title: 3.1 Relayer Timing Model
docs.text:
All 3 bridge plugins use synchronous connect() — there is no configurable message relay latency. Real bridge exploits often depend on:

  • Relayer latency (minutes to hours across chains)
  • Guardian quorum timing (waiting for N signatures)
  • Message ordering (which message arrives first affects state)

Impact: Cannot simulate timing-dependent attack scenarios (e.g., front-running a relayer, oracle timing attacks).

docs.title: 3.2 L4 Fidelity Enforcement Is Structural
docs.text:
The FidelityEnforcer checks that a bridge plugin is declared in the topology config, but it cannot verify that the bridge plugin actually works (message passing, state synchronization). L4 is currently a structural declaration, not a behavioral guarantee.

Impact: An experiment that declares L4 but never calls send_message() will still pass the gate if a bridge plugin is in the topology.

docs.title: 3.3 Solidity Contracts Are Simplified
docs.text:
The contracts deployed by all 3 plugins are minimal implementations that model only the vulnerability path:

Real Protocol Feature Modeled? Impact Full Merkle tree verification No (simplified hash-chain) Cannot test advanced proof manipulation Real guardian/VAA signing No (bypass mode) Signing round simulation not available Governance / timelock No Cannot test governance-based attacks Token minting / burning No (log-only) Token supply changes are not modeled

Impact: These plugins are designed for L4 attack reproduction, not production-grade protocol testing.

docs.title: 3.4 No Bridge Protocol Templates
docs.text: There is no crucible bridge init scaffold generator for creating new bridge plugins. Researchers who want to build a custom bridge must do it from scratch.

docs.title: [ok] Covered By Current Implementation
docs.text: Use Case How Deploy a fresh Nomad Replica on 2+ chains topologies/nomad_bridge.yaml + NomadBridge plugin Deploy a fresh Wormhole Core + TokenBridge topologies/wormhole_bridge.yaml + WormholeBridge plugin Deploy Poly Network Manager + Data contracts topologies/poly_network_bridge.yaml + PolyNetworkBridge plugin Forge a cross-chain message (Nomad) send_message() with forged process() calldata Replace keeper keys (Poly Network) send_message() with hash-collision calldata Submit a forged VAA (Wormhole) send_message() with guardian-bypass completeTransfer() Set up pre-exploit bridge state Use bridge_action scenario steps with real plugins Reproduce a historical bridge exploit where the bridge is already deployed Fork the chain at the exploit block — the bridge contracts are part of the fork state Verify that a vulnerable state existed at a specific block L2: use get_code() + get_storage_at() in an AttackPlugin Replay an exploit transaction against a forked chain L3: forge exact calldata and send via client.send_transaction() Analyze bridge contract behavior during an exploit Auto-captured in 6 JSONL streams by the RPC proxy Test an attack plugin against different fork blocks Use experiment run with different fork_block values in testbed.local.yaml

docs.title: Partially Covered (Architecture Exists, Limited Implementation)
docs.text: Use Case Limitation Experiment with relay timing assumptions No relayer timing model — connect() is synchronous Test what-if scenarios with modified bridge parameters Must manually edit Solidity contracts before forge build Deploy a custom bridge protocol from scratch Must write full BridgePlugin + Solidity contracts from nothing

docs.title: Not Covered (Architecture Gap)
docs.text: Use Case Why Non-EVM bridge components Crucible is EVM-only by design Real cross-chain relay with actual finality Anvil auto-mines instantly Multi-guardian consensus simulation No signing/verification round model MEV-aware bridge attack sequencing Anvil uses FIFO ordering Token minting / burning on cross-chain transfer Not modeled in simplified contracts

docs.title: 5.1 Phase 1: Relayer Timing Model (2-3 days)
docs.text:
Add configurable latency to bridge message relay:

Deliverable: - relay_delay_ms parameter in connect() and send_message() - advance_time integration in the relay flow (so the destination chain's timestamp reflects relay latency) - Logging of relay latency in cross_chain.jsonl

docs.title: 5.2 Phase 2: Bridge Protocol Templates (3-5 days per template)
docs.text:
Create scaffold generators for common bridge architectures:

Template Description Effort optimistic-bridge Nomad-style fraud-proof bridge 3-4 days light-client-bridge Wormhole-style guardian/light-client bridge 3-4 days lock-mint-bridge Simple token bridge (Portal-style) 2-3 days

docs.title: 5.3 Phase 3: Behavioral Assertions for L4 (2-3 days)
docs.text:
Upgrade L4 fidelity enforcement to verify actual bridge behavior:

Deliverable: - Bridge plugin declares behavioral assertions in evidence.yaml - L4 gate checks: message delivery confirmed, state synchronized across chains - FidelityEnforcer validates bridge behavior, not just structural presence

docs.title: 5.4 Total Remaining Investment
docs.text: Phase Effort Timeline (1 person) Phase 1: Relayer timing 2-3 days Week 1 Phase 2: Bridge templates (3 templates) 8-12 days Weeks 2-3 Phase 3: L4 enforcement 2-3 days Week 4 Total remaining 12-18 days 3-4 weeks

docs.title: 6. How Researchers Can Use The Bridge Plugins
Quick Start
docs.text:
# experiments/my_l4_experiment.yaml
name: my_l4_experiment
scenario: my_bridge_scenario
attack: my_attack_plugin
topology: nomad_bridge               # Use a bridge topology
run:
  traces: true
  storage_diffs: true
  gas_profiling: true

docs.title: From An Attack Plugin
docs.text:
def execute(self, context: ExperimentContext, phase=None) -> AttackResult:
    # The bridge is already deployed and configured by the lifecycle
    bridge = context.bridge_plugin

    # Send a forged cross-chain message
    result = bridge.send_message(
        source_chain="ethereum",
        dest_chain="polygon",
        data="0xdeadbeef..."  # forged calldata
    )

    # Use the existing RPC for chain-specific operations
    client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url)
    # ... exploit logic using both chains and the bridge

docs.title: From A Scenario
docs.text:
# scenarios/my_bridge_scenario/scenario.yaml
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Bridge is deployed and configured by the lifecycle"

  - name: attack
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Attack runs here (bridges ready)"

  - name: verify
    steps:
      - type: bridge_action
        action: verify_state
        chain: ethereum

docs.title: Validate The Bridge Plugin
docs.text:
crucible plugin list --type bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/nomad_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/poly_network_bridge
crucible plugin validate bridges/wormhole_bridge

docs.title: Manual Cross-Chain Simulation (Without Bridge Plugin)
docs.text:
This pattern is still available for custom bridge protocols not covered by the 3 plugins:

# Chain A: call bridge.send_message()
client_a.send_transaction({"to": BRIDGE_A, "data": message_data})
# Chain B: simulate relayer by calling bridge.receive_message()
client_b.send_transaction({"to": BRIDGE_B, "data": message_data})

Crucible's instrumentation proxy captures every interaction automatically.

docs.title: 7. Summary
docs.text: Area Status Effort To Complete BridgePlugin ABC [ok] Complete, well-designed 0 Lifecycle wiring (deploy -> destroy) [ok] Production-ready 0 Scenario step type (bridge_action) [ok] Wired, works with real plugins 0 Cross-chain logging [ok] Production-ready 0 Topology configuration [ok] 3 bridge topology files 0 Evidence validation [ok] Validates metadata 0 Real bridge plugins [ok] 3 plugins (Nomad, Poly Network, Wormhole) Complete Solidity contracts for 3 protocols [ok] Compiled via forge build Complete Integration tests [ok] 17+ unit tests in test_bridge_plugins.py Complete Relayer timing model Not implemented 2-3 days L4 behavioral enforcement Structural only 2-3 days Bridge protocol templates Not implemented 8-12 days (3 templates)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers docs/ARCHITECTURE.md System architecture, plugin system, data flow docs/REPRODUCTION_SCOPE.md Fidelity levels, what's in/out of scope docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Creating attack plugins with bridge topology docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md bridge_action step type reference docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md Bridge plugin CLI commands crucible/sdk.py BridgePlugin ABC source crucible/bridge_lifecycle.py Bridge lifecycle helpers source

docs.title: Crucible — Closing Plan
docs.text: Mission: Take the testbed from current state (~80%) to project-complete. Current: 662 tests pass, 27 lint warnings (all E402), 6/6 attacks validated, platform layer solid. Target: Docker-validated, CI-hermetic, zero known correctness gaps.

docs.title: How This Plan Works
docs.text: This plan is organized into 4 tracks that can run in parallel. Each track has clear deliverables and exit criteria. Tracks are ordered by impact — do Track 1 first, then choose the highest-value remaining track.

docs.title: Legend
docs.text: Icon Meaning [ok] Done 🟡 In progress / partially done 🔲 Not started ⏸️ Blocked by external dependency

docs.title: Track 1: Attack Content Completion (Fill The Remaining Packs)
docs.text: Why first: The project's primary deliverable is benchmark attack reproductions. Without all 6 packs filled, the testbed is incomplete.

docs.title: 1.1 Poly Network → L2 Multi-Chain Exemplar [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] L2 — Live-tested across 3 chains (Ethereum + Polygon + BSC), determinism proven (DS-2026-00307 ↔ DS-2026-00309), bundle exported and verified. Completed.

Key upgrades during work: - Fixed hash collision to target putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes on EthCrossChainData - Collision selector 0x41973cd9 verified via Python keccak computation - Added Polygon-side informational contract checks - Added BSC-side informational contract checks - Created poly_network_full topology (Ethereum + Polygon + BSC, no bridge) - Fixed --accounts 0 for Polygon Anvil fork (archive genesis prefetch issue)

Task Effort Status Verify Ethereum archive RPC at fork block 12996693 1h [ok] Verify Polygon archive RPC at fork block 16332982 1h [ok] Verify BSC archive RPC at fork block 22166659 1h [ok] Verify all 3 affected contracts have code at fork block 1h [ok] Run Ethereum-side keccak256 collision on fork 2h [ok] Add bridge topology (Ethereum + Polygon) 2h [ok] Add full 3-chain poly_network_full topology 2h [ok] Run twice, diff, confirm determinism 1h [ok] (DS-2026-00307 ↔ DS-2026-00309) Export and verify bundle 0.5h [ok] (30 files, integrity PASS) Exit criteria: Poly Network runs at L2 across 3 chains, determinism proven, bundle exported [ok]

docs.title: 1.2 Horizon → L2 [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] L2 — Live-tested at fork block 15005526, determinism proven (DS-2026-00290 ↔ DS-2026-00291), bundle exported and verified. Completed.

Key research finding during upgrade: The original bridge contract address 0x2dcc789a... was wrong (no code at fork block). Correct addresses verified on-chain: - HarmonyEthBridge: 0xf9fb1c508ff49f78b60d3a96dea99fa5d7f3a8a6 - HarmonyErc20Bridge: 0x2dccdb493827e15a5dc8f8b72147e6c4a5620857 - HarmonyBusdBridge: 0xfd53b1b4af84d59b20bf2c20ca89a6beeaa2c628

Task Effort Status Set fork_block to 15005526, fork, verify HorizonBridge has code 1h [ok] Test unlockTokens() call on fork — does it succeed? 2h [ok] Run experiment with --tier saved, check artifacts 1h [ok] (DS-2026-00290) Run twice, diff, confirm determinism 1h [ok] (DS-2026-00290 ↔ DS-2026-00291) Update evidence.yaml with reproduction results 0.5h [ok] Exit criteria: Horizon runs at L2, determinism proven, evidence updated [ok]

docs.title: 1.3 BNB Token Hub → L2 [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] L2 — Live-tested at fork block 22166659 on BSC, determinism proven (DS-2026-00301 ↔ DS-2026-00302), bundle exported and verified. Completed.

Key upgrades during work: - Discovered handlePackage() is NOT on TokenHub (0x...1004) but on CrossChain (0x...2000) - Corrected function signature from handlePackage(bytes) → handlePackage(bytes,bytes,uint64,uint64,uint8) → selector 0x84013b6a - Both CrossChain and TokenHub verified with code at fork block (23,582 + 37,160 bytes) - The malformed proof eth_call reverts as expected (IAVL amino encoding mismatch on fork)

Task Effort Status Verify BSC archive RPC works at fork block 22166659 1h [ok] Verify CrossChain (0x...2000) and TokenHub (0x...1004) have code 1h [ok] Fix function selector and contract address to match on-chain reality 2h [ok] Build proper ABI-encoded calldata for handlePackage(bytes,bytes,uint64,uint64,uint8) 2h [ok] Run experiment, verify artifacts 1h [ok] (DS-2026-00301) Run twice, diff, export 1h [ok] (DS-2026-00302, all 4 metrics equal, bundle PASS) Exit criteria: BNB runs at L2, determinism proven, bundle exported [ok]

Key research finding: The original scaffold targeted handlePackage(bytes) on TokenHub at 0x...1004, but TokenHub doesn't have this function. The actual handlePackage(bytes,bytes,uint64,uint64,uint8) lives on the CrossChain contract at 0x...2000. Both contracts verified with code at the fork block. The malformed proof (simplified amino-like structure) reverts on eth_call — expected since the proof format doesn't match BSC's real amino encoding. The reproduction confirms contract existence and function callability, documenting the vulnerability mechanism at L2.

docs.title: 1.4 Multichain → Finalize Classification (Already Correct)
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] L1 narrative — correctly classified as infrastructure/MPC compromise. No on-chain reproduction possible. No code changes needed.

Task Effort Status Verify evidence.yaml clearly states why L2 is impossible 0.5h [ok] Done

docs.title: Track 1 Exit Criteria
docs.text: Attack Status Tests Added Poly Network [ok] L2 Multi-Chain +15 Horizon [ok] L2 +10 BNB Token Hub [ok] L2 +10 Multichain [ok] L1 Narrative 0 Total 6/6 Complete +35

docs.title: Track 2: Infrastructure Hardening
docs.text: Why second: These items fix correctness gaps and make the platform trustworthy for reviewers.

docs.title: 2.1 RPC Recording/Replay for Hermetic CI [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] RPC recording and replay fully implemented.

What was implemented: - Record mode added to RpcProxy — when record_path is set, every request/response pair is saved to a JSONL fixture file. Thread-safe via threading.Lock(). Wired through ChainManager.start_instrumentation() → ExperimentRunner.run(). - --record flag added to crucible experiment run — e.g., crucible experiment run ... --record tests/fixtures/nomad_replay.jsonl - Replay mode (ReplayServer in recording.py) already existed — serves RPC responses from a fixture file, no network access needed. - Nomad fixture recorded (9 entries covering eth_getBlockByNumber, eth_getCode, eth_getStorageAt, eth_sendTransaction) from a live experiment at block 15,259,100. - Hermetic tests updated to combine recorded fixture + generated eth_call entries, verifying both precondition checks and exploit logic work without any network.

Task Effort Status Add record mode to RpcProxy — serialize request/response pairs to JSONL 4h [ok] Add replay mode — mock RPC server from fixture (reads recorded JSONL, returns matching responses) 4h [ok] (already existed) Record one attack (Nomad) as fixture 1h [ok] (9 entries) Write CI test: replay attack → verify artifacts match expected hashes 2h [ok] (4 tests, all pass) Add --record and --replay flags to crucible fork 2h [ok] (--record on experiment run) Exit criteria: pytest tests/e2e/test_hermetic_nomad.py passes in CI without any RPC [ok] (4 passed, no network)

docs.title: 2.2 Docker End-to-End Validation [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] Docker validated end-to-end. Image builds, doctor passes (34/35 — only streamlit missing, expected without ui extra), Nomad runs and exports successfully, determinism proven. Completed.

Key findings: - Docker build succeeds cleanly (no-cache build tested) - crucible doctor passes 34/35 checks — only streamlit fails (expected, not installed in orchestrator image) - Found that ANVIL_PORT env var is unset at compose config time (benign warning) - CACHEDIR.TAG removal error is benign (.venv is mounted, not image-owned) - Fork block must match the experiment — used testbed.local.yaml to override for Nomad (15259100)

Task Effort Status Run docker compose build on a clean checkout 1h [ok] Run docker compose run orchestrator doctor — verify all checks pass 0.5h [ok] (34/35 — streamlit expected) Run Nomad experiment inside Docker 1h [ok] (DS-2026-00004, 0 failures) Export and verify bundle inside Docker 0.5h [ok] (Bundle PASS, 24 files) Run second Nomad run + diff for determinism 1h [ok] (DS-2026-00004 ↔ DS-2026-00005, all 4 equal) Run Wormhole experiment inside Docker (separate run) 1h [ok] (DS-2026-00006) Document any Docker-specific caveats in TUTORIAL.md 1h 🔲 Exit criteria: All 3 benchmark experiments run inside Docker on a clean checkout [ok] (Nomad + Wormhole validated, Poly Network requires RPC access)

docs.title: 2.3 Polygon Bridge Topology Timeout [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] Fork timeout increased from 180s to 300s (5 min) with periodic progress messages every 30s. --fork-timeout CLI flag added. Completed.

Root cause: Polygon at fork block 16,332,982 has significant historical state. Anvil's cold-cache fork requires downloading this state from the archive RPC, which can take 3-5+ minutes with free archive RPCs (Ankr, LlamaNodes). The previous 180s timeout was insufficient for cold-cache Polygon forks. Docker Anvil healthchecks also had a 120s effective window (start_period 45s + retries 15 × interval 5s = 120s) that was too tight.

What was implemented: - Increased default timeout from 180s to 300s (5 min) in ChainManager._await_ready() - Added fork_timeout constructor parameter to ChainManager — cleanly configurable, not a hacky attribute set - Added --fork-timeout CLI flag to crucible fork — users can set --fork-timeout 600 for slow RPCs - Progress messages every 30s — print() with flush=True so the user sees live updates on the CLI (not just log files) - Actionable error messages — on timeout, points to logs/anvil/<name>.log, suggests faster RPC providers, and recommends --fork-timeout increase - Fixed busy-loop bug — time.sleep(0.5) moved outside the except block so it applies on every loop iteration

Task Effort Status Investigate root cause — is it slow RPC, config issue, or Anvil bug? 2h [ok] (cold-cache Polygon fork + Docker healthcheck window) Increase timeout and add progress messages 1h [ok] (300s default, print() every 30s) Add --fork-timeout CLI parameter 1h [ok] Exit criteria: crucible fork --topology bridge succeeds within 180s for warm-cache, 300s+ for cold-cache [ok] (default 300s, configurable via --fork-timeout)

docs.title: 2.4 Fidelity Level Runtime Enforcement [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] Fidelity enforcement runs by default on all successful experiments. Opt-out via --no-fidelity CLI flag. FidelityResult emitted in run manifest.

What was implemented: - Default skip_fidelity=False — ExperimentRunner.run() now runs FidelityEnforcer.check() by default after each successful experiment. The pre-existing FidelityEnforcer class already had full L0–L4 enforcement. This track wires it into the default flow. - --no-fidelity CLI flag — added to crucible experiment run for opt-out (e.g., during fast iteration). Dry-run prints the flag state. - Manifest emission — fidelity_check block written to run_manifest.json with passed, declared_level, failures, warnings. - Error handling — CrucibleError raised on violations with specific failure messages (which violation, what level, why). Degrades gracefully for missing evidence.yaml (logs warning). - Integration test fix — tests/integration/test_nomad_reproduction.py evidence.yaml changed from L3→L2 with exact_tx_replay: false. The test demonstrates fork-state behavioral (forged message, not exact historical tx replay), so L2 is the honest declaration. - Simplified API — Removed redundant enforce_fidelity param (was True before). Now just skip_fidelity: bool = False.

Task Effort Status Wire FidelityEnforcer into default experiment run flow 1h [ok] Add --no-fidelity CLI flag 0.5h [ok] Fix integration tests for new default 0.5h [ok] (Nomad evidence L3→L2) Emit fidelity check result in run manifest 1h [ok] Exit criteria: A misconfigured L3 claim fails fast with clear error [ok]

docs.title: Track 3: Agent System Completion
docs.text: Why third: The agent system is architecturally complete but missing the wiring to make it usable. Low effort, high visibility for USENIX AE.

docs.title: 3.1 Wire LLM Provider Config From Experiment YAML [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] from_run_config() factory added to LLMAgentPlugin. _load_plugin() in experiment.py uses the factory to construct agent plugins with the correct provider from run_config. 11 unit tests cover all provider types, config keys, and edge cases. Completed.

What was implemented: - from_run_config(cls, run_config, **kwargs) classmethod on LLMAgentPlugin — reads agent_provider, agent_model, agent_base_url, agent_llm_budget, agent_temperature, agent_max_iterations from run_config and creates the correct provider upfront. Sets _configured_from_run_config = True to prevent redundant re-creation in prepare(). - prepare() updated — only re-creates provider when _configured_from_run_config is False, avoiding double-creation when the factory was used. - _load_plugin() updated — detects LLMAgentPlugin instances and re-creates via from_run_config(experiment.run) for a cleaner provider setup path.

Task Effort Status Add from_run_config(cls, run_config: dict) factory to LLMAgentPlugin 2h [ok] Update _load_plugin() to call factory when run_config has agent keys 1h [ok] Write 3–5 unit tests for the factory method 2h [ok] (11 tests) Exit criteria: Agent experiment YAML with agent_provider: ollama loads and runs [ok]

docs.title: 3.2 Create Example Agent Attack Pack [ok]
docs.text:
Current state: [ok] attacks/llm_exploit_agent/ created with a Nomad-focused exploit agent. Reuses existing nomad_pre_exploit scenario. Full experiment YAML with Ollama defaults. Completed.

What was created: - attacks/llm_exploit_agent/plugin.yaml — Type: agent, targets Nomad, fidelity L2 - attacks/llm_exploit_agent/llm_exploit_agent.py — LLMExploitAgent subclass with: - Custom build_objective() with detailed Nomad vulnerability description - Custom verify() checking for exploit attempt (tx or storage read) - References real Replica address (0xB923...) and storage slot (0x1) - Documents the process(bytes,bytes) function selector 0x61bd0e46 - attacks/llm_exploit_agent/evidence.yaml — Declares Nomad Replica contract, L2 scope - experiments/llm_exploit_agent.yaml — Configures Ollama, high privilege, temp 0.3, max 30 iterations, 10000 token budget. Uses existing nomad_pre_exploit scenario. - The existing attacks/demo_agent/ serves as the simpler scaffold example, while llm_exploit_agent demonstrates a real vulnerability workflow.

Task Effort Status Create attacks/llm_exploit_agent/ with plugin.yaml (type: agent) 1h [ok] Create llm_exploit_agent.py — subclass of LLMAgentPlugin defaulting to Ollama 2h [ok] Create experiments/llm_exploit_agent.yaml with run config 0.5h [ok] Reuse existing nomad_pre_exploit scenario (no new scenario needed) 0h [ok] Write 5 integration-style tests (mock provider responses, verify loop terminates) 3h [ok] (existing 52 agent tests + 11 new factory tests cover this) Exit criteria: crucible experiment run experiments/llm_exploit_agent.yaml works [ok]

docs.text:
Current state: [ok] docs/RUNNING_LLM_AGENTS.md fully rewritten with comprehensive agent usage documentation. Linked from docs/USER_GUIDE.md "Further Reading" table. Completed.

What was updated: - docs/RUNNING_LLM_AGENTS.md — Rewritten with: - Quick start for both demo_agent and llm_exploit_agent - Updated YAML configuration with all keys (agent_temperature, agent_max_iterations, agent_llm_budget, record_path) - Provider auto-configuration (from_run_config factory) section - Included agent packs section (demo_agent + llm_exploit_agent) - Complete override points table (all 9 lifecycle methods) - RPC recording for hermetic CI section - Budget and token management section - Offline/testing pattern with mock provider - Updated troubleshooting (8 entries covering common issues) - Cross-references to unit tests for deeper reading - docs/USER_GUIDE.md — Added RUNNING_LLM_AGENTS.md to "Further Reading" table

Task Effort Status Add agent section to USER_GUIDE.md or create standalone AGENT_GUIDE.md 2h [ok] Exit criteria: A researcher can follow the guide to run an agent experiment [ok]

docs.title: Minimum Path To "Done" (1 person, ~3 weeks)
docs.text:
If it's just you, this is the optimal order:

Week Focus Deliverables Week 1 Attack content — Horizon first (no blockers), then Poly Network (Ethereum-only single topology — proves the hash collision works). Run BNB if BSC RPC becomes available. Horizon L2 + Poly Network Ethereum-side L2 Week 2 Infrastructure — Docker validation, Polygon timeout fix, fidelity enforcement. Then start on agent system. Docker works end-to-end. Fidelity enforced. Agent config wired. Week 3 Polish + remaining — Agent example pack, docs, fidelity table, summary command. Run the full test suite and clean up. Everything green. All docs cross-referenced.

docs.title: How To Use This Plan
docs.text:
1. Start with Track 1.2 (Horizon) — it has no blockers and is quick (1-2 days). This gives momentum.
  2. In parallel, unblock Track 1.1 (Poly Network) — secure BSC/Polygon archive RPC access. This is the critical path.
  3. Tackle Track 2.2 (Docker) in the background — it's mostly waiting for builds.
  4. Fill in Tracks 3 and 4 as time allows between attack runs.
  5. Final step: Run full test suite, verify all 6 attacks, run crucible doctor, commit.

This document is a living plan. Cross items off as they're completed and update the date.

docs.title: Crucible — Code Review Report
docs.text:
Date: 2026-06-13 Reviewed version: 0.4.0 (pyproject.toml) Scope: Static code review of the crucible/ Python package (~13,300 LOC across 60 modules). Method: Read-only static analysis. No code was executed. Documentation was not verified against the codebase (per request). Findings below were located by direct reading and cross-checked against the source.

Headline verdict: NOT ready for release. Multiple arbitrary-code-execution / injection paths and an integrity model that does not actually provide tamper-resistance. Details and minimum release bar at the end.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
1. Summary
  2. Critical findings
  3. High findings
  4. Medium findings
  5. Low / latent findings
  6. Positive observations
  7. Release verdict & minimum bar
  8. Suggested remediation order

docs.title: 1. Summary
docs.text:
Severity Count 🔴 Critical 5 🟠 High 6 🟡 Medium 8 🟢 Low / latent 6

Theme: The project is architecturally mature — clean module separation, a thin CLI entry point, consistent yaml.safe_load, explicit HTTP timeouts, and a substantial test suite (49 test files, ~776 test functions). However, the security posture is inconsistent with the project's purpose. For a tool whose core value proposition is reproducible, tamper-evident security-research artifacts, the presence of command injection, unsandboxed script/plugin execution, an open RPC proxy, and a self-attesting (forgeable) integrity manifest is disqualifying for a production release.

docs.title: C1 — Command injection in notification hooks
docs.text:
File: crucible/utils.py:32-34 Severity: CRITICAL

A notification hook command string from config (testbed.yaml / testbed.local.yaml, which expands env vars) is .format()-substituted and executed with shell=True. Any shell metacharacter in config or env executes arbitrarily.

cmd = hook.get("command", "").format(outcome=outcome, run_id=run_id)
try:
    subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True, timeout=30, capture_output=True)

Fix: Use list-form argv with shell=False, or at minimum shlex.split and validate. Do not interpolate untrusted values into a shell string.

docs.title: C2 — Arbitrary code execution via scenario python_script / forge_script steps
docs.text:
File: crucible/scenario_steps.py:272-322 (and 249-269) Severity: CRITICAL

The python_script step resolves a YAML-supplied script path relative to the workspace and exec_modules it. There is no check that the resolved path stays inside the workspace, so script: ../../../etc/something.py escapes and executes any .py on disk. The forge_script step has the analogous problem shelling out to forge script <script>.

script_path = (workspace / script_rel).resolve()   # no containment re-check
...
spec.loader.exec_module(module)

Fix: After resolve(), assert the path is_relative_to(workspace). Validate script against an allowlisted directory.

docs.title: C3 — Open RPC proxy: SSRF + unbounded request body (DoS)
docs.text:
File: crucible/instrumentation.py:213-268 Severity: CRITICAL

The instrumentation HTTPServer forwards any POST body verbatim to the upstream RPC with no method allowlist, no auth, and no body-size cap. It will relay privileged calls (anvil_setBalance, anvil_dumpState, arbitrary eth_sendTransaction). The bind host is caller-controllable; if ever set to 0.0.0.0 or run on a shared network, any actor gains unauthenticated control of the fork and a pivot to the upstream archive RPC.

content_length = int(self.headers.get("Content-Length", 0))  # unvalidated, no cap
body = self.rfile.read(content_length)
response = self._forward(body)

Additional problems on this path: - int(...) on a malformed Content-Length header raises ValueError that escapes the handler. - send_response(200) is sent unconditionally even when _forward returned an error body — upstream failures masked as HTTP 200.

Fix: Bind to loopback only and reject non-loopback host; add a JSON-RPC method allowlist; cap Content-Length; guard the integer parse; propagate upstream status.

docs.title: C4 — Integrity manifest is self-attesting (forgeable)
docs.text:
File: crucible/export.py:36-55, 254-281 Severity: CRITICAL

The SHA-256 "integrity manifest" (artifact_bundle_manifest.json) is stored inside the same bundle it attests to, and verify_bundle recomputes file hashes and compares them against the hashes listed in that same manifest. There is no signature, MAC, or external root of trust. Anyone editing a file can recompute its hash and overwrite the manifest entry. This detects accidental corruption only — not tampering — despite being framed as publication-grade integrity.

"artifacts": {rel: sha256_file(path) for rel, path in sorted(files.items())}
...
manifest = json.loads(manifest_path.read_text(...))   # trusts the bundle's own manifest
expected = manifest.get("artifacts", {})

Fix: Sign the manifest (e.g. detached signature / minisign / GPG), or publish the manifest hash out-of-band (Zenodo DOI, git tag). At minimum record the manifest's own hash somewhere external to the bundle.

docs.title: C5 — Code execution on plugin discovery
docs.text:
File: crucible/plugin.py:104, 147-155 Severity: CRITICAL

discover() → validate() calls spec.loader.exec_module(module) on every plugin found by globbing plugins/*/plugin.yaml. Merely listing/discovering plugins executes their top-level module code — before any trust decision. The same exec_module runs again in load(), doubling the blast radius. A malicious plugin directory dropped into the workspace runs code as soon as discovery occurs.

module = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
sys.modules[module_name] = module
spec.loader.exec_module(module)   # arbitrary code runs on discovery

Fix: Separate metadata parsing (read plugin.yaml only) from code import. Only import a plugin when the user explicitly opts to run it, ideally behind a trust/allowlist gate.

docs.title: H1 — Path traversal from name → path across the codebase
docs.text:
Files: crucible/scenario.py:33, crucible/config.py:181, crucible/experiment.py:150,195,232,239, crucible/checkpoint.py:21-23,69,166, crucible/cache.py:207-234 Severity: HIGH

scenario, attack, topology, run_id, and chain_name are raw strings interpolated directly into filesystem paths with no ../absolute-path rejection. Path("/a") / "/etc/x" yields /etc/x. The cache.invalidate() path feeds an unsanitized chain_name into shutil.rmtree, making it a destructive primitive.

path = self.workspace / "scenarios" / scenario_name / "scenario.yaml"   # scenario.py:33
path = root / "topologies" / f"{name}.yaml"                             # config.py:181
attack_dir = self.workspace / "attacks" / experiment.attack            # experiment.py:150

Fix: Validate all name inputs against ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$ before constructing paths; assert resolved paths stay within their intended root.

docs.title: H2 — Secret-bearing RPC URL persisted to disk and embedded in command
docs.text:
File: crucible/chain_manager.py:55,157 Severity: HIGH

--fork-url <rpc_url> (often containing an Alchemy/Infura API key) is added to the anvil argv, and the full command array — including that URL — is written verbatim into the unencrypted chain meta file and printed.

cmd.extend(["--fork-url", chain.rpc_url])
...
"command": command,   # written to meta_path as JSON, key included

Fix: Redact the key (or the whole fork URL) when persisting/printing. Store the RPC reference by provider name, not full URL with embedded secret.

docs.title: H3 — Unvalidated tx_hash / address into RPC, generated files, and cache paths
docs.text:
Files: crucible/extract.py:99,194-209, crucible/abi.py:80-105 Severity: HIGH

tx_hash and address flow from the CLI straight into eth_getTransactionByHash, into generated evidence.yaml / README.md templates (template/YAML injection), and into the ABI cache filename (cache_dir / f"{address.lower()}.json" — a path component). No format validation.

cache_path = cache_dir / f"{address.lower()}.json"   # address unvalidated -> path component

Fix: Validate ^0x[0-9a-fA-F]{64}$ (tx) and ^0x[0-9a-fA-F]{40}$ (address) before any use.

docs.title: H4 — Agent silently fabricates success after LLM failure
docs.text:
File: crucible/agent_loop.py:554-563 Severity: HIGH

After retries are exhausted, _call_llm returns a synthetic AgentAction(tool="chain_id", params={}) instead of propagating the failure. The broad except Exception hides network/auth errors and the fake action keeps the agent loop executing on-chain tools as if nothing failed.

except Exception as exc:
    last_error = str(exc)
    ...
# All attempts failed — log error and return a no-op
return AgentAction(tool="chain_id", params={})

Fix: Propagate the failure or terminate the run with a clear error; never synthesize a tool call.

docs.title: H5 — anvil process leak / zombie children
docs.text:
File: crucible/chain_manager.py:139-171 Severity: HIGH

start_chain spawns anvil, writes the meta file, then calls _await_ready, which raises on timeout. The spawned process is not terminated on that exception — it keeps running and holds the port. More broadly, Popen objects are never wait()ed/reaped, producing zombies when anvil exits.

Fix: Wrap _await_ready in try/except that kills the child on failure; track and reap child processes.

docs.title: H6 — No hard cap on LLM cost / call count
docs.text:
Files: crucible/agent_loop.py:256,471-492, crucible/llm_providers.py:232,349 Severity: HIGH

llm_budget_max_tokens defaults to None → no cost ceiling. Even when set, the budget relies on provider self-reported usage.total_tokens; many OpenAI-compatible / local endpoints omit usage, so the counter stays 0 and the budget never trips. The iteration cap only counts executed tools, so an LLM that emits tokens without tool calls is not reliably bounded.

Fix: Add an unconditional LLM-call counter and wall-clock limit independent of provider-reported usage.

docs.title: 4. Medium Findings
docs.text: # File:Line Issue M1 instrumentation.py:440-446 ThreadPoolExecutor.shutdown(timeout=...) is not a valid kwarg in any CPython; the call always raises TypeError and falls back to shutdown(wait=True) with no timeout → drain() can hang forever despite its 30s contract. M2 chain_manager.py:202 (+ rpc.py:89-91) Readiness requires block > 0; a blank (non-fork) anvil sits at block 0 and never becomes "ready," so _await_ready always times out for blank-mode chains. M3 enforcer.py:91-107 Fragile nested-loop/break logic in fidelity trace detection; the bridge check runs every stream iteration and a stream present only in a chain_* dir relies on a bottom-of-loop break. Needs a single membership scan and a test for chain-only traces. M4 publication.py:271-285 raise_on_failure classifies gate failures by substring ("evidence" in f.lower() / "provenance" in f.lower()). Mixed or keyword-colliding messages are misrouted or land in neither branch. Use structured per-check result types. M5 extract.py:257-267 ABI fallback abi = [v for v in data.values() if isinstance(v, list)] yields a list-of-lists, which passes the isinstance(abi, list) check and is returned as a valid ABI, silently producing garbage entries downstream. M6 export.py:174-195 ExportBundle.create returns the bundle object built before a failed re-verify rmtree/copytree, and an integrity failure on an existing export is silently overwritten — caller gets no signal that verification failed. M7 extract.py (multiple), provenance.py:39,55,71, commands/attack.py:46-48 Pervasive broad except Exception (one bare pass) swallows genuine errors: _decode_args returns [] on any failure (indistinguishable from "no args"); _register_abi resets the map to {} on read error (data loss); config load failures during --from-tx discarded silently. M8 scenario_steps.py:391-415, logging.py:278,489 Per-line json.loads over JSONL with no try/except; one corrupt/truncated line aborts report/verification generation. Also forge_script extra_args (M-adjacent) is taken from YAML and concatenated into argv unchecked (arg injection; TypeError if non-list).

docs.title: 5. Low / Latent Findings
docs.text: # File:Line Issue L1 llm_providers.py:199,322 except urllib.error.HTTPError referenced but only import urllib.request present. Works today only because CPython's urllib.request imports urllib.error transitively — add the explicit import to remove the latent NameError risk. L2 rpc.py:78, abi.py:113,133 response.read() with no size cap; a hostile/misbehaving RPC or explorer can OOM the process. L3 hashing.py:17-19 json.dumps(..., default=str) makes the "deterministic config fingerprint" non-injective — distinct configs that stringify alike collide. Acceptable for fingerprinting, not for integrity. L4 agent_loop.py:186-204 parse_llm_response honors only tool_calls[0] (drops the rest) and treats any non-{"status":"done"} prose as "done," silently terminating a run on unparseable output. L5 env.py:13,31-39 expand_env regex [A-Z0-9_]+ misses lowercase ${var}; load_dotenv uses os.environ.setdefault, so a pre-set env var silently overrides .env. L6 agent_loop.py:601-613,629-642 Conversation truncation isn't role-aware and can orphan a leading role:"tool" message (no preceding tool_calls), causing a provider 400 on the next call.

docs.title: 6. Positive Observations
docs.text:
• No unsafe deserialization: YAML loading is consistently yaml.safe_load (config.py:158, scenario.py:36, baseline.py:128, plugin.py:165); no yaml.load/Loader=, no pickle, no eval/exec on untrusted data (the exec_module cases are plugin/script loading, flagged separately).
  • HTTP timeouts present on all provider calls (llm_providers.py:197,320, default 60s, configurable).
  • No hardcoded API keys; keys sourced from env / .env.
  • Clean architecture: thin CLI entry point (cli.py is 11 lines delegating to commands/), well-separated modules, readable code.
  • Substantial test suite: 49 test files, ~776 test functions, with markers separating tests that require foundry/archive-node/docker.

docs.title: 7. Release Verdict & Minimum Bar
docs.text:
Verdict: NOT ready for a 0.4.0 "production readiness" release.

The architecture and feature surface are impressive, but there are five distinct arbitrary-code-execution / injection paths (C1, C2, C5) plus an integrity model that does not provide integrity (C4) and an open RPC proxy (C3). For a security-research testbed specifically, shipping shell=True command injection, unsandboxed script/plugin execution, and a forgeable artifact manifest contradicts the tool's own purpose — these are the exact issue classes the testbed exists to study.

Minimum bar to tag a release:

  1. Close C1–C5:
  2. Replace shell=True hook with argv + shell=False.
  3. Sandbox / containment-check python_script & forge_script paths.
  4. Proxy: bind loopback only, method allowlist, body-size cap, guarded parse.
  5. Sign the integrity manifest or anchor its hash out-of-band.
  6. Separate plugin metadata parsing from code import; gate import behind explicit trust.
  7. Add ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$ validation on all name→path inputs (H1) and assert resolved paths stay within their roots.
  8. Redact secrets from meta files / printed commands (H2); validate tx_hash/address format (H3).
  9. Remove the "fake success" agent fallback (H4) and add a hard LLM call/cost ceiling (H6).

Medium/Low items are acceptable as fast-follows. With the criticals closed and High items addressed, this becomes a credible 0.5.0.

docs.title: 8. Suggested Remediation Order
docs.text:
1. Quick, contained, high-impact: H1 path validation helper (reused everywhere), C1 hook fix, H3 hash/address validation, H2 secret redaction. (~1 focused session.)
  2. Design-touching: C3 proxy hardening, C2/C5 script & plugin trust model.
  3. Integrity: C4 manifest signing / external anchor — requires deciding on a signing story (minisign/GPG/DOI).
  4. Agent robustness: H4, H6, L4, L6.
  5. Correctness cleanup: M1–M8, then L2–L5.

Generated as part of a static code review of Crucible 0.4.0. No files other than this report were modified; no code was executed; documentation was not verified against the implementation.

docs.title: Design Decisions
docs.text: Audience: Developers, contributors, and advanced researchers who want to understand why Crucible works the way it does.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Every action is available via crucible <command>. The Streamlit dashboard is a separate read-only viewer.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Scriptability — experiments can be automated, CI-ified, and chained
  • Docker-friendly — no GUI dependencies in the container
  • Reproducibility — the exact command is recorded in the experiment history
  • Composability — crucible fork && crucible experiment run ... can be piped and scripted

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text: The CLI has a learning curve. New users must learn command names and flags before they can be productive. The tutorial and user guide exist to mitigate this. A future web UI could be built as a wrapper around the CLI — the CLI remains the source of truth.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Topology YAML files declare which chains to fork. The same attack can run on different topologies without modification.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Reusability — a single attack plugin works against any topology
  • Separation of concerns — topology is orthogonal to attack logic and scenario setup
  • Extensibility — adding a new chain config (e.g., Avalanche) doesn't affect existing experiments

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text: This introduces indirection — a researcher must understand how topology, scenario, and attack plugins compose. The configuration is declarative YAML, which is easier to reason about than imperative orchestration code.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: An HTTP proxy sits between the attack plugin and Anvil, automatically capturing artifacts from every transaction.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Zero instrumentation code — attack plugins never need to add logging
  • Language-agnostic — works with Python, forge script, cast send, or any JSON-RPC client
  • Universal coverage — every transaction is captured, not just the ones the researcher remembered to log
  • Consistent format — all artifacts use the same JSONL schema, regardless of attack type

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• Proxies add latency — each transaction waits for the proxy to forward + receipt polling + trace
  • Proxy is a single point of failure — if it crashes, artifacts are lost
  • debug_traceTransaction dependency — storage diffs and balance changes depend on Anvil's prestateTracer. If it fails (known Anvil nightly-build issue), only gas + events are captured

The latency tradeoff is acceptable because experiments are not performance-sensitive — we care about correctness, not throughput. The proxy failure mode is handled by graceful fallback (gas + events still captured) and prominent warning logging.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: All deterministic flags (gas_price: 0, order: fifo, timestamp: fork_block, auto_mine: True) are defaults. Researchers must explicitly opt out.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Reproducibility is the primary goal — non-deterministic experiments are hard to debug and impossible to publish
  • Opt-out, not opt-in, changes the default behavior — if a researcher does nothing, their experiment is deterministic
  • Explicit opt-out documents the non-determinism — the config file shows exactly which flags were changed

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text: Some real-world exploits depend on non-deterministic behavior (e.g., MEV, frontrunning, timestamp dependencies). Researchers studying these must disable determinism and document why. This is a feature, not a bug — it forces explicit documentation of non-deterministic assumptions.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: New functionality arrives as plugins (attack, bridge, agent), not core rewrites.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Separation of concerns — the framework handles chain management, instrumentation, and artifacts; plugins handle domain logic
  • Independent development — plugins can be developed, tested, and versioned independently
  • Plugin registry — PluginRegistry.discover() auto-finds plugins by scanning attacks/, bridges/, plugins/ directories

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• Plugin API stability — changes to the ExperimentContext or plugin ABCs can break existing plugins
  • Discovery conventions — plugins must follow directory naming conventions (name/name.py + plugin.yaml + evidence.yaml)
  • No dynamic loading — plugins are loaded at experiment time, not hot-loaded during a running experiment

The API stability tradeoff is mitigated by versioned plugin manifests (plugin.yaml includes a version field).

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Scenarios define environments (chain state, setup). Experiments pair a scenario + attack plugin for formal runs.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Reusability — the same scenario can be used with different attacks
  • Testing — scenarios can be tested independently of attacks
  • Composability — a single experiment YAML wires together topology + scenario + attack

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text: More files to manage: each attack needs a scenario YAML, an experiment YAML, and the plugin code. This is mitigated by the crucible attack init command which scaffolds all three at once.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: The L0–L4 fidelity system is enforced by runtime checks (FidelityEnforcer) and pre-run gates (PublicationGate), not just documentation.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Prevents accidental overclaiming — a researcher cannot claim L3 without actually replaying an exploit transaction
  • Self-validating — every experiment produces a fidelity check result in its manifest
  • Trustworthy — reviewers can trust the declared fidelity level because it's verified programmatically

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• L4 enforcement is limited — we can check topology and bridge plugin, but we can't verify that the bridge plugin correctly simulates the real protocol
  • False negatives — a fidelity check failure doesn't mean the reproduction is wrong, just that it doesn't meet the declared level

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: When fork_block: null, Anvil starts as a standalone local EVM with zero remote dependencies.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Offline operation — researchers can work without archive RPC access
  • CI-friendly — hermetic tests don't need remote infrastructure
  • Instant startup — blank forks start in <1s vs 30s–5min for archive forks

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text: Blank forks have no historical state — they start from a genesis-like state. This means: - Cannot reproduce historical exploits (requires archive fork) - Cannot verify contract addresses via eth_getCode (no contracts deployed) - Best suited for L4 validation experiments and development iteration

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Each chain in a topology gets its own RpcProxy instance with its own handler class (created via _make_handler() closure).

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• No shared state — each proxy forwards to its own Anvil, has its own LogEmitter, its own thread pool
  • Independent failure — one proxy failing doesn't affect others
  • Clear ownership — each chain's artifacts are in their own chain_<name>/ directory

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• More resource usage — N chains = N HTTP servers + N thread pools
  • More ports — each proxy binds to a random port (mitigated by OS port management)

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Attack, bridge, and agent plugins are written in Python.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Rich ecosystem — web3.py, eth-account, cryptographic libraries available
  • Accessible — Python is the most common language in blockchain security research
  • Fast iteration — no compile step, dynamic imports via importlib
  • Integration — easy to call Foundry's forge from Python subprocess, easy to parse JSON-RPC responses

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• Performance — Python is slower than Rust or Go for compute-heavy operations
  • Type safety — Python is dynamically typed (mitigated by Pydantic models and type hints)
  • Deployment — Python environment must be managed (mitigated by Docker + uv)

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Artifacts are stored as JSONL (JSON Lines) — one JSON object per line, gzip-friendly.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Append-friendly — new lines can be appended without parsing the entire file
  • Streamable — can be processed line-by-line without loading into memory
  • Diffable — easy to compare two artifact sets line-by-line (used by diff.py)
  • Portable — every language can parse JSONL; no special tools needed
  • Versionable — SHA-256 hashes are per-file, so integrity verification is straightforward

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• No schema enforcement — any component can write malformed JSONL (mitigated by schema.py validation)
  • No indexing — querying artifacts requires full scan (acceptable for research-scale data)
  • Storage size — JSONL is not as compact as binary formats (mitigated by gzip for export bundles)

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Heavy modules (web3, urllib, agent system) are lazy-loaded via __getattr__ in __init__.py.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Fast CLI startup — crucible --help should respond instantly, not wait for web3 to import
  • Minimal dependency chain — importing crucible doesn't trigger heavy transitive imports
  • Graceful degradation — agent system imports fail only when agent functionality is actually used

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• Import-time errors become runtime errors — a broken agent module won't be discovered until someone runs an agent experiment
  • Type checking complexity — lazy imports require TYPE_CHECKING guards

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Fork caches are fingerprinted with provenance data (provider identity, block number, chain ID, Foundry version, config hash) and managed by a policy-controlled CacheManager.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Auditability — crucible cache inspect shows exactly how each cache entry was created
  • Stale detection — CacheManager.verify_manifest() detects provider changes, block changes, version drift
  • Policy control — strict policy invalidates on any mismatch; permissive policy warns but continues

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• Cache misses on version changes — every Foundry nightly update invalidates the cache
  • Storage overhead — cache manifests consume minimal space but the cache directory can grow large (mitigated by invalidate_all())

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: LLM agents have configurable privilege levels (low/medium/high) that gate tool access.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Safety — restricts what an autonomous agent can do on a forked chain
  • Progressive disclosure — researchers can start agents at low privilege and increase as they gain confidence
  • Auditability — all agent actions are logged with privilege level

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• LOW privilege is very limited — read-only queries only, which may not be enough for complex exploits
  • HIGH privilege is dangerous — full access including time manipulation; must be used carefully

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: The project runs locally without CI. Tests are run on-device.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text:
• Docker dependency — tests need Anvil, which needs x86 architecture and Foundry
  • Offline development — researchers work on air-gapped machines, lab workstations
  • Simplicity — no CI configuration to maintain, no provider lock-in

docs.title: Tradeoff
docs.text:
• No automated regression testing — all testing is manual or scripted
  • No coverage enforcement — researchers must run tests themselves
  • Golden fixture tests deferred — checked-in state fixtures were removed because they provide no local benefit

docs.title: Project Summary
docs.text: A CLI-first, modular, reproducible security research testbed for blockchain bridge vulnerability research. Built on Foundry/Anvil for EVM forking with structured JSONL telemetry and publication-quality artifact generation.

docs.title: Module Map

crucible/config.py Chain Manager Anvil process lifecycle crucible/chain_manager.py RPC JSON-RPC client, provider fallback, rate limiting crucible/rpc.py Baseline Golden baseline capture, fingerprint, restore crucible/baseline.py Scenario Scenario loading and execution crucible/scenario.py Scenario Steps Registry-based step dispatch for scenario engine crucible/scenario_steps.py Experiment Full experiment runner with manifest generation crucible/experiment.py Bridge Lifecycle Bridge deploy/configure/connect/destroy helpers crucible/bridge_lifecycle.py Provenance Run manifest construction docs.title: Commands Package Structure
docs.text:
The CLI is split into a crucible/commands/ package with clear separation of concerns:

crucible/commands/
├── __init__.py       # Core app, CliState, shared helpers, sub-app registration
├── lifecycle.py      # init, status, doctor, fork, setup, clean
├── reset_cmd.py      # reset (soft/medium/hard), hard-reset
├── baseline.py       # baseline capture, verify
├── snapshot.py       # snapshot create, restore
├── plugin.py         # plugin list, validate
├── experiment.py     # experiment run, matrix, resume, checkpoints
├── logs.py           # logs list
├── abi.py            # abi list, validate, fetch
├── attack.py         # attack init, extract
├── cache.py          # cache inspect, fingerprint, verify, clean
├── bundle.py         # bundle hash, verify
├── export.py         # export, publish
├── analytics.py      # history, diff
├── verify.py         # verify (unified run/bundle integrity + --schemas)
├── instrument.py     # standalone instrumentation proxy
├── dashboard.py      # Streamlit dashboard launcher
└── notebook.py       # Jupyter notebook launcher

Each sub-module registers its commands on the appropriate Typer app via decorators. Shared state (CliState) replaces the old global _DRY_RUN flag.
