docs.title: Crucible — EVM Bridge Security Research Testbed
Project Summary
docs.text:
Crucible is a CLI-based research testbed for deterministic reproduction of historical EVM bridge exploits. It forks real chains at specific blocks using Foundry's Anvil, runs attack plugins against the forked state, and automatically captures publication-grade artifacts.

Total project scope: ~15,000 lines of Python across 39 core modules, 10 attack/bridge plugins, 6 benchmark experiments, and 29 documentation files.

docs.title: 1. Chain Forking & Lifecycle Management
docs.text:
The testbed starts and manages Anvil subprocesses for each chain in a topology. It supports 9 topology configurations (single chain, dual chain, bridge topologies, etc.) and a 3-tier reset system:

Tier Speed Method Soft <5s anvil_loadState snapshot restore Medium <30s Kill + relaunch + restore Hard <5min Re-fork from archive RPC

Each fork uses deterministic flags: fixed gas price (0), FIFO transaction ordering, pinned fork-block timestamp, and auto-mining.

docs.title: 2. 6 Reproduced Bridge Exploits ($1.9B+ Total)
docs.text:
All 6 benchmark attacks are complete at their documented fidelity levels:

Attack Chain Damage Fidelity What It Proves Wormhole Guardian Bypass Ethereum $326M L2 Forged VAA accepted via completeTransfer() Nomad Replica Fraud Ethereum $190M L2 process() accepts forged message when committedRoot == 0 Poly Network PrivKey Ethereum $611M L2 Hash collision replaces bridge keeper Horizon Multisig Drain Ethereum $100M L2 3 bridge contracts verified, multisig flow demonstrated BNB Token Hub Proof BSC $570M L2 IAVL proof bypass mechanism documented Multichain Router Permit Multi $1.4B L1 Correctly classified as MPC infrastructure compromise

4 of 5 replayable experiments verified deterministic via two-run diff (storage, events, gas, balances all equal).

docs.title: 3. Auto-Instrumentation RPC Proxy
docs.text:
The architectural centerpiece — a transparent HTTP proxy sits between the attack plugin and Anvil, auto-capturing:

  • Gas usage (per transaction from receipt)
  • Contract events (decoded from receipt logs)
  • Storage diffs (before/after via debug_traceTransaction)
  • Balance changes (ETH/token pre/post state)

Zero instrumentation code in attack plugins. Works transparently with Python, Forge scripts, or cast commands.

docs.title: 4. Plugin SDK (3 Types)
docs.text:
Plugin Type Purpose Count AttackPlugin Exploit reproduction 8 packs BridgePlugin Cross-chain bridge simulation 4 implementations AgentPlugin LLM-driven autonomous agents 2 packs

Plus an LLM agent system with OpenAI, Anthropic, and Ollama providers, 9 agent tools across 3 privilege levels, and full reasoning chain logging.

docs.title: 5. Publication Pipeline
docs.text:
Every experiment run produces:

  • 6 JSONL artifact streams (run log, events, storage diffs, gas profile, balance diffs, cross-chain messages)
  • run_manifest.json — full provenance (git commit, Foundry version, Python version, config fingerprint)
  • log_integrity.json — SHA-256 hashes of all streams
  • report.md — human-readable summary
  • Export bundles — self-contained directories with integrity manifests for publication

7 publication gates enforce evidence completeness, baseline verification, Foundry version pinning, and artifact completeness.

docs.title: 6. Artifact Protection
docs.text: crucible clean --all never touches logs or datasets. Tiered retention (scratch/saved/published) and .protected markers prevent accidental deletion. Datasets survive container restarts via bind mount.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
CLI (crucible/cli.py → commands/)
  ├── Chain Manager — Anvil lifecycle, port management, health polling
  ├── Scenario Engine — YAML-based phase execution (11 step types)
  ├── Experiment Runner — formal run lifecycle, manifest writing
  ├── Plugin Registry — discovery, validation, dynamic loading
  ├── Instrumentation Proxy — transparent RPC capture
  ├── Log Manager — 6 JSONL stream writers + integrity hashing
  ├── Fidelity Enforcer — L0-L4 runtime verification
  └── Publication Gates — 7 checks before export

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text: Component Technology Language Python 3.12 CLI Framework Typer (Click-based) Chain Forking Foundry Anvil Containerization Docker + Compose Data Format JSONL (JSON Lines) Integrity SHA-256 Plugins Dynamic import via importlib LLM Agents OpenAI / Anthropic / Ollama Dashboard Streamlit Testing pytest (808 unit tests)

docs.title: Key Metrics
docs.text: Metric Value Core Python modules 39 CLI commands 20+ Unit tests 808 passing, 0 failing Attack packs 6 benchmarks + 2 demos Bridge plugins 4 (3 real + 1 demo) Topology configs 9 Documentation 29 user-facing docs + case studies Total damage covered ~$3.2B Deterministic experiments 4 of 5 Docker services 6 (orchestrator, dashboard, 4 chain daemons)

docs.title: Development History
docs.text: The project went through 3 major phases:

docs.title: Phase 1: Platform Foundation (v0.2.0)
docs.text: Core infrastructure: chain lifecycle, plugin system, experiment runner, basic artifact capture. ~6,000 lines.

docs.title: Phase 2: Attack Content & Validation (v0.3.0)
docs.text: All 6 benchmark attacks upgraded from scaffolds to working reproductions. 21 bugs found and fixed across 2 validation rounds (20 bugs + 1 enhancement). Determinism verified. Export bundles created.

docs.title: Phase 3: Production Readiness (v0.4.0)
docs.text: Git history cleaned, CI pipeline removed (local-only project), .venv fixed, artifact protection hardened, all 808 tests passing (up from 801 during pre-release). 2 additional bugs found and fixed during final pre-release testing. Documentation website built with MkDocs + Material theme for public GitHub Pages deployment.

docs.title: Deliverables
docs.text: Deliverable Location Status Source code crucible/ ✅ 39 modules Attack plugins attacks/ ✅ 8 packs (6 + 2 demo) Bridge plugins bridges/ ✅ 4 implementations Experiments experiments/ ✅ 12 configs Tests tests/unit/ ✅ 808 passing Documentation docs/ ✅ 29 user-facing + 14 internal Website website/ ✅ MkDocs + Material, ready for GitHub Pages Artifact bundles datasets/ ✅ 4 verified exports Docker Dockerfile + docker-compose.yml ✅ Build + doctor + experiments all pass

docs.title: Limitations & Known Issues
docs.text:
1. RPC dependency — Archive nodes required for historical forks. Ethereum works on Free Ankr tier; BSC/Polygon need paid provider (~$39/mo GetBlock)
  2. Non-EVM boundaries — Crucible is EVM-only. Attacks involving Solana, Cosmos, Harmony, or Substrate chains are modeled, not reproduced
  3. Poly Network determinism — Non-deterministic across 3 chains due to archive RPC variability (expected behavior)
  4. Cross-provider verification — Not done; only one RPC provider available
  5. Local environment — CI pipeline fully removed; all testing is on-device via Docker

Report generated June 2026 — Crucible v0.4.0

docs.title: Crucible — Final Pre-Release Scan (Independent Deep Review)
docs.text: Prepared for: Engineering team Date: 2026-06-15 Reviewed at: working tree on top of c7f48f5 (includes the in-flight SI-7/SI-10 follow-up fixes to crucible/logging.py and crucible/chain_manager.py). Method: Static analysis only — no code executed. Two independent adversarial sweeps (release-blocker correctness; SI-7/SI-10 fix stress-test) plus line-by-line verification of every finding against source. Findings the sweeps raised were not taken on trust — each was confirmed or rejected against the actual code, and the rejections are documented below so the team can see what was checked.

docs.title: Executive summary
docs.text:
The security and scientific-integrity work from prior rounds holds. This final scan went looking for anything those rounds missed and surfaced one genuine release blocker plus four worth-fixing robustness issues. It also caught two false alarms from the automated sweeps, which were disproven against source.

Status Count IDs 🔴 ~~Release blocker~~ → ✅ Fixed 1 B1 🟠 ~~Fix before release~~ → ✅ Fixed 4 B2, C1, M4, M5 🟡 ~~Polish / hardening~~ → ✅ Fixed 2 D1, D2 ⚪ Open robustness (deferred, non-blocking) 1 D4 ✅ Raised by tooling, disproven against source 2 H2, H3 ✅ Additionally checked this pass and found clean 5 assertion→outcome, provenance gating, dataset-counter, external-hash (N3), dangerous patterns

All actionable findings are now fixed. The 🔴 release blocker (B1), the four recommended fixes (B2, C1, M4, M5), and the two polish items (D1, D2) have been addressed and verified (805/805 tests pass, code review clean). The only remaining item is D4 (HTTP read size cap), which was already deferred as non-blocking.

docs.title: B1 — A crash in fidelity enforcement leaves the run marked success with no fidelity record
docs.text:
File: crucible/experiment.py:468-470 (with :431, :459, :491)

except Exception as enf_exc:
    fidelity_failures = [f"Fidelity check error: {enf_exc}"]
    logger.warning("Fidelity enforcement check failed: %s", enf_exc)

When FidelityEnforcer.check() raises any unexpected exception (malformed evidence.yaml, a KeyError/IndexError in a check helper, an I/O error reading run.jsonl, etc.): - fidelity_passed stays None (initialised at :431, never reassigned because the exception fires before :449). - outcome stays "success" — the only downgrade to "failure" is at :459, inside the if not result.passed branch, which is never reached. - At :491, if fidelity_passed is not None: is False, so no fidelity_check block is written to the manifest at all, and the captured fidelity_failures error string is silently dropped.

Impact: a run whose fidelity verification crashed is published with outcome: "success" and a manifest that looks like fidelity was simply never requested. For a USENIX artifact this is the worst case — a claimed-reproduced result that was never actually verified, with no trace in the artifact. Same family as SI-8 (success-but-captured-nothing) and SI-11 (advisory gates).

Fix: in the except, set fidelity_passed = False and outcome = "failure" (and an appropriate non-zero exit_code) so the crash is recorded and the manifest reflects it. Add a regression test that forces check() to raise and asserts outcome == "failure" and a persisted fidelity_check.

docs.title: B2 — A failed reproduction (AttackResult.success == False) can publish as outcome: success
docs.text:
Files: crucible/sdk.py:77-81 (AttackPlugin.run), :99-101 (default verify), :34; crucible/experiment.py:388-393, :444; crucible/enforcer.py:60-63

AttackPlugin.run() does execute() → verify() and raises only when verify_result.passed is False:

result = self.execute(context)          # result.success may be False
verify_result = self.verify(context)
if not verify_result.passed:
    raise AssertionError(...)            # the ONLY failure signal
return result                            # result.success is never checked

Two gaps compound: 1. result.success is never gated. execute() returns AttackResult(success: bool) — the plugin's own determination of whether the exploit reproduced. run() ignores it; experiment.py only console-prints it (:389) and passes it to the enforcer (:444), where _check_result_available checks only is None (enforcer.py:62), not .success. It is not persisted to the manifest. 2. Default verify() always passes — VerificationResult(passed=True) (sdk.py:101). A plugin that doesn't override verify() with real expected_state assertions never raises.

Impact: an attack plugin whose execute() returns success=False (the exploit did not reproduce) but which relies on the default verify() will have its run recorded as outcome: "success", pass fidelity (L2 only checks that artifact streams exist), and be exportable — with the success=False self-report discarded entirely. A negative result is published as a positive one. This is the same false-success family as B1 and SI-8, and it's unsafe-by-default (the path of least resistance for a plugin author is the unsafe one).

Severity: MEDIUM — depends on plugins defining real verify() assertions, but the default behavior and the silent discard of success=False make it a genuine scientific-honesty gap.

Fix: in run(), also fail or explicitly record when result.success is False (e.g. raise unless the plugin opted into "negative result expected"); and persist attack_result.success / attack_result.error into run_manifest.json so the exploit outcome is always visible regardless of gating. Consider making the base verify() return passed=False (or abstract) so "no assertions" is not silently "passed."

docs.title: C1 — Cache invalidation ignores a customized fork_cache.path; invalidate() can be a no-op against anvil's real cache
docs.text:
Files: crucible/cache.py:79,216 vs crucible/chain_manager.py:86; default crucible/config.py:52

CacheManager.__init__ hardcodes self._cache_base = self.workspace / ".fork_cache", and CacheManager is constructed with only (workspace, policy) — it never receives config.fork_cache.path. Meanwhile anvil's --cache-path is self.workspace / self.config.fork_cache.path / name / str(fork_block or "latest").

  • Default config is safe: ForkCacheConfig.path defaults to ".fork_cache", so the two coincide and invalidate() (shutil.rmtree) does delete anvil's cache.
  • Customized path breaks it: if a user sets fork_cache.path to anything else in testbed.yaml, CacheManager targets .fork_cache/... while anvil reads/writes <custom>/.... Verify, invalidate, and the manifest all operate on the wrong tree → the SI-10 strict-policy invalidation silently does nothing and stale forked state is reused.
  • Edge: for fork_block == 0, anvil uses str(0 or "latest") → "latest" while CacheManager uses "0" — directory mismatch even under default config.

Fix: pass config.fork_cache.path into CacheManager and derive _cache_base from it (or assert it equals .fork_cache). Use a consistent fork_block/"latest" key on both sides.

docs.title: M4 — Instrumentation teardown is not exception-safe; one proxy failure leaks the rest and can skip artifact finalization
docs.text:
File: crucible/chain_manager.py:406-409 (called from experiment.py:404/:664, both inside finally)

for name, proxy in self._proxies.items():
    proxy.drain()
    proxy.stop()
self._proxies.clear()

No per-proxy try/except. If drain()/stop() raises for one chain, chains 2..N are never drained or stopped — their HTTPServer threads and ThreadPoolExecutors leak for the process lifetime. Worse, because this runs in the experiment finally before write_report/write_integrity/write_artifact_manifest (experiment.py:508-516), an exception here both masks the original experiment exception and skips all artifact finalization (no log_integrity.json, no bundle manifest). Most relevant to the multi-chain L4 bridge experiments the tool targets.

Fix: wrap each proxy's drain()/stop() in its own try/except that logs and continues, so every proxy is torn down and finalization always runs.

docs.title: M5 — crucible verify <archive> extracts tarballs without a safe filter (path traversal on Python 3.11/3.12)
docs.text:
File: crucible/commands/verify.py:43,46

with tarfile.open(path, "r:gz") as tar:
    tar.extractall(tmp)

extractall is called with no filter= argument. The project supports python >=3.11 (pyproject.toml); on 3.11/3.12 the default tar extraction filter is the legacy unsafe behavior, so a crafted bundle with ../ members, absolute paths, or symlinks can write outside the temp directory. crucible verify is explicitly meant to validate third-party bundles from reviewers — i.e. untrusted input. (On 3.14 the default is already 'data', so current dev machines are incidentally safe, but shipped support includes 3.11/3.12.) The .zip path via shutil.unpack_archive is safe — zipfile sanitizes traversal.

Fix: pass filter="data" to both extractall calls (available 3.9+).

docs.title: D1 — SI-10 manifest docstring overstates what readiness proves
docs.text:
File: crucible/chain_manager.py:281-285

The new comment says the manifest "attests that a successfully populated cache exists." But _await_ready (:333) only polls eth_blockNumber, which anvil answers as soon as its RPC server is up — before it has lazily fetched/cached forked state (anvil caches on-demand per call). The manifest reliably attests config/provenance identity, not cache completeness. Soften the wording (or add an actual populated-cache check if completeness attestation is desired).

docs.title: D2 — SI-7 byte-identical guarantee is intra-platform only
docs.text:
File: crucible/logging.py (emit open() and canonicalize_stream's write_text)

Log handles are opened without newline=, so Windows writes and Linux ; sha256_file hashes raw bytes. Two identical runs on the same OS are byte-identical (the SI-7 goal — met). Two runs on different OSes are not, and a bundle produced on Windows then verified on Linux (or via the verify extract path) could mismatch on line endings. If "reproducible across machines" is a publication claim, pin newline=" " on the emit open() and on write_text.

docs.title: D4 — External HTTP responses are read without a size cap
docs.text:
Files: crucible/rpc.py:78, crucible/abi.py:115,136, crucible/llm_providers.py:199,322

json.loads(response.read().decode("utf-8")) reads the full body with no maximum. A malicious or misbehaving archive RPC / block-explorer API / LLM endpoint could return a huge body and exhaust memory. Risk is bounded in practice because these endpoints are operator-configured (you point Crucible at your own RPC/Etherscan/OpenAI), which is why this was deferred in earlier rounds — but it's the one remaining defense-in-depth gap. Cap with a bounded response.read(MAX_BYTES) and reject oversized bodies (mirrors the proxy's existing _MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH guard).

docs.title: ✅ Raised by automated sweeps, then disproven against source
docs.text:
These are documented so the team can see they were investigated, not skipped.

  • H2 — "a one-sided / coincidentally-empty stream can still report *_equal: true." False. In crucible/diff.py, _check_empty marks a stream incomparable only when both sides are empty, yielding verdict None — not True. A one-empty/one-full case compares [] == [x] → False. There is no path that produces a false true from an empty capture.
  • H3 — "balance-diff equality can be a false negative due to unstable sort." Neutralised by the SI-7 fix. balance_diffs.jsonl is now full-line canonically sorted on disk before diff reads it, and Python's stable sort preserves that order for ties → identical content compares equal across runs. Storage equality additionally compares the full dict including before, so no false-equal either.

docs.title: What was checked and found clean
docs.text:
• Assertion → run outcome is sound. Scenario assert steps raise AssertionFailed (scenario_steps.py:145,240,242,515); scenario.run → _run_step → handle_step has no swallowing try/except, so a failed assertion propagates to experiment.py:396 → outcome = "failure". (The separate B2 gap is about AttackResult.success, not scenario assertions.)
  • Provenance fail-open is properly gated. git_commit == "unknown" fails check_provenance (publication.py:143-152) and check_git_provenance (:198-216); a dirty tree warns. Per the SI-11 fix these gates block in publication mode. Outside publication mode an unknown/dirty manifest is allowed by design.
  • Dataset-counter race is not reachable. next_dataset_id (ids.py) has a documented TOCTOU window, but run_matrix executes experiments sequentially (matrix.py:51, plain for loop, no thread/process pool), so duplicate IDs can't occur as shipped. Worth a file lock only if parallel matrix execution is ever added.
  • External-hash anchor is consumed (old N3 closed). verify_bundle reads <bundle>.sha256 from the parent dir and folds external_hash_valid into the verdict (export.py:278-302), so it no longer trusts only the in-bundle manifest.
  • No dangerous execution patterns. Repo-wide grep: no shell=True, eval(, exec(, pickle.load, or unsafe yaml.load. The only extractall is M5.
  • SI-7 fix (stream canonicalization) is sound: canonicalize-then-hash is idempotent (no line drop/dup; unparseable lines preserved), all trace workers are drained (drain() → executor.shutdown(wait=True), plus the SI-9 accept-flag) before canonicalization, and verify re-hashes the same canonicalized bytes. Sorting also repairs latent order-sensitivity in the gas/events diff comparison. Covered by new tests in tests/unit/test_logging.py.
  • SI-10 fix (manifest write moved after readiness) is sound: a crashed/timed-out fork now leaves no manifest, so the next run rebuilds (subject to the C1 path caveat above).
  • All 13 scientific-integrity findings (SI-1…SI-13) remain closed — see SCIENTIFIC_INTEGRITY_AUDIT.md.

docs.title: Resolved action items
docs.text:
Items 1–6 are now fixed and verified (805/805 unit tests pass, code review clean):

  1. B1 ✅ — fidelity crash now sets fidelity_passed=False, outcome="failure", exit_code=ASSERTION_FAILED
  2. B2 ✅ — AttackResult.success==False gated (outcome→failure); persisted to manifest
  3. C1 ✅ — CacheManager accepts cache_base= parameter; all call sites pass config.fork_cache.path; fork_block==0 key fixed
  4. M4 ✅ — per-proxy try/except in stop_instrumentation; use list(self._proxies.keys()) for safe iteration
  5. M5 ✅ — filter="data" on both tar.extractall() calls
  6. D1 / D2 ✅ — docstring softened; newline="\n" pinned for cross-platform byte-identical artifacts

D4 (HTTP read size cap) remains deferred as non-blocking — risk is bounded because all endpoints are operator-configured.

At this point the release candidate is credible; the scientific-integrity, security, and correctness posture is solid.

Static review only. No code was executed and no files other than this report were modified in producing it. Line numbers reflect the working tree at the time of review.

docs.title: Crucible — Comprehensive Findings & Improvement Plan
docs.text: Date: June 9, 2026 Audit scope: Full codebase review — architecture, modularity, correctness, test coverage, documentation, UX Test suite: 754 unit tests passing, 0 failures, 0 skipped

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
Crucible is a well-architected EVM bridge security research testbed. The 4-layer architecture (Foundation → Runtime → Orchestration → CLI) is cleanly separated. The instrumentation proxy is the standout feature — making artifact capture automatic and universal. The plugin SDK, scenario engine, fidelity levels, and publication gates provide the academic rigor needed for USENIX-grade research.

The codebase is production-quality in its core paths but has specific gaps in completeness and polish that would elevate it from "functional" to "exceptional."

docs.title: Architecture & Design
docs.text: Aspect Assessment Layer separation Excellent — L1 (stdlib-only) through L4 (CLI) with clear import rules Plugin system Well-designed ABCs: AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, AgentPlugin Instrumentation proxy Architectural centerpiece — transparent, automatic artifact capture Scenario engine Registry pattern for step handlers (decorator-based) is extensible Fidelity levels (L0-L4) Enforced programmatically, not just documentation Configuration Pydantic models, deep merge, env expansion — robust Provenance tracking Git commit, platform, config fingerprint — all captured Determinism by default Opt-out design is correct for reproducibility

docs.title: Implementation Quality
docs.text: Aspect Assessment Type hints Comprehensive — from __future__ import annotations throughout Docstrings Detailed with examples, Args/Returns/Raises Error handling CrucibleError hierarchy with exit codes Process safety PID validation, anvil-only kill guard, port retry with backoff Concurrency ThreadPoolExecutor for traces, threading.Lock for recording Cross-platform Windows taskkill fallback, POSIX/Windows signal handling Test coverage 754 tests, 0 failures — mocking is thorough

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Doc Quality ARCHITECTURE.md Excellent — Mermaid diagrams, layer rules, data flow TUTORIAL.md Step-by-step Nomad reproduction — 8 commands ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Complete plugin creation guide CLI_REFERENCE.md All commands documented BENCHMARKS.md Attack pack matrix with status

docs.title: 🎯 Goal Alignment Assessment
docs.text:
How well does Crucible meet the user's stated goals?

Goal Score Assessment "Easy like defihacklabs" 6/10 DefiHackLabs is single-click. Crucible requires RPC config → init → fork → experiment run. The CLI is scriptable but has a steeper initial setup. The --from-tx auto-extract feature bridges this gap partially. "Researcher just needs to sit in attacker seat" 8/10 Once configured, crucible experiment run experiments/something.yaml is indeed one command. The "sit in attacker seat" experience is good for attack plugins. Less so for agent plugins (need API keys). "Don't worry about artifact collection" 9/10 This is Crucible's strongest feature. The instrumentation proxy captures everything automatically. Artifact integrity hashing closes the loop. The agent_reasoning.jsonl gap is the only exception. "One-click reset for multi-phase experiments" 8/10 3-tier reset (soft/medium/hard) is well-designed. Soft reset (<5s via anvil_loadState) is the "one-click" experience. Hard reset (<5 min for cold cache) could use more progress feedback. "Granular/modular lego-like architecture" 8/10 Plugin system, step registry, and topology-driven composition are very modular. The ExperimentRunner god-object is the main anti-modularity concern. "Multi-chain flexibility" 7/10 Topology system supports single/dual/multi-chain declaratively. But only 1 bridge plugin exists (demo_bridge). L4 multi-chain fidelity is declared but not validated with a real bridge plugin. "CLI-first with optional UI wrapper" 9/10 Every action is available via CLI. The Streamlit dashboard is a complementary viewer. This is well executed. "Automation for repetitive tasks" 7/10 Cleanup, reset, and baseline are automated. Matrix experiments cover parameter sweeps. Missing: automated baseline monitoring, cron-based cleanup, GitHub Actions CI/CD. "Comprehensive structured logging for research purposes" 9/10 7 JSONL streams with per-chain isolation, SHA-256 integrity hashing, and cross-chain correlation IDs. The agent_reasoning.jsonl integrity gap is the only weakness. "USENIX-grade research rigor" 8/10 Fidelity levels are enforced programmatically. Publication gates (7 checks) enforce completeness. Provenance tracks everything. Missing: strict schema validation at write time (not just at export). "Low learning curve" 5/10 Tutorial is good but the tool has 20+ CLI commands, 4 config file types, 3 plugin types, 2 output formats, and 2 deployment modes. A new researcher faces a significant surface area.

Overall Goal Alignment Score: 7.6/10

The testbed is strongest where it matters most (artifact collection, modularity, multi-chain) but the initial setup and learning curve need attention for the "easy like defihacklabs" aspiration.

docs.title: 1a. ExperimentRunner has too many responsibilities
docs.text:
File: crucible/experiment.py — 638 lines

The ExperimentRunner class handles: - Config loading - Plugin loading (with agent/attack preference logic) - Foundry version checking - Cleanup enforcement - Evidence validation - Publication gates - Baseline verification - Run orchestration (bridge deploy → scenario → instrumentation → plugin → fidelity → manifest) - Manifest writing - Cross-chain message tracking - Export - Matrix running - Run lookup

This is a god-object pattern. The run() method alone spans ~200 lines with try/except/finally containing multiple phases. Making changes here is risky — the tests (22 tests) provide good coverage but don't exercise every path.

Recommendation: Extract into separate concerns: - ExperimentRunner.run() orchestrator → keep thin, delegate to phase managers - New PhaseRunner class for each phase - Move _load_plugin() to PluginRegistry - Move _validate_evidence_against_chain() to evidence.py (partially done already)

docs.title: 1b. Agent reasoning logs bypass integrity hashing
docs.text:
File: crucible/agent_loop.py, crucible/logging.py

The 7th artifact stream (agent_reasoning.jsonl) is written directly by LLMAgentPlugin via _log_reasoning_step(), NOT through the LogEmitter system. This means: - It's NOT included in log_integrity.json hashing - It's NOT tracked in STREAM_SCHEMAS in schema.py - A corrupted agent reasoning log would go undetected

Recommendation: Route agent reasoning through LogEmitter.emit() with a dedicated stream, or at minimum include it in write_integrity() and STREAM_SCHEMAS.

docs.title: 1c. No integration tests for the agent system
docs.text:
Files: All agent modules — agent_loop.py (480 lines), agent_tools.py (298 lines), llm_providers.py (399 lines)

The agent system has unit tests (94 in test_llm_providers.py + agent tests) but zero integration tests that verify: - An actual LLM provider integration end-to-end - Agent tools actually interact with a running fork correctly - Agent reasoning logs are properly written and retrievable

This matters because the agent system is the second major use case (after attack plugins).

docs.title: 2a. The resume() path is untested
docs.text:
File: crucible/experiment.py — resume() method (~100 lines) Tests: Zero

The resume feature (restarting interrupted experiments from checkpoints) has no unit tests. It's a complex method that involves: - Loading checkpoint manifests - Restoring chain state from snapshots - Skipping completed phases - Writing updated manifests with resume metadata

A regression here would silently produce incorrect results.

docs.title: 2b. Bridge plugin coverage is thin
docs.text:
Files: crucible/bridge_lifecycle.py, bridges/demo_bridge/

Only one bridge plugin (demo_bridge) exists. The BridgePlugin ABC has 7 methods but bridge_lifecycle.py only exercises deploy and destroy. The send_message, configure, connect, and reset methods are never tested in integration.

Recommendation: Implement at least one real bridge plugin (e.g., Nomad's xApp or Wormhole's core bridge) to validate the ABC design before publishing. This is important because the multi-chain L4 fidelity claim depends on bridge plugins working correctly.

docs.title: 2c. No hermetic end-to-end test with real fork
docs.text:
File: tests/e2e/test_hermetic_nomad.py

The E2E test exists but uses a JSONL fixture (recorded RPC calls via ReplayServer from crucible/recording.py) instead of actually forking from an archive node. This is fine for CI but means the test doesn't validate the full pipeline: - Config loading → chain forking → scenario execution → instrumentation → artifact capture

A true hermetic E2E test would: 1. Start an Anvil fork at a known block (using a cached state fixture) 2. Run an experiment end-to-end 3. Verify all 7 artifact streams exist with expected content 4. Assert manifest fields are correct 5. Export and verify the bundle

Note: crucible/recording.py (ReplayServer + record_fixture) is well-implemented and provides good infrastructure for this. The gap is in using it for a full pipeline test rather than just replaying RPC calls.

docs.title: 3a. CLI output still has silent gaps
docs.text: Addressed partially (progress spinners in library modules), but: - crucible resume has no progress indicators during chain state restoration - crucible export does not validate the bundle until after creation — user waits for archive creation before finding errors - crucible baseline verify doesn't show per-chain progress when checking multiple chains

docs.title: 3b. Docker compose health checks missing
docs.text:
File: docker-compose.yml

The docker-compose configuration has depends_on but no healthcheck directives. If an Anvil daemon starts slowly, the orchestrator will try to connect before it's ready. The _await_ready method handles this at the Python level, but Docker Compose would show no status of the delay.

docs.title: 3c. No CONTRIBUTING.md or development setup guide
docs.text: While documentation is extensive for users, there's no concise guide for new contributors on: - Setting up a development environment - Running tests - Code style conventions (Ruff config exists but isn't documented) - How to add a new step type to the scenario engine - How to add a new CLI command

docs.title: 3d. pyproject.toml had inconsistent dependency grouping — FIXED
docs.text: pandas and streamlit were in both [project.dependencies] and [project.optional-dependencies]. Now they only appear in main deps (which is correct since the dashboard imports them at module level). The redundant entries in optional groups (analysis, ui) have been removed.

docs.title: 3e. agent_reasoning.jsonl stream not in schema
docs.text:
File: crucible/schema.py

The STREAM_SCHEMAS dict has 6 streams but not agent_reasoning.jsonl. This means: - The schema validator won't catch corruption in agent reasoning logs - Export bundle validation skips it - It's an invisible gap for agent-based experiments

docs.title: 4a. crucible/analysis.py — Public API surface is adequate but undocumented
docs.text:
File: crucible/analysis.py — 105 lines

Provides 7 public functions for loading experiment data into pandas DataFrames: - find_run(), load_run(), list_runs() — run discovery - load_events(), load_gas(), load_storage_diffs(), load_balance_diffs(), load_cross_chain() — artifact loading

Gaps: - No load_agent_reasoning() function (consistent with the integrity gap) - No load_balance_diffs() documented in the module docstring (it exists but is missing from the docstring's "Available functions" list) - No type annotations for returned DataFrames (columns vary, but a documented minimum schema would help) - The module's _load_jsonl_df() helper loads ALL entries — for large runs with thousands of storage diffs, this could be memory-intensive. Consider adding pagination or filter parameters.

Recommendation: Add documented column schemas for each DataFrame, add load_agent_reasoning(), and consider lazy-loading or chunked reading for large artifact streams.

docs.title: 4b. crucible/interfaces/__init__.py — Public API is thin
docs.text:
File: crucible/interfaces/__init__.py — 4 lines

Re-exports only 4 classes: AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, CruciblePlugin, ExperimentContext.

Gaps: - Missing: VerificationResult, FidelityResult, GateResult — useful for plugin authors writing custom verification - Missing: ChainRuntime, JsonRpcClient — plugins frequently need these - Missing: AgentPlugin — not re-exported despite being a first-class plugin type - Missing: LogEmitter, LogManager — for plugins that need advanced logging

Currently, plugin authors must import from crucible.sdk, crucible.rpc, crucible.chain_manager directly. A richer interfaces/__init__.py would make the SDK surface clear and stable.

Recommendation: Expand the public API to include all types a plugin author needs (e.g., VerificationResult, FidelityResult, ChainRuntime, JsonRpcClient, AgentPlugin, LogEmitter) and document the stable API surface.

docs.title: 4c. crucible/recording.py — Well-implemented infrastructure
docs.text:
File: crucible/recording.py — 175 lines

Provides ReplayServer and record_fixture for hermetic CI testing. The implementation is clean: - Thread-based HTTP server with daemon thread - Fixture lookup via canonical method+params key - Helpful error message when fixture entry is not found (lists available methods) - Suppressed default logging

No issues found. The module is well-designed and serves its purpose. It's used by tests/e2e/test_hermetic_nomad.py.

docs.title: 5. Test Coverage Gaps
docs.text: Module Tests Risk experiment.py 22 unit Good — all critical paths covered chain_manager.py 49 unit Good — process safety well tested resume() 0 High — complex, untested bridge_lifecycle.py 0 Medium — deploying bridge plugins scenario.py 1 unit Low-medium — step dispatch via registry scenario_steps.py 27 unit Good — 11 step types covered export.py 18 unit Good Hermetic E2E 1 Medium — uses fixture, not real fork

docs.title: Phase A — Core Hardening (1-2 weeks)
docs.text: Priority Item Effort Impact P0 Extract ExperimentRunner into focused classes 2 days Reduces risk for all future changes P0 Add agent_reasoning.jsonl to integrity hashing [ok] Done Closes data integrity gap P0 Add agent_reasoning.jsonl to schema validation [ok] Done Catches corruption P1 Write unit tests for resume() [ok] Done 7 tests added, all passing P1 Write integration test for agent system (LLM → tool → fork) 2 days Validates agent pipeline

docs.title: Phase B — Bridge & Multi-Chain (1-2 weeks)
docs.text: Priority Item Effort Impact P1 Implement real bridge plugin (Nomad xApp or Wormhole) 3 days Validates BridgePlugin ABC P1 Integration test for multi-chain + bridge experiment 2 days Validates L4 path P2 E2E test with real Anvil fork (cached state fixture) 2 days True pipeline validation

docs.title: Phase C — Polish & DX (1 week)
docs.text: Priority Item Effort Impact P2 Make pandas import lazy in monitoring.py 1 hour Keeps CLI install lean P2 Clean up redundant optional deps in pyproject.toml [ok] Done Clean dependency spec P2 Expand crucible/interfaces/__init__.py public API [ok] Done 13 exports, clear SDK surface P3 Add load_agent_reasoning() to analysis.py [ok] Done Completeness P3 Add progress indicators to resume, export, verify 1 day UX consistency

docs.title: Phase D — Strategic (Month-long)
docs.text: Priority Item Effort Impact P2 Hermetic E2E test with real Anvil 2 days CI-readiness P2 Add more attack packs (L3/L4) 5 days Demonstrates full capability P3 Documentation site (e.g., mkdocs) 3 days Better discoverability P3 Benchmark suite for timing/shape 2 days Performance regression detection

docs.title: 1. Visual Diff Dashboard
docs.text: Currently crucible diff outputs text. A Streamlit-based visual diff comparing two runs side-by-side (storage slots, event timelines, balance flows) would dramatically improve the researcher experience. Think: "Git diff but for blockchain experiments."

docs.title: 2. Attack Pack Registry / Marketplace
docs.text: A discoverability tool: crucible attack search "bridge" that lists known attack packs from a public registry (GitHub-based or simple YAML index). Researchers could share reproductions like packages.

docs.title: 3. REPL Mode for Interactive Experimentation
docs.text: crucible shell — an interactive Python REPL with: - Pre-loaded context, chains, rpc objects - Tab completion for chain names, RPC methods - Auto-instrumentation of every transaction - History persistence - "Record → Export" workflow

docs.title: 4. Automated Baseline Drift Detection
docs.text: Currently baselines capture state at a point in time. A crucible baseline monitor command that periodically checks for baseline drift would alert researchers when: - Their local modifications have drifted from the golden state - RPC provider changes have caused block differences - Cache corruption has occurred

docs.title: 5. Fuzzing Engine Integration
docs.text:
Plug in a fuzzer (like Echidna or Medusa) as a step type: 

- type: fuzz
  contract: "0x..."
  corpus: [tx1, tx2]
  duration_seconds: 300

This would make Crucible not just a reproduction tool but a vulnerability discovery tool.

docs.title: 6. Self-Contained Experiment Snapshots
docs.text:
Export experiments as a single portable .crucible file containing: - The experiment YAML - The chain state snapshot (compressed JSON or Anvil cache) - The attack plugin code - The scenario YAML

Share via: crucible experiment share DS-2026-00042 → nomad_repro.crucible Replay via: crucible experiment run nomad_repro.crucible

No RPC needed, no git clone needed. One file, one command.

docs.title: 7. USENIX Artifact Packaging Shortcut
docs.text:
A single command that outputs a usenix-artifact.zip ready for submission: 

crucible publish --package usenix --run DS-2026-00042 --output usenix-artifact.zip

It would auto-generate: - README.md with submission instructions - Dockerfile (if using Docker mode) - runs/ directory with run manifest + all artifacts - BIBLIOGRAPHY.md with citations from evidence.yaml - CHECKLIST.md with the AE checklist

docs.title: 8. Interactive Scenario Builder
docs.text:
crucible scenario init with an interactive wizard that: - Asks which chains to use - Asks what setup steps are needed (impersonate, send_tx, assert) - Generates the scenario YAML with proper structure - Opens it in the default editor

This would lower the learning curve significantly — currently, scenario authoring requires knowing the YAML schema by heart.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• docs/REPRODUCIBILITY.md references fidelity levels but doesn't include the table — add it inline
  • docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md should be verified — does the fork command section document --fork-timeout?
  • docs/INSTRUMENTATION_ARCHITECTURE.md is excellent but would benefit from a sequence diagram showing the proxy interaction flow
  • Tutorial mentions 8 commands — number each step for clarity

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text:
• experiment.py had import sys that's now correctly removed (replaced by progress.get_console()) [ok]
  • Some docstrings in scenario_steps.py use Args: blocks but don't include all parameters
  • enforcer.py _check_chain_state() correctly accesses self.historical.get("chains", []) where self.historical is the historical_scope dict from evidence.yaml [ok]
  • analysis.py module docstring doesn't document the public function API at all (single-sentence description)
  • analysis.py doesn't have load_agent_reasoning() — requires the P0 integrity fix first (adding agent_reasoning.jsonl to LogEmitter/integrity hashing)

docs.title: Test Quality
docs.text:
• test_experiment.py tests are well-structured with fixtures and helpers
  • test_chain_manager.py is thorough — 49 tests covering all process safety paths
  • test_monitoring.py tests are good but depended on pandas at import time (now fixed by installing pandas) [ok]
  • Some tests use from module import function inside test functions (lazy import) — this is fine but inconsistent with the project pattern

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• _trace_transaction in instrumentation.py has retry + backoff — good
  • CacheManager.fingerprint() re-reads all cache state — could be slow with large caches
  • ExperimentRunner.run() scans all run.jsonl files in the finally block — this is O(files) which is fine for typical runs
  • analysis.py _load_jsonl_df() loads ALL entries into memory — for runs with millions of storage diffs, this could be an issue

docs.title: 📊 Summary
docs.text: Dimension Score (1-10) Notes Architecture 9 Clean layers, but ExperimentRunner is a god object Modularity 8 Plugin system is excellent; step registry is extensible; interfaces/__init__.py is thin Correctness 9 754 tests passing; process safety is well handled Test coverage 7 Core paths covered; resume/agent-integration untested Documentation 9 Extensive; missing CONTRIBUTING.md, CLI_REFERENCE.md has minor gaps UX 7 Spinners help; residual silent gaps; no REPL; learning curve is significant Security 9 PID guards, anvil-only kill, EOA checks Reproducibility 9 Fidelity enforcement, provenance, integrity hashing (except agent_reasoning.jsonl) Goal Alignment 7.6 Strongest on artifact collection (9/10); weakest on learning curve (5/10) Overall 8.2 Production-ready core with polish gaps in secondary paths

docs.title: [ok] Completed Fixes
docs.text:
Fix Files Changed Status Agent reasoning integrity hashing agent_loop.py, logging.py, schema.py [ok] Unit tests for resume() tests/unit/test_experiment.py [ok] 7 new tests Expanded public SDK surface crucible/interfaces/__init__.py [ok] 4→13 exports load_agent_reasoning() + improved docstring crucible/analysis.py [ok] Removed redundant optional deps pyproject.toml [ok]

Test suite: 761 passing, 0 failures, 1 warning.

Generated by comprehensive codebase audit — 761 tests passing, 0 failures.

docs.title: Toward a Modular, Researcher-First Blockchain Security Testbed
docs.text: Status: Planning | Date: June 2026 | Audience: Research team & contributors

docs.title: 1. Vision
docs.text:
Crucible should feel like this:

The researcher sits in the attacker's seat, writes or runs exploit logic, and Crucible automatically forks the right state, instruments every action, records publication-grade artifacts, and leaves the researcher with a complete dataset ready for analysis.

The system must remain: - EVM-only — no scope creep into non-EVM chains - Modular and plug-and-play — new capabilities arrive as plugins, not core rewrites - Easy to extend — researchers should not need to be framework engineers - USENIX-grade — strong enough for artifact evaluation and reproducibility requirements

docs.title: 2.1 Researcher-First UX
docs.text:
The framework should not make researchers think about internal plumbing.

Target workflow: 

1. crucible init
2. crucible doctor
3. crucible attack init my_attack --template historical-single
4. Write exploit logic in attack.py or Exploit.s.sol
5. crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack_replay.yaml
6. crucible export <run_id>

docs.title: 2.2 Instrumentation by Default
docs.text: All formal experiment runs must be instrumented automatically. The researcher should not need to opt into logging or tracing.

docs.title: 2.3 Plugins for Capabilities
docs.text: New capabilities arrive as plugins — no core rewrites: - attack — exploit reproduction plugins (current) - bridge — bridge protocol simulation (current) - agent — LLM-driven autonomous agents (new) - analyzer — post-run analysis extensions (future)

docs.title: 2.4 Strong Defaults, Optional Overrides
docs.text: If a researcher does nothing special, Crucible still: - Collects all artifacts (gas, events, storage diffs, balance diffs) - Produces a human-readable report - Hashes outputs for integrity verification - Preserves full provenance (git, versions, fingerprints) - Supports reruns and canonical comparison

docs.title: 3.1 New Plugin Type: AgentPlugin
docs.text:
Purpose: Allow an LLM-based or scripted autonomous agent to operate the testbed as an attacker, research assistant, or fuzz-style explorer.

New SDK interface (crucible/sdk.py):

class AgentPlugin(CruciblePlugin):
    """Interface for AI/autonomous agent modules."""

    def metadata(self) -> dict[str, Any]: ...

    def setup(self, context: ExperimentContext, tools: AgentToolbox) -> None:
        """Called once before run(). Agent can inspect environment."""

    def run(self, context: ExperimentContext, tools: AgentToolbox) -> AgentRunResult:
        """Main agent loop — observe, reason, act, repeat."""

    def verify(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> VerificationResult:
        """Post-run assertions."""

    def cleanup(self, context: ExperimentContext) -> None:
        """Teardown."""

New result type (crucible/sdk.py):

@dataclass
class AgentRunResult:
    success: bool
    actions: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
    observations: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
    error: str | None = None

docs.title: 3.2 Agent Tool Layer
docs.text:
New file: crucible/agent_tools.py

Exposes safe, structured tools to AI agents:

Tool Description list_chains() Available chains in current topology get_balance(chain, account) ETH or token balance get_storage(chain, address, slot) Storage slot value get_code(chain, address) Contract bytecode call(chain, to, data) eth_call (read-only) impersonate(chain, account) anvil_impersonateAccount send_tx(chain, tx) eth_sendTransaction advance_time(chain, seconds) evm_increaseTime + evm_mine assert_balance(...) Check balance condition

Design rule: Every tool call must: 1. Execute the underlying action 2. Auto-emit a semantic log entry 3. Attach structured metadata for analysis

docs.title: 3.3 Agent Execution in Experiment Runner
docs.text:
Files to change: crucible/experiment.py, crucible/config.py, crucible/plugin.py

The experiment runner gains an optional agent path alongside attack:

experiment.run()
  → load scenario
  → start instrumentation proxies (always)
  → if experiment.attack: load AttackPlugin → attack.run(context)
  → if experiment.agent:  load AgentPlugin → agent.run(context, tools)
  → drain instrumentation
  → write manifest + report + integrity
  → always

ExperimentConfig gains optional agent field:

class ExperimentConfig(BaseModel):
    name: str
    scenario: str
    attack: str | None = None
    agent: str | None = None      # NEW
    topology: str = "single"
    ...

docs.title: 3.4 Semantic Auto-Logging
docs.text:
New file: crucible/action_log.py

Introduce an ActionRecorder that wraps all high-level actions with automatic semantic logging:

Action Auto-logged fields impersonate chain, account, timestamp send_tx chain, from, to, data_preview, value, tx_hash, role advance_time chain, seconds, new_timestamp assert_balance chain, account, min_value, actual_value forge_script script, chain, sender, exit_code

Files to change: crucible/scenario_steps.py, crucible/experiment.py

Every registered step handler auto-emits step_declared → step_started → step_completed / step_failed. This removes the need for manual context.log.step() in most scenarios.

docs.title: 3.5 Instrumentation Config Block
docs.text:
File to change: crucible/config.py

Add first-class instrumentation settings:

class InstrumentationConfig(BaseModel):
    enabled: bool = True
    auto_start: bool = True
    collect_events: bool = True
    collect_storage_diffs: bool = True
    collect_balance_diffs: bool = True
    collect_gas_profile: bool = True
    trace_timeout_seconds: int = 10
    max_workers: int = 10

docs.title: 3.6 Attack Scaffolding — crucible attack init
docs.text:
New file: crucible/attack_templates.py

Command:

crucible attack init my_attack --template <type>

Template types:

Template Best for Generates historical-single One-chain exploit (DeFiHackLabs style) plugin.yaml, evidence.yaml, attack.py, scenario.yaml, experiment.yaml historical-bridge Dual-chain bridge exploit Same + bridge config tx-replay Extract from tx hash Uses existing attack extract + scaffold foundry-poc Solidity-first workflow Exploit.s.sol + forge config ai-agent LLM-driven agent agent plugin.yaml + agent.py

Output: 

attacks/<name>/
  plugin.yaml
  evidence.yaml
  attack.py
scenarios/<name>_pre_exploit/
  scenario.yaml
experiments/<name>_replay.yaml

docs.title: 3.7 Declarative Assertions
docs.text:
New file: crucible/assertions.py

Move standard postconditions out of Python where possible:

# attacks/<name>/assertions.yaml
assertions:
  - type: balance_increased
    chain: ethereum
    account: "0xAttacker"
    token: "0xWETH"
    min_delta: 1000000000000000000
  - type: slot_changed
    chain: ethereum
    contract: "0xBridge"
    slot: "0x01"
    expected_after: "0x01"

This makes packs more declarative and easier for non-framework engineers.

docs.title: 3.8 Analysis Ergonomics
docs.text:
Restore file: crucible/analysis.py

Convenience functions for post-run analysis:

def load_run(run_id: str) -> pd.DataFrame: ...
def load_events(run_id: str) -> pd.DataFrame: ...
def load_storage_diffs(run_id: str) -> pd.DataFrame: ...
def load_gas(run_id: str) -> pd.DataFrame: ...
def load_balance_diffs(run_id: str) -> pd.DataFrame: ...
def load_manifest(run_id: str) -> dict: ...
def compare_runs(run_a: str, run_b: str) -> dict: ...

docs.title: 3.9 Unified Artifact Index
docs.text:
New file in crucible/logging.py or new file crucible/report_index.py

Generate a machine-readable artifact_index.json alongside the report:

{
  "run_id": "DS-2026-00001",
  "chains": ["ethereum", "polygon"],
  "streams": {
    "run.jsonl": {"entries": 47, "path": "run.jsonl"},
    "events.jsonl": {"entries": 12, "path": "chain_ethereum/events.jsonl"},
    "gas_profile.jsonl": {"entries": 8, "path": "chain_ethereum/gas_profile.jsonl"}
  },
  "tx_hashes": ["0xabc...", "0xdef..."],
  "contracts_touched": ["0xBridge", "0xToken"]
}

docs.title: Canonical docs (keep + maintain)
docs.text: Doc Audience README.md Everyone — first impression docs/USER_GUIDE.md New users — quick start docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md All users — command reference docs/ARCHITECTURE.md Developers — system design docs/RESEARCH_WORKFLOW.md Researchers — academic workflow docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md Researchers — writing scenarios docs/ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md Researchers — creating attacks docs/INSTRUMENTATION_ARCHITECTURE.md Developers — RPC proxy design docs/internal/IMPLEMENTATION_STATUS.md Everyone — what's done/left docs/internal/SECURITY_AUDIT.md Reviewers — mainnet isolation docs/internal/FUTURE_ARCHITECTURE_PLAN.md Contributors — roadmap

docs.title: Archived docs (moved to docs/archive/)
docs.text: Previously useful but superseded or no longer maintained as canonical references.

docs.title: 6. Success Criteria
docs.text:
Crucible meets the goal when all of the following are true:

  1. [ok] A researcher can create a new attack pack with one scaffold command
  2. [ok] A researcher can write only exploit logic and still get a full artifact bundle
  3. [ok] All experiment runs are instrumented by default
  4. [ok] An AI agent can operate through a first-class plugin type
  5. [ok] Logs and artifacts are coherent enough for notebook analysis and paper figures
  6. [ok] Docs are reduced to one canonical source per topic
  7. [ok] Adding a new plugin type or implementation does not require invasive core edits
  8. [ok] Remaining benchmark packs are upgraded from scaffold to evidence-backed reproductions

docs.title: 7. Non-Goals
docs.text:
Crucible is not trying to be: - A mempool simulator - A non-EVM research platform (Solana, Cosmos, etc.) - A validator-node orchestration framework - A zk-proof execution environment (v1)

This focus is a strength, not a limitation.

docs.title: Crucible Testbed — Final Review (Complete)
docs.text: Date: June 8, 2026 Scope: Full codebase (~21K LOC), 28 docs, 526 tests, 8 attack packs, architecture, strategic plan Perspective: Systems architect + USENIX Security program committee Purpose: Single document for the team. Covers everything: what's working, what's broken, what's overengineered, and what to do next.

docs.title: 1.1 Test Suite
docs.text: Metric Result Tests passing 526 / 526 Failures 0 Skipped 15 (environment-dependent: Docker, Anvil, web3) Test functions 514 def test_* (some parameterized → 526 cases) Coverage All core modules have dedicated test files

docs.title: 1.2 Module Health
docs.text:
Module Lines Assessment extract.py 609 Largest — complex but justified (ABI decode + auto-gen) scenario_steps.py 497 8 handlers, well-separated agent_loop.py 480 LLM conversation loop — inherently long logging.py 416 LogEmitter + LogManager — clean split experiment.py 404 [ok] Down from 501 — matrix and foundry checks extracted instrumentation.py 381 The crown jewel — proxy + trace capture All others <300 [ok]

Median module size: ~150 lines. Excellent for a research codebase.

docs.title: 1.3 Error Handling
docs.text:
All 22 except Exception: blocks use logger.debug(exc_info=True). Zero silent swallows. Distribution:

Location Count Why broad catches are correct here Instrumentation proxy 4 Must never crash on trace failures Provenance 3 subprocess calls — git/forge may not exist Extract 5 Best-effort ABI decoding with fallbacks Notification hooks 2 Fire-and-forget webhooks Filesystem/baseline/cache 4 Cleanup ops shouldn't block main flow Agent loop 1 Reasoning log write failure

docs.title: Part II: Goal Completion
docs.text:
# Original Goal Status Evidence 1 Fork chain (historical or live) and run experiments [ok] fork_block in testbed.yaml; omit for latest 2 Recreate historical exploits or test new ones [warn] 3/6 Nomad [ok], Wormhole [ok], Poly Network [ok], Horizon 🚧, BNB 🚧, Multichain L1 3 Agent plugin for AI-driven operation [ok] AgentPlugin ABC → LLMAgentPlugin → 9 tools × 3 privilege levels 4 USENIX-grade logging and analysis [ok] 6 JSONL streams, SHA-256 integrity, provenance, publication gates, export bundles 5 Logging independent of researcher expertise [ok] Transparent RPC proxy — zero logging code needed 6 Handle single-chain and multi-chain (bridge) [ok] 5 topology profiles, BridgePlugin SDK, per-chain log dirs

Overall platform: ~93% complete. The gap is research content (3/6 attacks validated) and CI, not the platform itself.

docs.title: Part III: Correctness Findings (Verified Against Code)
3.1 Soft-Reset State Leakage [warn] CRITICAL
docs.text:
anvil_loadState merges state — it does not wipe. The restore chain is:

baseline.restore() → chain_manager.restore() → JsonRpcClient.load_state() → anvil_loadState

If two experiment runs use soft reset between them, storage slots and accounts created by Run 1 that aren't in the baseline snapshot will persist into Run 2. This breaks determinism guarantees for parameter sweeps and A/B comparisons.

Fix: Change default from "soft" to "medium" in reset_cmd.py:18 (10 minutes).

docs.title: 3.2 Silent Artifact Degradation [warn] CRITICAL
docs.text:
The run_manifest.json contains no completeness indicator. The manifest records outcome: "success" even if the instrumentation proxy silently failed to trace transactions.

The specific failure path (instrumentation.py:203-213): 

def _do_trace(self, tx_hash):
    receipt = self._wait_for_receipt(tx_hash)
    if not receipt:
        log.step(..., status="failure")  # logged to run.jsonl
        return  # but no flag set, no counter incremented

The failure IS logged to run.jsonl, but: - The manifest doesn't check it - The publication gates don't check it - A researcher exporting the bundle has no way to know artifacts are incomplete

Fix: Add artifacts_complete: true/false + trace_failures count to run_manifest.json. Additionally, compare Anvil's actual transaction count against traced count to catch proxy-bypass gaps.

docs.title: 3.3 Evidence.yaml Has No Runtime Validation
docs.text:
evidence.py:36-97 validates structure only (sections present, fidelity level valid, lists non-empty). It does NOT check: - Is fork_block actually the block claimed? - Does the contract at affected_contracts[0] actually have code at that fork block? - Is the exploit_transaction hash valid?

Fix: Pre-flight check after chain fork: eth_getCode(contract) + eth_getBlockByNumber(fork_block). This would have caught 4 of the 13 bugs found during A-Z validation (EOA-vs-contract address errors).

docs.title: 3.4 No CI-Runnable E2E Attack Test
docs.text:
The one real reproduction test requires an archive node and is always skipped. CI proves nothing about whether attacks still reproduce after refactors.

Fix: RPC response recording/replay (10-200KB fixtures vs 50-200MB state dumps). The RpcProxy already intercepts all traffic — add serialize/deserialize modes.

docs.title: 3.5 LLM Agent Has No Dollar-Cost Budget
docs.text:
agent_loop.py iterates until is_done() or max_steps. max_steps controls tool calls, not API spend. A frontier model can burn $50+ in a single experiment.

Fix: Add llm_budget parameter (USD or tokens) with hard-stop.

docs.title: 4.1 The Threat Model Is Implicit — It Needs to Be Explicit
docs.text:
A USENIX reviewer will ask: "What can go wrong between your fork and the real chain?"

What Crucible guarantees (EVM-level fidelity):

Property Guarantee State fidelity Identical storage/balance/code at block N Execution fidelity Same EVM opcode execution Gas accounting Same opcode costs Event emission Same code paths Artifact integrity SHA-256 hashed, provenance-tracked

What Crucible does NOT guarantee (protocol-level fidelity):

Property Limitation Mempool dynamics Anvil auto-mines FIFO — no MEV, no front-running Block timing Timestamp frozen at fork block Cross-chain relay timing Bridge plugins are synchronous mocks Consensus behavior Single-node devnet — no reorgs, no finality delays External oracle state Oracle values reflect fork block, not exploit window

This is not a weakness — it's a scope boundary. The paper must say: "We reproduce the EVM execution of N bridge exploits." Not: "We faithfully reproduce bridge exploits." The qualifier matters.

docs.title: 4.2 The Instrumentation Proxy Has an Observability Boundary
docs.text:
The proxy captures eth_sendTransaction and eth_sendRawTransaction that flow through it. It CANNOT capture: - Plugin opening a direct connection to Anvil (bypassing proxy) - Forge script with hardcoded RPC URL - Internal Anvil state changes via cheatcodes

The transparent URL substitution (runtime_for() returns proxy URL) makes the common case work, but there's no enforcement.

Fix: Post-run consistency check: 

expected_tx_count = latest_block.txs - fork_block.txs
actual_traced     = count(gas_profile.jsonl entries)
if mismatch → artifacts_complete = false

docs.title: 4.3 The Bridge Model Is a Mock, Not a Simulation
docs.text:
BridgePlugin.send_message() default implementation: 

def send_message(self, context, source_chain, dest_chain, data, correlation_id=None):
    context.log.step(f"bridge send_message {source_chain} -> {dest_chain}", ...)
    return {"source_tx_hash": "0x", "correlation_id": correlation_id or ""}

This is a no-op stub. The bridge behavior is whatever the researcher codes. For L2/L3 (single-chain), this doesn't matter — the exploit happens on one chain. For L4 (multi-chain), a reviewer will ask: "How do you know the mock is faithful?"

Fix (documentation, not code): L4 evidence.yaml must require: (1) protocol specification reference, (2) behavioral assertions, (3) excluded components with impact analysis.

docs.title: 4.4 Fidelity Levels Describe Metadata, Not Behavior
docs.text:
The current system: - L0: narrative only - L1: conceptual (no additional checks) - L2: must have chains + affected_contracts in evidence.yaml - L3: must have exploit_transactions in evidence.yaml - L4: must have excluded_components

Problem: An L3 attack that lists exploit_transactions but doesn't replay them is still classified L3. No runtime check that declared fidelity matches actual execution.

Fix: For L3, compare reproduction results against real chain receipts (gas, event count, balance change).

docs.title: 4.5 Cross-Chain Timing Is Undefined
docs.text:
cross_chain.jsonl records correlation IDs but not timing. In real bridge exploits, relay delay and finality are part of the attack. In Crucible, both chains are instant-mine — send_message() is synchronous.

Fix (documentation): State explicitly: "Cross-chain timing is not simulated. Exploits depending on relay latency or finality gaps are not reproducible at L4 without a custom BridgePlugin."

docs.title: 4.6 The Diff Engine Proves Structural Equality, Not Semantic Equivalence
docs.text:
diff.py:compare_runs() compares storage diffs, events, gas, and balances structurally. Two runs of the "same exploit" that drain 100 ETH via different transaction counts will fail the diff even though they're semantically equivalent.

This is fine for its intended purpose (proving determinism of identical runs). But crucible verify should use hash comparison (from log_integrity.json), not diff comparison.

docs.title: Part V: Why Crucible, Not Just Foundry
docs.text:
This is the most important architectural question. Foundry (Anvil + Forge) already does most of what's needed:

// DeFiHackLabs pattern — 300+ exploits, cited in papers
contract NomadExploit is Test {
    function testExploit() public {
        vm.createSelectFork("mainnet", 15259100);
        replica.process(fraudulent_message);
        assertGt(token.balanceOf(attacker), 0);
    }
}

What Foundry handles: fork at any block, run exploit transactions, traces (-vvvv), gas reports, snapshot/restore, determinism.

What Crucible genuinely adds — three things Foundry can't do:

# Capability Why Foundry can't Crucible's approach 1 Structured, machine-readable artifacts forge test -vvvv → unstructured text. Can't hash, diff, or verify it. RPC proxy → typed JSONL + SHA-256 integrity 2 Multi-chain coordination Forge forks one chain per test N Anvil instances, each with instrumentation proxy 3 AI agent in the attack loop Forge is Solidity-only AgentPlugin + LLM loop + 9 privileged tools

Everything else — config management, plugin YAML manifests, scenario DSL, baseline management, topology profiles, 17 CLI subcommands — is orchestration. It exists because Foundry has no experiment management layer, and academic reproducibility needs one. But it's not the core value.

docs.title: Where the Orchestration IS Justified
docs.text:
• Multi-chain topologies — Managing 3 Anvil instances needs structured config
  • Parameter sweeps — Running 10 fork blocks needs experiment YAML
  • Publication mode — Provenance + gates need explicit configuration
  • Team collaboration — YAML files are git-tracked and reviewable

docs.title: Where the Orchestration Is Overweight
docs.text:
Layer Issue Leaner alternative Plugin manifest (plugin.yaml + PluginRegistry) Dynamic importlib loading with YAML discovery Convention: attacks/<name>/<name>.py with class Attack Scenario engine (8 step types) Mini-DSL for setup phases Let researchers write setup in Python 3-tier reset system Exists because Crucible manages Anvil externally Foundry's vm.snapshot() is one line Topology YAML profiles 5-line YAML files CLI flags: --chains eth,bsc --bridge wormhole

Recommendation: Keep what exists (it works, 526 tests pass), but make the simple path simple. Convention over configuration for the common case. YAML and plugins stay for power users.

docs.title: Agreed — No Debate
docs.text: Finding Verdict Code evidence Soft-reset leakage [ok] Confirmed chain_manager.py:222-227 → anvil_loadState merges Silent degradation [ok] Confirmed provenance.py:240-250 — no artifacts_complete field Evidence pre-flight [ok] Feasible evidence.py:36-97 checks structure, not chain state No CI E2E test [ok] Confirmed Only reproduction test requires archive node LLM budget gap [ok] Confirmed agent_loop.py — max_steps controls calls, not spend Docs golden path [ok] Confirmed 29 docs, no tutorial

docs.title: Agreed Pushbacks
docs.text: Finding Verdict Reason Checked-in state fixture [ok] Too heavy 50-200MB per attack. RPC replay (10-200KB) is better. Prompt injection from YAML [ok] Low risk Files are committed and reviewed, not uploaded. Deterministic correlation IDs [ok] Design choice Counter-based is more debuggable. Both valid. Schema validation at write time [ok] Too heavy Per-emit() jsonschema → hot path overhead. Bundle-time is better.

docs.title: Best Ideas
docs.text: Idea Verdict crucible verify <bundle> The killer feature. Closes the reproducibility loop. P1. Per-attack fidelity table Cheap credibility win. 30 minutes. --from-tx scaffolder Dramatically lowers attack authoring cost. P3.

docs.title: 8.1 artifacts_complete Flag
docs.text:
# In experiment.py, finally block after stop_instrumentation():
trace_failures = sum(
    1 for entry in log_mgr._root._load_jsonl("run.jsonl")
    if entry.get("action") == "auto_trace" and entry.get("status") == "failure"
)
artifacts_complete = trace_failures == 0

# In provenance.py build_run_manifest(), add to return dict:
return {
    ...
    "outcome": outcome,
    "artifacts_complete": artifacts_complete,
    "trace_failures": trace_failures,
    ...
}

# In publication.py, new gate:
@staticmethod
def check_artifacts_complete(manifest: dict) -> GateResult:
    if not manifest.get("artifacts_complete", True):
        failures = manifest.get("trace_failures", 0)
        return GateResult(
            passed=False,
            failures=[f"{failures} transactions failed auto-instrumentation"],
        )
    return GateResult(passed=True)

docs.title: 8.2 Evidence Pre-Flight Check
docs.text:
# New method on ExperimentRunner
def _validate_evidence_against_chain(self, experiment: ExperimentConfig) -> None:
    evidence = load_evidence(self.workspace / "attacks" / experiment.attack)
    historical = evidence.get("historical_scope", {})

    for contract_entry in historical.get("affected_contracts", []):
        address = contract_entry.get("address", "")
        chain = contract_entry.get("chain", "ethereum")
        if not address:
            continue

        runtime = self.chain_manager.runtime_for(chain)
        client = JsonRpcClient(runtime.rpc_url)
        code = client.get_code(address)

        if not code or code == "0x":
            raise CrucibleError(
                f"Evidence claims {address} on {chain} is a contract, "
                f"but it has no code at the fork block. "
                f"This usually means the address is wrong (EOA vs contract)."
            )

docs.title: Part IX: Overall Verdict
docs.text:
Platform Layer:        ██████████████████ 98%
Agent System:          █████████████████  95%
Research Content:      ████████████       60%  (3/6 attacks validated)
Documentation:         ██████████████████ 98%
Test Coverage:         ██████████████████ 95%  (526 tests, 0 failures)
Architecture Quality:  ████████████████   8.5/10

Overall: ~93% complete

docs.title: The Core Value Proposition
docs.text: Crucible adds three things Foundry can't do: (1) structured, hashable, diffable artifacts captured transparently, (2) multi-chain coordination, (3) AI agent integration. Everything else is orchestration — useful for reproducibility, but not the differentiator.

docs.title: What Would Make a USENIX Reviewer Accept This
docs.text:
1. Explicit fidelity boundary — "We reproduce EVM execution, not protocol behavior"
  2. artifacts_complete flag — Proves artifacts are complete, not assumed
  3. Evidence pre-flight validation — The testbed proves state matches claims
  4. At least one hermetic CI test — Demonstrates reproducibility without trusted infrastructure
  5. crucible verify — Closes the loop: anyone can re-derive and verify

docs.title: What Would Make a USENIX Reviewer Reject This
docs.text:
1. Claiming "faithful reproduction" without qualifying what "faithful" means
  2. Publishing artifacts from a run where traces silently failed
  3. Bridge simulation at L4 without documenting what the mock does and doesn't simulate
  4. No way for a reviewer to independently verify the reproduction

The architecture is sound. The claims need to be as precise as the code.

docs.title: Platform Layer [ok] (Operationally Complete)
docs.text:
• Python package and CLI with uv lockfile
  • topology and testbed config parsing with Pydantic validation
  • environment variable expansion with .env support
  • deterministic Anvil command generation with gas, fee, order, timestamp controls
  • Anvil process lifecycle management (start, stop, status, health check polling)
  • RPC provider fallback system with priority-based failover and token-bucket rate limiting
  • RPC budget enforcement — per-run request budget with configurable max_requests_per_run and fail_if_exceeded
  • snapshot and baseline RPC wrappers (dumpState / loadState)
  • baseline manifest capture, fingerprint verification (SHA-256), and restoration
  • structured JSONL log streams (run, events, storage diffs, gas profile, cross-chain)
  • log integrity hashing and auto-generated report.md
  • plugin SDK with AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, CruciblePlugin interfaces
  • plugin registry with discovery, manifest validation, and dynamic loading
  • evidence manifest validation with fidelity level enforcement
  • scenario engine with YAML atomic steps, forge_script, and python_script execution
  • formal experiment runner with manifest generation, artifact export, and matrix runner
  • 3-tier reset system: soft (anvil_loadState), medium (kill+relaunch+restore), hard (re-fork+re-bootstrap)
  • phase-level checkpointing and experiment resume with phase-skipping
  • SQLite history index with tag/outcome filtering
  • canonical run diff (storage, events, gas comparison)
  • RPC/cache estimator
  • ABI registry listing, validation, and fetching (Etherscan/Sourcify)
  • bootstrap.py execution in setup workflow
  • --dry-run global flag for safe previews
  • runtime cleanup commands (cache, checkpoints, logs, states, orphans)
  • dataset versioning (DS-YYYY-NNNNN monotonic IDs)
  • canonical bootstrap.py template for pre-attack state preparation
  • comprehensive __init__.py public API
  • crucible doctor — environment validation command covering Python imports, Foundry, config, RPC resolution, directories, Streamlit, Docker
  • Environment validation via crucible doctor (new)
  • RPC budget enforcement — per-run request budget via RpcProviderManager (removed from class-level TokenBucket state)
  • Bridge lifecycle integration — deploy/configure/connect before scenario, destroy after attack (ordering fix)
  • Bridge destroy in finally blocks — bridge_plugin.destroy() moved to finally block in both run() and resume() so bridge state is always cleaned up (even on exception)
  • RPC provider fallback — 4 fallback providers per chain (LlamaRPC, Ankr, PublicNode) configured in testbed.yaml
  • DeterminismConfig — removed deprecated coinbase field; backward-compat via extra = "ignore"
  • user, maintenance, attack-pack, benchmark, research, operations, handoff, status docs- user, maintenance, attack-pack, benchmark, research, operations, handoff, status docs
  • 461 unit tests covering all core components

docs.title: Agent System Layer [ok] (Phase 4–6 Complete)
docs.text:
• AgentPlugin ABC — observe → think → act lifecycle with privilege enforcement
  • AgentContext — wraps ExperimentContext with tool history, audit, and safe parameter filtering
  • 9 agent tools: chain_id, get_balance, get_code, get_storage, call_contract, get_logs (low), impersonate, send_transaction (medium), advance_time (high)
  • 3 privilege levels: low (read-only), medium (impersonate+send), high (full access)
  • Tool-based security — parameter filtering against tool schema, privilege gating on every call
  • verify_tracing scenario step — post-capture verification of instrumentation artifact streams
  • bridge_action scenario step — bridge operations during scenarios (send_message, relay_messages, check_delivery, verify_state)
  • LLMProvider ABC — abstract base for LLM provider implementations
  • OpenAIProvider — raw urllib-based OpenAI-compatible provider with tool calling
  • AnthropicProvider — raw urllib-based Anthropic Messages API with tool use
  • create_provider() factory — openai, anthropic, ollama, local, custom provider types
  • Ollama/local endpoint support — auto-detects localhost, no API key required for local endpoints
  • LLMAgentPlugin — concrete AgentPlugin using LLM for autonomous decision-making
  • System prompt builder — constructs prompts with tool descriptions, task, objective, constraints
  • Response parser — handles OpenAI function_calls, Anthropic tool_use, and done JSON signal
  • Conversation management — history truncation at 50 messages, retry on transient failures
  • Run-config integration — provider type, model, objective, task configured via experiment YAML run block
  • tool_def_to_openai() — converts ToolDef to OpenAI tool format with proper parameter schemas
  • crucible attack extract — auto-generate attack packs from on-chain transactions (fetches tx + ABI + decodes function call)
  • ExportBundle — publication-ready artifact bundles with manifests, reports, provenance, assertions, integrity hashing
  • Signing — bundle hash computation and verification (SHA-256 aggregate + per-file)
  • CacheManager — fork cache fingerprinting, provenance verification, strict/permissive policy enforcement
  • PublicationGate — 6 research-quality gates (evidence completeness, baseline, Foundry version, provenance, etc.)
  • Comprehensive crucible doctor — validates Python, Foundry, config, RPC, ports, Streamlit, Docker
