docs.title: Crucible — Thorough Re-Check After Commit 74d0b56
docs.text:
Date: 2026-06-13 Reviewed: HEAD = 74d0b56, covering the full fix series 2ad6702 → e0e1a39 → b0916fc → 74d0b56. Method: Static review. Two parallel deep passes — (A) auditing the security-fix commits for regressions/bypasses, (B) re-checking the deferred Medium/Low findings — plus direct verification of the headline items. No code executed.

V-HIGH-11 is fixed (verified in both docs/ and website/), so every item from the prior three reports is now closed.

However, this deeper pass found that two of the "fixed" CRITICALs don't fully achieve their stated security goal, plus one new regression introduced by the C3 fix. Details below. These are new findings, not previously reported.

docs.title: Part 1 — Confirmation: prior reports fully closed
docs.text:
Item Status C1, C2, C5, H2, H5 ✅ fixed C3 (proxy) ✅ hardened — but see N1/N2 C4 (manifest) ✅ external hash written — but see N3 H1 (all name→path sites) ✅ wired — but see N5 V-HIGH-1…12, V-MED-2, V-LOW-1 ✅ fixed V-HIGH-11 (abi default) ✅ fixed — docs/ + website/ now say "Etherscan (default, requires ETHERSCAN_API_KEY)", matching abi.py:42 M1 drain() hang ✅ fixed (no longer hangs; see L-note) M2 blank-fork readiness ✅ fixed in chain_manager.py:228 (block >= 0) H4 agent fake-success ✅ fixed — _call_llm now raises instead of fabricating a chain_id no-op

So: nothing from the earlier reports remains open. The items below are newly discovered in this deeper sweep.

docs.title: Part 2 — NEW findings from the deep pass
🔴 N1 (NEW REGRESSION) — Batch JSON-RPC requests crash the proxy
docs.text:
File: crucible/instrumentation.py:296-301 

try:
    req = json.loads(body)
    method = req.get("method", "")      # body may be a JSON array → list has no .get
except (json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError):   # AttributeError is NOT caught
    self._send_error(400, "invalid JSON-RPC request")
    return
A batch JSON-RPC request is a top-level JSON array (valid per spec; emitted by web3.py/ethers when batching). json.loads returns a list; list.get raises AttributeError, which is not in the caught tuple → the exception escapes do_POST. BaseHTTPRequestHandler doesn't handle handler exceptions, so the connection is dropped with a server-side traceback and no response. - This is a regression introduced by the C3 fix. The pre-fix code only parsed the method opportunistically inside a broad except Exception, so batch requests forwarded fine. - Impact: any client sending a batch (or a bare []) breaks; remote-triggerable crash/DoS of the instrumentation proxy. - Fix: detect isinstance(req, list) and either reject with a clean 400 or validate each element's method before forwarding.

docs.title: 🟠 N2 — C3 allowlist permits the exact privileged methods its own comment says it blocks
docs.text: File: crucible/instrumentation.py _ALLOWED_METHODS The header comment claims privileged anvil_* methods are blocked to stop "a compromised attack plugin … calling anvil_setBalance, anvil_impersonateAccount." But the set explicitly allowlists anvil_setBalance, anvil_setStorageAt, anvil_setCode, anvil_setNonce, anvil_impersonateAccount, anvil_setChainId, evm_revert, evm_setNextBlockTimestamp, etc. - Net: C3 genuinely closes the SSRF + DoS holes (open relay, unbounded body) — that's real and good. But it does not enforce least-privilege; a plugin reaching the proxy can still mint balance, impersonate, overwrite code, or roll back instrumentation. - These cheatcodes are needed by the reset/impersonation lifecycle, so this may be acceptable — but then the comment is misleading and should be corrected, and ideally the lifecycle's privileged calls should use a separate internal path not exposed to plugins. - Severity: MEDIUM (by-design trade-off, but the stated guarantee isn't met).

docs.title: 🟠 N3 — C4 external hash is write-only; no verifier consumes it
docs.text: File: crucible/export.py:217-230 (writer) vs verify_bundle:278+, verify_run, commands/bundle.py _write_external_hash() writes datasets/<run_id>.sha256, but no code path ever reads it. verify_bundle still recomputes file hashes and compares them against the in-bundle artifact_bundle_manifest.json (self-attesting). Grep confirms .sha256 appears only in the writer. - Net: the anchor exists on disk but the integrity check still trusts the bundle's own manifest, so an attacker who edits an artifact + recomputes the manifest still passes verify. The tamper-evidence gap C4 targeted is not actually closed in the verify path. - Also, the .sha256 lands in the same datasets/ dir next to the bundle (co-located), so it's not a trust boundary unless distributed out-of-band. - Fix: have verify_bundle read <run_id>.sha256, recompute the manifest's hash, and compare before trusting the manifest's artifacts map. For publication-grade, sign it or anchor via Zenodo DOI. - Severity: MEDIUM if "tamper-evident" is a hard requirement; LOW if "corruption-detectable + optional external anchor" suffices.

docs.title: 🟢 N4 — validate_name regex allows a leading -
docs.text: File: crucible/utils.py _VALID_NAME_RE = ^[A-Za-z0-9_.\-]+$ Docstring claims to block "leading-dash (arg injection)" but the regex accepts -rf, --fork-url, etc. Current call sites use names as path components (where ..///\ are blocked, so safe), so not exploitable today — but latent if any name is ever passed as a bare CLI token. Also . alone passes (though .. is blocked). - Fix: reject a leading - and a name equal to ..

docs.title: 🟢 N5 — Chain-name → path surface still unvalidated
docs.text: File: crucible/chain_manager.py:428 (meta_path), :75 (anvil --cache-path); config.py:179 validates topology name but not the chains: list[str] entries meta_path(chain_name) builds states_dir / f"{chain_name}.meta.json" with no validate_name. Mitigated by start_topology requiring chain_name in self.config.chains, but config chain keys themselves are never validated — a key like ../../foo in testbed.yaml would write outside states/. Requires a malicious/edited config, so low risk, but it's the one H1 surface left uncovered. - Fix: validate_name on chain keys at config load (or in meta_path).

docs.title: Part 3 — Deferred Medium/Low items still open (from original review)
docs.text:
These were never in scope of the fix commits; status re-confirmed against current source:

# Item Status Location D1 import urllib.error missing in llm_providers.py (uses urllib.error.HTTPError at :199,:322 via transitive import only) 🔴 still present llm_providers.py:27 D2 No unconditional LLM call-count / wall-clock cap; token budget only trips if llm_budget_max_tokens set and provider returns usage 🟠 partial agent_loop.py:471,664; llm_providers.py:91 D3 parse_llm_response still reads only tool_calls[0] (drops multi-tool); prose-as-done was fixed 🟠 partial agent_loop.py:187 D4 Unbounded response.read() (no size cap) on external calls 🔴 still present rpc.py:78, abi.py:115,136, llm_providers.py:198,321, proxy _forward/_trace/_wait_for_receipt D5 rpc.py health_check uses block > 0 — same blank-fork bug as M2 but in a different method, not fixed 🔴 still present rpc.py:214 D6 extract.py broad excepts (_decode_args→[], _register_abi→{}) hide failures 🔴 still present extract.py:303,350,708 D7 enforcer._check_contracts fragile nested-loop/break (functionally correct) 🟡 unchanged enforcer.py:92-107 D8 M1 drain(timeout=) no longer hangs, but the timeout param is still a no-op (TypeError → fallback to shutdown(wait=True)) 🟡 cosmetic instrumentation.py:554

docs.title: Verified-correct (no issue)
docs.text:
• C2 forge_script & python_script: .resolve() is done before relative_to(workspace.resolve()) on the chosen path → symlink-to-/etc escapes are blocked. ✅
  • C3 ordering: Content-Length cap enforced before rfile.read(); allowlist checked before _forward; non-integer/missing length rejected; method match is case-sensitive (correct for JSON-RPC). ✅ (the gaps are N1/N2, not ordering)
  • H1 core rules: validate_name correctly rejects .., /, \, null byte, empty/whitespace, >128 chars, non-[A-Za-z0-9_.-]. Absolute paths blocked. ✅ (except N4 leading-dash)
  • H4 fix: the synthetic chain_id no-op is gone everywhere; _call_llm raises on exhausted retries. ✅
  • No new regressions found in the other touched files (cache, checkpoint, plugin, config, experiment, scenario_steps) beyond the above.

docs.title: Recommended fix order
docs.text:
1. N1 — batch-request crash (new regression, remote-triggerable). Quick: handle isinstance(req, list).
  2. N3 — make verify_bundle actually consume <run_id>.sha256, else C4 gives no real tamper-evidence.
  3. N2 — correct the misleading allowlist comment and/or split privileged lifecycle calls off the plugin-facing proxy.
  4. D1 — add import urllib.error (one line, removes latent crash).
  5. D4 / D5 — cap external response.read(); fix rpc.py:214 block > 0 → >= 0.
  6. N4, N5, D2, D3, D6 — hardening / robustness fast-follows.

docs.title: Bottom line
docs.text:
All previously-reported issues are closed, and the genuinely dangerous original blockers (command injection, script/plugin exec, open-relay SSRF, body-DoS, path traversal, secret leak) are resolved. The remaining items are narrower:

  • N1 is a real new regression worth fixing before release (breaks batch clients).
  • N2/N3 mean C3 and C4 are "improved but not complete" against their stated security goals — a judgment call about how strong the proxy-privilege and artifact-integrity guarantees need to be for your release bar.
  • The rest (D1–D8, N4, N5) are robustness/defense-in-depth fast-follows, none release-blocking.

With N1 fixed and a conscious decision on N2/N3, this is a credible release candidate.

Static re-verification only; no files other than this report were modified, no code executed. Line numbers reflect the source at commit 74d0b56.

docs.text: Experiment Topology Result Details Unit Tests — ✅ 799 passed All test modules pass demo_experiment single ✅ PASSED Auto-merge fires (fork_block → 12,996,693), evidence validates, instrumentation captures artifacts demo_agent single ⚠️ FAILED Expected — no OpenAI API key configured in Docker. LLM calls refused. Infrastructure works. nomad_replay bridge ❌ FAILED Auto-merge ✅ (fork_block updated to 15,259,100), but Polygon evidence validation fails — contracts not found at fork block. Likely Polygon RPC archive depth issue or wrong fork block. wormhole_replay bridge ❌ FAILED Both Ethereum (8545) and Polygon (8546) running. Evidence validation fails for Polygon contracts (0x98f3c9..., 0x3ee18b...). Wrong fork block or RPC archive depth for Polygon. horizon_replay single ❌ FAILED Attack plugin crashes/terminates Anvil process. eth_getCode returns connection refused during execution. Bug in horizon attack plugin or its interaction with Anvil. multichain_replay single ❌ FAILED Expected topology is single (Ethereum only) but experiment requires chain config that references Polygon or BSC ports. Evidence/topology mismatch. bnb_token_hub_replay single_bsc ❌ FAILED BSC Anvil doesn't start — likely BSC RPC archive not available at requested fork block.

docs.title: 4.3 Not Tested
docs.text: Experiment Topology Reason wormhole_solidity_replay bridge Needs Foundry script execution, same bridge topology as wormhole — likely same Polygon evidence failure nomad_bridge_l4 nomad_bridge L4 multi-chain with bridge plugin — needs both chains working poly_network_replay poly_network_full Needs all 3 chains (ETH + POLY + BSC) — BSC likely unavailable llm_exploit_agent single Needs LLM provider — no API key configured benchmark_matrix (manifest) Aggregate file, not runnable directly

docs.title: 5.1 Auto-merge is WORKING ✅
docs.text:
The auto-merge feature correctly reads evidence.yaml fork_blocks and updates the config. Evidence: - fork_block updated from 12996693 to 15259100 (from evidence.yaml) — for nomad_replay

No regressions from the auto-merge change.

docs.title: 5.2 Docker Infrastructure — WORKING ✅
docs.text:
• crucible fork successfully starts Anvil as a subprocess inside the orchestrator container
  • crucible experiment run runs and produces artifacts
  • Instrumentation captures events, gas, storage diffs (when trace available)
  • Unit tests all pass

docs.title: 5.3 Root Causes of Experiment Failures
docs.text:
Polygon RPC Archive (nomad_replay, wormhole_replay): - Ankr free tier may not have full Polygon archive depth - The fork blocks for Nomad (15,259,100 ETH / ~16M POLY) and Wormhole require archive access back to block ~14M on Polygon - Two possible fixes: (1) Use a paid Polygon archive provider, (2) Adjust fork blocks to available depths

Horizon crashes Anvil (horizon_replay): - The horizon_multisig_drain attack plugin is calling a method that terminates or corrupts the Anvil process - Need to debug the attack plugin's execute() method — likely an anvil_dumpState or improper RPC call

Topology mismatch (multichain_replay, bnb_token_hub_replay): - multichain_replay uses topology single but scenario may expect multiple chains - bnb_token_hub_replay needs BSC archive — Ankr free tier BSC may not have full archive depth

docs.title: 5.4 What a Researcher Must Now Do
docs.text:
# 1. Create attack / evidence.yaml (fork_block is auto-merged!)
# 2. Run:
crucible clean --all
crucible fork --topology single
crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack.yaml --no-baseline

No testbed.local.yaml editing needed. No code changes to crucible internals.

docs.title: 6. Recommendations
docs.text: Priority Issue Fix High Horizon attack kills Anvil Debug horizon_multisig_drain execute() — likely wrong RPC call or missing impersonation Medium Polygon evidence validation fails at evidence.yaml fork blocks Verify Polygon archive depth for Ankr free tier; add fallback Polygon RPC providers Medium BSC fork fails BSC archive requires paid provider — document this clearly Low demo_agent has no LLM access in Docker Document that LLM experiments need API key env vars set in .env Low multichain_replay topology mismatch Verify experiment's topology: single is correct; may need topology: bridge

docs.text: Category Pass Fail Notes Unit Tests 799 0 All passing Core Workflow ✅ demo_experiment — fork + auto-merge + instrumentation + artifacts all work Auto-merge ✅ Verified — fork_block correctly read from evidence.yaml Docs ✅ 6 docs updated — All consistent Docker Build ✅ Built — No build errors Docker Compose ✅ Fixed — fork_cache volume conflict resolved Attack Replays 0 6 tested All fail due to external deps (RPC archive depth, Anvil crash, no API keys)

The remaining roadmap items (Phases 7-10 below) are documented for future work but are not required for the current testbed goals.

docs.title: Original Roadmap (Archived for Reference)
docs.text: The following phases were planned but are now deferred. They remain valid if the scope expands later.

docs.title: Phase 7: Agent Finishing + Docker Validation
docs.text: Goal: Agent system is fully usable; any researcher can run an agent experiment end-to-end inside Docker.

docs.title: 7.1 Auto-wire LLM provider config from experiment YAML (🔥 High)
docs.text:
The gap: LLMAgentPlugin.__init__() accepts provider_type, model, api_key as constructor args, but the experiment runner's _load_plugin() doesn't pass run_config values through. The YAML config model implies it works, but it doesn't.

The fix: - Add a from_run_config(run_config: dict) -> Self classmethod (or constructor factory) to LLMAgentPlugin that reads agent_provider, agent_model, agent_base_url, agent_objective, agent_task from the run config - Or: move provider creation from __init__ to prepare() so it has access to context.run_config - Update _load_plugin() in experiment.py to pass run_config to the factory

Files: crucible/agent_loop.py, crucible/experiment.py Tests: 3–5 new unit tests for factory method Effort: Small (half day)

docs.title: 7.2 Create example agent attack pack (🔥 High)
docs.text:
The gap: No concrete attacks/ example exists. Users can't see the pattern end-to-end.

Deliverable: attacks/llm_exploit_agent/ with: - plugin.yaml — manifests type: agent - llm_exploit_agent.py — subclass of LLMAgentPlugin defaulting to Ollama - experiments/llm_exploit_agent.yaml — run config with agent_provider: ollama - scenarios/llm_exploit_agent/scenario.yaml — simple fork scenario - Tests: 5 integration-style tests (mock provider responses, verify loop terminates)

Files: New directory attacks/llm_exploit_agent/, new experiment YAML, new scenario YAML Tests: 5–8 Effort: Medium (1 day)

docs.title: 7.3 Agent user guide (🟡 Medium)
docs.text:
The gap: No documentation on how to run an agent experiment.

Deliverable: A new agent user guide (created in a future PR) covering: - Quick-start: run the example agent plugin - YAML config reference and provider setup - Troubleshooting and custom plugin authoring

Files: New doc Effort: Small (half day)

docs.title: 7.4 Validate Docker end-to-end (🟡 Medium)
docs.text:
The gap: Dockerfile + compose exist but haven't been validated on a clean environment with an actual attack run.

Deliverable: - scripts/validate_docker.sh — runs docker compose up, verifies crucible doctor, runs demo experiment, exports result - Or: update existing AE scripts to include a Docker smoke test - Fix any Docker issues discovered during validation

Files: scripts/validate_docker.sh (new), possibly Dockerfile fixes Tests: 1 integration test (test_docker_e2e, requires Docker) Effort: Medium (1 day)

docs.title: Phase 7 Deliverables Summary
docs.text: Item Effort Tests Status Auto-wire provider config 0.5d +5 🔲 Example agent plugin 1d +8 🔲 Agent user guide 0.5d 0 🔲 Docker validation 1d +3 🔲 Total 3d +16

docs.title: Phase 8: Poly Network → L2 Multi-Chain Exemplar (~85%)
docs.text: Goal: A full multi-chain bridge attack (3 chains: Ethereum + BSC + Polygon) running at L2 fidelity, proving the multi-chain pipeline works end-to-end.

docs.title: 8.1 Verify fork blocks for all 3 chains (P0)
docs.text:
The Poly Network exploit spanned Ethereum (block 12,996,062), BSC, and Polygon. Each needs: 1. Verify the archive node has state at the fork block 2. Confirm all affected contracts exist at that block 3. Capture the exploit transaction calldata

Effort: 1–2 days (mostly waiting for RPC calls)

docs.title: 8.2 Implement full exploit logic
docs.text:
The Poly Network exploit works through EthCrossChainManager._executeCrossChainTx which has a keccak256 hash collision vulnerability. The attack: 1. Calls _executeCrossChainTx with a crafted cross-chain message 2. The hash of the function signature collides with putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes(bytes,bytes,uint64) 3. This replaces the keeper/public key 4. Attacker now controls the bridge and can drain all assets

Deliverable: Full poly_network_privkey.py with actual calldata construction.

Effort: 2–3 days (requires understanding the hash collision mechanism)

docs.title: 8.3 Prove determinism (two runs, diff)
docs.text:
Run the Poly Network attack twice and verify canonicalized state diffs are identical. Export the result.

Effort: 0.5 day (run time + verification)

docs.title: 8.4 Multi-chain topology test
docs.text:
• Update topologies/poly_network.yaml for 3-chain topology
  • Verify the experiment runner correctly starts all 3 chains, bridges, and instruments each
  • Verify cross-chain correlation logging works

Effort: 1 day

docs.title: Phase 8 Deliverables Summary
docs.text: Item Effort Tests Status Verify fork blocks (3 chains) 1.5d 0 🔲 Implement exploit calldata 2.5d +15 🔲 Determinism proof 0.5d +5 🔲 Multi-chain topology 1d +10 🔲 Total 5.5d +30

docs.title: 9.1 BNB Token Hub → L2
docs.text:
Current state: L2 scaffold with fork block (22,166,659), tx hash, and handlePackage() selector identified. What's needed: - BSC archive node access (hardest part — BSC archive nodes are scarce) - Verify 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000001004 (TokenHub) has code at fork block - Construct the malformed IAVL Merkle proof that bypasses verification - Demonstrate handlePackage() accepts it via eth_call - Two-run determinism verification

Technique: The IAVL proof vulnerability allows forging cross-chain messages. The proof structure lacks height validation.

Effort: 2–3 days

docs.title: 9.2 Horizon (Harmony) → L2
docs.text:
Current state: L2 scaffold with fork block (15,005,526), bridge address (0x2dcc...), and multisig analysis done. What's needed: - Verify the HorizonBridge contract has code at the fork block - demonstrate unlockTokens() function is callable and state changes

Note: This is a multisig infrastructure compromise, not a contract bug. The reproduction demonstrates that the contracts behaved correctly under exploit conditions — which is valuable for the "defense analysis" narrative.

Effort: 1–2 days

docs.title: 9.3 Multichain → classification (not L2)
docs.text:
Current state: Correctly classified as L1 MPC infrastructure narrative. No on-chain reproduction possible.

What's needed: - Finalize the evidence.yaml to clearly state why L2 is impossible - Add documentation distinguishing infrastructure vs contract exploits - No code changes needed — this pack is correctly scoped

Effort: 0.5 day

docs.title: Phase 9 Deliverables Summary
docs.text: Item Effort Tests Status BNB Token Hub → L2 2.5d +15 🔲 Horizon → L2 1.5d +10 🔲 Multichain classification 0.5d +5 🔲 Total 4.5d +30

docs.title: 10.1 Real bridge plugin implementations
docs.text:
The gap: bridges/demo_bridge/ exists but doesn't implement real bridge logic. The BridgePlugin lifecycle (deploy→configure→connect→destroy) has never been exercised with real contracts.

Deliverable: At least one working bridge plugin (e.g., bridges/wormhole_bridge/) that: - Deploys mock Wormhole Core + TokenBridge contracts to both chains - Configures guardian set - Simulates VAA relaying via connect() - Properly cleans up via destroy()

Why Wormhole? It's the most documented bridge with the most available Solidity source code.

Effort: 3–4 days (Solidity deployment scripts + Python plugin)

docs.title: 10.2 Patch validation workflow
docs.text:
The gap: patches/ directory doesn't exist. The patch validation YAML spec (validation.yaml) from the implementation spec has never been built.

Deliverable: - patches/ directory structure with at least one example patch - patches/wormhole_guardian_fix/ — a mock patch that prevents the forged VAA exploit - crucible patch validate command that: 1. Runs the experiment unpatched (records outcome) 2. Deploys the patch contract 3. Runs the experiment again 4. Asserts the attack fails and normal operations still work 5. Emits side-by-side diff report

Effort: 3–4 days

docs.title: 10.3 Push tests past 549

docs.text: Risk Likelihood Impact Mitigation BSC archive node unavailable Medium Blocks BNB pack Use publicnode.com BSC archive (rate-limited but works). Fallback: document as "requires archive node" Poly Network hash collision too complex Medium Delays multi-chain exemplar Start with Horizon (simpler multisig drain) as the multi-chain exemplar instead Docker validation reveals issues Low Delays Phase 7 Already smoke-tested in CI. Real validation requires a clean VM Bridge Solidity contracts hard to deploy Medium Delays Phase 10 Use minimal mock contracts, not full production clones Researcher bandwidth Medium Overall timeline Phases are independent — multiple researchers can work in parallel

Which items can be worked on simultaneously:

               ├── 10.1 Bridge plugin (Alice)
               └── 10.2 Patch validation (Bob)

Total elapsed: ~5 weeks with 2–3 people

docs.title: What USENIX AE Reviewers Will Actually Check
docs.text:
Ranked by what the AE committee cares about most:

  1. 🥇 Run the demo experiment — docker compose run crucible experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml must work on a fresh checkout
  2. 🥇 Verify determinism — Run twice, crucible diff shows identical results
  3. 🥈 Run a real attack — Nomad replay at L2 with export and bundle verification
  4. 🥈 Read the documentation — Evidence manifests, fidelity levels, architecture docs
  5. 🥉 Try the agent system — Run the example agent against the demo scenario
  6. 🥉 Inspect the artifact bundle — Verify JSONL schemas, integrity hashes, provenance

Our focus should follow this priority order. Phase 7 (Docker validation) and Phase 8 (multi-chain L2 exemplar) directly address items 1, 2, and 3. Phase 10 (bridge plugins + patches) is lower priority for AE but important for the complete platform vision.

docs.title: Housekeeping (After Every Phase)
docs.text:
• Update docs/internal/IMPLEMENTATION_STATUS.md with new capabilities and test count
  • Run pytest tests/unit/ and verify 0 failures
  • Run ruff check crucible/ tests/ and verify 0 warnings
  • Run pytest --cov=crucible and check coverage hasn't regressed
  • Verify CI workflows (unit.yml, integration.yml, docker-smoke.yml) pass
  • Commit and tag progress (e.g., v0.3.0-phase7)

This roadmap is a living document. Update it as priorities shift and new gaps are discovered.

docs.title: Crucible — Scientific-Integrity Audit & Remediation Status
docs.text: Prepared for: Engineering team Date: 2026-06-13 Reviewed at commit: 2566b45; deep re-review of c7f48f5 re-opened SI-7 & SI-10, both re-closed in the current working tree. Audit type: Adversarial deep-dive on the testbed's core scientific claims — deterministic reruns, diff proves reproducibility, complete artifacts with SHA-256 integrity, cache/provenance fingerprinting. Method: Static analysis + targeted source verification. No code executed. Every "Fixed" status below was verified against the source (the actual diff and current files, not the commit messages). A final 100×-depth pass scrutinised the SI-7/SI-10 "closed" claims and the concurrency/teardown paths behind them.

docs.title: Executive summary
docs.text:
The earlier security review (injection, SSRF, path traversal, secrets) is fully closed — see REMAINING_ISSUES_REPORT.md. This document covers a separate and deeper class: places where the testbed could silently produce or certify scientifically wrong results that still pass every gate (verify, fidelity enforcement, tests). These are correctness/honesty issues, not security issues.

Two remediation passes (f1a15de, then 2566b45) closed the findings. Current state:

Status Count Findings ✅ Fixed & verified 13 SI-1, SI-2, SI-3, SI-4, SI-5, SI-6, SI-7, SI-8, SI-9, SI-10, SI-11, SI-12, SI-13 🟡 Residual 0 — ❌ Open 0 —

Bottom line for release planning: All 13 findings from the Scientific Integrity Audit are closed. SI-1–SI-6, SI-8, SI-9, SI-11–SI-13 were closed and verified across the first two passes. SI-7 and SI-10 were marked closed at c7f48f5 but a deeper review found each was only partially closed (SI-7: concurrent trace workers still interleaved JSONL lines nondeterministically → per-stream hashes differed across runs; SI-10: the manifest write moved before the fork spawned, so a crashed fork left a misleading "valid" cache marker). Both are now genuinely closed in the working tree, with unit tests for the SI-7 case. One LOW doc-comment cleanup remains (schema.py). Security, crash-safety, and scientific-honesty are complete.

docs.title: SI-1 — diff event comparison no longer erases payloads
docs.text: Was: _event_key keyed events on row.get("args", {}), a field the emitter never wrote, so every event collapsed to (chain, address, event_name, "{}") — a 1-wei transfer and a full-bridge-drain to a different recipient compared equal. Now: crucible/diff.py:_event_key (lines 161–172) keys on the real emitted fields topics + data. Event amounts/recipients are compared again. ✓

docs.title: SI-2 — diff honesty (empty streams + gas granularity)
docs.text: Was: boolean verdicts returned True when both sides were empty (empty == empty), and gas was compared as a scalar sum — [100k,50k] vs [90k,60k] both summed to 150k → gas_equal: True. Now: crucible/diff.py:compare_runs (lines 104–137): - Both-empty streams are tracked in an incomparable list and the verdict returns None (incomparable), not True. - Gas is compared per-transaction (gas_a_list == gas_b_list), not by total. - One-sided-empty captures emit a warnings entry ("capture may have failed"). ✓

docs.title: SI-3 — Concurrent artifact writes are now thread-safe
docs.text: Was: LogEmitter.emit() did write()+flush() on a shared file handle with no lock, while up to 10 instrumentation trace-workers wrote concurrently → torn/merged JSONL lines, then SHA-256'd as "valid." Now: crucible/logging.py:166 adds self._lock = threading.Lock(), and emit() (lines 181–183) wraps the write+flush in it. Concurrent traces can no longer corrupt the streams. ✓ (highest-value fix)

docs.title: SI-4 — block.prevrandao is now pinned
docs.text: Was: unpinned; RNG/MEV-dependent contracts diverged run-to-run. Now: DeterminismConfig.prevrandao (default all-zero) is passed via --block-prevrandao in anvil_command (chain_manager.py:79). ✓

docs.title: SI-5 — Block timestamp is now resolved and pinned
docs.text: Was: the default timestamp: "fork_block" sentinel never produced a --timestamp flag, so multi-tx exploits with deadlines/TWAPs/vesting could see wall-clock-derived block-timestamp spacing. Now: chain_manager.py:142–157 resolves the sentinel by fetching the fork block's real timestamp via eth_getBlockByNumber and pins it with --timestamp (chain_manager.py:77). Falls back to Anvil's deterministic default (-1) when resolution isn't possible, and None is honored as "leave unpinned" for tests. ✓

docs.title: SI-6 — auto_mine config flag is now wired
docs.text: Was: DeterminismConfig.auto_mine existed but was never read — a dead determinism knob. Now: when auto_mine=False, --block-time 1 is passed (chain_manager.py:83–84). ✓

docs.title: SI-8 — A trace that succeeds-but-captured-nothing is no longer silently "complete"
docs.text: Was: artifacts_complete was purely trace_failures == 0. A reverted tx, a wrong-tx-hash receipt, or an empty capture all counted as a successful, complete trace. Now: crucible/instrumentation.py:_do_trace: - Validates receipt.transactionHash matches the submitted tx_hash (lines 411–413). - On a reverted tx (status != "0x1", lines 426–436): gas is captured, events/storage are skipped, and the status is recorded as a failure rather than silently logged as success. - Flags empty_capture=True (line 488) when a trace produced no data at all, so "nothing happened" is distinguishable from "tracer returned empty." ✓

docs.title: SI-9 — Submit-after-drain race no longer drops a transaction silently
docs.text: Was: a tx fired as teardown began scheduled a trace after the executor was shut down; the RuntimeError was swallowed → no artifact, no failure record, trace_failures stayed 0 → false artifacts_complete. Now: crucible/instrumentation.py sets a _proxy_accepting flag — True on start (line 610), False in drain() (lines 629–630). _schedule_trace checks the flag and refuses new work once draining begins (line 393), so late submissions are rejected deterministically rather than lost. ✓

docs.title: SI-11 — Publication gates now BLOCK in publication mode
docs.text: Was: post-run gates fired with the manifest, but failures were logged/printed only — a run with incomplete artifacts or dirty provenance still completed and could be exported. Now: crucible/experiment.py:525–551: in publication mode (publication_mode or config.publication.enabled), post-run gate failures call PublicationGate.raise_on_failure(post_result), which raises and surfaces as a CrucibleError (non-zero exit). Outside publication mode they remain advisory warnings, as intended. ✓ (upgraded from advisory since the prior review)

docs.title: SI-12 — Config fingerprint no longer embeds the API key
docs.text: Was: config_fingerprint hashed the full rpc_url (with embedded API key) → leaked the key into published manifests and changed when only the key rotated. Now: provenance.get_config_fingerprint (lines 115–123) strips rpc_url to scheme://hostname before hashing (and to "redacted" if unparseable). ✓

docs.title: SI-13 — Fingerprint injectivity hardened
docs.text: Was: sha256_json(default=str) made the hash non-injective — list/tuple collided, and enum/Path/set collapsed to str(). Now: crucible/hashing.py:10–30 adds CanonicalEncoder(json.JSONEncoder): - tuple → {"__tuple__": [...]} (distinct from list) - set → sorted list, Path → str, Enum → .value - raises TypeError on genuinely ambiguous/non-serialisable types rather than silently stringifying. sha256_json uses cls=CanonicalEncoder (line 47). ✓

docs.title: SI-7 — Artifacts are byte-identical across runs
docs.text:
Was: every artifact line carried "timestamp": utc_now() (wall clock) plus run_id, scenario, and attack — so two runs of the same experiment could never produce byte-identical streams, making cross-run SHA-256 verification impossible by construction.

First pass (f1a15de / c7f48f5): timestamp was removed, then run_id/scenario/attack were dropped from per-line entries — they now live on the LogEmitter instance and in run_manifest.json / log_integrity.json only (crucible/logging.py:emit()).

Deep-review gap found: removing those fields was necessary but not sufficient. The instrumentation trace workers run on a ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) (crucible/instrumentation.py:600); each transaction's _do_trace emits its event/storage/gas/balance lines from a different thread. The SI-3 lock keeps each line atomic, but the order lines land in is governed by thread-completion timing — so two identical runs produced the same set of lines in different order, and write_integrity() hashes the file as-written. For any multi-transaction experiment, the per-stream SHA-256 still differed run-to-run.

Now (this pass): crucible/logging.py adds canonicalize_stream() and a CANONICAL_STREAMS set (events.jsonl, storage_diffs.jsonl, gas_profile.jsonl, balance_diffs.jsonl, cross_chain.jsonl). Both LogEmitter.write_integrity and LogManager.write_integrity canonically sort these streams (re-serialise each line with sorted keys, then sort the lines) before hashing — and this runs before write_artifact_manifest, so both the log_integrity.json and the artifact-bundle hashes see stable files. These streams are set-like (their meaning is order-independent; diff already compares them as sorted collections), so canonicalisation is loss-free. Two identical runs now produce byte-identical data streams and matching digests. ✓ Scope note: run.jsonl and agent_reasoning.jsonl are deliberately left in emission order (they are execution-narrative / decision-order logs) and are not part of the byte-identical guarantee; the structured streams that diff and verify-equivalence rely on are. Covered by tests/unit/test_logging.py::test_data_streams_hash_identically_across_runs.

docs.title: SI-10 — Cache manifest verify/invalidate before spawn; manifest write after readiness
docs.text:
Was: cache manifest was written but never verified on reuse (f1a15de added the write, no read path). 2566b45 added verify/invalidate but ran it after _await_ready, so a stale cache was still used for the current run before being invalidated.

Deep-review gap found: c7f48f5 moved the whole verify+write block to before the Anvil spawn. That fixed the verify-too-late problem, but the write was now unconditional and pre-spawn: if the fork failed (_await_ready timeout → process terminated/raised), a cache_manifest.json asserting "valid" was left behind over a partial/never-populated cache dir (same path as Anvil's --cache-path). Since verify_manifest only compares provenance (provider/block/chain_id/foundry/config_hash), not cache completeness, the next run would see warm/valid and reuse the partial cache.

Now (this pass): in chain_manager.py:start_chain(), the verify + invalidate step stays before the spawn (so a config-mismatched cache is deleted before Anvil touches it), but the manifest write is moved to after _await_ready succeeds. A crashed or timed-out fork therefore leaves no manifest, so the next run correctly sees a cold cache and rebuilds rather than trusting a stale "valid" marker. ✓

docs.title: SI-7 follow-up — stale doc comment (LOW, non-blocking)
docs.text: crucible/schema.py:20–24 and the COMMON_FIELD_TYPES map still describe timestamp/run_id/scenario/attack/experiment as fields "always present" on every entry — no longer true after SI-7. This does not affect crucible verify: those fields were never in any stream's required list, and the type-check only fires when a field is present, so validation still passes. Worth a one-line comment correction for accuracy.

docs.title: Recommended sequencing
docs.text: All 13 findings are closed and verified. SI-7 and SI-10 were re-opened during a deeper review (concurrency-ordering and pre-spawn-write respectively) and closed in this pass with accompanying unit tests. The only remaining nit is the LOW doc-comment cleanup in schema.py noted above.

docs.title: Scope notes
docs.text:
• Security & crash-safety: fully resolved in prior rounds (CODE_REVIEW_REPORT.md, DOCS_VERIFICATION_REPORT.md, REMAINING_ISSUES_REPORT.md).
  • This audit is about output trustworthiness, which matters specifically because the docs target USENIX-grade artifact evaluation — the failure modes above are exactly what artifact evaluators look for.
  • All findings are static; none were reproduced at runtime, but the SI-7 fix is covered by unit tests asserting cross-run hash equality. Line-level evidence is cited against the source. Verified-in-source: SI-1, SI-2, SI-3, SI-4, SI-5, SI-6, SI-8, SI-9, SI-11, SI-12, SI-13 at 2566b45; SI-7 and SI-10 re-opened and re-closed in the current working tree (crucible/logging.py, crucible/chain_manager.py, tests/unit/test_logging.py).

No code was modified in producing this report.

docs.title: Audit Result: [ok] Fully Isolated — No Write Operations Reach Mainnet
docs.text: Layer Outbound Calls Writes to Mainnet? Risk Anvil fork eth_getBlockByNumber, eth_getStorageAt, eth_getCode (archive RPC) [fail] Read-only None — Anvil never forwards eth_sendTransaction upstream RPC Proxy Forwards to 127.0.0.1:8545 only [fail] Local only None — upstream_url is always the local Anvil Attack Packs Use runtime_for().rpc_url → always 127.0.0.1:N [fail] Local only None Scenario Steps context.chains.runtime_for(chain).rpc_url → local Anvil [fail] Local only None LogManager Writes JSONL files to local disk only [fail] Local only None — no network calls crucible doctor --rpc Pings configured archive RPC [fail] Read-only Checks connectivity, no writes crucible attack extract Fetches ABI from Sourcify / Etherscan APIs [fail] Read-only Reads contract metadata, no writes Notification hooks Optional webhook POST Configurable Disabled by default

docs.title: Key Isolation Guarantees
docs.text:
1. Anvil's --fork-url is read-only. Anvil uses the archive RPC only to fetch historical state at the fork block. It never submits transactions upstream. This is a Foundry-level guarantee.

  2. All transaction traffic goes to 127.0.0.1. Every attack pack, forge script, and external tool that goes through the testbed connects to the local Anvil fork. The RPC proxy only forwards to http://127.0.0.1:<port>.

  3. No private keys on mainnet. The --auto-impersonate flag means all transactions use impersonated accounts — no real private keys are loaded. State-changing ops (anvil_setStorageAt, anvil_setBalance) are local-only anvil RPC methods that don't exist on mainnet nodes.

  4. LogManager is purely local. The per-chain LogEmitter instances write JSONL files to the local logs/ directory. No log data is ever transmitted over the network.

  5. crucible attack extract is read-only. The only external API calls are to Sourcify and Etherscan for ABI lookup — no transactions, no state writes. The extracted data is written locally.

docs.title: Residual Risk (Low)
docs.text: Risk Mitigation A forge script with --rpc-url pointing to mainnet instead of local Anvil The experiment runner replaces --rpc-url with the local Anvil URL in forge_script scenario steps A notification webhook sending experiment data to an external URL Disabled by default; researcher must explicitly configure it ETH_RPC_URL leaking via .env into version control .env is in .gitignore Sourcify/Etherscan API calls from crucible attack extract Read-only; no keys needed for Sourcify; Etherscan key is optional

docs.title: Answer: [ok] Yes — Zero Hardcoded Data, Zero Per-Experiment Code
docs.text: The logging system operates at two independent layers:

docs.title: Layer 1: LogManager (Orchestrator) + LogEmitter (File Writer)
docs.text:
Created automatically by ExperimentRunner.run():

log_mgr = LogManager(run_dir, run_id, experiment.scenario, experiment.attack, experiment.name)
  • LogManager creates one LogEmitter per chain (via for_chain(name)) — each writes to a separate chain_<name>/ subdirectory
  • Root-level run.jsonl captures the experiment lifecycle (shared, not per-chain)
  • Bridge events go to a bridge/ subdirectory
  • Every entry is tagged with timestamp, run_id, scenario, attack — metadata passed at construction, not hardcoded
  • No hardcoded addresses, no chain-specific logic, no attack-specific branches

docs.title: Layer 2: Instrumentation Proxy (Automatic Artifact Capture)
docs.text:
The RPC proxy auto-captures artifacts from any transaction on the fork:

Transaction → RPC Proxy → Forward to Anvil → Background trace
                                            → log.gas()          — gas_profile.jsonl
                                            → log.event()        — events.jsonl
                                            → log.storage_diff() — storage_diffs.jsonl
                                            → log.balance_diff() — balance_diffs.jsonl
  • The proxy has zero knowledge of what attack is running
  • It captures every eth_sendTransaction and eth_sendRawTransaction regardless of sender
  • Same logic applies to Nomad, Poly Network, Horizon, or any future attack

docs.title: What the Researcher MUST Do vs. What's Automatic
docs.text: Task Automatic? Details Log stream creation [ok] Yes LogManager created by ExperimentRunner, creates per-chain LogEmitter instances Gas/Events/Storage/Balance capture [ok] Yes RPC proxy captures from every tx report.md generation [ok] Yes write_report() called in finally block Integrity hashing [ok] Yes write_integrity() called in finally block Use proxy URL (NOT hardcoded localhost) [ok] Required pattern Must use runtime_for().rpc_url instead of hardcoded http://127.0.0.1:8545 Custom step logging (optional) ⏭️ Optional context.log.step() for experiment-phase annotations Attacker account setup [fail] Manual Impersonate + fund (done by attack pack) Transaction construction [fail] Manual The actual exploit logic (researcher's work)

docs.title: The One Rule
docs.text:
Always use runtime_for().rpc_url — never hardcode http://127.0.0.1:8545:

# [ok] Correct — gets proxy URL (auto-artifacts)
client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url)

# [fail] Wrong — bypasses proxy (no artifacts)
client = JsonRpcClient("http://127.0.0.1:8545")

Other than that, the researcher writes the exploit and gets artifacts automatically — no per-attack configuration, no logging code, no hardcoded data.

docs.title: Crucible Strategic Plan — Philosophy, Review Findings & Improvement Roadmap
docs.text: Date: June 7, 2026 Purpose: A single document that captures the project's core philosophy, summarizes findings from an expert architecture review, and lays out our prioritized plan to close gaps and improve the researcher experience. Audience: The team, collaborators, and anyone who wants to understand where Crucible is going and why.

docs.title: 1. Core Philosophy — The "Attacker's Seat" Principle
docs.text: This document does not describe a pivot. Crucible was designed from day one around this model: the testbed provides Chain + Bridge + Artifact Collector, and the researcher sits in the attacker's seat — forking a real chain, running attacks like they normally would, and getting complete artifacts automatically. The expert review confirmed the architecture is sound; the work ahead is about closing correctness gaps and reducing friction, not re-architecting.

docs.title: What's Already Been Done
docs.text:
Before the review, the following cleanup and hardening was completed:

What Count/Details Removed internal review/audit docs from root 8 files Removed docs/archive/ (superseded docs) 12 files Removed streamlit_app.py (deprecated) 1 file Removed leftover solidity_verify attack dirs 2 directories Moved canonical spec docs to docs/design/ 2 files Fixed docker-compose.yml (pointed to deleted file) Now points to dashboard/app.py Created .dockerignore, .gitattributes, .env.example Prevents secrets in Docker, cross-platform line endings Created docs/DEPLOYMENT.md, docs/MAINTENANCE.md SSH clone guide, comprehensive management guide Git initialized with clean commit Crucible v0.2.0 — 163 files, 19747 insertions Full test suite passing 521 tests passing locally, 520 in Docker (1 expected dashboard e2e timeout) Verified no hardcoded secrets Codebase scan: all API keys read from environment variables

This is the foundation the improvement plan builds on.

docs.title: 2. Architecture
docs.text:
Crucible's architecture has three layers:

flowchart TD
    subgraph Crucible["CRUCIBLE"]
        A["Chain Manager<br>forks mainnet"]
        B["Bridge Manager<br>connects chains"]
        C["Artifact Collector<br>(RPC proxy +<br>structured logs)"]

        D["Attack Plugin<br>(researcher writes this)<br>'sits in attacker's seat'"]

        A --- D
        B --- D
        C --- D
    end

The testbed does: chain forking, bridge deployment, RPC proxying, transaction tracing, artifact collection, integrity hashing, publication bundling.

The researcher does: write an attack plugin (or use an existing one), run it, read the artifacts. That's it.

docs.title: 2.1 Two-Phase Workflow
docs.text:
Phase What the researcher does What Crucible does How often Setup Configure testbed.yaml + experiment.yaml + run crucible baseline Sets up the environment — starts Anvil forks, deploys bridges, prepares baselines One-time per attack/project Attack Run crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack.yaml, then write attack plugin code Runs the attack, auto-captures every transaction's gas/events/storage/balance, saves structured artifacts Repeatedly

The setup phase is explicit by design — it serves as documentation of exactly what was forked, when, and on which chains. These YAML files are checked into git, reviewed, and versioned. They're the researcher's lab bench: set it up once, document it, then run experiments on it. The automation is reserved for what happens during the attack: the RPC proxy auto-captures every transaction, the logging system auto-organizes artifacts, and the integrity hashing auto-verifies them.

docs.title: 2.2 What Makes a Good Attack Plugin
docs.text:
An attack plugin is just a Python class with a run() method that gets a live chain, deployed bridge, and auto-instrumenting RPC proxy:

class NomadReplicaFraud(AttackPlugin):
    def run(self, context):
        client = JsonRpcClient(context.chains["ethereum"].rpc_url)
        # The researcher does what they'd do on a real chain
        # Every transaction is auto-traced by the RPC proxy
        client.send_transaction(to=replica, data=forged_call)

No logging boilerplate. No artifact management. No chain lifecycle. The testbed handles all of that. This is the automation that matters — not the setup, but the attack execution.

docs.title: 2.3 Fidelity Boundary — What Crucible Guarantees (and What It Doesn't)
docs.text:
Every research instrument needs an explicit threat model. A reviewer will ask: "What can go wrong between your fork and the real chain, and how do you know your reproduction is faithful?"

What Crucible guarantees (EVM-level fidelity):

Property Guarantee Mechanism State fidelity Fork starts with identical storage, balance, and code as the real chain at block N Anvil fetches from archive node Execution fidelity EVM opcodes execute identically Anvil uses the same EVM as Ethereum clients Gas accounting Gas is metered with identical opcode costs Same EVM implementation Event emission Events are emitted from the same code paths Deterministic execution Artifact integrity Artifacts are hashed and provenance-tracked SHA-256 + run_manifest.json

What Crucible does NOT guarantee (protocol-level fidelity):

Property Limitation Impact Mempool dynamics Anvil auto-mines with FIFO ordering Attacks depending on MEV, front-running, or block inclusion order are not reproducible Block timing timestamp: fork_block freezes time Attacks depending on block.timestamp changes across blocks are not reproducible Cross-chain relay timing Bridge plugins are synchronous mocks Real bridge exploits may depend on relay latency, guardian quorum timing, or message ordering Consensus behavior Anvil is a single-node devnet Reorgs, uncle blocks, and finality delays are not reproducible External oracle state Oracle values reflect the fork block Oracles that were manipulated during the exploit window may show different values

This is not a weakness — it's a scope boundary. Crucible reproduces the EVM execution of bridge exploits. It does not reproduce mempool ordering, cross-chain relay timing, consensus behavior, or external oracle dynamics. The fidelity level (L0–L4) describes how much of the original attack's EVM behavior is replayed, not the completeness of the end-to-end attack path.

Publication bundles should state this boundary explicitly. A USENIX reviewer will respect scoped claims. They will not respect unqualified claims of "faithful reproduction."

docs.title: 2.4 Why Crucible, Not Just Foundry
docs.text:
Foundry (Anvil + Forge) already does most of what's needed to reproduce an exploit:

// DeFiHackLabs pattern — works, has been cited in papers
contract NomadExploit is Test {
    function testExploit() public {
        vm.createSelectFork("mainnet", 15259100);
        replica.process(fraudulent_message);
        assertGt(token.balanceOf(attacker), 0);
    }
}
// Run: forge test --match-test testExploit -vvvv

What Foundry already handles: forking at any block, running exploit transactions, showing traces and events (-vvvv), gas reports, snapshot/restore, deterministic execution.

What Crucible adds — the three things Foundry can't do:

Capability Why Foundry can't How Crucible does it Structured, machine-readable artifact capture forge test -vvvv outputs unstructured text to stdout. You can't hash it, diff it, or programmatically verify it. RPC proxy intercepts all transactions and emits typed JSONL streams (events, storage diffs, gas, balances) with SHA-256 integrity hashes. Multi-chain coordination Forge can only fork one chain per test. Bridge exploits that span Ethereum + BSC need two simultaneous forks. Topology system manages N Anvil instances, each with its own instrumentation proxy, coordinated by a single experiment runner. AI agent in the attack loop Forge is a Solidity-native tool. No LLM integration. AgentPlugin + LLMAgentPlugin + 9 privileged tools allow an LLM to explore and exploit a forked chain autonomously.

Everything else — config management, plugin discovery, scenario YAML, baseline management, 17 CLI subcommands — is orchestration. It exists because Foundry has no experiment management layer, and academic reproducibility needs one (fork block documentation, evidence metadata, provenance tracking, export bundles). But the orchestration is not the core value. The core value is: transparent instrumentation that produces publication-grade artifacts without the researcher writing logging code.

If the orchestration ever becomes a barrier to the researcher experience, it should be made optional (convention over configuration), not removed. The simple path must stay simple: run an attack, get artifacts.

docs.title: 3. Expert Review Summary
docs.text: An independent architecture review was conducted (June 2026) covering ~21k LOC Python, 8 attack packs, the Streamlit dashboard, the LLM agent system, and 29 documentation files.

docs.title: 3.1 Overall Verdict
docs.text:
7.5–8 / 10. Excellent bones; a handful of correctness/credibility items stand between you and "press the button, trust the output."

The review confirmed the architecture is solid — the plugin system, topology abstraction, artifact pipeline, and provenance machinery are all genuinely well-designed. The gaps are about closing trust and verification loops, not adding features.

docs.title: 3.2 What We Agree With (no debate)
docs.text: Finding Our Assessment Soft-reset state leakage — anvil_loadState merges instead of wiping. After a soft reset, newly-created accounts from the previous run persist. Real correctness gap. Verified in code. Fix: change default to medium (kill+relaunch). Silent degradation in artifact path — except Exception: logger.debug(...) then continue means incomplete artifacts can look like a successful run. Verified in code. Fix: add artifacts_complete flag to manifest. Evidence.yaml has no run-time validation — fork block and contract addresses are declared but never checked against the live fork. Verified in code. Fix: pre-flight bytecode + block height check. No CI-runnable E2E attack test — the one reproduction test requires an archive node, so CI proves nothing about whether attacks still reproduce. Verified in code. Fix: RPC recording/replay fixture system. LLM agent has no dollar-cost budget — token caps exist but nothing limits actual API spend. Verified in code. Fix: add llm_budget parameter. 29 docs with no golden path — comprehensive but unguided. No single "run these 5 commands" tutorial. Confirmed. Fix: 10-minute TUTORIAL.md. Checkpoint save/restore lacks integrity validation — missing/corrupt snapshot silently skipped. Reported, consistent with code patterns. Fix: hash+verify. Port/process lifecycle not verified — TOCTOU race, failed kills hidden, partial cleanup. Reported. Fix: retry+verify pattern.

docs.title: 3.3 What We Nuanced or Disagreed With
docs.text: Finding Our Position Hermetic E2E with checked-in state fixture Agree it's needed, disagree with "check in 200MB state dump." Better approach: RPC response recording/replay at the framework level (see §4.2). Prompt injection from experiment YAML Low risk — experiment files are committed and reviewed, not uploaded by untrusted users. Not worth prioritizing. Cross-chain correlation IDs should be deterministic Design trade-off, not a bug. Counter-based IDs are more debuggable during development. Current approach is fine. Schema validation at log-write time Too heavy on the hot path. Better: validate at bundle time (export/publication) with zero runtime cost.

docs.title: 3.4 Best Ideas from the Review
docs.text: Idea Priority Why crucible verify <bundle> — re-run an archived bundle and hash-compare artifacts P1 Closes the reproducibility loop. This turns "we logged it" into "anyone can re-derive it." Per-attack fidelity table in BENCHMARKS.md P3 Cheap, high credibility. Shows why each attack can't go higher than its current fidelity level. --from-tx attack scaffolder P3 Pulls tx data from a real exploit and pre-fills plugin.yaml + evidence.yaml. Dramatically lowers authoring cost. Determinism harness — crucible doctor --determinism that runs same experiment twice and diffs artifact hashes P3 Makes non-determinism visible instead of suspected.

docs.title: 4. The Researcher Experience — Current vs. Target
Researcher Workflow
docs.text:
# 1. Setup (one-time per attack/project)
git clone <repo>
cp .env.example .env                             # fill in RPC URL
pip install -e .
crucible doctor                                  # verify everything is ready

# Write testbed.yaml (chains, fork blocks, logging)
# Write experiments/my_attack.yaml (name, scenario, attack, topology)
# Write scenarios/my_scenario/scenario.yaml (phase definitions, if needed)

crucible baseline                                # capture initial state

# 2. Attack (the real work, repeated)
crucible experiment run experiments/my_attack.yaml
# ... researcher writes attack plugin code ...
# Artifacts auto-saved to logs/my_attack/DS-2026-00042/

# 3. Inspect results
cat logs/my_attack/DS-2026-00042/report.md       # human-readable summary
ls logs/my_attack/DS-2026-00042/chain_ethereum/  # raw artifact streams

The setup phase is not toil — it's documentation. Every YAML file is a git-tracked, reviewable artifact that captures exactly what was done. The automation is reserved for what happens during the attack: the RPC proxy, artifact collection, and integrity hashing. This separation of concerns is intentional and is one of Crucible's strengths.

docs.title: 5. Mitigation Plan — What We Need to Fix
P0 — Trust & Correctness (before any external release)
docs.text:
These ensure the environment is honest and artifacts are trustworthy. Without these, a reviewer or careful co-author can say "how do you know this is faithful?" and Crucible can't give a crisp answer.

# Item What Why Effort 1 artifacts_complete flag After a run, scan run.jsonl for auto-trace failures and compare Anvil's actual transaction count against the number of traced transactions (to catch any traffic that bypassed the proxy). Include complete: true/false + trace_failures + tx_count_mismatch in run_manifest.json. Add a publication gate that rejects incomplete runs. Silent degradation: a run can produce incomplete artifacts and still report "success." The RPC proxy only captures transactions that flow through it — a plugin that bypasses the proxy produces invisible gaps. 2 hrs 2 Default reset → medium Change reset_cmd.py default from soft to medium (kill + relaunch Anvil). Document soft mode for explicitly-stateless quick iterations only. anvil_loadState merges, doesn't wipe. Soft reset leaves state from previous runs behind, breaking determinism guarantees. 10 min 3 Evidence pre-flight validation At experiment start, call eth_getCode(affected_contract) and eth_getBlockByNumber(fork_block) against the live fork. Fail fast if bytecode is empty or block doesn't match. Converts "the researcher claims it forked the right state" into "the testbed proves it." This is the highest-leverage rigor upgrade. 4 hrs 4 Hermetic CI test via RPC recording Build a framework-level RPC recording/replay system. The existing RpcProxy already intercepts all traffic — add record mode (serialize request/response pairs) and replay mode (mock RPC server from fixture). Any attack can be recorded once and replayed in CI without an archive node. Without this, CI proves nothing about whether attacks still reproduce. The one real reproduction test is always skipped. 2-3 days

docs.title: P1 — Researcher Experience (the "attacker's seat" feeling)
docs.text:
These make working with the testbed feel seamless — the setup is explicit, but the attack loop is frictionless.

# Item What Why Effort 5 Optional run alias Add --name flag to experiment runs so researchers can label them (e.g. --name "nomad-v2-repro"). Display in history, dashboard, and manifest. Run IDs are opaque (DS-2026-00001). A human-readable label makes finding runs later trivial. No infra change, just a new field. 2 hrs 6 crucible verify <bundle> Re-run an archived bundle from scratch and hash-compare all artifacts against the original. Report pass/fail per stream. Closes the reproducibility loop. This is what turns Crucible from "a tool that logs things" into "a tool that proves reproducibility." The core diffing infrastructure already exists in diff.py. 1-2 days 7 10-minute TUTORIAL.md "Reproduce the Nomad hack in 10 minutes" — 8 copy/paste commands with expected output, artifact locations, and a "what just happened" section. 29 docs with no golden path. A single tutorial is the highest-ROI docs change possible. 3 hrs

docs.title: P2 — Robustness (catches edge cases, prevents waste)
docs.text: # Item What Why Effort 8 LLM spend cap llm_budget parameter (USD or tokens) on agent experiments. Hard-stop the agent loop when budget exceeded. Record in manifest. Running parameter sweeps with frontier models can burn $50+ in a single experiment with no warning. 2 hrs 9 Checkpoint integrity Validate JSON after write, SHA-256 hash it, verify hash on restore. Fail fast on corrupt/missing checkpoint instead of silently continuing. Phase checkpoints are the safety net for long-running experiments. A corrupt checkpoint currently causes silent non-determinism. 4 hrs 10 Port/process lifecycle Verify ports are free after stop_all() before declaring cleanup done. Add retry/backoff around chain start. Log failed kills at WARNING (not swallowed). Track per-target success in clean --all. Orphan Anvil processes holding ports are the most common source of cryptic "port in use" errors. 4 hrs 11 Consolidate redundant docs 4 roadmap-ish docs (IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN, IMPLEMENTATION_STATUS, ROADMAP_TO_100, FUTURE_ARCHITECTURE_PLAN) and 2 handoff docs create confusion. Consolidate into docs/internal/ and keep only user-facing docs in the root docs directory. 29 docs works against the "low learning curve" goal. Clean separation of user docs vs internal planning docs makes navigation easier. 2 hrs

docs.title: P3 — Polish & Strategic (nice to have, high credibility when done)
docs.text: # Item What Why Effort 12 Per-attack fidelity table in BENCHMARKS.md Show fidelity level (L0–L4) per attack with the explicit reason it can't go higher. Pre-empts the "is this real?" question from reviewers. The current honesty is a credibility asset — make it visible. 30 min 13 Run tiers (scratch / saved / published) Auto-clean scratch runs after N days. Preserve saved/published runs indefinitely. crucible publish <run_id> marks a run as immutable and optionally backs it up to a configurable remote. Currently all runs are equal. A publication run and a scratch test run get the same retention policy. Tiering solves this cleanly. 1 day 14 Bundle-time JSONL schema validation At export time, validate every JSONL line has the expected fields for its stream type. Catch field-type drift across code versions. Same coverage as per-write validation, zero runtime cost. 4 hrs 15 --from-tx attack scaffolder crucible new-attack --from-tx 0x... --chain eth — pull the tx, fork block, touched contracts, and pre-fill plugin.yaml / evidence.yaml. Dramatically lowers the cost of authoring a new attack from a blog post or DeFiHackLabs link. On-mission. 2-3 days

docs.title: Phase 1: Trust & Correctness (next sprint)
docs.text:
Day Items Deliverable Day 1 1, 2 (artifacts_complete + default reset) Clean-slate guarantee and visible completeness indicator Day 2-3 3 (evidence pre-flight validation) Run-time proof that fork state matches evidence claims Day 4-6 4 (RPC recording/replay) Hermetic CI test for any attack, no archive node needed Day 7 7 (TUTORIAL.md) 10-minute golden path for new users

Exit criteria: All P0 items complete. CI verifies at least one attack reproduces hermeticially. A new user can follow the tutorial and see artifacts within 10 minutes.

docs.title: Phase 2: Researcher Experience (next sprint)
docs.text:
Day Items Deliverable Day 1 5 (run alias) Named runs for easy identification Day 2-4 6 (crucible verify) Reproducibility loop closed Day 5-7 11 (docs consolidation) User docs vs internal docs clearly separated

Exit criteria: All P1 items complete. A returning researcher can run, name, and verify experiments in a consistent workflow.

docs.title: Phase 3: Robustness & Polish
docs.text: Sprint Items Deliverable Sprint 3 8, 9, 11 (LLM budget, checkpoint integrity, port lifecycle) Fewer cryptic errors, safer agent experiments Sprint 4 12, 13, 14, 15 (fidelity table, run tiers, schema validation, scaffolder) Publication-ready polish and attack authoring tools

docs.title: Why RPC Recording/Replay Instead of Checked-In State Fixtures
docs.text:
Approach Fixture Size CI Speed Fragility Anvil state dump (checked-in) 50–200 MB per attack Fast (no fork) High — Foundry version changes can break loading RPC response recording (checked-in) 10–200 KB per attack Fast (no network) Low — just JSON request/response pairs Live archive node (current) None Slow (network I/O) Medium — depends on RPC provider availability

RPC recording is the clear winner: small fixtures, fast execution, hermetic, low maintenance. The infrastructure already exists (the RpcProxy intercepts all traffic). We just need to add serialize/deserialize modes.

docs.title: Why the YAML Configs Stay
docs.text:
The experiment YAML, topology configs, and scenario definitions are not going away. They serve real needs:

  • Multi-chain topologies — configuring two chains and a bridge is complex. A YAML file documents it reproducibly.
  • Parameter sweeps — experiments/benchmark_matrix.yaml runs the same attack at 10 different fork blocks. This requires structured config.
  • Publication mode — asserting evidence completeness, reproducibility gates, and provenance checks need explicit configuration.
  • Team collaboration — YAML files are checked into git, reviewed, and versioned. CLI flags are ephemeral.

The fix is: make them optional for the simple case, not required for all cases.

docs.title: Why the Report Layer Exists
docs.text:
report.md is a post-processing aggregation of the raw JSONL artifact streams. It exists because:

  • Human readability — open any markdown viewer and see summary counts, phase breakdowns, gas profiles, storage changes, and event frequencies at a glance.
  • Shareability — attach to publication bundles, include in PRs, send to non-technical stakeholders.
  • Quick inspection — "Did the attack run? How many events did it emit? Any failures?" answered in 3 seconds.

It's not the source of truth (the JSONL streams are). It's a convenience layer on top.

docs.title: 8. Open Questions for the Team
docs.text:
1. Run retention policy — What's the default? Keep all runs indefinitely? Auto-clean after 30 days? Tiered by importance?
  2. Remote artifact storage — Do we want crucible push to S3/IPFS/archive? In scope for Phase 2 or Phase 3?
  3. Dashboard — The current dashboard is a view layer over the JSONL/logs. Does it need deeper integration (e.g., running experiments from the UI), or is "read-only view" the right scope?
  4. Community/OSS — Do we want to accept external attack plugins? If so, what's the review process? (Impacts P0 items like evidence validation — external plugins need stricter gates.)

docs.title: 9. Summary: Where We Excel, Where We Lack, What We'll Do
Where We Excel
docs.text: Area Why Architecture Plugin system, topology abstraction, and scenario engine are genuinely composable. The separation of chains/bridges/artifacts/attacks is clean and correct. Artifact pipeline 6 typed JSONL streams, deterministic serialization, SHA-256 integrity manifests, provenance tracking. Above the bar for most academic artifact submissions. Attack fidelity honesty Each attack's fidelity level is documented accurately. No over-claiming. The testbed is honest about what it can and can't reproduce (EVM-only, Solana sysvar root cause, etc.). This is a credibility asset. Cross-platform Windows, macOS, and Linux all supported. shutil.which(), CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP on nt, msvcrt vs fcntl file locking — this is genuinely cross-platform. Testing 521 unit tests, integration tests for CLI/experiment/baseline/publication, dashboard e2e tests. Good coverage for a research tool. Docker deployment Fully containerized. docker compose up runs everything. docs/DEPLOYMENT.md covers SSH clone → Docker deployment.

docs.title: Where We Lack
docs.text: Area The Gap Trust verification The testbed claims forks are at the right block — but never proves it at run time. Evidence.yaml declares contracts and blocks but never checks them against the live fork. Fixing this is the single highest-leverage rigor upgrade (item 3). Silent degradation A run can lose trace data (receipt not found, trace failed) and still report outcome: "success". The researcher has no machine-readable way to know artifacts are incomplete (item 1). Reset correctness Soft reset (the default) uses anvil_loadState which merges, not wipes. State from previous runs can leak into the next run. This breaks determinism guarantees for parameter sweeps and A/B comparisons (item 2). CI attack testing The one real reproduction test requires an archive node and is always skipped in CI. CI proves nothing about whether attacks still reproduce after refactors (item 4). Fidelity definitions Fidelity levels (L0–L4) describe what metadata is present in evidence.yaml, not what behavior was demonstrated. An L3 attack that lists exploit_transactions but doesn't replay them is still classified L3. No runtime check that declared fidelity matches actual execution (item 16). Bridge simulation model BridgePlugin.send_message() is a researcher-written mock, not a replayed protocol. At L4 fidelity, a reviewer will ask "how do you know the mock is faithful?" — and there's no built-in answer. Bridge plugins need protocol specification references and behavioral assertions (item 17). LLM spend safety Agent experiments have iteration caps but no dollar-cost budget. A parameter sweep with frontier models can burn significant API spend with no warning (item 8). Process lifecycle Port race conditions, swallowed kill errors, and partial cleanup failures are the most common source of cryptic errors (item 10). Onboarding 29 docs with no golden path. No single "run these commands" tutorial that takes a new user from zero to reproduced attack (item 7).

docs.title: What We'll Do (The Tasklist)
docs.text:
# Item Why (one line) Effort 1 artifacts_complete flag So incomplete artifacts are visible, not silent S 2 Default reset → medium So state leakage between runs is eliminated XS 3 Evidence pre-flight validation So the testbed proves state matches claims M 4 RPC recording/replay system So CI can test any attack without an archive node L 5 Optional run alias (--name) So researchers can label runs for easy retrieval XS 6 crucible verify <bundle> So anyone can re-derive and verify published artifacts M 7 10-minute TUTORIAL.md So a new user reproduces a hack in 10 minutes S 8 LLM spend cap So agent experiments don't accidentally run up bills S 9 Checkpoint integrity validation So corrupt checkpoints fail fast, not silently S 10 Port/process lifecycle verification So orphan processes don't cause cryptic errors S 11 Consolidate redundant docs So the 29 docs don't overwhelm new users S 12 Per-attack fidelity table in BENCHMARKS.md So reviewers see honesty at a glance XS 13 Run tiers (scratch/saved/published) So important runs survive cleanup policies M 14 Bundle-time JSONL schema validation So field-type drift is caught at export time S 15 --from-tx attack scaffolder So new attacks are authored from a single tx hash L 16 Fidelity level runtime enforcement So L3 claims are verified (reproduction results compared to real chain receipts) M 17 Bridge plugin evidence requirements So L4 bridge plugins must declare protocol spec, behavioral assertions, and excluded components with impact analysis S

(Effort: XS = hours, S = half day, M = 1-2 days, L = 2-3 days)

docs.title: Phased Delivery
docs.text:
Phase Focus Items Target 1 Trust & Correctness 1, 2, 3, 4, 7 Next sprint 2 Researcher Experience 5, 6, 11 Next sprint 3 Robustness 8, 9, 10 Sprint 3 4 Polish + Rigor 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17 Sprint 4

This document was generated from discussions following an expert architecture review on June 7, 2026. It captures the consensus between the project lead and the reviewing team on priorities and approach.

docs.title: Technical Overview
docs.text: Audience: Researchers and developers who want to understand how Crucible works, why it was built this way, and how the pieces fit together.

docs.title: Architectural Philosophy
docs.text: Crucible's architecture is guided by four core design principles:

docs.title: 1. Separation of Exploit Logic from Infrastructure
docs.text:
Crucible isolates artifact collection and chain management from the core exploit logic. Researchers authoring attack plugins can focus exclusively on exploit mechanics (e.g., crafting calldata, invoking contracts, and asserting state changes) without managing underlying infrastructure. The framework abstracts away:

  • Historical chain fork synchronization
  • Transaction instrumentation (event and storage diff capture)
  • Cross-run determinism verification
  • Artifact packaging for publication

docs.title: 2. Default Determinism
docs.text:
Blockchain state forks inherently exhibit non-deterministic properties due to RPC provider variations, transaction ordering, and fluctuating gas prices. Crucible enforces deterministic execution by default through the following mechanisms:

  • Fixed gas price (--gas-price 0) to eliminate gas-based variability.
  • FIFO transaction ordering (--order fifo) to bypass mempool sorting behavior.
  • Pinned fork blocks to ensure every run originates from an identical historical state.
  • Static fork-block timestamps to eliminate time-dependent execution divergence.
  • Auto-mine execution to resolve block timing variance.

These constraints ensure that unaltered execution paths yield identical artifacts across successive runs.

docs.title: 3. Transparent and Universal Instrumentation
docs.text:
A significant source of friction in blockchain security research is the overhead of instrumenting transaction logic to capture execution traces. Crucible utilizes an RPC proxy layer that intercepts communication between the attack execution and the local chain fork.

This proxy architecture enables universal artifact capture (gas profiles, event emissions, storage diffs, and balance changes) regardless of the attack's implementation language or tooling (e.g., Python, forge script, or cast), without requiring explicit instrumentation code within the attack logic.

docs.title: 4. Self-Validating Publication Artifacts
docs.text: Every experiment run generates a standardized, self-validating artifact bundle. These bundles employ SHA-256 hashing to ensure data integrity and include comprehensive run manifests that capture the execution provenance (git commit hash, Foundry version, and configuration fingerprint). Furthermore, automated fidelity enforcers verify that the empirical behavior of the run aligns with its declared reproducibility level.

docs.title: Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    subgraph L4 ["CLI Layer"]
        A["crucible/commands/ (20 modules)<br>Thin wrappers, zero business logic"]
    end
    subgraph L3 ["Orchestration Layer"]
        B["ExperimentRunner, ScenarioEngine, BaselineManager<br>PublicationGate, HistoryIndex, ExportBundle<br>FidelityEnforcer, CheckpointManager"]
    end
    subgraph L2 ["Runtime Layer"]
        C["ChainManager, PluginRegistry, JsonRpcClient<br>RpcProxy, BridgeLifecycle, LLMAgentPlugin<br>RpcProviderManager"]
    end
    subgraph L1 ["Foundation Layer"]
        D["config.py, sdk.py, logging.py, errors.py<br>provenance.py, evidence.py, hashing.py, filesystem.py<br>env.py, ids.py, utils.py"]
    end

    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1

docs.title: Layer Rules
docs.text: Layer Imports From Responsibility CLI (L4) All lower layers Command parsing, delegation — zero business logic Orchestration (L3) L2, L1 Coordinates multi-step workflows, manages state transitions Runtime (L2) L1 only Manages external processes (Anvil), I/O, plugin lifecycle Foundation (L1) stdlib only No upward imports, no external process dependencies

docs.title: Experiment
docs.text:
An experiment is a YAML file that wires together a topology, scenario, and attack plugin:

# experiments/nomad_replay.yaml
name: nomad_replay
scenario: nomad_pre_exploit
attack: nomad_replica_fraud
topology: single

Running crucible experiment run experiments/nomad_replay.yaml triggers the full experiment lifecycle: fork chains → validate evidence → deploy bridge → run scenario steps → start instrumentation → execute attack → verify fidelity → capture artifacts.

docs.title: Topology
docs.text:
A topology declares which chains to fork and (optionally) which bridge plugin to deploy:

# topologies/bridge.yaml
name: bridge
chains: [ethereum, polygon]
bridge: nomad_bridge

Topologies are reusable — the same attack can run on different topologies without modification. Adding a new chain config doesn't affect existing experiments.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
A scenario defines the chain environment before the attack runs — contract state, impersonated accounts, initial conditions:

# scenarios/nomad_pre_exploit/scenario.yaml
phases:
  - name: setup
    steps:
      - type: log
        message: "Setting up Nomad Replica contract state"
      - type: impersonate
        chain: ethereum
        account: "0x..."
      - type: send_tx
        chain: ethereum
        to: "0xB92..."
        data: "0x..."

Scenarios use a registry-based step dispatcher — each step type (log, send_tx, impersonate, forge_script, assert_balance, etc.) is registered via a decorator. New step types can be added without modifying the engine.

docs.title: Attack Plugin
docs.text:
An attack plugin is a Python class that subclasses AttackPlugin and implements:

class MyExploit(AttackPlugin):
    def execute(self, context, phase=None):
        # Forge calldata, send transactions, check results
        return AttackResult(success=True, ...)

    def verify(self, context):
        # Check on-chain state matches expectations
        return VerificationResult(passed=True, ...)

The plugin receives an ExperimentContext through which it accesses chain state (context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum") → JsonRpcClient), logs events, and reads configuration.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Every attack plugin must include an evidence.yaml that documents:

  • Incident metadata — name, date, affected bridge, damage
  • Sources — advisories, postmortems, transaction explorers
  • Historical scope — chains, fork blocks, exploit transactions, affected contracts
  • Reproduction scope — fidelity level (L0–L4), modeled components, excluded components
  • Acceptance criteria — required assertions that must pass

Evidence is validated against live chain state before every run — fork blocks are checked via eth_getBlockByNumber, contract addresses via eth_getCode.

docs.title: Fidelity Levels
docs.text:
The L0–L4 fidelity system is enforced at runtime, not just documented. Each level has specific requirements:

Level Name Requirements Runtime Checks L0 Narrative Description None L1 Conceptual Plugin returns a result Result exists L2 Fork-State Chains + contracts verified Trace artifacts exist, chains match topology L3 Exact Replay Exploit transactions declared Transaction actions in run.jsonl L4 Multi-Chain 2+ chains, bridge, excluded_components Cross-chain artifacts, bridge plugin loaded

docs.title: Instrumentation Proxy
docs.text:
The instrumentation proxy is a transparent HTTP proxy that sits between the attack plugin and Anvil:

Attack Plugin → Proxy (port random) → Anvil (port 8545)

After every eth_sendTransaction, the proxy automatically:

  1. Polls eth_getTransactionReceipt to get the receipt (gas used, logs)
  2. Calls debug_traceTransaction with prestateTracer in diffMode to get storage diffs and balance changes
  3. Emits all data to JSONL artifact streams

The attack plugin never knows it's being instrumented. ChainManager.runtime_for() silently returns the proxy URL instead of the direct Anvil URL.

docs.title: Phase 1: Setup
docs.text:
CLI → ExperimentRunner → ScenarioEngine → ChainManager
                                         ↘ PluginRegistry
  1. Load config — testbed.yaml + testbed.local.yaml → Pydantic-validated TestbedConfig
