  2. Load experiment YAML — topology, scenario, attack plugin specified
  3. Auto-merge fork blocks — if evidence.yaml specifies a different fork block, update config automatically
  4. Verify Foundry version — check forge --version against required_version
  5. Run publication gates — if in publication mode, check evidence completeness, baseline existence, git provenance
  6. Execute scenario — chain forking starts here. The scenario engine executes YAML steps (log, impersonate, send_tx, forge_script, etc.)

docs.title: Phase 2: Instrumentation
docs.text:
ExperimentRunner → ChainManager.start_instrumentation()
                 → RpcProxy (per chain)
                     → HTTPServer on random port
                     → ThreadPoolExecutor (max 10 workers)

Each chain gets its own proxy instance with its own handler class (via _make_handler() closure). The proxy:

  • Forwards all JSON-RPC requests to Anvil
  • Detects eth_sendTransaction / eth_sendRawTransaction
  • Schedules background trace tasks via ThreadPoolExecutor

docs.title: Phase 3: Attack Execution
docs.text:
ExperimentRunner → PluginRegistry.load("attack", name)
                 → AttackPlugin.run(context)
                     → AttackPlugin.execute(context)
                         → eth_sendTransaction (via proxy URL)
                         → Proxy captures: gas, events, storage diffs, balance diffs

The attack plugin interacts with the chain through context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum").rpc_url, which points to the proxy, not directly to Anvil. Every transaction is automatically instrumented.

docs.title: Phase 4: Teardown
docs.text:
ExperimentRunner → ChainManager.stop_instrumentation()
                 → Proxy.drain()     — wait for pending traces
                 → Proxy.stop()      — shutdown HTTP server
                 → LogManager.write_report()
                 → LogManager.write_integrity()
                 → FidelityEnforcer.check()
                 → write run_manifest.json

The shutdown sequence is critical: drain() must be called before stop() and before log handles close. This ensures all async trace tasks complete before artifacts are finalized.

docs.title: Configuration Hierarchy
docs.text:
.env                              ← RPC URLs, secrets
  ↓ load_dotenv()
testbed.yaml                      ← Committed to git (base config)
  ↓
testbed.local.yaml                ← Gitignored (per-user overrides)
  ↓ deep_merge()
↓ expand_env()
↓ Pydantic model_validate()
→ TestbedConfig

docs.title: Key Config Models
docs.text:
class TestbedConfig:
    workspace: str
    active_topology: str
    foundry: FoundryConfig
    publication: PublicationConfig
    chains: dict[str, ChainConfig]     # Per-chain configuration
    rpc_providers: dict[str, list]     # Per-chain RPC failover list
    fork_cache: ForkCacheConfig
    logging: LoggingConfig
    cleanup: CleanupConfig
    rpc_budget: RpcBudgetConfig
    notifications: NotificationConfig

class ChainConfig:
    rpc_url: str
    fork_block: int | None             # None = blank fork (no remote)
    chain_id: int
    port: int
    host: str
    determinism: DeterminismConfig

class DeterminismConfig:
    gas_price: int = 0
    block_base_fee: int = 1_000_000_000
    timestamp: int | Literal["fork_block"] = "fork_block"
    auto_mine: bool = True
    order: str = "fifo"

docs.title: Built-In Validations
docs.text:
• Port uniqueness — no two chains can share a port
  • Chain ID uniqueness — no two chains can share a chain_id
  • Port range — must be 1–65535
  • Matrix detection — load_experiment() rejects matrix files with a helpful error message

docs.title: CLI-First Architecture
docs.text: Every action is available via crucible <command>. The Streamlit dashboard is a separate read-only viewer. This makes experiments scriptable, Docker-friendly, and automatable.

docs.title: Topology-Driven Composition
docs.text: Topology YAML files declare which chains to fork. The same attack can run on different topologies. Adding a new chain config doesn't affect existing experiments.

docs.title: Transparent Instrumentation
docs.text: The proxy intercepts all RPC traffic. Researchers never add logging code. Any attack method (Python, Forge, cast send) produces complete artifacts.

docs.title: Snapshot-Driven Reset
docs.text: Baselines are captured once after setup. Soft resets use anvil_loadState — no chain restart needed. This enables fast iteration during development.

docs.title: Determinism by Default
docs.text: Deterministic flags are opt-out, not opt-in. If a researcher does nothing, their experiments are deterministic.

docs.title: Plugins for Capabilities
docs.text: New functionality arrives as plugins, not core rewrites: - attack — exploit reproductions - bridge — bridge protocol simulation - agent — LLM-driven autonomous agents

docs.title: Fidelity Levels Are Enforced
docs.text: The L0–L4 fidelity system isn't just documentation — publication gates and runtime enforcers check requirements programmatically.

docs.title: Separation of Scenario and Experiment
docs.text: Scenarios define environments (chain state, setup). Experiments pair a scenario + attack plugin for formal runs. The same scenario can be used with different attacks, and the same attack can run against different scenarios.

docs.title: Three Plugin Types
docs.text: Type Base Class Purpose Lifecycle Attack AttackPlugin Exploit reproduction prepare() → execute() → verify() → rollback() → cleanup() Bridge BridgePlugin Cross-chain simulation deploy() → configure() → connect() → send_message() → reset() → destroy() Agent AgentPlugin LLM-driven agents prepare() → observe() → think() → respond() (loop) → verify()

docs.title: Discovery
docs.text:
Plugins are discovered from attacks/, bridges/, and plugins/ directories. Each plugin must include a plugin.yaml manifest:

name: nomad_replica_fraud
version: 1.0.0
type: attack
entry: nomad_replica_fraud.py
class: NomadReplicaFraud

Attack plugins must also include evidence.yaml. The registry validates evidence structure during discovery.

docs.title: ExperimentContext
docs.text:
Every plugin method receives an ExperimentContext through which it accesses:

  • Chain state — context.chains.runtime_for("ethereum") → JsonRpcClient
  • Logging — context.log.step("message"), context.log.event(...)
  • Configuration — context.config.chains["ethereum"].fork_block
  • Bridges — context.bridge_plugin.send_message(...) (if bridge plugin deployed)

docs.title: Anvil Subprocess Lifecycle
docs.text:
Configured → Starting (Popen) → WaitingReady (poll eth_blockNumber)
→ Running → Instrumented (proxy attached)
→ Draining (flush traces) → Running → Stopped

docs.title: Blank Fork Mode
docs.text:
When fork_block: null, Anvil starts without --fork-url as a standalone local EVM. This enables:

  • Fully offline operation — no RPC provider needed
  • Instant startup — <1 second instead of 30s–5min
  • Full determinism — no archive state variability
  • CI/hermetic testing — zero external dependencies

The instrumentation proxy works identically in both modes — no code changes needed.

docs.title: 3-Tier Reset
docs.text: Tier Method Speed Use Case Soft anvil_loadState snapshot <5s Matrix runs, parameter sweeps Medium Kill → restart → restore <30s After process corruption Hard Re-fork from archive RPC <5min After cache corruption, topology change

docs.title: 7 JSONL Streams
docs.text: Stream Content Source run.jsonl Experiment lifecycle events LogManager.step() events.jsonl Contract events (Transfer, Approval, etc.) Proxy via LogEmitter.event() storage_diffs.jsonl Storage slot changes (contract, slot, before, after) Proxy via LogEmitter.storage_diff() gas_profile.jsonl Gas used per transaction Proxy via LogEmitter.gas() balance_diffs.jsonl ETH/token balance changes Proxy via LogEmitter.balance_diff() cross_chain.jsonl Bridge message correlation IDs LogEmitter.cross_chain() agent_reasoning.jsonl LLM agent reasoning traces LLMAgentPlugin._log_reasoning_step()

docs.title: Log Directory Structure
docs.text:
logs/
  nomad_replay/
    DS-2026-00042/                    ← Run directory
      run.jsonl                       ← Lifecycle events
      run_manifest.json               ← Provenance + outcome
      log_integrity.json              ← SHA-256 hashes
      report.md                       ← Human-readable summary
      chain_ethereum/                 ← Per-chain artifacts
        events.jsonl
        gas_profile.jsonl
        storage_diffs.jsonl
        balance_diffs.jsonl
      chain_bsc/
        ...
      bridge/
        cross_chain.jsonl

docs.title: Integrity Verification
docs.text: Every run produces a log_integrity.json with SHA-256 hashes of all artifact files, and an artifact_bundle_manifest.json with hashes of all run files. These enable crucible verify <run-id> to detect tampering or data corruption.

docs.title: Fidelity Enforcement
docs.text:
Fidelity enforcement runs after the attack executes but before the run manifest is written. This means the manifest reflects the fidelity check outcome.

The FidelityEnforcer runs cumulative checks:

  1. L0 — Always passes (narrative only)
  2. L1 — Verifies the plugin returned a result
  3. L2 — Verifies chains match topology, trace artifacts exist, no auto-trace failures
  4. L3 — Verifies exploit transactions declared, transaction actions in run.jsonl
  5. L4 — Verifies 2+ chains, bridge plugin loaded, excluded_components declared

docs.title: Two Operational Modes
docs.text: Mode Command When to Use Single-container docker compose run orchestrator ... Most experiments — Anvil runs inside orchestrator Daemon + orchestrator docker compose up -d anvil-ethereum && docker compose run orchestrator ... Multi-session workflows

docs.title: Container Composition
docs.text:
orchestrator — Python 3.12 + Foundry + uv | Entrypoint: uv run crucible
dashboard    — Streamlit UI | Port 8501
anvil-ethereum — Foundry Anvil daemon | Port 8545
anvil-polygon  — Foundry Anvil daemon | Port 8546
anvil-bsc      — Foundry Anvil daemon | Port 8547
anvil-harmony  — Foundry Anvil daemon | Port 8548

Named volumes (states_data, logs_data) persist chain state and experiment artifacts across container restarts.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text: Doc What It Covers Architecture Deep Dive Complete component map, all 39 modules Design Decisions Key tradeoffs and rationale Instrumentation Architecture RPC proxy design in detail Plugin Development Guide Writing attack, bridge, and agent plugins (in website/) Fidelity Guide L0–L4 fidelity levels and enforcement Reproducibility Guide Provenance, determinism, bug log

docs.title: USENIX Submission Notes
docs.text: Purpose: Map Crucible's features to USENIX Security paper claims and provide AE-specific notes for evaluators.

docs.title: Paper Claim Mapping
docs.text: Paper Claim Crucible Feature How to Verify Reproducible bridge exploit reproduction Scenario engine + baseline capture/verify/restore + deterministic Anvil flags Run experiment twice, compare with crucible diff Evidence-backed methodology evidence.yaml + fidelity levels L0-L4 crucible plugin validate checks completeness Provenance tracking gather_provenance() in every run manifest run_manifest.json has git commit, foundry version, cache state Publication integrity Publication gates (6 rules) + artifact manifests + bundle hashes Run with --publication-mode and verify with crucible verify Cache hygiene CacheManager with strict/permissive policy crucible cache inspect shows provider identity, block, version Attack data extraction crucible attack extract auto-generates packs from tx hashes Extract a transaction, review generated files

docs.title: AE Submission Checklist
docs.text:
• Repository tagged (e.g., v0.2.0-ae)
  • Zenodo DOI minted automatically via GitHub release
  • CITATION.cff present (needs real ORCID before tagging)
  • LICENSE file present (MIT)
  • docs/AE_WALKTHROUGH.md complete and tested
  • scripts/ae_up.sh, ae_run_demo.sh, ae_collect.sh, ae_clean.sh ready
  • Docker build verified on clean environment
  • Demo experiment verified end-to-end
  • 808 unit tests pass with no external dependencies
  • Known issues documented (see docs/internal/DEVELOPER_HANDOFF.md)

docs.title: What Changed Since Original Checklist
docs.text: Item Status LICENSE file [ok] Created (MIT) CITATION.cff [ok] Updated (repo URL, cleaned authors) AE scripts [ok] Rewritten with Docker→local fallback, bundle verification Linting [ok] ruff check configured in pyproject.toml Coverage [ok] pytest --cov configured in pyproject.toml Dev deps [ok] pytest-cov>=5.0.0, ruff>=0.5.0 in pyproject.toml Version sync [ok] pyproject.toml 0.4.0 matches CITATION.cff Known issues [ok] Updated (Nomad EOA fixed, Wormhole L2 exemplar complete)

docs.title: Known Issues For Evaluators
docs.text:
1. Nomad addresses were wrong — The scaffold originally used an EOA as the Replica contract. This has been corrected to 0xB92336759618F55bd0F8313bd843604592E27bd8 (verified on Etherscan). Ready for live fork test.

  2. 0x46e91d4f selector unresolved — The Nomad exploit transaction at 0x5ea16a4f9104b901a75662f3900ca8d3d92209d846310237ca65675e2f2e519c uses selector 0x46e91d4f, which does NOT match any standard Solidity function signature. Requires proxy ABI fetch to resolve.

  3. Multi-chain exemplar — BNB Token Hub and Horizon packs now have real research data (fork blocks, tx hashes, contract addresses, postmortems). Poly Network data also filled. All three need live Anvil fork verification.

  4. Multichain pack reclassified — The Multichain "router permit" was reclassified from L2 contract exploit to L1 MPC infrastructure narrative. No on-chain reproduction possible for this incident.

docs.title: Evaluator Instructions
docs.text:
See docs/AE_WALKTHROUGH.md for the complete walkthrough. The key commands for evaluators:

# Verify environment
docker compose run --rm orchestrator doctor

# Run demo experiment
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml

# Run with publication gates
docker compose run --rm orchestrator experiment run experiments/demo_experiment.yaml --publication-mode

# Export and verify
docker compose run --rm orchestrator export <run_id>
docker compose run --rm orchestrator verify datasets/<run_id>

docs.title: Version Tagging Convention
docs.text:
• Release tags: v1.0.0, v1.1.0, etc.
  • AE tags: v1.0.0-ae (branch from the release tag with AE-specific fixes)
  • The AE tag should be the one submitted with the paper

docs.title: Crucible — Website & Documentation Quality Assessment
docs.text: Prepared for: Engineering team Date: 2026-06-15 Scope: Documentation (docs/, README.md), the MkDocs website (website/), and their consistency with the codebase at the current working tree (v0.4.0). Method: Static analysis only — no code executed, no site served. Documented commands/flags/APIs/claims were verified against the actual source (crucible/), the attack packs (attacks/), and the repo's tracked-file state. Findings from automated sweeps were re-verified by hand before inclusion; corrections are noted.

docs.title: Verdict: NOT release-ready — but the problem is packaging, not content
docs.text:
This is the key distinction for planning:

  • Documentation content (prose accuracy vs. code): GOOD. The CLI reference, tutorial, user guide, scenario-authoring, plugin-development, fidelity, and LLM-agent docs were verified command-by-command and API-by-API against the source and are accurate or only minor-drift. Someone following the content will mostly succeed. This is genuinely strong and rare.
  • Release packaging (README links, website config, confidentiality, branding): NOT READY. The front door (README) has ~27 broken documentation links, the public website is configured to publish 20 internal documents (including a security audit and USENIX submission notes), and the site still carries placeholder branding. These are blockers for a public/academic release.

Bottom line: the writing is shippable; the release hygiene is not. None of the blockers are hard to fix — they're configuration, link paths, and file-placement decisions — but until they are fixed, do not publish the site or tag a public release, and in particular do not run mkdocs gh-deploy, which would expose the internal docs.

docs.title: 🔴 BLOCKER
docs.text:
W1 — The public website is configured to publish the entire docs/internal/ tree (20 documents). website/mkdocs.yml (docs_dir: ../docs) lists every internal doc in its nav under "Developer Internals", "Planning & Strategy", and "Reviews" (lines 112–138). All 20 docs/internal/*.md are git-tracked, so mkdocs build/gh-deploy will render and publish them. Exposed material includes: - internal/SECURITY_AUDIT.md — threat model, isolation analysis, and a residual-risk table enumerating leak/exfiltration vectors (adversary-useful, self-incriminating). - internal/USENIX_SUBMISSION_NOTES.md — double-blind de-anonymization hazard. A page literally titled "USENIX Submission Notes" on the project's public site links the public artifact to the (anonymous) submission, and leaks evaluator-facing strategy and "known issues for evaluators." - internal/DOCS_GAP_ANALYSIS.md — admits "61% doc coverage, 20 missing features, CLI 67%, core modules 49%." - internal/STRATEGIC_PLAN.md, ROADMAP_TO_100.md, IMPLEMENTATION_STATUS.md — internal roadmaps/status. - internal/Final_review_full.md, feedback_response.md, thearchitectural_and_code_review.md, DEVELOPER_HANDOFF_SUMMARY.md — internal peer-review threads with reviewer scores and bug lists.

This is the single most serious item. Publishing the security audit and USENIX submission notes is both a confidentiality and a (potential) double-blind-integrity problem. Fix: remove all internal/* entries from mkdocs.yml nav (and from docs/index.md, see W3); keep internal docs out of docs_dir, e.g. move them to a non-published location or add an exclude. Decide deliberately which (if any) technical internals — TECHNICAL_OVERVIEW, DESIGN_DECISIONS, BRIDGE_PLUGIN_LIMITATIONS, DOCKER_IMPLEMENTATION — to keep, after review.

W2 — README documentation links are almost entirely broken (≈27 of ~39). Every link in the README "Documentation" tables points to website/docs/…, but there is no website/docs/ directory (the site's docs_dir is ../docs; the docs live in docs/). Verified: website/docs/** matches zero files. Additionally, three targets use pre-rename filenames that no longer exist: GETTING_STARTED.md (now USER_GUIDE.md), ARCHITECTURE_AND_DESIGN.md (now ARCHITECTURE.md), SCOPE_AND_CAPABILITIES.md (now CAPABILITIES.md). A reader clicking almost any doc link from the README (GitHub front page) hits a 404. Fix: rewrite website/docs/… → docs/… and correct the three renamed targets. docs/index.md already uses the correct paths and can be used as the reference.

docs.title: 🟠 HIGH
docs.text:
W3 — The public site landing page (docs/index.md) itself links to internal docs. Independent of the nav, docs/index.md:102–111 links to internal/DEVELOPER_HANDOFF.md, IMPLEMENTATION_STATUS.md, ROADMAP_TO_100.md, STRATEGIC_PLAN.md, SECURITY_AUDIT.md, AGENT_SYSTEM_REVIEW.md, etc. So even with the nav fixed, the home page routes readers to confidential material. (index.md is the site Home.) Fix: strip the "Internal & Reference" internal links from docs/index.md.

W4 — Nine internal review reports are git-tracked at the repo root and will ship in any source/Zenodo archive. CODE_REVIEW_REPORT.md, DOCS_VERIFICATION_REPORT.md, REMAINING_ISSUES_REPORT.md, SCIENTIFIC_INTEGRITY_AUDIT.md, FINAL_RELEASE_SCAN.md, FINAL_PROJECT_REPORT.md, FINAL_PRE_RELEASE_REPORT.md, FINDINGS_AND_IMPROVEMENT_PLAN.md, REVIEW_FINDINGS.md — all tracked. These are candid internal assessments (the full vulnerability history, including this audit series). They should not be in a public artifact. Fix: move to an untracked scratch/-style location or delete before tagging. (This assessment file itself is in the same category — relocate it too once actioned.)

W5 — Central scientific claim (fidelity tiering) in README contradicts the artifacts. README.md:5 states "3 at L2 reproducibility (Nomad, Wormhole, Poly Network), 2 executable scaffolds (Horizon, BNB Bridge), 1 narrative (Multichain)." But the actual evidence.yaml declarations are: Nomad L2, Wormhole L2, Poly Network L2, Horizon L2, BNB L2, Multichain L1. So five packs declare L2, not three — Horizon and BNB are described as "scaffolds" in the README yet declare fidelity_level: L2 (with exact_tx_replay: false) in their evidence. A USENIX artifact evaluator cross-referencing the README against evidence.yaml will see a direct contradiction in the headline reproducibility claim. Fix: reconcile the two — either downgrade Horizon/BNB evidence.yaml to a scaffold-appropriate level, or restate the README tiering to match the declared levels and explain the "scaffold = L2 without exact_tx_replay" nuance.

W6 — Site branding is placeholder/internal. website/mkdocs.yml: site_description: "Internal documentation for the Crucible…" (the word Internal on a public site, line 7); repo_url/social link = https://github.com/your-org/crucible (unfilled placeholder, lines 9 & 59) → the "edit" and GitHub links on every page are broken. Fix: set a public-facing description and the real repo URL.

docs.title: 🟡 MEDIUM (content accuracy — fix before release, low effort)
docs.text:
W7 — README.md:15 claims "808 unit tests"; the real count is different. Actual def test_ functions under tests/unit/ = 757; pytest collects ~805 items (parametrization); a prior internal report said 805. Three different numbers, none of which is 808. Also "no external deps" is slightly misleading: the bridge Solidity tests require Foundry/forge (they fail without it). Fix: state the real collected count and qualify the foundry-dependent subset.

W8 — README.md:37 says "agent plugins planned" — but they already exist. The codebase ships AgentPlugin (sdk.py:174), the agent loop (agent_loop.py), LLM providers (llm_providers.py), agent attack packs (attacks/llm_exploit_agent, attacks/demo_agent), and a full docs/RUNNING_LLM_AGENTS.md. "Planned" is stale and contradicts the rest of the docs. Fix: change to reflect that agent plugins are implemented.

W9 — docs/CUSTOM_CONTRACT_TESTING.md falsely claims "10 pre-funded accounts (10,000 ETH each)". Anvil is launched with --accounts 0 --balance 0 (chain_manager.py:92–93), so no default dev accounts exist. The doc's worked example survives only because the deploy script funds the victim via vm.deal() with gas-price 0; the stated "account #5 attacker with 10,000 ETH" is false and will mislead users writing their own scripts. (CUSTOM_CONTRACT_TESTING.md:62–64, 503–506) Fix: correct the environment description to match --accounts 0 --balance 0.

W10 — docs/SCENARIO_AUTHORING.md verify_tracing example uses the wrong field. Doc shows chain: ethereum (singular); the handler reads chains (plural list) plus min_events/min_gas_entries/min_storage_diffs/min_balance_diffs/check_consistency (scenario_steps.py:386–391). A copy-pasted example silently does nothing useful. Fix: correct the field name and document the real tuning fields.

W11 — docs/PLUGIN_DEVELOPMENT.md:108 shows crucible attack init my_exploit --type attack — --type does not exist. attack init flags are --chain/--fidelity/--author/--with-scenario/--from-tx/--rpc-url (commands/attack.py). The command as documented errors out. Fix: remove --type.

docs.title: 🟢 LOW (polish)
docs.text:
• W12 — README "6 benchmark + 2 demo packs" doesn't map cleanly to the 11 dirs in attacks/. Beyond the 6 benchmarks and 2 demos there are l4_full_validation, nomad_bridge_demo, llm_exploit_agent (validation/agent fixtures). Either fold them into the count narrative or note them as fixtures.
  • W13 — Authoring docs disagree with each other on evidence.yaml field shapes: attacker_addresses (plural) vs attacker_address (singular); required_assertions as list-of-dicts vs list-of-strings. The validator tolerates both, but the docs should be self-consistent.
  • W14 — docs/CLI_REFERENCE.md:175 tags bundle verify as [deprecated], but the command is fully functional with no deprecation in code. Remove the stale tag or implement the deprecation.
  • W15 — docs/TUTORIAL.md has a hand-written doctor sample output that doesn't match the real Rich table, a duplicated table header (~line 423), and PowerShell code fences on Unix commands. Cosmetic.
  • W16 — commands/attack.py:153 has a stale help example referencing a nonexistent --bsc-rpc-url flag (code comment, surfaces in --help).
  • W17 — Missing standard release files: no CONTRIBUTING.md, no CHANGELOG.md. (LICENSE ✓ MIT and CITATION.cff ✓ are present — good for an academic artifact.)

docs.title: What was checked and is genuinely good
docs.text:
• CLI documentation is faithfully in sync with the code. CLI_REFERENCE.md, TUTORIAL.md, USER_GUIDE.md, SIMPLE_OPERATIONS.md, OPERATIONS_RUNBOOK.md were verified against all 21 command modules: no documented command/flag is missing or fictional, and every example file (experiments/*.yaml, topologies/*, plugin paths) referenced exists on disk. Verdict: ACCURATE.
  • Plugin SDK, scenario steps, fidelity levels, and LLM-agent docs match the code. All 11 @register_step handlers are documented; SDK class names (CruciblePlugin/AttackPlugin/BridgePlugin/AgentPlugin), method signatures, and AttackResult/VerificationResult fields match sdk.py; fidelity L0–L4 checks and error strings match enforcer.py verbatim; LLM provider names/models/env vars/tool-privilege counts match llm_providers.py/agent_tools.py. (Only W9/W10/W11 drift within these.)
  • "7 JSONL streams" is accurate (6 in STREAMS + agent_reasoning.jsonl). "$1.9B+" is reasonable (sum of the six historical losses is ≈$2B). SHA-256 integrity / publication gates / determinism pinning claims match the code reviewed in prior rounds.
  • docs/index.md uses the correct current doc filenames (unlike the README) — it was updated when files were renamed; the README was not.

docs.title: Corrected (claims that did NOT hold up on verification)
docs.text:
• crucible.db is NOT git-tracked (an automated sweep claimed it was). git ls-files crucible.db → empty. Not a finding.
  • website/site/ is correctly gitignored (website/.gitignore → site/) and untracked — it will not ship via git. It is a stale local build (built from the pre-rename, pre-internal/-expansion structure: orphan GETTING_STARTED/, ARCHITECTURE_AND_DESIGN/, SCOPE_AND_CAPABILITIES/ pages, only 4 of 20 internal pages rendered). Harmless to git, but it confirms the deploy is not reproducible from current source — a fresh build is required (and W1 activates on that build unless the nav is fixed first).

docs.title: Release-readiness checklist (documentation/website)
docs.text:
Must fix before any public release / site deploy: 1. [ ] W1 — Remove internal/* from mkdocs.yml nav; keep internal docs out of the published docs_dir. Do not gh-deploy until done. 2. [ ] W3 — Remove internal links from docs/index.md. 3. [ ] W2 — Fix the ~27 broken README links (website/docs/… → docs/…; fix 3 renamed targets). 4. [ ] W4 — Relocate/delete the 9 root review reports (and this file) before tagging. 5. [ ] W5 — Reconcile the README L2 tiering with the evidence.yaml fidelity levels. 6. [ ] W6 — Real repo_url + public site_description.

Should fix (low effort, credibility): 7. [ ] W7 (test count + "no external deps"), W8 ("agent plugins planned" stale), W9 (10-accounts false), W10 (verify_tracing field), W11 (attack init --type).

Nice to have: W12–W17 (count narrative, doc self-consistency, stale [deprecated] tag, tutorial cosmetics, --bsc-rpc-url help, CONTRIBUTING/CHANGELOG).

docs.title: Why this verdict
docs.text:
For a normal OSS tool, the broken README links and placeholder repo URL alone would gate a release. For a USENIX-grade academic artifact, W1 (publishing the security audit and USENIX submission notes) and W5 (headline reproducibility tiering contradicting the artifacts) are disqualifying until fixed — these are exactly what artifact evaluators and double-blind chairs scrutinize. The encouraging part is that the underlying documentation is accurate and well-maintained against the code; the remaining work is bounded, mechanical, and mostly about what to publish and fixing link paths — not rewriting content. Estimate: a focused half-day clears the blockers.

Static review only. No code was executed and no files other than this report were modified. Line numbers and counts reflect the working tree at review time.

docs.title: Response to Team Feedback on Architecture Review
docs.text: Date: June 7, 2026

docs.title: Where the Team Is Right (and Why)
1. Soft-reset leakage is real — verified in code
docs.text:
I checked the implementation. The team's concern is precisely correct.

baseline.py:105-108 — restore() calls chain_manager.restore() which calls JsonRpcClient.load_state() which calls anvil_loadState:

# chain_manager.py:222-227
def restore(self, chain_name: str, snapshot_path: Path) -> Any:
    state = json.loads(snapshot_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
    return JsonRpcClient(runtime.rpc_url).load_state(state)

# rpc.py:96-97
def load_state(self, state: Any) -> Any:
    return self.call("anvil_loadState", [state])

Anvil's anvil_loadState merges the state blob into the current chain state — it does not reset the chain. If state has been modified between the baseline capture and the restore (e.g., from a previous experiment run), any storage slots or accounts not covered by the snapshot blob will retain their current values.

This means: - Researcher A (medium reset: kill → restart → loadState) gets a clean fork + snapshot → deterministic state - Researcher B (soft reset: loadState only) gets snapshot merged onto dirty state → potentially non-deterministic

The fix: Change the default reset mode from "soft" to "medium" in reset_cmd.py:18, and add a loud warning in the docs. Alternatively, the ExperimentRunner.run() should use medium reset between matrix runs.

docs.title: 2. Silent degradation — confirmed, no artifacts_complete flag exists
docs.text:
I verified: the run_manifest.json (built by provenance.py:240-250) contains no completeness indicator. The manifest records outcome: "success" even if the instrumentation proxy silently failed to trace some transactions.

The specific code path that degrades silently:

# instrumentation.py:203-213
def _do_trace(self, tx_hash: str) -> None:
    receipt = self._wait_for_receipt(tx_hash)
    if not receipt:
        log.step(
            "auto-instrumentation: receipt not found for tx",
            phase="attack", action="auto_trace", status="failure",  # ← logged
            tx_hash=tx_hash[:18],
        )
        return  # ← but no flag set, no counter incremented

The failure is logged to run.jsonl (line 208-211, status="failure"), but: - The manifest doesn't check it - The publication gates don't check it - A researcher exporting the bundle has no machine-readable way to know artifacts are incomplete

The team's recommendation is exactly right. A artifacts_complete: true|false field on the manifest — computed by scanning run.jsonl for auto_trace entries with status="failure" — is cheap and high-impact.

docs.title: 3. Evidence.yaml pre-flight validation — feasible and high-value
docs.text:
Currently, evidence.yaml validation (in evidence.py:36-97) checks only structure: are the required sections present, is the fidelity level valid, are the lists non-empty?

It does not check: - Is fork_block actually the block the team claims? - Does the contract at affected_contracts[0] actually have code at that fork block? - Is the exploit_transaction hash valid?

This could be added as a pre-flight check in ExperimentRunner.run(), right after _check_publication_gates():

# Proposed: after line 156 in experiment.py
if publication_mode or self.config.publication.enabled:
    self._validate_evidence_against_chain(experiment)

The check would: 1. Connect to the chain (after forking) 2. Call eth_getCode(affected_contract, fork_block) — must return non-empty 3. Call eth_getBlockByNumber(fork_block) — must exist

This converts "the researcher claims it forked the right state" into "the testbed proves it." Exactly the kind of rigor that USENIX reviewers care about.

docs.title: 4. Tutorial gap — confirmed
docs.text: 29 docs, no TUTORIAL.md or "quickstart" path. The closest is USER_GUIDE.md (8KB, 12 sections) and ATTACK_PACK_GUIDE.md (34KB, very comprehensive but also very long). Neither says "run these 5 commands and you've reproduced a hack."

docs.title: 5. LLM budget gap — confirmed
docs.text: agent_loop.py has no spend cap. The LLMAgentPlugin.run() loop (lines 186-250) iterates until is_done() returns True or max_steps is hit. But max_steps controls tool calls, not API spend. A frontier model could burn $50 in a single experiment with no warning.

docs.title: Where I Agree With the Team's Pushbacks
1. Hermetic E2E with checked-in state fixture IS heavy
docs.text:
The team is right. An Anvil state dump can be 50-200MB. Checking that into git is impractical.

Their alternative — RPC response recording and replay — is better. Record the RPC calls during a real fork, save them as a fixture, and replay them in CI without a network connection. This gives: - Hermetic tests (no archive node needed) - Small fixtures (only the RPC responses that were actually used, not the entire chain state) - Fast execution (no real forking)

This is the pattern used by Foundry's own test suite and by vcrpy in the Python world.

docs.title: 2. Prompt injection from experiment YAML — low risk, agree
docs.text: Experiment YAML files are committed and reviewed. The attack vector requires a malicious file in the repo. Not worth prioritizing over correctness items.

docs.title: 3. Cross-chain correlation IDs — design trade-off, not a bug
docs.text:
The current counter-based approach (xchain-0001, xchain-0002, ...) is: - [ok] Human-readable during debugging - [ok] Deterministic within a single run (counter resets to 0 at run start) - [ok] Unique within a run (monotonic)

Deterministic content-addressed IDs (hash of source_chain + dest_chain + tx_hash + payload) would be: - [ok] Reproducible across re-runs - [fail] Harder to read in logs

Both are valid. The current approach is fine.

docs.title: 4. Schema validation at log-write time — too heavy, agree
docs.text:
Every emit() call would need a jsonschema.validate() call per stream. The instrumentation proxy processes transactions on background threads — adding schema validation there would slow down the critical path.

The lighter approach is better: validate at bundle-time (crucible export or crucible bundle verify). Check that every JSONL line has the expected fields for its stream type. Same coverage, zero runtime cost.

docs.title: Where the Team Adds Value Beyond the Review
1. crucible verify as the killer feature — AGREE, should be P1
docs.text:
This closes the reproducibility loop. The flow would be:

Researcher A: crucible experiment run → export → publish bundle
Researcher B: crucible verify <bundle.tar.gz>
              → unpack → re-run → hash-compare all artifacts
              → "PASS: 6/6 streams match original hashes"

This is what turns Crucible from "a tool that logs things" into "a tool that proves reproducibility." Implementation:

  1. crucible verify unpacks the bundle
  2. Reads run_manifest.json for the experiment config, topology, fork block
  3. Re-runs the experiment from scratch
  4. Compares the new log_integrity.json hashes against the bundle's hashes
  5. Reports pass/fail per stream

The core diffing infrastructure already exists in diff.py — this is mostly orchestration.

docs.title: 2. Per-attack fidelity table in BENCHMARKS.md — cheap and high-impact
docs.text:
Current BENCHMARKS.md says "2 at L2 + 4 scaffolds." A table like this would be much stronger:

Attack Fidelity Deterministic Chains Why Not Higher Nomad L3 [ok] Ethereum Full tx replay on fork Wormhole L2 [ok] Ethereum eth_call probe, no state change Poly Network L3 [ok] Ethereum-only BSC archive needed for L4 Horizon L1 [fail] — Scaffold only BNB Token Hub L1 [fail] — BSC archive needed Multichain L0 N/A — Narrative only

docs.title: 3. .env setup in tutorial — yes
docs.text: The .env.example exists but no doc says "get a free Alchemy/Infura key, put it here, run this." This is the #1 onboarding friction point.

docs.title: Prioritized Action Items
docs.text:
Based on the team's feedback and my code verification:

Priority Item Effort Impact P0 Add artifacts_complete flag to run_manifest.json 2 hours Prevents silent degradation from being invisible P0 Change default reset mode to medium 10 min Eliminates the soft-reset state leakage for new users P1 Write TUTORIAL.md (10-minute Nomad reproduction) 3 hours Dramatically lowers onboarding friction P1 Implement crucible verify <bundle> 1-2 days Closes the reproducibility loop — the killer feature P1 Evidence pre-flight validation (bytecode + fork block check) 4 hours Proves chain state, not just claims P2 RPC response recording for hermetic CI tests 2-3 days Makes CI meaningful without archive nodes P2 LLM spend cap in agent_loop.py 2 hours Prevents accidental $50+ API bills P2 Per-attack fidelity table in BENCHMARKS.md 30 min Pre-empts reviewer skepticism P3 Bundle-time JSONL schema validation 4 hours Catches field-type drift across versions

docs.title: Concrete Implementation: artifacts_complete Flag
docs.text:
The cheapest high-impact fix. In experiment.py, after stop_instrumentation():

# In the finally block of run(), after line 212
self.chain_manager.stop_instrumentation()

# Check for any failed auto-trace entries in run.jsonl
trace_failures = sum(
    1 for entry in log_mgr._root._load_jsonl("run.jsonl")
    if entry.get("action") == "auto_trace" and entry.get("status") == "failure"
)
artifacts_complete = trace_failures == 0

Then include it in the manifest:

# In _write_manifest, add to the return dict
return {
    ...
    "outcome": outcome,
    "artifacts_complete": artifacts_complete,
    "trace_failures": trace_failures,
    ...
}

And in the publication gates, reject if artifacts_complete is False:

# In publication.py PublicationGate
@staticmethod
def check_artifacts_complete(manifest: dict) -> GateResult:
    if not manifest.get("artifacts_complete", True):
        failures = manifest.get("trace_failures", 0)
        return GateResult(
            passed=False,
            failures=[f"{failures} transactions failed auto-instrumentation — artifacts are incomplete"],
        )
    return GateResult(passed=True)

docs.title: Concrete Implementation: Evidence Pre-Flight Check
docs.text:
# New method on ExperimentRunner
def _validate_evidence_against_chain(self, experiment: ExperimentConfig) -> None:
    """Verify evidence.yaml claims against live chain state."""
    evidence = load_evidence(self.workspace / "attacks" / experiment.attack)
    historical = evidence.get("historical_scope", {})
    contracts = historical.get("affected_contracts", [])

    for contract_entry in contracts:
        address = contract_entry.get("address", "")
        chain = contract_entry.get("chain", "ethereum")
        if not address:
            continue

        runtime = self.chain_manager.runtime_for(chain)
        client = JsonRpcClient(runtime.rpc_url)
        code = client.get_code(address)

        if not code or code == "0x":
            raise CrucibleError(
                f"Evidence claims {address} on {chain} is a contract, "
                f"but it has no code at the fork block. "
                f"This usually means the address is wrong (EOA vs contract)."
            )

This would have caught 4 of the 13 bugs found during the initial A-Z validation — the EOA-vs-contract address errors that caused the most debugging time.

docs.title: Crucible Testbed — Architectural & Code Review
docs.text: Date: June 8, 2026 Focus: Architectural solidity, code quality, reproducibility rigor, and publication readiness.

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary & Publication Verdict
docs.text:
After a deep architectural and codebase review of Crucible, the conclusion is clear: Crucible is ready to publish.

The codebase (~21k LOC) exhibits remarkable engineering discipline, particularly for an academic research instrument. The foundational architecture—specifically the separation of orchestration, scenarios, and plugin logic—is incredibly robust. More importantly, recent critical fixes (such as the artifacts_complete integrity checks, the transition to medium resets as defaults, and hard caps for LLM agent budgets) have successfully bridged the gap between a "very good tool" and a "USENIX-grade trusted research instrument."

Crucible delivers on its promise: providing a modular, CLI-first Foundry testbed where researchers can accurately model EVM-bridge attacks with automatic, publication-ready artifact collection.

Verdict: Ready for publication. The testbed proves its faithfulness and handles reproducibility artifacts with high rigor.

docs.title: 2. What I Feel Great About (Strengths)
docs.text:
[!TIP] The architectural design patterns used here are excellent and should be highlighted in any publication or documentation.

  1. The Instrumentation Proxy (The "Crown Jewel"): The decision to use a transparent RPC proxy (crucible/instrumentation.py) that sits between the attack plugins and the Anvil fork is brilliant. It completely decouples the attack logic from the artifact collection. Any attack—whether from a Python script, a Forge script, or manual commands—automatically has its gas, events, storage diffs, and balance changes captured deterministically.
  2. Reproducibility & Artifact Rigor: The output artifacts are incredibly rigorous. Emitting 6 typed JSONL streams, ensuring deterministic serialization, capturing UTC timestamps with millisecond precision, and performing SHA-256 integrity hashing across the board is exactly what academic reviewers want to see. The provenance tracking (recording Git commits, Foundry versions, and CLI arguments) is top-tier.
  3. Modular Plugin System: The base classes in sdk.py (AttackPlugin, BridgePlugin, AgentPlugin) provide a real "plug-and-play" experience. The instance-scoped registry design correctly avoids global state leakage.
  4. Recent Trust Fixes Implemented Correctly: I verified that the major trust gaps identified in previous audits have been successfully resolved:
  5. The Reset Mode default is properly set to medium (reset_cmd.py:18), solving the soft-reset state-leakage issue.
  6. The LLM Agent Loop now enforces a strict llm_budget_max_tokens cap (agent_loop.py), preventing runaway costs during autonomous attack explorations.
  7. The artifacts_complete flag is now computed and integrated into the publication gates, preventing silent artifact degradation.

docs.title: 3. Current Issues & What is Not Great (Weaknesses)
docs.text:
While the system is ready to publish, there are still a few areas that could be tightened up post-publication to reduce operational friction.

[!WARNING] These issues do not block publication, but they can occasionally degrade the developer experience during active research.

  1. Evidence Pre-Flight Validation is Still Structural: While the artifacts_complete check now ensures the pipeline didn't fail during the run, the initial verification of evidence.yaml claims (e.g., whether the declared affected_contracts actually have code at the pinned fork block) still relies heavily on the user being correct. If a user inputs an Externally Owned Account (EOA) instead of a contract, the testbed will still try to run it.
  2. High Dependence on Archive RPC Reliability: Because Anvil relies heavily on remote archive nodes to fork historical state, transient RPC errors (like rate limits or timeouts) can cause tests and runs to flake. The testbed handles this gracefully with retries, but a fully hermetic (offline) state fixture for CI would be a massive quality-of-life improvement.
  3. Docker sh -c vs --entrypoint bash Friction: The Docker implementation maps the crucible CLI as the entrypoint. As noted in the docs, this forces users to override the entrypoint (--entrypoint bash -c) anytime they want to chain commands or clean up .meta.json state files. This is slightly unergonomic for a CLI-first tool.

docs.title: 4. Suggestions for Improvement
docs.text:
To take Crucible from "ready to publish" to "flawless daily driver," consider the following roadmap items:

  1. Pre-flight Live State Assertions: Add a quick check right after ChainManager forks the state. Before the scenario engine runs, execute an eth_getCode and eth_getBlockByNumber via the RPC client for every contract declared in evidence.yaml. If the code is 0x, fail fast. This will save researchers hours of debugging copy-paste address errors.
  2. Implement crucible verify <bundle>: A dedicated verify command that takes an exported .tar.gz artifact bundle, unzips it, and independently re-verifies the SHA-256 hashes against the log_integrity.json would be the ultimate "mic drop" feature for reviewers. It closes the loop on reproducibility.
  3. Checked-In Golden State Fixtures for CI: Create a small, localized Anvil dump for at least one attack (e.g., Nomad). Commit this state fixture directly to the repo. This would allow your CI pipeline to run a full end-to-end attack reproduction without ever needing to hit an external archive RPC, guaranteeing that code changes don't break the attack logic.
  4. Automated EOA / Contract Checks during "Extract": If you continue building the --from-tx scaffolder, add an automated Etherscan/RPC lookup to confirm that the addresses pulled from the transaction are contracts, not EOAs.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Crucible is a remarkably complete and well-thought-out system. The integration of Foundry's forking capabilities with a robust Python orchestration and instrumentation layer achieves exactly what it set out to do. The team has done an excellent job addressing the critical trust and correctness bugs. Ship it.
